Radio Operations (217) -

Comply with All Rules and Regulations
Critical Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Live up to every rule and regulation.

Details:
• Follow all FCC or local regulations for amateur radio operations.
• Non-compliance can result in legal action and loss of license.
regulations,compliance,amateur radio
Obtain a License Before Transmitting
Critical Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not attempt to send until you get your license.

Details:
• Operating without a valid license is illegal and can result in fines or equipment seizure.
• Ensure you are fully licensed before transmitting on any frequency.
license,transmitting,legal
Emergency Radio Procedures
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Emergency Radio Procedures

• Use the distress frequency (Channel 0) for immediate assistance. This is the standard channel used in emergencies.
• Repeat your message if no response is received. Ensure that your signal is being...
Emergency Radio Procedures

• Use the distress frequency (Channel 0) for immediate assistance. This is the standard channel used in emergencies.
• Repeat your message if no response is received. Ensure that your signal is being transmitted clearly and consistently.
• Provide detailed information about your location, situation, and needs. Include coordinates, number of people involved, and any medical conditions or injuries.
• Stay on the frequency until help arrives. Do not change channels unless instructed by a rescue team.
• Monitor other frequencies for updates or instructions from authorities. Stay informed about the status of your situation and any changes in the rescue plan.

Important Notes:
• Keep your voice steady and avoid panic when transmitting.
• If you are unable to communicate, use visual signals (e.g., mirrors, flares) to attract attention.
emergency radio, survival communication, distress call
Interrupt an Ongoing Conversation on Radio (Emergency Use Only)
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Interrupt an ongoing conversation to communicate an urgent message.

Steps:
1. Begin your transmission with the words "Priority! Priority!" or "Break Break."
2. This signals that your message is more urgent than the...
Objective: Interrupt an ongoing conversation to communicate an urgent message.

Steps:
1. Begin your transmission with the words "Priority! Priority!" or "Break Break."
2. This signals that your message is more urgent than the current conversation.
3. Clearly state your callsign and the nature of your message.
4. Keep your communication as brief and to-the-point as possible to minimize disruption.

Notes:
• Use this method only in emergencies, as it may be considered rude or disruptive otherwise.
• Be prepared for other users on the channel to express frustration due to the interruption.
• Always clarify that you are interrupting for a valid reason.
interrupt radio conversation, emergency communication, priority call, break break
Adjust RF Gain for Optimal Signal Reception
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Adjust the RF gain to ensure clear and undistorted signal reception.

Procedure:
1. Start by setting the RF gain to maximum.
2. Gradually reduce the RF gain until you achieve the best sound quality.
3. If the RF gain is set...
Objective: Adjust the RF gain to ensure clear and undistorted signal reception.

Procedure:
1. Start by setting the RF gain to maximum.
2. Gradually reduce the RF gain until you achieve the best sound quality.
3. If the RF gain is set too high, signals from nearby or strong stations may become distorted or intermittent.
4. Be cautious: if a weak signal calls in and the RF gain is turned down, it may not be heard.
5. Learn which stations on your network require higher or lower RF gain settings through experience.
6. Always begin with maximum RF gain and adjust as necessary for each station.
RF gain adjustment, signal distortion, transceiver settings
Avoid Improper Spark Gap Connections to Aerial and Ground
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Never connect one side of the spark gap to the aerial wire and the other side to the ground. This is explicitly prohibited by regulatory authorities for safety reasons.
spark gap, aerial wire, ground connection
Avoid Overloading Oscillation Vacuum Tubes
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not overload an oscillation vacuum tube as it will greatly shorten its life. Use two in parallel.

Details:
• This prevents overheating and damage to the tubes.
• Always use a second tube in parallel for redundancy and longevity.
vacuum tube,overloading,lifespan
Avoid Proximity to Overhead Wires
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not let your aerial come too close to overhead wires of any kind.

Details:
• This can cause electrical hazards and signal interference.
• Maintain a safe distance from power lines, telephone lines, or other overhead cables.
aerial,wires,safety
Avoid Running Aerial Parallel to Electric Wires
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not run your aerial directly under, over, or parallel with electric light or other wires.

Details:
• This can cause signal interference and potential electrical hazards.
• Route the aerial away from power lines and other conductive...
Do not run your aerial directly under, over, or parallel with electric light or other wires.

Details:
• This can cause signal interference and potential electrical hazards.
• Route the aerial away from power lines and other conductive materials.
aerial,wires,interference
Disconnect Solar-Powered Transceivers from Batteries When Not in Use
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Transceivers powered by solar-charged batteries should be disconnected from the battery when not in use.
2. This precaution is necessary to prevent lightning strikes on solar panels from being conducted through the battery...
Procedure:
1. Transceivers powered by solar-charged batteries should be disconnected from the battery when not in use.
2. This precaution is necessary to prevent lightning strikes on solar panels from being conducted through the battery and into the transceiver.
3. Ensure that this procedure is followed consistently, especially during thunderstorms or periods of high electrical activity.
solar battery safety, lightning protection, transceiver maintenance
Disconnect Transceiver from Aerial During Thunderstorms
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Always disconnect the transceiver from the aerial after use.
2. Never use the transceiver during a thunderstorm.
3. Ensure that the radio operator has been given these instructions and understands their importance.
4....
Procedure:
1. Always disconnect the transceiver from the aerial after use.
2. Never use the transceiver during a thunderstorm.
3. Ensure that the radio operator has been given these instructions and understands their importance.
4. Display these instructions in a prominent position, using as many languages as necessary to ensure clarity for all users.
5. After disconnecting the aerial, it can be connected to earth or left "floating" (not connected to anything), but it should never be left within 30 cm of the transceiver.
lightning protection, aerial disconnection, radio safety, thunderstorm protocol
Ensure Proper Grounding for Aerial Systems
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Have an outside ground connected to your aerial system.

Details:
• The ground should be a low-resistance connection to the earth.
• Avoid using water pipes as the sole grounding point; use dedicated grounding rods if necessary.
grounding,aerial,system
Establish Emergency Communications Procedures
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Define the protocols for initiating emergency communications during a crisis or unexpected event.
2. Determine the specific frequencies and methods to be used in an emergency situation.
3. Identify who is responsible for...
Procedure:

1. Define the protocols for initiating emergency communications during a crisis or unexpected event.
2. Determine the specific frequencies and methods to be used in an emergency situation.
3. Identify who is responsible for activating emergency communication procedures.
4. Ensure all personnel are trained on these procedures and understand their roles during emergencies.
5. Document the procedures clearly and ensure they are accessible to all relevant individuals.

Note: Emergency communications must be reliable and swift to prevent delays in critical decision-making.
emergency,communication,procedure
Establish Long-Range Radio Communication in Northern Operations
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Use radio communication at longer ranges than conventional operations due to the dispersed nature of military operations in northern regions.
2. Consult the *Communication Supplement on Facilities North of 60°* prepared by...
Procedure:

1. Use radio communication at longer ranges than conventional operations due to the dispersed nature of military operations in northern regions.
2. Consult the *Communication Supplement on Facilities North of 60°* prepared by Northern Region Headquarters (NRHQ) for guidance on communication facilities.
3. Utilize aircraft as airborne relays and air dispatch services to overcome communication challenges caused by terrain and extreme climatic conditions.
4. Ensure communication liaison with other agencies to enhance operating capabilities in the absence of infrastructure.
radio,communication,northern operations,long range,military
Establishing a Permanent Base Station with Private Wire
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Contact local telephone administration: Arrange for the installation of a private wire to connect the base station and control unit.
2. Pay installation and maintenance fees: Be prepared to cover the costs associated with...
Procedure:

1. Contact local telephone administration: Arrange for the installation of a private wire to connect the base station and control unit.
2. Pay installation and maintenance fees: Be prepared to cover the costs associated with setting up and maintaining the line.
3. Install the private wire: Connect the base station (located on high ground) to the control unit (in an accessible location such as a building or office).
4. Test the system: Ensure that communication is clear and reliable between both ends of the connection.

Notes:
• This method is used by organizations like HM Coastguard and air traffic control authorities for long-range communications.
• The use of private wires ensures consistent and reliable signal transmission.
private wire, permanent setup, radio infrastructure, base station installation
Evaluate Transceiver for Ease of Maintenance and Modification
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure the transceiver is easy to maintain and modify as needed.

Procedure:
1. Check Dismantling Capability: Determine if the transceiver can be easily dismantled for servicing. Some models (e.g., TWC from U.S.A. or AEL from...
Objective: Ensure the transceiver is easy to maintain and modify as needed.

Procedure:
1. Check Dismantling Capability: Determine if the transceiver can be easily dismantled for servicing. Some models (e.g., TWC from U.S.A. or AEL from U.K.) use plug-in circuit boards that allow simple replacement of faulty components.
2. Fault Diagnosis and Replacement: If the transceiver uses modular boards, it may be possible to identify and replace a faulty board without requiring advanced technical knowledge. This simplifies repairs and reduces downtime.
3. Modification Potential: Assess whether the transceiver can be modified (e.g., adding an additional frequency). Some models require only a single circuit board addition (e.g., Traeger from Australia), while others may need soldering multiple components into various parts of the unit.
4. Verify Technical Documentation: Ensure that the technical manual includes full circuit diagrams, testing procedures, and numerical values for all components. This is essential for troubleshooting and modifications.
5. Request Detailed Information During Purchase: If detailed information is not included in the manual, request it as part of the purchase arrangement to avoid difficulties later.
maintenance, modification, circuit board, technical manual, radio repair
Examples of Radio Transmissions
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Examples of Radio Transmissions

• Distress Call: "This is [Your Name] on Channel 0. I am in distress at coordinates [Latitude, Longitude]. Please send assistance immediately."
• Request for Help: "This is [Your Name] on Channel 0. I...
Examples of Radio Transmissions

• Distress Call: "This is [Your Name] on Channel 0. I am in distress at coordinates [Latitude, Longitude]. Please send assistance immediately."
• Request for Help: "This is [Your Name] on Channel 0. I need help with a medical emergency at coordinates [Latitude, Longitude]."
• Location Update: "This is [Your Name] on Channel 0. I am now located at coordinates [Latitude, Longitude]. I am safe but require assistance."
• General Communication: "This is [Your Name] on Channel 0. I am traveling along the [Trail Name]. I will be passing through [Location] in approximately [Time]."

Important Notes:
• Always provide your name, location, and situation when transmitting.
• Use clear and concise language to ensure effective communication.
radio examples, emergency transmission, survival gear
Final Inspection and Installation Notes
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Inspect equipment: Take a final look around the installation site to ensure all equipment is undisturbed and in its correct position.
2. Check external cables: Verify that external cables have not been moved or disturbed...
Procedure:

1. Inspect equipment: Take a final look around the installation site to ensure all equipment is undisturbed and in its correct position.
2. Check external cables: Verify that external cables have not been moved or disturbed by onlookers or unauthorized personnel.
3. Collect tools and test equipment: Gather all tools, test equipment, and any spare parts used during installation.
4. Document non-standard features: Make final notes in your records regarding any non-standard aspects of the installation for future reference and maintenance planning.
5. Leave operating instructions: Ensure that all necessary information (e.g., how to use the transceiver, battery care) is left with the operator or person in charge in a secure and accessible location.
6. Educate on battery usage:
- Instruct operators on how to top up the acid level in the battery.
- Explain the daily usage limits for the radio to maintain full battery charge.
7. Address common questions: Be prepared to answer questions such as:
- *Can we run a light off the radio battery in the evenings?* → No, this would drain the battery and risk damaging the equipment.
- *Can we charge another battery off the solar panel?* → No, this may compromise the power supply for the radio system.
8. Emphasize proper usage: Stress that misuse of the radio battery (e.g., using it for personal devices) can lead to damage and reduce its lifespan.
9. Ensure accountability: Make sure operators understand the importance of maintaining a reliable radio link by following proper procedures and respecting power source limitations.

Notes:
• Proper documentation and operator education are essential for long-term system reliability.
• Unauthorized use of equipment can lead to significant operational failures.
installation inspection,battery maintenance,operator education
Handle Multiple SELCALL Codes for a Single Individual in an Emergency Scenario
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Assign multiple SELCALL codes to the individual based on their roles and locations. For example, one code may be used when they are acting as the Duty Officer, while another is used when they are serving as the First...
Procedure:

1. Assign multiple SELCALL codes to the individual based on their roles and locations. For example, one code may be used when they are acting as the Duty Officer, while another is used when they are serving as the First Officer.
2. Ensure that all radios associated with these roles have the correct SELCALL codes programmed in. This includes both handheld and mobile radios.
3. Carry multiple radios if necessary. In situations where an individual may be assigned to multiple roles or locations, they should carry more than one radio with different SELCALL codes programmed into them.
4. Defeat the SELCALL system on a secondary radio when needed. If the primary radio is not available (e.g., due to being inside a vehicle), use the 'def' button or menu option on another radio to disable SELCALL and allow it to receive calls intended for other codes.
5. Verify that all radios are functioning properly in different environments. For example, handheld radios may have limited range and signal strength when used inside vehicles due to metal interference, so ensure mobile radios with better antennas are available for use in such scenarios.
multiple SELCALL codes, emergency roles, radio functionality
Implement Fallback Communication Strategies for Repeaters
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify alternative communication methods: Prepare backup systems such as direct simplex mode or other repeater locations in case the primary repeater fails.
2. Ensure redundancy: Have at least one additional repeater or...
Procedure:

1. Identify alternative communication methods: Prepare backup systems such as direct simplex mode or other repeater locations in case the primary repeater fails.
2. Ensure redundancy: Have at least one additional repeater or a portable base station available to maintain communication during outages.
3. Train users on fallback procedures: Ensure all operators know how to switch to backup systems if the main repeater is unavailable due to power loss, theft, or technical failure.
4. Regular maintenance checks: Assign personnel to monitor and service the repeater at least every 8 hours (e.g., battery replacement, fuel top-up for generators).

Details:
• Repeaters are vulnerable to failure, so having a fallback strategy is essential.
• If the repeater fails, all radios relying on it will become non-functional until the issue is resolved.
• Users may be confused if handhelds go out of the repeater’s coverage area (e.g., driving over hills or into another town).
fallback strategy,emergency communication,failure plan,repeater backup,radio redundancy
Install Lightning Protection for Receiving Sets
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Use a lightning arrester or better, a lightning switch, for your receiving set.

Details:
• This protects the equipment from electrical surges caused by lightning strikes.
• Ensure the device is properly grounded.
lightning,protection,receiving set,arrester
Install Lightning Switch for Transmitting Sets
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Use a lightning switch with your transmitting set.

Details:
• This provides additional protection against lightning strikes during transmission.
• Ensure the switch is properly grounded and tested regularly.
lightning,switch,transmitting set
Install a Two-State LED Power Indicator for Battery Monitoring
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Monitor battery voltage levels using an LED indicator.

Procedure:
1. Replace the simple power light with a two-state LED (indicator light):
- The LED should be green when the +12V DC supply is above 11.6 volts.
- It...
Objective: Monitor battery voltage levels using an LED indicator.

Procedure:
1. Replace the simple power light with a two-state LED (indicator light):
- The LED should be green when the +12V DC supply is above 11.6 volts.
- It should switch to flashing red when the voltage drops below 11.6 volts.
2. Use supporting circuitry, such as transistors and zener diodes, to operate the LED indicator.
3. A competent radio engineer can design this circuit based on a reference (e.g., Fig.5.7 in the handbook).
4. The LED used by the author was Radio Spares (RS) 588-112.
5. This setup is especially useful when operating from a battery and no voltmeter is available.
LED power indicator, battery monitoring, voltage detection, radio circuitry
Leave the Net in Alphanumeric Sequence
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Purpose: Properly exit a radio net following established procedures.

Steps:
1. When you receive a close down order from the NCS (Net Control Station), respond in alphanumeric sequence.
2. Example of closing the net:
- NCS: NET THIS...
Purpose: Properly exit a radio net following established procedures.

Steps:
1. When you receive a close down order from the NCS (Net Control Station), respond in alphanumeric sequence.
2. Example of closing the net:
- NCS: NET THIS IS E3E CLOSE DOWN OVER
- Position 1: E3E (NCS) THIS IS E13 ROGER OUT
- Position 2: E3E (NCS) THIS IS E39 ROGER OUT
- Position 3: E3E (NCS) THIS IS E46 ROGER OUT
3. Ensure that all positions in the net acknowledge and exit in sequence.
4. The NCS confirms the closure of the net.
net closure, alphanumeric sequence, radio communication
Limit Power Output Near Naval Stations
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not use an input of more than 1/2 a kilowatt if you live within 5 nautical miles of a naval station.

Details:
• This prevents interference with military communications.
• Use a power meter to ensure compliance.
power output,naval stations,regulations
Limit Wave Length for Restricted Licenses
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not send on more than a 200-meter wave if you have a restricted or general amateur license.

Details:
• This is a regulatory requirement to prevent interference with other services.
• Use a frequency meter to ensure compliance.
wave length,license,regulations
Maintain Continuous Communication
High Priority Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Objective: Ensure communication is maintained with higher headquarters, OPs, and within the unit.

Steps:
1. Maintain continuous communication with higher headquarters, observation posts (OPs), and within the unit.
2. Rotate roles...
Objective: Ensure communication is maintained with higher headquarters, OPs, and within the unit.

Steps:
1. Maintain continuous communication with higher headquarters, observation posts (OPs), and within the unit.
2. Rotate roles between patrol’s Radio Telephone Operators (RTOs) to allow for:
- Continuous radio monitoring
- Radio maintenance
- Serving as runners for the PL
- Conducting other priorities of work
communication, radio monitoring, RTO rotation, continuous communication
Mitigate Communication Disruptions from Environmental Factors
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Be aware that high-frequency (HF) transmission and reception can be affected by magnetic storms, aurora borealis, and ionospheric disturbances, potentially causing blackouts lasting hours or days.
2. Ensure proper installation and...
Procedure:

1. Be aware that high-frequency (HF) transmission and reception can be affected by magnetic storms, aurora borealis, and ionospheric disturbances, potentially causing blackouts lasting hours or days.
2. Ensure proper installation and operating procedures are followed to maintain HF communication capabilities under most conditions.
3. Address the combined effects of terrain, cold, ice, dampness, and dust on communication equipment by allocating maximum effort from operators and repair personnel for maintenance and supply.
HF communication,environmental factors,maintenance,terrain,cold

Table:

|Numeral|Spoken As|Numeral|Spoken As|

|1|WUN|6|SIX|

number pronunciation, radio communications, phonetic numbers
Operate Radio Communications in Combat Situations
High Priority Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
### Turn Off Locator Beacon
• Disable the locator beacon to avoid detection.

### Keep Locator Beacon with You
• Carry the disabled locator beacon with you to supplement radio communications.

### Follow On/Off Operations Plans
• Adhere to plans or...
### Turn Off Locator Beacon
• Disable the locator beacon to avoid detection.

### Keep Locator Beacon with You
• Carry the disabled locator beacon with you to supplement radio communications.

### Follow On/Off Operations Plans
• Adhere to plans or orders for on/off operations.
combat, locator beacon, radio communications
Operate SINCGARS Single-Channel (SC)
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Conditions: Given an operational single-channel ground and airborne radio system (SINCGARS), KYK-13/TSEC with keys or AN/CYZ-10, C-11291 CM, distant station, TM 11-5820-890-10-8, TM 11-5820-890-10-3, TM 11-5820-890-10-1, ACP 125 US Suppl-1, DA...
Conditions: Given an operational single-channel ground and airborne radio system (SINCGARS), KYK-13/TSEC with keys or AN/CYZ-10, C-11291 CM, distant station, TM 11-5820-890-10-8, TM 11-5820-890-10-3, TM 11-5820-890-10-1, ACP 125 US Suppl-1, DA Pam 738-750, FM 24-19, FM 24-18, and unit signal operation instructions (SOI) or ANCD with SOI data loaded.

Standards: Conduct a secure communications check in single channel (SC) mode with a distant station and change the radio functions using the control monitor.

### Performance Steps:

1. Perform starting procedures.
2. Load the traffic encryption key (TEK).
3. Enter the net.
- a. Use the correct procedures.
- b. Conduct a secure communications check.
4. Prepare the control monitor for operation.
5. Change the radio functions using the control monitor.
6. Exit the net.
7. Perform stopping procedures.

### Evaluation Preparation:
• Setup: Ensure that the radio set is complete and operational with (CM) installed on the radio.
• Brief Soldier: Tell the Soldier all performance measures must be completed correctly within 20 minutes. All performance measures must be done in sequence.
SINCGARS, single-channel radio, secure communications check, control monitor, traffic encryption key, TEK, net entry, radio operation
Operating Procedures for Radio Communications
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Operating Procedures for Radio Communications

1. Turn on the radio and check the battery level. Ensure it is fully charged before use.
2. Select an appropriate frequency channel. Use standard channels (e.g., Channel 0) for emergency...
Operating Procedures for Radio Communications

1. Turn on the radio and check the battery level. Ensure it is fully charged before use.
2. Select an appropriate frequency channel. Use standard channels (e.g., Channel 0) for emergency communication.
3. Press the transmit button and speak clearly. Keep your voice steady and avoid shouting or whispering.
4. Wait for a response before transmitting again. Allow time for the other party to respond before sending another message.
5. Keep messages short and to the point. Provide essential information such as your location, situation, and request for assistance.
6. Monitor other channels for emergency signals. Stay alert for distress calls or other important communications.
7. Turn off the radio when not in use to conserve battery life. Avoid unnecessary transmissions that could drain power quickly.
radio operating, communication procedures, survival gear
Optimize Radio Frequency Selection for Northern Operations
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Emphasize the selection and use of proper frequencies for radio communication in northern latitudes.
2. Use radio propagation graphs, charts, and prediction data to determine optimal frequencies for communication.
3. Employ...
Procedure:

1. Emphasize the selection and use of proper frequencies for radio communication in northern latitudes.
2. Use radio propagation graphs, charts, and prediction data to determine optimal frequencies for communication.
3. Employ channel evaluation techniques on northern HF circuits if equipment allows.
4. If sophisticated sounding equipment is unavailable, implement simultaneous transmissions on several frequencies (similar to the "Fleet Broadcast") from a base station to allow outstations and mobile units to select the best frequency.
frequency selection,propagation graphs,HF communication,northern operations
Prepare SINCGARS (Manpack) for Operation
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Purpose: Correctly prepare and assemble a SINCGARS manpack radio for operational use.

Performance Steps:
1. Install the battery: Use one of the following batteries:
- Rechargeable: BB-390 A/U or BB-590/U
- Nonrechargeable:...
Purpose: Correctly prepare and assemble a SINCGARS manpack radio for operational use.

Performance Steps:
1. Install the battery: Use one of the following batteries:
- Rechargeable: BB-390 A/U or BB-590/U
- Nonrechargeable: BB-5590/U (Lithium)
2. Connect the battery case to the radio set.
- Refer to TM 11-5820-890-10-8, page 2-14 for instructions.
3. Assemble the radio and pack frame: Assemble the pack and fasten the assembled radio into the carrying case.
- Refer to TM 11-5820-890-10-8 for assembly details.
4. Install the antenna: Connect the AS-3683/PRC to the radio.
- Refer to TM 11-5820-890-10-8, page 5-3 for instructions.
5. Connect the handset H-250/U: Connect the handset or handheld remote control radio device (HRCRD) C-12493/U.
- Refer to TM 11-5820-890-10-8, page 3-12 for instructions.
6. Preset function controls: Adjust the function controls on the radio for operation.
- Refer to TM 11-5820-890-10-8, page 5-1 for setup details.
sincgars setup, manpack radio, battery installation, antenna connection
Prepare for Radio Transmissions in Combat
High Priority Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
### Locate Spare Radio and Batteries
• Find a spare radio and batteries. Keep them warm and dry.

### Use Concealment Sites
• Utilize concealment sites that optimize line of sight (LOS) for transmissions.

### Face Recovery Asset
• Orient the...
### Locate Spare Radio and Batteries
• Find a spare radio and batteries. Keep them warm and dry.

### Use Concealment Sites
• Utilize concealment sites that optimize line of sight (LOS) for transmissions.

### Face Recovery Asset
• Orient the antenna towards the recovery asset.

### Antenna Positioning
• Keep the antenna perpendicular to the intended receiver (refer to Figure III-1).

### Avoid Grounding the Antenna
• DO NOT ground the antenna by placing fingers on it or attaching objects like a space blanket, vegetation, etc.

### Keep Transmissions Short
• Limit transmissions to 3-5 seconds maximum. Use data burst if available.

### Move After Transmission
• In combat situations, move after each transmission if possible.

### Transmit in the Blind
• If transmitting without visual contact, ensure a clear LOS towards the equator.

### Terrain Masking
• Use terrain masking to hinder enemy direction finding.
transmission techniques, combat radio use
Radio Communications - Citizen Band Radio
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Citizen Band (CB) Radio

• Use CB radio for short-range communication. It is ideal for use in remote areas where mobile networks are unavailable.
• Ensure your CB radio is fully charged and operational before departure. Carry spare...
Citizen Band (CB) Radio

• Use CB radio for short-range communication. It is ideal for use in remote areas where mobile networks are unavailable.
• Ensure your CB radio is fully charged and operational before departure. Carry spare batteries if possible.
• Familiarize yourself with the operation of your CB radio. Know how to adjust frequencies, send and receive signals, and troubleshoot common issues.
• Use standard CB radio channels for emergency communication. Channel 0 (17.925 MHz) is commonly used for distress calls.

Important Notes:
• CB radio has a limited range; it may not be effective in mountainous or remote areas.
• Always use clear and concise language when communicating over the radio.
citizen band, radio communication, survival gear
Radio Operator Duties During Execution
High Priority Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Title: Radio Operator Duties During Execution

Procedure:
1. Serve as en route recorder during all phases of the mission.
2. Record all enemy contact and report it to higher in a SALUTE format (S-Size, A-Area, L-Locations, U-Unit, T-Time,...
Title: Radio Operator Duties During Execution

Procedure:
1. Serve as en route recorder during all phases of the mission.
2. Record all enemy contact and report it to higher in a SALUTE format (S-Size, A-Area, L-Locations, U-Unit, T-Time, E-Equipment).
3. Report all OPSKEDs (Operational Sensitive Key Data) to higher headquarters.
4. Consolidate and record all PIR (Priority Intelligence Requirements).
radio operator, execution, salute format, opsked, pir
Setting Up a Base Station for Optimal Antenna Placement
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select an optimal location: Choose a spot that allows the antenna to have maximum height and line-of-sight transmission capabilities.
2. Improvise a mast if necessary: Use available materials (e.g., metal poles, trees) to...
Procedure:

1. Select an optimal location: Choose a spot that allows the antenna to have maximum height and line-of-sight transmission capabilities.
2. Improvise a mast if necessary: Use available materials (e.g., metal poles, trees) to elevate the antenna for better signal strength.
3. Consider staff availability: If the ideal location is not near where staff are stationed, consider using a remote-controlled base station setup.
4. Use a remote controller: Place the control unit in an area where staff can monitor and manage communications effectively.
5. Connect via cable: Use ordinary telephone cables or temporary field telephone cabling to link the base station to the remote controller.
6. For permanent setups: Arrange for a private wire from local telephone administration to connect the base station and control unit permanently.

Notes:
• The term *base station* refers to its usage, not the equipment itself.
• A modest or improvised setup can be just as effective as expensive equipment if used properly.
base station, antenna placement, remote control, radio operations, communications setup
Setting Up a Crossband Repeater System Using a Dual-Band Mobile Radio
High Priority Source: Survival Communications Primer
Objective: Set up a crossband repeater system using a dual-band mobile radio for extended communication range in canyonland terrain.

### Required Equipment:
• Dual-band mobile radio (e.g., Yaesu FT-8800)
• **Power supply or 12V deep cycle...
Objective: Set up a crossband repeater system using a dual-band mobile radio for extended communication range in canyonland terrain.

### Required Equipment:
• Dual-band mobile radio (e.g., Yaesu FT-8800)
• Power supply or 12V deep cycle battery
• Dual-band base antenna
• Coaxial feed line (coax)

### Steps to Set Up:
1. Select a Dual-Band Mobile Radio: Choose a radio that supports crossband repeating functionality, such as the Yaesu FT-8800.
2. Install Power Supply: Connect a 12V power supply or deep cycle battery (AGM/Gel cell) to the mobile radio for continuous operation.
3. **Mount Antenna and Feed Line:
- Install a dual-band base antenna on your vehicle or fixed location.
- Run coaxial feed line from the antenna to the radio, ensuring it is properly secured and routed to avoid damage.
4. Program the Radio: Use the owner’s manual to configure the mobile radio for crossband repeating functionality (e.g., set up frequency pairs for 2m and 70cm bands).
5. **Test the System:
- Power on the radio and verify that it is transmitting and receiving signals across both bands.
- Use handheld transceivers (HTs) to test communication range in canyonland terrain.

### Notes:
• This system allows your HTs to communicate over extended distances by using the mobile radio as a repeater.
• Ensure the antenna is properly tuned for optimal performance in low-foliage, canyon-like environments.
crossband repeater,dual-band mobile radio,Yaesu FT-8800,antenna setup,coaxial feed line
Understand Auroral Effects on Radio Communication
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Recognize that the reflecting properties of the ionosphere are influenced by the density and uniformity of Earth's magnetic field, which is affected by solar position and radiation activity.
2. Understand that sky wave...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that the reflecting properties of the ionosphere are influenced by the density and uniformity of Earth's magnetic field, which is affected by solar position and radiation activity.
2. Understand that sky wave communication is subject to increased absorption and diffusion during intense auroral activity, leading to signal fading, distortion, or short-term blackouts.
3. Note that the greatest auroral activity occurs between latitudes 60° and 70° North Geomagnetic Latitude, with peak occurrences every 27–28 days, especially during fall/winter periods.
4. Be aware that all frequencies up to HF are affected by auroras but not uniformly; some frequencies will experience greater disruption than others.
auroral effects,ionosphere,solar activity,signal distortion
Understand Radio Signal Propagation Methods
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Recognize that radio signals can be transmitted via two methods: ground wave (direct path between antennae) or sky wave (reflected from the ionosphere).
2. Understand that the reflective power of the ionosphere varies with...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that radio signals can be transmitted via two methods: ground wave (direct path between antennae) or sky wave (reflected from the ionosphere).
2. Understand that the reflective power of the ionosphere varies with frequency and time of day, which imposes limitations on usable frequencies.
3. Note that at higher frequencies (over 40 MHz), most energy passes through the ionosphere without reflection.
4. Tactical radio equipment operating in the line-of-sight band (30 MHz and upwards) is generally not affected by auroral activity, though increased ionization during auroras may occasionally extend the range of tactical FM sets.
signal propagation,ground wave,sky wave,frequency limitations
Use Group Call SELCALL to Reach a Specific Individual in an Emergency
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify the group call SELCALL code assigned to the target individual's radio group. This is typically programmed into all radios within that specific group.
2. Send the group call SELCALL code from your transmitter. This...
Procedure:

1. Identify the group call SELCALL code assigned to the target individual's radio group. This is typically programmed into all radios within that specific group.
2. Send the group call SELCALL code from your transmitter. This will activate all mobiles in the designated group, opening their speakers and allowing them to hear the transmission.
3. Verbally state the callsign and name of the person required. After sending the group call SELCALL, clearly announce the individual's callsign and name so that they can identify themselves.
4. Wait for the intended recipient to respond. Once the correct individual answers, they should state the SELCALL code of the radio they are currently using.
5. Reconfigure the call to use only their specific SELCALL code. This will close off other radios in the group and allow a private conversation between you and the intended recipient.
group call SELCALL, emergency communication, radio group activation
Use Hot-Wire Ammeter in Aerial Circuit
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not operate a transmitting set without a hot-wire ammeter in the aerial.

Details:
• This allows you to monitor current flow and prevent overloading.
• Ensure the ammeter is properly calibrated and installed.
hot-wire ammeter,aerial,monitoring
Use Lightning Arrestors for Additional Protection
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Install gas discharge lightning arrestors into the coaxial cable between the aerial and transceiver.
2. These devices provide additional protection against lightning strikes passing through the aerial to the transceiver.
3....
Procedure:
1. Install gas discharge lightning arrestors into the coaxial cable between the aerial and transceiver.
2. These devices provide additional protection against lightning strikes passing through the aerial to the transceiver.
3. Ensure that the arrestor is properly connected and maintained for optimal performance.
lightning protection, gas discharge arrestors, coaxial cable safety
Use Safety Spark Gap for High Voltage Circuits
High Priority Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Use a safety spark gap between the grid and the filament terminals where the plate potential is above 2,000 volts.

Details:
• This prevents electrical arcing and damage to sensitive components.
• Ensure the spark gap is properly installed...
Use a safety spark gap between the grid and the filament terminals where the plate potential is above 2,000 volts.

Details:
• This prevents electrical arcing and damage to sensitive components.
• Ensure the spark gap is properly installed and tested regularly.
safety spark gap,voltage,protection
Using HTs with a Mobile Repeater System for Long-Range Communication
High Priority Source: Survival Communications Primer
Objective: Use handheld transceivers (HTs) in conjunction with a mobile repeater system to achieve long-range communication.

### Required Equipment:
• Dual-band mobile radio (e.g., Yaesu FT-8800)
• **12V power supply or deep cycle battery...
Objective: Use handheld transceivers (HTs) in conjunction with a mobile repeater system to achieve long-range communication.

### Required Equipment:
• Dual-band mobile radio (e.g., Yaesu FT-8800)
• 12V power supply or deep cycle battery (AGM/Gel cell)
• Dual-band base antenna
• Coaxial feed line (coax)
• HTs (e.g., Yaesu FT-60R)

### Steps to Use:
1. Set Up Mobile Repeater System: Ensure the mobile radio is configured as a crossband repeater with dual-band operation, and that it is powered by a 12V power supply or deep cycle battery.
2. **Install Antenna and Feed Line:
- Mount a dual-band base antenna on your vehicle or fixed location.
- Connect the coaxial feed line from the antenna to the mobile radio.
3. **Program HTs for Dual-Band Operation:
- Configure your HTs (e.g., Yaesu FT-60R) to use both 2m and 70cm bands, ensuring they are set up to communicate with the mobile repeater system.
4. **Test Communication Range:
- Power on the mobile radio and HTs.
- Test communication range in canyonland terrain by transmitting from an HT and receiving on another HT via the mobile repeater system.

### Notes:
• This setup allows you to use HTs for long-range communication without needing a dedicated base station repeater.
• Ensure that both the mobile radio and HTs are properly programmed and tuned for optimal performance in low-foliage, canyon-like environments.
HT communication,mobile repeater,Yaesu FT-60R,dual-band operation,long-range
Utilize VHF and Microwave Equipment for Northern Communication
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Use very high frequency (VHF) and microwave radio-relay equipment for short-range communication in northern latitudes.
2. Be aware that auroral reflected signals may disrupt VHF communications, especially amplitude modulated (AM)...
Procedure:

1. Use very high frequency (VHF) and microwave radio-relay equipment for short-range communication in northern latitudes.
2. Be aware that auroral reflected signals may disrupt VHF communications, especially amplitude modulated (AM) voice transmissions.
3. Prioritize the use of FM-based tactical radio sets in areas affected by auroras due to their relative immunity and potential range extension during high ionization periods.
VHF communication,microwave,aurora effects,FM radio
Adjust Squelch Control for Optimal Reception
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure the squelch control is set to an appropriate level for reliable reception.

Procedure:
1. Locate the squelch control on the front panel of the transceiver.
2. If the squelch is set too high, it may block weaker signals...
Objective: Ensure the squelch control is set to an appropriate level for reliable reception.

Procedure:
1. Locate the squelch control on the front panel of the transceiver.
2. If the squelch is set too high, it may block weaker signals from being heard (e.g., as experienced by an aid agency in Kinshasa).
3. Adjust the squelch control to a lower level so that all desired signals can be received without interference.
4. In situations where the squelch feature is not needed, consider wiring the control permanently off:
- Remove the wire from the 'variable' arm of the control.
- Solder it to the 'squelch off' end.
5. To indicate that this modification has been made, remove the squelch control knob from the front panel.
squelch control, signal reception, radio modification, squelch off
Assess Transceiver for Ease of Use and User Interface Design
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure the transceiver has a user-friendly interface suitable for its intended use.

Procedure:
1. Evaluate Control Layout: Avoid models with excessive controls or indicators that may not be used regularly. A basic SSB...
Objective: Ensure the transceiver has a user-friendly interface suitable for its intended use.

Procedure:
1. Evaluate Control Layout: Avoid models with excessive controls or indicators that may not be used regularly. A basic SSB transceiver should have at minimum: ON/OFF, VOLUME, CLARIFIER, and CHANNEL SELECTOR (if multiple channels are available).
2. Check for Additional Features: Look for a SQUELCH control and TRANSMIT indicator, which are commonly found on most models.
3. Earphone Compatibility: Determine if the transceiver has an earphone socket or can be modified to include one. Earphones are useful for hearing weak signals in noisy environments.
4. Power Source Switching: If you plan to use both AC mains and DC battery power, check how easily the transceiver can switch between these sources (e.g., via a switch, plug change, or soldering).
5. External Power Supply Considerations: Be aware that external power supply units require interconnecting cables, plugs, and sockets, which may wear out over time and become a source of failure.
user interface, controls, earphone socket, power switching, radio design
Assign and Train Radio Operators
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine who will operate the radio: Identify individuals or teams responsible for operating the station.

2. Assess time commitment: Consider how much time per day will be required to send, receive, and process messages....
Procedure:

1. Determine who will operate the radio: Identify individuals or teams responsible for operating the station.

2. Assess time commitment: Consider how much time per day will be required to send, receive, and process messages. At small stations, this may have minimal impact on existing workloads, while at large HQ stations, it may require full-time availability.

3. Plan for staffing costs: Decide who will pay the wages of a dedicated radio operator if one is needed.

4. Train operators in advance: Ensure that personnel are trained to operate the equipment and understand procedures before deployment.

5. Provide log books: Maintain records of all messages sent and received using log books, as this is usually a legal requirement under your radio licence.
radio operator, training, staffing, log book, message record, legal compliance
Avoid Misuse of CTCSS in Simplex Mode for Emergency Communications
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the Limitations of CTCSS: Understand that CTCSS does not provide true privacy or prevent stronger signals from overriding your transmission.
2. Assess Frequency Usage: In simplex mode, avoid using CTCSS unless...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the Limitations of CTCSS: Understand that CTCSS does not provide true privacy or prevent stronger signals from overriding your transmission.
2. Assess Frequency Usage: In simplex mode, avoid using CTCSS unless you are certain that all users on the same frequency are programmed with the same tone and understand how it works.
3. Monitor for Interference: Be aware that other users may be transmitting without knowing they are on the same frequency, which can lead to missed communications or delays in emergency situations.
4. Use Alternative Methods for Privacy: If privacy is required, consider using different frequencies or more advanced systems like SELCALL (Selective Calling) instead of relying solely on CTCSS.
5. Educate Users: Ensure that all users on the same frequency understand how CTCSS works and its limitations to avoid confusion during critical communications.

Important Notes:
• CTCSS is not suitable for simplex mode in emergency or high-priority communication scenarios due to potential interference and missed messages.
• Always verify that your radio system is configured correctly and all users are aware of the limitations of CTCSS.
CTCSS,Simplex mode,emergency communications,frequency interference,privacy limitations
Avoid Operating a Radio from a Battery While Being Charged
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. The radio should not be operated from a battery while the battery is being charged by any other means than by a solar charger.
2. This is because excess voltage, e.g., from a vehicle alternator with a faulty regulator, can damage...
Procedure:
1. The radio should not be operated from a battery while the battery is being charged by any other means than by a solar charger.
2. This is because excess voltage, e.g., from a vehicle alternator with a faulty regulator, can damage the transceiver.
battery safety, radio operation, electrical hazards
Avoid Using Hard Fiber for High-Frequency Panels
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not use hard fiber for panels in high-frequency current applications. It is a poor insulator and may lead to electrical failures or safety hazards.
hard fiber, high-frequency panels, insulation
Calculate Average Power Requirement for a Transceiver
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine PEP of the Transceiver: Identify the Peak Envelope Power (PEP) rating from the transceiver's specifications.
2. Calculate Average Input Power:
- Use the formula: *Average power = 2/3 × PEP*.
- Example: For...
Procedure:

1. Determine PEP of the Transceiver: Identify the Peak Envelope Power (PEP) rating from the transceiver's specifications.
2. Calculate Average Input Power:
- Use the formula: *Average power = 2/3 × PEP*.
- Example: For a 150 watt PEP transceiver, average power = 2/3 × 150 = 100 watts.
3. Determine Current Requirement from Battery:
- Use the formula: *Current (amps) = Power (watts) / Voltage (volts)*.
- Example: For a 150 watt PEP transceiver with a 12 volt battery, current = 100 watts / 12 volts ≈ 8.3 amps average.
4. Account for Peak Current:
- The transceiver may require up to twice the average current during transmission (e.g., 18-20 amps peak for a 150 watt PEP transceiver).
5. Receiving Power Consumption: During reception, power consumption is significantly lower (typically 0.2 to 0.5 amps for a 150 watt PEP transceiver).
power calculation, battery current, average power, peak current
Configure HF Link for Data Transmission
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Assess signal quality: Monitor the HF link for signal fading and noise levels.
2. Adjust baud rate: Set a lower baud rate (e.g., 300 bauds) to accommodate potential signal degradation on HF links.
3. **Implement error...
Procedure:

1. Assess signal quality: Monitor the HF link for signal fading and noise levels.
2. Adjust baud rate: Set a lower baud rate (e.g., 300 bauds) to accommodate potential signal degradation on HF links.
3. Implement error correction: Ensure data systems are equipped with error detection and correction mechanisms to handle missed or corrupted packets automatically.
4. Use voice link for packet radio: If necessary, use a voice radio link to carry packet radio, but limit the baud rate based on the quality of the link.
5. Monitor performance: Continuously monitor the link's performance and adjust parameters as needed to maintain reliable data transmission.
HF, VHF, UHF, data transmission, baud rate, signal fading, error correction
Configure Repeater Systems with CTCSS for Enhanced Communication Reliability
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand Repeater Functionality: A repeater system receives a signal on one frequency and retransmits it on another, allowing communication over greater distances.
2. Program the Repeater with a CTCSS Tone: Select a...
Procedure:

1. Understand Repeater Functionality: A repeater system receives a signal on one frequency and retransmits it on another, allowing communication over greater distances.
2. Program the Repeater with a CTCSS Tone: Select a unique CTCSS tone that will be used by all mobile units communicating through the repeater. Program this tone into the repeater's settings.
3. Ensure Mobile Units Are Programmed Correctly: All mobile units must be programmed with the same CTCSS tone as the repeater to ensure successful communication.
4. Verify Repeater Activation Settings: Configure the repeater to only activate when a mobile unit transmits the programmed CTCSS tone. This prevents random interference from activating the repeater unnecessarily.
5. Test the System Thoroughly: Conduct tests with multiple users on the same frequency to ensure that the repeater activates correctly and only responds to signals with the programmed CTCSS tone.
6. Monitor for Interference: Even with CTCSS, stronger signals from other sources may still interfere with communication. Regularly monitor the system for any signs of interference or malfunction.

Important Notes:
• Proper configuration of the repeater and mobile units is essential to ensure reliable communication.
• Always verify that all users understand how the CTCSS tone works in conjunction with the repeater system.
Repeater systems,CTCSS configuration,communication reliability,frequency interference,radio operations
Configure a Modem for Data Transmission Over Radio Channels
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Modems (MOdulator/DEModulator) are used to transmit data over audio channels such as telephone lines or radio links by modulating and demodulating signals.

### Implementation Steps
1. Ensure that the modem at both ends of the communication...
Modems (MOdulator/DEModulator) are used to transmit data over audio channels such as telephone lines or radio links by modulating and demodulating signals.

### Implementation Steps
1. Ensure that the modem at both ends of the communication link is compatible with the same signaling system.
2. Use a pair of tones for modulation, ensuring that the transceiver can handle the specific tone frequencies used in the chosen signaling system.
3. If using a modem with an SSB (Single Sideband) transceiver:
- A tuning aid is necessary to adjust the clarifier and set the tones at the correct pitch.

### Compatibility Notes
• Modems are compatible with AM, FM, and SSB transceivers depending on their design.
• Different signaling systems use various tone pairs and durations; ensure compatibility between both ends of the link.
modem setup, data transmission, radio channels, SSB tuning aid, tone modulation
Defeat SELCALL to Receive Calls on a Different Radio
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Locate the 'def' button or menu option on your radio. This allows you to temporarily disable the SELCALL system on your current radio.
2. Press the 'def' button or navigate through the menu to defeat SELCALL. This will...
Procedure:

1. Locate the 'def' button or menu option on your radio. This allows you to temporarily disable the SELCALL system on your current radio.
2. Press the 'def' button or navigate through the menu to defeat SELCALL. This will allow your radio to receive calls intended for other radios with different SELCALL codes.
3. Listen for incoming calls by ear. Since the SELCALL system is disabled, you must rely on spoken communication to identify if a call is intended for you.
4. Verify the caller's identity and purpose. If you are not the intended recipient, inform them that they should use the correct SELCALL code or speak directly to the person who is available.
5. Re-enable SELCALL when no longer needed. Ensure the system is reactivated to maintain security and prevent unintended communication with other stations.
defeat SELCALL, disable code, receive calls, radio settings
Determine Available Supply Voltage for Transceiver Installation
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure the transceiver is compatible with the actual voltage available at the installation site.

Procedure:
1. Assess Actual Voltage: Check the real voltage supply at the intended installation location, as it may differ from...
Objective: Ensure the transceiver is compatible with the actual voltage available at the installation site.

Procedure:
1. Assess Actual Voltage: Check the real voltage supply at the intended installation location, as it may differ from nominal values (e.g., 220V AC may drop to 185V during certain times).
2. Verify Transceiver Specifications: Confirm whether the transceiver requires a fixed voltage or can operate within a range of voltages (e.g., 210–240V).
3. Consult Manufacturer: Contact the manufacturer to determine the actual AC voltage range over which the transceiver will function, as this may be broader than what is stated in the specification.
4. Test Performance at Lower Voltages: Be aware that performance (e.g., power output) can decrease at lower voltages. For example, a transceiver rated for 13.6V DC may produce significantly less PEP when operated from a 12V battery.
5. Select Compatible Transceiver: Ensure the chosen transceiver is compatible with the actual voltage available to avoid performance issues or failure.
voltage, transceiver, power supply, compatibility, AC, DC
Determine Communication Purpose and Audience for Effective Transmission
Standard Source: Survival Communications Primer
Procedure:

1. Assess the reason for transmitting. This will determine whether you are calling for help or communicating within your group.
2. If calling for help (life-threatening situation):
- Transmit with enough power to ensure...
Procedure:

1. Assess the reason for transmitting. This will determine whether you are calling for help or communicating within your group.
2. If calling for help (life-threatening situation):
- Transmit with enough power to ensure aid can hear your call.
- Keep power levels low enough to conserve battery life for extended use, especially if you need to guide aid to your location.
3. If communicating within your group:
- Use the lowest possible power level that still allows effective communication.
- Stay within legal limits of your radio equipment (e.g., type accepted frequencies).
4. Be aware of frequency regulations:
- Use radios that are type accepted for specific bands (e.g., AIR band vs. Marine band). Note: Some radios may be type accepted for multiple bands, but this is rare.
- Amateur radio equipment is not type accepted and must follow ham regulations when used on amateur bands.
communication purpose, audience, legal limits, frequency regulations, emergency communication, group communication
Determine Primary and Backup Frequencies for Communications
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Identify the primary communication frequency that will be used for regular operations.
2. Select a backup frequency to use in case the primary frequency becomes unavailable or compromised.
3. Ensure both frequencies are compatible...
Procedure:

1. Identify the primary communication frequency that will be used for regular operations.
2. Select a backup frequency to use in case the primary frequency becomes unavailable or compromised.
3. Ensure both frequencies are compatible with all communication devices within the convoy.
4. Document the selected frequencies and ensure they are communicated to all relevant personnel.

Note: This step is critical for maintaining uninterrupted communication during operations.
frequencies,communication,backup,primary
Determine Required Transmitter Power for Reliable Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand PEP and Average Power Relationship: The Peak Envelope Power (PEP) of an SSB transceiver is approximately 3 times the average power output.
2. Estimate Range Requirements:
- For communication up to 300 km, a...
Procedure:

1. Understand PEP and Average Power Relationship: The Peak Envelope Power (PEP) of an SSB transceiver is approximately 3 times the average power output.
2. Estimate Range Requirements:
- For communication up to 300 km, a 25 watt PEP transmitter may be sufficient.
- For ranges up to 1,000 km, a 100 watt PEP transmitter is typically required.
3. Consider Interference Factors:
- In practice, due to interference and crowded channels, many users opt for higher power transmitters (up to 1,000 watts illegally).
4. Select Appropriate Power:
- Based on the Questionaire data, 74% of transceivers used are 100 watts PEP or above.
- If unsure about operating with less than 100 watts, select a transceiver capable of producing at least 100 watts PEP.
5. Power Adjustment:
- You can reduce the power output of a 100 watt transceiver if needed but cannot increase the power of a lower wattage model without additional equipment.
transmitter power, PEP, communication range, interference, radio selection
Enter the Net Using Correct Procedures
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Use the correct procedures to enter the net as specified in the training material.
2. Conduct a secure communications check to ensure that all systems are functioning properly and that the link is secure.
entering the net, secure communications check, radio procedures
Establish a Radio Network Timetable
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To establish an effective radio network timetable:

1. Determine the time slots for different departments or functions within your organisation. For example:
- 0900 to 1000: Education
- 1000 to 1100: Medical
- 1200: General Rendezvous for...
To establish an effective radio network timetable:

1. Determine the time slots for different departments or functions within your organisation. For example:
- 0900 to 1000: Education
- 1000 to 1100: Medical
- 1200: General Rendezvous for all other matters.

2. If the network covers a large area, subdivide it on a regional basis:
- Allocate specific times for each region to handle its own business.
- Schedule times when regions can contact central HQ control for other matters.

3. Avoid changing an existing timetable unless you are certain that the channel will be free at the new time:
- Listen on the channel for several days to determine when it is in use.
- Ensure changes do not disrupt ongoing operations.
radio network, timetable, communication schedule, radio operations
Establish a Secure Radio Room Location
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select a permanent location for the transceiver and its battery that is secure.
2. Install burglar bars and reliable locks to protect the equipment from theft or unauthorized access.
3. **Avoid moving the transceiver...
Procedure:

1. Select a permanent location for the transceiver and its battery that is secure.
2. Install burglar bars and reliable locks to protect the equipment from theft or unauthorized access.
3. Avoid moving the transceiver and/or battery during the day to a safe storage place at night, as this can lead to damage due to constant movement and careless handling.
4. Ensure that the location is dry, even if it requires covering the equipment and desk with a plastic sheet, especially in areas prone to tropical rain.
5. Avoid rooms where other people are working or talking as background noise will be picked up by the microphone and transmitted.
6. Use sound-absorbing screens or telephone hoods (like those used in airports) to reduce echo and background noise.
7. Consider placing the equipment in a separate room for privacy, if possible, to prevent conversations from being overheard.
8. If privacy is not possible, have the operator wear earphones to prevent others from overhearing the conversation.

Warnings/Notes:
• Moving the transceiver frequently can cause damage and inconvenience.
• Acid burns from improper handling of vented batteries can occur, as seen in one case where a lady's clothing was spoiled by acid.
• Radio conversations are not private; anyone with a suitable receiver can listen on your frequency.
radio room, secure location, radio equipment, privacy, sound absorption, tropical rain, earphones
Establishing an HQ Station for Radio Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Overview: Setting up and managing an HQ station is crucial for effective radio communication within a group.

Procedure:
1. Assign Operator Role: Either the administrator of the group should operate the radio themselves or delegate the...
Overview: Setting up and managing an HQ station is crucial for effective radio communication within a group.

Procedure:
1. Assign Operator Role: Either the administrator of the group should operate the radio themselves or delegate the task to another person who will act as a message relayer.
2. Consider Advantages and Disadvantages: If the admin operates the radio, they can answer queries and resolve issues immediately. However, this may add extra workload to an already busy schedule. Alternatively, delegating the role requires ensuring that the operator has enough time to perform the task properly.
3. Operator Qualifications: The operator must be fluent in the language used for transmission, able to read and take down messages accurately over the air, and not expected to answer questions immediately but rather pass them on correctly to the right person.
4. Time Commitment: Operating a radio station is time-consuming. For example, in Zaire, an operating session between HQ and 4 outlying stations typically took over 1 hour, including preparation, message exchange, logging, and follow-up actions.

Notes: The operator must be trained to handle messages accurately and maintain proper records. This ensures that all communications are clear and efficient.
hq station, radio operator, message relay, communication protocol, log book, shortwave radio
Exit the Net Properly
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Follow the correct procedures to exit the net as specified in the training material.
2. Ensure that all communications links are properly closed and secured before exiting.
3. Confirm that the radio system is no longer transmitting or...
Steps:

1. Follow the correct procedures to exit the net as specified in the training material.
2. Ensure that all communications links are properly closed and secured before exiting.
3. Confirm that the radio system is no longer transmitting or receiving on the network.
exiting the net, secure communications shutdown, radio procedures
Handle 'Fail to Battery' Facility Correctly
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. If the radio installation has a 'fail to battery' facility (see para.3.2.1.2.), then the importance of switching the transceiver off as well as disconnecting the mains must be stressed.
2. With a 'fail to battery' facility, when...
Procedure:
1. If the radio installation has a 'fail to battery' facility (see para.3.2.1.2.), then the importance of switching the transceiver off as well as disconnecting the mains must be stressed.
2. With a 'fail to battery' facility, when the mains plug is removed without switching off at the transceiver controls, the transceiver will automatically be connected to the battery and so remain on.
fail to battery, power management, radio operation
Implement CTCSS for Frequency Sharing in VHF/UHF Radios
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand CTCSS Functionality: Continuous Tone Controlled Squelch System (CTCSS) allows users to share a frequency without hearing other users' transmissions.
2. Program Your Radio with a Unique CTCSS Tone: Select a...
Procedure:

1. Understand CTCSS Functionality: Continuous Tone Controlled Squelch System (CTCSS) allows users to share a frequency without hearing other users' transmissions.
2. Program Your Radio with a Unique CTCSS Tone: Select a specific tone from the available range and program it into your radio. This tone is emitted continuously while transmitting but not heard by the receiver's loudspeaker.
3. Ensure Compatibility Across Devices: All radios that need to communicate on the same frequency must be programmed with the same CTCSS tone for successful communication.
4. Monitor for Interference: Even though CTCSS prevents hearing other users, it does not block stronger signals from overriding your transmission. Monitor for potential interference from stronger stations on the same frequency.
5. Use Caution in Simplex Mode: In simplex mode, CTCSS may lead to missed communications if another user is transmitting without knowing you are on the same frequency. It is generally advised against unless weaker interfering signals are expected.
6. Verify Repeater Settings: If using a repeater system, ensure that it is configured to only activate when your mobile unit transmits the programmed CTCSS tone. This prevents random interference from blocking the repeater.

Important Notes:
• CTCSS does not provide true privacy or prevent stronger signals from overriding weaker ones.
• Users may experience confusion if they do not understand how CTCSS works, especially in emergency situations where timely communication is critical.
• Always be aware that other users on the same frequency can hear your transmissions even if you cannot hear theirs.
CTCSS,VHF,UHF,frequency sharing,radio operations,interference,repeater systems
Install Aerials Correctly to Ensure Longevity and Safety
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Allow several hours for aerial erection to ensure proper installation.
2. Whenever possible, have a competent person install the aerial.
3. Avoid incorrect installations such as:
- Erecting the aerial at 90 degrees to...
Procedure:
1. Allow several hours for aerial erection to ensure proper installation.
2. Whenever possible, have a competent person install the aerial.
3. Avoid incorrect installations such as:
- Erecting the aerial at 90 degrees to the required direction
- Tying the coaxial cable to the top of a pole
- Cutting pieces off the aerial during installation to make it fit between posts
4. Ensure that the aerial is installed in the correct length and orientation for optimal performance.
5. Avoid placing the aerial too close to the ground or other obstructions.
aerial installation, radio setup, proper orientation, coaxial cable safety
Load the Traffic Encryption Key (TEK)
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Access the encryption key loading function on the radio.
2. Input the correct traffic encryption key (TEK) as per the unit's secure communications protocols.
3. Confirm that the TEK has been successfully loaded into the system.
TEK loading, encryption key, secure communications
Maintain a Message Log Book for Radio Communications
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To maintain an accurate message log book at each station:

1. Use a bound log book rather than loose leaf pages, as required by some governments.
2. Use duplicate pads that provide one page to be left in the book and a second carbon copy on a...
To maintain an accurate message log book at each station:

1. Use a bound log book rather than loose leaf pages, as required by some governments.
2. Use duplicate pads that provide one page to be left in the book and a second carbon copy on a tear-out page for passing messages.
3. Before starting contact, write down the operator's name, date, and time across the page.
4. Create columns in the log book for:
- To/From: Indicate whether the message is incoming or outgoing.
- Message: Record the content of the message.
- Action: Document the response taken to the message.
5. Assign serial numbers to each message by the HQ operator and inform other stations of these numbers for future reference.
6. Ensure that a person at each station is responsible for ensuring all messages are passed on, even if they are not the operator.
7. After each contact, have the log book circulated among personnel so recipients can acknowledge receipt and enter new messages in their own words.
8. Avoid sending messages based on the operator's interpretation; instead, record them as written to prevent errors.
9. If operators write messages down rapidly on scrap paper first, ensure they are copied into the log book as soon as possible while the details are fresh in memory.
10. Make the log book available for review at a specific time and place each day.
message log, radio communication, message record, log book
Maintaining Stand-by Operators for Radio Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Overview: Having a stand-by operator is essential to ensure continuity in radio communication.

Procedure:
1. Designate a Stand-by Operator: Choose an experienced and reliable individual who can take over the duties of the primary...
Overview: Having a stand-by operator is essential to ensure continuity in radio communication.

Procedure:
1. Designate a Stand-by Operator: Choose an experienced and reliable individual who can take over the duties of the primary operator when necessary.
2. Training Requirements: The stand-by operator must be trained in operating procedures, controls, and message handling to ensure they are prepared for emergencies or unexpected absences.
3. Regular Practice: The stand-by operator should operate the radio at least once a month to maintain familiarity with the equipment and procedures.

Notes: This ensures that there is always someone available who can step in without delay or confusion, maintaining the integrity of communication during critical times.
stand-by operator, radio training, emergency backup, communication continuity
Make Initial Radio Contact
Standard Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
### Establish Contact
• Make initial contact as soon as possible or as directed in applicable plans/orders.
initial contact, radio communications
Minimize Transmission Power for Effective Communication
Standard Source: Survival Communications Primer
Procedure:

1. Use the minimum power required to communicate. This reduces airwave pollution and conserves battery life.
2. Limit transmission range by using low power, especially in emergency situations where you may not want everyone...
Procedure:

1. Use the minimum power required to communicate. This reduces airwave pollution and conserves battery life.
2. Limit transmission range by using low power, especially in emergency situations where you may not want everyone to hear your message.
3. Avoid unnecessary high-power transmissions, as they can interfere with other radio operators and drain batteries faster.
4. Consider the context of communication:
- In life-threatening emergencies, use enough power to ensure aid can receive your call but keep it low enough to conserve battery for extended use.
- For non-life-threatening group communications, keep power levels as low as possible while still maintaining effective communication within the group.
5. Be aware of legal limitations:
- Use radios that are type accepted for specific frequency bands (e.g., AIR band vs. Marine band).
- Amateur radio equipment is not type accepted and must follow ham regulations when used on amateur bands.
transmission power, airwave pollution, battery conservation, emergency communication, legal limitations, radio operations
Mitigate Interference Using RF Gain and Audio Gain Controls
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Reduce interference from nearby transmitters while maintaining communication quality.

Procedure:
1. If interference is caused by nearby transmitters not on the same frequency, reduce the RF gain as much as possible while...
Objective: Reduce interference from nearby transmitters while maintaining communication quality.

Procedure:
1. If interference is caused by nearby transmitters not on the same frequency, reduce the RF gain as much as possible while still being able to hear the station you are communicating with.
2. Compensate for any loss in volume using the audio gain or volume control.
3. Ensure that communication remains clear and audible despite interference.
interference mitigation, RF gain, audio gain
Modify Internal Connections for Easy Maintenance in Remote Locations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Facilitate rapid maintenance of a transceiver by using plug-in circuit boards.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that all main components are on plug-in circuit boards, allowing technicians to replace or repair them quickly during field...
Objective: Facilitate rapid maintenance of a transceiver by using plug-in circuit boards.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that all main components are on plug-in circuit boards, allowing technicians to replace or repair them quickly during field visits.
2. For high-current connections (e.g., from the power supply), modify soldered wire connections to use plugs and sockets:
- Use high current snap-on car electrical type connectors for the power supply connection.
3. This modification reduces the need for soldering during repairs and improves reliability in remote or field environments.
plug-in circuit boards, maintenance, high current connections, remote repair
Operating Transceivers and Training Operators
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Prepare for operation: Remove all test equipment before starting the operating procedure.
2. Follow setup instructions: Switch on the transceiver, connect cables as described in the manual, and ensure all connections are...
Procedure:

1. Prepare for operation: Remove all test equipment before starting the operating procedure.
2. Follow setup instructions: Switch on the transceiver, connect cables as described in the manual, and ensure all connections are secure.
3. Train operator: Allow the operator to perform all operations (e.g., sending and receiving messages) under supervision.
4. Test communication: Make a radio contact with another station at a prearranged time to verify functionality.
5. Observe operator technique: Check how the operator sends, receives, and records messages during testing.
6. Shut down properly: After testing, instruct the operator to switch off the transceiver and disconnect cables as necessary.
7. Consider local context: In remote or culturally sensitive areas (e.g., African villages), be prepared for large crowds gathering around the equipment. Ensure only trained operators handle the transceiver.
8. Train in advance: Conduct training sessions in a quiet environment before the first formal contact to avoid distractions and ensure proper understanding.

Notes:
