• Operator training is critical, especially in areas with limited technical knowledge or where local populations may be excited by new technology.
• Ensure operators are familiar with procedures to prevent accidental damage or misuse of equipment.
operator training,transceiver setup,radiocommunication testing
Prevent Damage from Lightning When Operating a Radio
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. The operator should be told never to operate when lightning can be seen and thunder heard.
2. Always disconnect the aerial and power cables after use because of danger from lightning.
3. If he understands that lightning can come...
Procedure:
1. The operator should be told never to operate when lightning can be seen and thunder heard.
2. Always disconnect the aerial and power cables after use because of danger from lightning.
3. If he understands that lightning can come through the cables to the radio and damage it or strike the operator, he will take more notice of the instructions.
lightning safety, radio operation, electrical hazards
Prevent Hydrogen Gas Hazards from Batteries
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. The operator should be told never to smoke or use a candle or naked light near a battery.
2. This is because hydrogen gas is released from a vented battery (i.e., a car type battery) when it is being charged.
3. If connected to a...
Procedure:
1. The operator should be told never to smoke or use a candle or naked light near a battery.
2. This is because hydrogen gas is released from a vented battery (i.e., a car type battery) when it is being charged.
3. If connected to a solar panel, the battery will be continually charged throughout the day.
battery safety, hydrogen gas, fire prevention
Proper Transmitting Technique for Clear Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure clear and effective communication by following proper transmitting techniques.

Procedure:
1. Press the transmit button before beginning to speak into the microphone.
2. Cease speaking immediately before releasing the...
Objective: Ensure clear and effective communication by following proper transmitting techniques.

Procedure:
1. Press the transmit button before beginning to speak into the microphone.
2. Cease speaking immediately before releasing the transmit button.
3. Speak clearly and at a reasonable pace; do not shout, as this can distort the signal.
4. If your transceiver has a transmit light, observe it during pauses in speech to ensure that it turns off when you stop talking.
5. Adjust either your voice volume or proximity to the microphone so that the transmit light corresponds with your speech patterns.
6. If the distant station reports poor reception, avoid shouting into the microphone as this may activate Automatic Level Control (ALC) circuits and reduce intelligibility.
7. Use a trial-and-error method to improve communication: ask the other operator if they receive you better in one transmission or another after adjusting your position or technique.
transmitting technique, communication clarity, ALC circuits
Radio Operator Duties During Planning
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Title: Radio Operator Duties During Planning

Procedure:
1. Enter the net (communications network) at the specified time.
2. Ensure that all frequencies, COMSEC fills, and net IDs are preset in squad/platoon radios.
3. Inform the SL...
Title: Radio Operator Duties During Planning

Procedure:
1. Enter the net (communications network) at the specified time.
2. Ensure that all frequencies, COMSEC fills, and net IDs are preset in squad/platoon radios.
3. Inform the SL and PL of changes to call signs, frequencies, challenge and password, and number combination based on the appropriate time in the ANCD (Annex to the Net Control Document).
4. Ensure the proper function of all radios and troubleshoot any deficiencies, reporting them to higher headquarters if necessary.
5. Weatherproof all communications equipment to ensure it remains operational under adverse conditions.
radio operator, comsec fills, net entry, weatherproofing, ancd
Sending and Receiving Messages on Shortwave Radio
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Keep messages clear and concise, avoiding unnecessary information.
2. Speak into the microphone at a moderate pace; avoid shouting to prevent distortion.
3. Begin by stating the **callsign of the station you are...
Procedure:

1. Keep messages clear and concise, avoiding unnecessary information.
2. Speak into the microphone at a moderate pace; avoid shouting to prevent distortion.
3. Begin by stating the callsign of the station you are addressing, followed by your own callsign.
4. Finish each message clearly with both callsigns repeated, followed by the word "Over" or "Over to you."
5. Avoid long pauses or hesitations during transmission, as they may be mistaken for the end of a message or equipment failure.
6. Ensure that all messages are understood on both sides; if in doubt, have the receiver read back the message to confirm accuracy.
7. In noisy or difficult reception conditions, it is essential to request the sender to spell out words using the phonetic alphabet (see para. 7.3.5 for details).
8. Never transmit medical terms or names of people and places without confirming their spelling if there is any doubt in understanding.
message transmission, radio communication, phonetic alphabet, clear speech, message confirmation
Set Up Cross Band Repeating for Enhanced Signal Transmission
Standard Source: Survival Communications Primer
Procedure:

1. Gather Equipment: Ensure you have an HT (Handheld Transceiver) and a dual-band mobile radio (which may be in an automobile).
2. Configure Frequencies:
- The HT should transmit on UHF-A.
- The Mobile unit must receive...
Procedure:

1. Gather Equipment: Ensure you have an HT (Handheld Transceiver) and a dual-band mobile radio (which may be in an automobile).
2. Configure Frequencies:
- The HT should transmit on UHF-A.
- The Mobile unit must receive on UHF-A and simultaneously retransmit on VHF-A.
3. Base Repeater Configuration: The base repeater should receive on VHF-A and retransmit simultaneously on VHF-B.
4. Receiving Options:
- Option 1: The HT receives the signal directly from the base repeater on VHF-B.
- Option 2: The mobile unit receives VHF-B, retransmits it simultaneously on UHF-B, and the HT receives on UHF-B (this option is harder on the mobile equipment and may be legally questionable).
5. Considerations:
- This method allows communication with a main 'base' repeater even if you are inside a building or blocked by topography.
- Be aware that Option 2 increases wear and tear on the mobile unit and may not be entirely legal.
cross band repeating, HT, mobile radio, UHF-A, VHF-A, base repeater, signal amplification
Set Up Facsimile (Fax) Transmission Over Radio Channels
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Facsimile (fax) machines transmit static black-and-white images over audio channels by frequency modulating an audio sub-carrier.

### Implementation Steps
1. Connect the fax machine to a transceiver that supports audio channel...
Facsimile (fax) machines transmit static black-and-white images over audio channels by frequency modulating an audio sub-carrier.

### Implementation Steps
1. Connect the fax machine to a transceiver that supports audio channel transmission.
2. Ensure the transceiver is set up with the correct modulation settings:
- Use a nominal 1900 Hz sub-carrier frequency for transmission.
- White areas are transmitted at 2300 Hz, and varying shades of grey to black are transmitted from 2300 Hz down to 1500 Hz.
3. Ensure the receiving end has a compatible fax machine or receiver capable of demodulating the signal and converting it back into an image.

### Performance Notes
• Transmission quality depends on the stability of the audio channel and proper tuning of the sub-carrier frequency.
fax transmission, radio channels, sub-carrier modulation, image transmission
Set Up Selective Calling System for Radio Network Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Selective calling systems allow you to send individual signals to specific stations within a network, similar to how a telephone works by ringing a bell or buzzer at the target station.

### Requirements
• A selective calling unit with a...
Selective calling systems allow you to send individual signals to specific stations within a network, similar to how a telephone works by ringing a bell or buzzer at the target station.

### Requirements
• A selective calling unit with a unique calling code for each station in the network (especially for HQ station).
• Units that can both transmit and receive codes (for all stations except the HQ, which may have additional capabilities).

### Implementation Steps
1. Assign a unique calling code to each station within the network.
2. Install selective calling units on all transceivers:
- Some units are plugged into an auxiliary socket of the transceiver.
- Others are battery-powered and must be held in front of the microphone while transmitting.
3. Ensure that the transceiver is switched on and has its aerial connected to receive calls.
4. Leave the transceiver switched on with the loudspeaker off during periods when you expect to receive calls.
5. Safety Precautions:
- Disconnect the aerial and other external components when lightning is near.

### Compatibility Notes
• Tone-based calling units are best suited for AM and FM transceivers but can be used on SSB if tuned within ±500 Hz of the desired frequency.
• Pulse width modulated tone units work with all transceiver types (AM, FM, SSB).

### Performance
• The calling signal is transmitted for approximately 5 to 10 seconds.
• These units are designed to minimize false alarms.
selective calling, radio network, unique code, transceiver setup, lightning safety, tone-based calling
Set Up a Transportable Radio Unit for Optimal Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select the appropriate transportable unit: Choose a transportable radio with higher output power than handheld units, as they are designed for better communication range and reliability.

2. Ensure proper battery setup:...
Procedure:

1. Select the appropriate transportable unit: Choose a transportable radio with higher output power than handheld units, as they are designed for better communication range and reliability.

2. Ensure proper battery setup: Transportables require larger and heavier batteries due to their increased power requirements. Make sure these batteries are fully charged and securely installed in the unit.

3. Use an external antenna when stationary: While transportable units come with a built-in rubber duck helical antenna for mobility, this should only be used while on the move. When stationary, connect a temporary external antenna to maximize signal strength and range. The external antenna should be clipped to a fitting in a good position (e.g., elevated or unobstructed location).

4. Integrate battery charger: Many transportable units have built-in battery chargers. Ensure the charger is functioning properly and not misplaced, as it is crucial for maintaining operational readiness.

5. Avoid common mistakes: Do not rely solely on the internal antenna when stationary, as this reduces the effectiveness of the transportable unit. Also, ensure that all components (including the charger) are accounted for during transportation to prevent loss or misplacement.
transportable radio,external antenna,battery setup,radiocommunication,radio operation
Setting Up Outlying Stations for Radio Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Overview: Outlying stations require trained operators to ensure effective communication with the HQ station.

Procedure:
1. Operator Qualifications: The operator at an outlying station must be familiar with radio operation and able to...
Overview: Outlying stations require trained operators to ensure effective communication with the HQ station.

Procedure:
1. Operator Qualifications: The operator at an outlying station must be familiar with radio operation and able to take their turn on the air with other stations as instructed by the controlling (HQ) station.
2. Training Requirements: It is advisable for multiple individuals in the group to be trained in radio operations so that they can handle emergencies or take over if needed.
3. Consistency in Operation: Ideally, one regular operator should be assigned daily duties that include operating the radio to maintain consistency and reliability in communication.

Notes: Operators must be prepared to follow instructions from the HQ station and ensure that messages are transmitted and received accurately.
outlying station, radio operator, communication protocol, emergency training
Switch Between USB and LSB for Interference Reduction
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Reduce interference by switching between upper (USB) and lower (LSB) sidebands when necessary.

Procedure:
1. If a station is experiencing interference on one sideband, check if the other sideband is free from interference and not...
Objective: Reduce interference by switching between upper (USB) and lower (LSB) sidebands when necessary.

Procedure:
1. If a station is experiencing interference on one sideband, check if the other sideband is free from interference and not in use by another station.
2. Switch to the unused sideband and inform the other stations of the change.
3. After communication is complete, return the USB/LSB switch to its normal position for subsequent contacts.
sideband switching, interference reduction
Time Allocation for HQ Station Operations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Overview: Proper time management is essential when operating an HQ station to ensure efficient communication.

Procedure:
1. Preparation Time (5 minutes): Obtain the keys, go to the room, unlock it, connect cables, and set up the radio...
Overview: Proper time management is essential when operating an HQ station to ensure efficient communication.

Procedure:
1. Preparation Time (5 minutes): Obtain the keys, go to the room, unlock it, connect cables, and set up the radio controls for contact.
2. Message Exchange (20 minutes): Send and receive messages between HQ and outlying stations.
3. Log Book and Documentation (10 minutes): Ensure that all log books are completed correctly and make written copies of any necessary messages for distribution.
4. Shut Down Procedures (5 minutes): Switch off the radio, disconnect cables, and lock up the room securely after operations.
5. Follow-Up Communication (20 minutes): Contact individuals who have received messages or have questions to ensure clarity and resolution of issues.

Notes: This breakdown is based on a real-world example from Zaire and should be adapted as needed depending on operational requirements.
time management, radio operation, log book, message exchange, communication protocol
Use Listening Procedures for Radio Communications
Standard Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
### Use Reception Times
• Follow reception times outlined in applicable plans/orders or as directed by recovery forces.
listening procedures, radio communications
Use Morse Code for Long-Distance Communication Over Radio Channels
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Morse code is a method of transmitting messages using short and long signals (dots and dashes) over radio channels.

### Implementation Steps
1. Ensure both the sending and receiving stations have operators who can send and receive Morse...
Morse code is a method of transmitting messages using short and long signals (dots and dashes) over radio channels.

### Implementation Steps
1. Ensure both the sending and receiving stations have operators who can send and receive Morse code at a minimum speed of 8 words per minute for effective communication.
2. Equip each station with:
- A morse key to manually transmit signals (dots and dashes).
- A tone oscillator if needed to generate the correct audio tones for transmission.
3. Tune the transceiver to an appropriate frequency that supports Morse code transmission.
4. Transmit messages by sending a sequence of dots and dashes corresponding to letters, numbers, and symbols as defined in the international Morse code standard.
5. At the receiving end, decode the received signals into readable text using a Morse code interpreter or trained operator.

### Performance Notes
• Morse code is especially useful when signal strength is low or there is significant interference on the channel.
• It remains a reliable method of communication in situations where voice transmission is not feasible.
morse code, radio communication, long-distance transmission, tone oscillator
Use SELCALL to Communicate with a Specific Station
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure your radio has a SELCALL code assigned. Each station must have a unique SELCALL code programmed into its circuit board.
2. Send the SELCALL code before transmitting your message. This will activate only the receiver...
Procedure:

1. Ensure your radio has a SELCALL code assigned. Each station must have a unique SELCALL code programmed into its circuit board.
2. Send the SELCALL code before transmitting your message. This will activate only the receiver associated with that specific SELCALL code, allowing you to communicate without interference from other users.
3. Listen for confirmation of receipt. If the receiving station is equipped with a revertive calling feature, it will provide an indication (e.g., a light or sound) at the sending end confirming that the SELCALL was received and the alert horn has been triggered.
4. Use spoken communication if necessary. If the recipient is not near their radio or has defeated the SELCALL system, they may need to hear your message by voice. In this case, you should verbally state the callsign and name of the person required after sending the SELCALL code.
5. Close other radios during conversation. Once the intended recipient answers, they can identify the SELCALL of the radio they are using, allowing the call to be set up again while silencing other radios in the group.
SELCALL, selective calling, radio communication, unique code, revertive calling
Use a Rheostat with Spark Coils and Direct Current
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not attempt to operate your spark coil with 110-volt direct lighting current without connecting in a rheostat. A rheostat is necessary to control the current safely and effectively.
spark coil, 110-volt direct current, rheostat
Use a Wavemeter for Accurate Transmitter Tuning
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not attempt to tune your transmitter by guesswork to send out waves of given length. Always use a wavemeter for accurate and safe tuning.
transmitter tuning, wavemeter, radio frequency
Use an Electrolytic Interrupter with Spark Coils and Alternating Current
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Do not attempt to operate your spark coil with 110-volt alternating lighting current without connecting in an electrolytic interrupter. This device is required for safe operation with alternating current.
spark coil, 110-volt alternating current, electrolytic interrupter
Using Repeaters as an Alternative to Base Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify the need for a repeater: Use this option if a private wire is not practical or there are no staff available to manage traffic.
2. Install a repeater: Set up a repeater in a location that can effectively relay...
Procedure:

1. Identify the need for a repeater: Use this option if a private wire is not practical or there are no staff available to manage traffic.
2. Install a repeater: Set up a repeater in a location that can effectively relay signals between distant points.
3. Ensure coverage area: Position the repeater where it can cover both the base station and the target communication areas.
4. Test signal strength and clarity: Verify that the repeater is functioning properly and providing reliable communication.

Notes:
• Repeaters are useful in remote or sparsely populated areas where direct connections may be difficult to establish.
• They can extend the range of a base station significantly.
repeater setup, alternative communication, radio relay, emergency communications
Using a Base Station for Repeating Messages
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Assign a dedicated operator: Have someone permanently monitor the channel to repeat messages for handheld radios.
2. Ensure continuous coverage: Use the base station frequently to relay communications between different...
Procedure:

1. Assign a dedicated operator: Have someone permanently monitor the channel to repeat messages for handheld radios.
2. Ensure continuous coverage: Use the base station frequently to relay communications between different locations.
3. Maintain situational awareness: Keep an ear on the channel at all times, especially in high-traffic or emergency situations.

Notes:
• Base stations are often used as central hubs for relaying messages across a network.
• Even simple equipment can be highly effective if properly utilized.
message repeating, radio relay, communications hub, base station operations
Adjust Transceiver Clarifier Control for Optimal Sound
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Adjust the clarifier control to achieve the best sound quality based on individual hearing.

Procedure:
1. Locate the clarifier control on your transceiver.
2. Turn the control until the pitch or sound is clear and comfortable for...
Objective: Adjust the clarifier control to achieve the best sound quality based on individual hearing.

Procedure:
1. Locate the clarifier control on your transceiver.
2. Turn the control until the pitch or sound is clear and comfortable for you.
3. Note that the optimal setting may vary between individuals, so adjust it according to your personal hearing preferences.
4. Repeat this process for each station you listen to, as different stations may require different settings.
clarifier control, sound quality, transceiver adjustment
Assign and Use Official Call Signs for Radio Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Each station should be assigned an official call sign by the licensing authority of its country (e.g., 9SD67 or 9SD47).
2. In practice, only the last part of the call sign is used for communication (e.g., 67 and 47).
3. A...
Procedure:

1. Each station should be assigned an official call sign by the licensing authority of its country (e.g., 9SD67 or 9SD47).
2. In practice, only the last part of the call sign is used for communication (e.g., 67 and 47).
3. A list of all call signs in the network should be kept at each station.
4. All operators must use the assigned call signs instead of place names to avoid confusion.
5. One station, typically the HQ station, should have control over the network and issue instructions to other stations.
6. When calling a specific station, the operator should state the called station’s number first, followed by their own (e.g., *67 from 47*).
call sign assignment, radio station control, HQ communication, network coordination

+ 117 more procedures. Get the full library in the app.

Radio Usage (51) -

Teach Radio Users Proper Communication Techniques
Critical Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Teach all individuals who own a radio how to use it properly with specific emphasis on the following:
- Listening before transmitting.
- Disabling CTCSS and DCS features.
- Disabling scrambling functions.
- Speaking in...
Procedure:

1. Teach all individuals who own a radio how to use it properly with specific emphasis on the following:
- Listening before transmitting.
- Disabling CTCSS and DCS features.
- Disabling scrambling functions.
- Speaking in plain language.
- Directing all communications to a specific person or unit.
- Responding only to known persons.
- Making general calls when necessary.
- Ensuring enough batteries are on hand for at least seventy-two hours of continuous use.
- Keeping the radio and batteries in an accessible location, away from areas prone to damage during earthquakes or storms.
- In advance warning of an emergency, putting the radio on your belt or clipping it to clothing.
- Carrying packs of extra batteries in pockets for emergencies like building collapses.
- Before a disaster, contacting other neighborhood members and checking in with Block Captains who should have paper and pencil ready to record names and addresses.
- Having a current map with address numbers, telephone numbers, and family names recorded on it.
- After a disaster, Block Captains should contact every person that checked in and attempt to reach those who did not.
- Block Captains should prioritize persons in distress by allowing them to check in before others.
- Persons in distress should begin transmitting immediately if their vocal cries for help are not heard. Use the format: “This is [Name] on [Location]. Please help me.” Stop transmitting when called or asked to do so, and indicate injuries if necessary.
- If unable to speak, develop a signaling method (e.g., tapping or rubbing the radio against an object) that is understood by all neighbors. Use one tap for yes, two taps for no, as a last resort.
- Prevent open-microphone conditions by switching to an alternate channel if necessary.
radio training, communication protocol, emergency use
Establish Block Captains for Neighborhood Communication
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Assign a Block Captain to each neighborhood who is responsible for calling all residents on the radio during and after a disaster.

2. The Block Captain should verify the welfare of people in homes and buildings around them.

3....
Procedure:

1. Assign a Block Captain to each neighborhood who is responsible for calling all residents on the radio during and after a disaster.

2. The Block Captain should verify the welfare of people in homes and buildings around them.

3. Ensure that Block Captains are trained and equipped with radios for effective communication.
Block Captain, neighborhood communication, disaster response
Assess the Role of Radio in Emergency Alert Dissemination
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Recognize Radio's Reach: Despite concerns about its effectiveness compared to digital media, radio continues to be a critical communication channel for reaching the public.

2. Review Research Data: According to 2019...
Procedure:

1. Recognize Radio's Reach: Despite concerns about its effectiveness compared to digital media, radio continues to be a critical communication channel for reaching the public.

2. Review Research Data: According to 2019 research from Nielsen and Westwood One, over 90% of Americans are reached weekly by radio, with TSL on AM/FM being 10 times greater than that of streaming platforms.

3. Consider Radio in Vehicles: In the aftermath of a nuclear detonation, radios in cars and trucks may become essential tools for seeking emergency AWN broadcasts due to communication disruptions.

4. Avoid Exaggerating Access: Caution is needed to avoid overstating the access provided by AM/FM radio for purposes of AWN dissemination.

5. Plan for Continued Use: Given its reliability, radio should be incorporated into emergency planning and alerting strategies.
radio usage, emergency communication, AWN dissemination, nuclear detonation
Assign Neighborhood Radio Setup Volunteers
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Designate a neighborhood communications volunteer to set up FRS radios for individuals who may not be able to do it themselves.

2. This ensures that all members of the community have access to functioning communication devices...
Procedure:

1. Designate a neighborhood communications volunteer to set up FRS radios for individuals who may not be able to do it themselves.

2. This ensures that all members of the community have access to functioning communication devices during emergencies.

3. The volunteer should provide guidance on proper setup and usage.
radio setup, volunteer, neighborhood communication
Avoid Interference and Unintended Distress Calls
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Unintended interference or distress calls can hinder rescue efforts during emergencies:

1. Train users to avoid panic-driven behavior, such as broadcasting random signals or calling out unnecessarily.
2. Ensure that all individuals using...
Unintended interference or distress calls can hinder rescue efforts during emergencies:

1. Train users to avoid panic-driven behavior, such as broadcasting random signals or calling out unnecessarily.
2. Ensure that all individuals using radios are aware of the importance of following communication protocols and not causing interference with ongoing rescues.
3. Provide clear instructions on how to use the push-to-talk function correctly to avoid accidental transmissions.
4. Encourage users to stay calm and communicate only when necessary, especially in high-stress situations.
radio interference, distress calls, communication protocols, panic prevention, emergency training
Avoid Redesigning Communication Systems for CERT Teams
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. CERT teams should not be responsible for designing or re-designing communication systems.

2. This work should be handled by disaster planners who are already familiar with local emergency protocols.

3. CERT volunteers should...
Procedure:

1. CERT teams should not be responsible for designing or re-designing communication systems.

2. This work should be handled by disaster planners who are already familiar with local emergency protocols.

3. CERT volunteers should focus on rescue and public welfare tasks.
CERT, communication system design, disaster planning
Coordinate with Local Business Radio Users for Emergency Communication
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Collaborate with local businesses and their radio users to enhance emergency communication capabilities:

1. Identify local business radio channels that are in use by companies with large fleets or high-powered simplex radios.
2. Coordinate...
Collaborate with local businesses and their radio users to enhance emergency communication capabilities:

1. Identify local business radio channels that are in use by companies with large fleets or high-powered simplex radios.
2. Coordinate with these businesses to utilize their repeaters or frequencies during emergencies for broader coverage.
3. Ensure that all parties involved understand the importance of following communication protocols and avoiding interference.
4. This collaboration can significantly improve the effectiveness of emergency response efforts in affected areas.
business radio channels, emergency coordination, repeaters, simplex radios, communication planning
Designate Floor Captains in Large Buildings
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. In large buildings, assign Floor Captains and alternates who carry radios for emergency communications.

2. Every building should have a communications plan integrated with its evacuation plan.

3. Not every employee needs to...
Procedure:

1. In large buildings, assign Floor Captains and alternates who carry radios for emergency communications.

2. Every building should have a communications plan integrated with its evacuation plan.

3. Not every employee needs to carry an FRS radio, but key personnel involved in the neighborhood disaster plan should do so.
Floor Captain, building communication, emergency planning
Encourage CERT Training for Volunteers
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Encourage as many volunteers as possible to take CERT (Community Emergency Response Team) training.

2. This ensures that individuals responding to calls for help can do so with confidence and without injury.

3. CERT training...
Procedure:

1. Encourage as many volunteers as possible to take CERT (Community Emergency Response Team) training.

2. This ensures that individuals responding to calls for help can do so with confidence and without injury.

3. CERT training should focus on rescue and public welfare, not on designing communication systems.
CERT training, emergency response, volunteer preparation
Implement Wilderness Protocol for FRS/GMRS Radios
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Wilderness protocol is a method used to conserve battery life and improve the chances of rescue during emergencies:

1. All stations (fixed, portable, or mobile) should monitor Personal Radio Service frequencies every three hours.
2....
Wilderness protocol is a method used to conserve battery life and improve the chances of rescue during emergencies:

1. All stations (fixed, portable, or mobile) should monitor Personal Radio Service frequencies every three hours.
2. Stations with sufficient power resources should monitor for five minutes starting at the top of every hour, or continuously.
3. This protocol helps ensure that trapped individuals who may not be able to operate a radio properly can still be heard.
4. It also reduces unnecessary battery drain and minimizes interference during critical rescue operations.
wilderness protocol, FRS radios, GMRS radios, battery conservation, radio monitoring
Include Amateur Radio Operators in Neighborhood Planning
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Identify amateur radio operators in the neighborhood and include them in communications planning.

2. These individuals can serve as a link between the neighborhood and public safety agencies, especially in areas with long...
Procedure:

1. Identify amateur radio operators in the neighborhood and include them in communications planning.

2. These individuals can serve as a link between the neighborhood and public safety agencies, especially in areas with long distances between neighborhoods.

3. Ensure that amateur radio operators are prepared to assist during emergencies.
amateur radio, emergency communication, neighborhood planning

Procedure:

1. Ensure that the neighborhood is included in a multi-hazard functional plan for its jurisdiction.

2. Maintain up-to-date contact information, including addresses and phone numbers, for every local neighborhood.

3. This can be...
Procedure:

2. These individuals can act as a link between the...
Procedure:

2. These individuals can act as a link between the neighborhood and designated resource points up the communication chain.

3. Ensure that all equipment is compatible with the GMRS frequency range for effective long-range communication.

2. VHF maritime channels cannot be legally used on land, and may be busy with maritime distress activities.

3. This ensures that maritime...
Procedure:

2. VHF maritime channels cannot be legally used on land, and may be busy with maritime distress activities.

Prevent Criminal Exploitation of Emergency Radio Systems
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Criminal activity can exploit emergency radio systems, so it is important to be vigilant:

1. Be aware that individuals with criminal intent may attempt to fake distress calls or identify the location of law enforcement and search teams for...
Criminal activity can exploit emergency radio systems, so it is important to be vigilant:

1. Be aware that individuals with criminal intent may attempt to fake distress calls or identify the location of law enforcement and search teams for looting purposes.
2. Train users to report suspicious behavior such as unusual transmissions, false distress signals, or unauthorized use of frequencies.
3. Ensure that all radios used in emergency situations are monitored by trained personnel who can verify the authenticity of distress calls.
4. Coordinate with local law enforcement to monitor and respond to potential threats related to radio misuse during emergencies.
criminal exploitation, fake distress calls, radio security, emergency monitoring, law enforcement coordination
Program Emergency Frequencies on a Scanner or Radio
High Priority Source: Survival Communications Primer
If you have a scanner or other wideband receive radio, follow these steps to program frequencies:

1. Access a frequency database: Visit a website such as...
If you have a scanner or other wideband receive radio, follow these steps to program frequencies:

1. Access a frequency database: Visit a website such as [http://www.radioreference.com/modules.php?name=RR](http://www.radioreference.com/modules.php?name=RR) or a similar resource. This is a user-maintained database with public frequencies used by police, fire, municipal, county, and state agencies.

2. Identify relevant frequencies: Look for frequencies that may be useful now or in the future. Many modern radios have several hundred memory slots, so you should be able to store plenty of frequencies.

3. Program the frequencies: Save any selected frequencies into your radio's memory for easy access during emergencies.

Note: Some larger municipalities use 'trunked' or digital radio systems. This means that a specific group may switch between different frequencies or channels. While this can make monitoring difficult with simple ham radios, you can still monitor transmissions if you know the list of frequencies used by an agency. In some cases, conversations will switch to another channel, but you may be able to get the gist from the few seconds available.

Alternative option: Listen to neighboring safety frequencies such as smaller municipalities or county sheriff’s channels. You may also find interdepartmental 'aid' frequencies that allow different departments/cities to communicate and call for assistance.
scanner,program,frequencies,radioreference.com,emergency services
Scan All Frequencies for Trapped Individuals
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Scanning all frequencies increases the likelihood of detecting individuals who may be trapped and unable to operate a radio properly:

1. Use radios with squelch codes disabled (CTCSS and DCS settings should be turned off).
2. Continuously...
Scanning all frequencies increases the likelihood of detecting individuals who may be trapped and unable to operate a radio properly:

1. Use radios with squelch codes disabled (CTCSS and DCS settings should be turned off).
2. Continuously scan through available frequencies to listen for any distress signals or calls for help.
3. This method is especially useful in areas where individuals may not know how to use a radio effectively.
4. Ensure that all radios used are set to wideband mode to capture the broadest range of signals.
frequency scanning, trapped individuals, radio operation, CTCSS, DCS

High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Develop a simple method of wearing the radio on the belt, around the neck, or clipped to clothing.

2. This is especially important for elderly and sick individuals who may be at higher risk during emergencies.

3. The method...
Procedure:

1. Develop a simple method of wearing the radio on the belt, around the neck, or clipped to clothing.

2. This is especially important for elderly and sick individuals who may be at higher risk during emergencies.

3. The method should ensure that the radio remains accessible and secure in all situations.
radio wear, elderly, security
Select Appropriate Radio Channels for Neighborhood Use
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Choose the most suitable radio channels for your neighborhood’s communication needs.

Steps:
1. Scan FRS and GMRS channels to monitor current activity in your area.
2. Select the quietest available frequencies for local use (e.g.,...
Objective: Choose the most suitable radio channels for your neighborhood’s communication needs.

Steps:
1. Scan FRS and GMRS channels to monitor current activity in your area.
2. Select the quietest available frequencies for local use (e.g., FRS channel 14 for very-local communications).
3. Avoid busy GMRS repeater output channels that may be used by higher-powered radio stations.
4. Consider using CTCSS/DCS tones to reduce interference and improve communication clarity on selected channels.
FRS channel selection, GMRS repeater usage, CTCSS/DCS tones
Select FRS Radios with Alkaline Battery Compatibility
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Choose an FRS radio that uses regular alkaline batteries instead of rechargeable ones.

2. This is crucial because during a power outage, you may not have access to electricity needed to recharge batteries.

3. Ensure the selected...
Procedure:

1. Choose an FRS radio that uses regular alkaline batteries instead of rechargeable ones.

2. This is crucial because during a power outage, you may not have access to electricity needed to recharge batteries.

3. Ensure the selected model can be easily replaced with standard alkaline batteries for continuous use in emergencies.
alkaline battery, FRS radio, emergency power
Set Expectation for FRS Channel One as Emergency Frequency
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Action: Set the public expectation of FRS channel one as an emergency radio frequency to summon assistance on demand.

Details:
• OEMs (Office of Emergency Management) warn that public safety may not be able to respond to telephone calls...
Action: Set the public expectation of FRS channel one as an emergency radio frequency to summon assistance on demand.

Details:
• OEMs (Office of Emergency Management) warn that public safety may not be able to respond to telephone calls immediately after a disaster.
• Calling for help or rescue on a Family Radio Service portable radio does not increase your chances of emergency assistance.
FRS channel one, emergency frequency, OEM warnings
Share Benefits of Post-Disaster Radio Communication

Action: Disaster communications planners should share the benefits of communicating after an emergency.

• This includes assessing the welfare of persons by radio in a very local way.
post-disaster communication, welfare assessment, community support
Standardize FRS Radio Models in Neighborhoods
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. A neighborhood should standardize on a particular make and model of FRS radio for individuals with no prior experience using radios, as this reduces the likelihood of operational errors during emergencies.

2. This standardization...
Procedure:

1. A neighborhood should standardize on a particular make and model of FRS radio for individuals with no prior experience using radios, as this reduces the likelihood of operational errors during emergencies.

2. This standardization ensures that all members can use the same equipment without confusion or difficulty.

3. It is especially important for those who may be likely to have problems using their radios due to age, health conditions, or lack of familiarity with technology.
FRS radio, standardization, neighborhood communication
Test Neighborhood Radio Systems Regularly
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Test the neighborhood’s radio system at least four times a year.

2. A good time for testing may be just before a Neighborhood Watch meeting or local disaster preparedness meeting.

3. Encourage informal use of radios throughout...
Procedure:

1. Test the neighborhood’s radio system at least four times a year.

2. A good time for testing may be just before a Neighborhood Watch meeting or local disaster preparedness meeting.

3. Encourage informal use of radios throughout the year to ensure familiarity with the equipment.
radio test, neighborhood communication, emergency readiness
Train Individuals on Proper Use of FRS/GMRS Radios
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Training is essential for effective use of FRS and GMRS radios during emergencies:

1. Provide basic training sessions to teach users how to operate the push-to-talk function, change channels, and monitor frequencies.
2. Emphasize the...
Training is essential for effective use of FRS and GMRS radios during emergencies:

1. Provide basic training sessions to teach users how to operate the push-to-talk function, change channels, and monitor frequencies.
2. Emphasize the importance of disabling CTCSS and DCS settings to ensure that all users can communicate with each other without interference.
3. Train individuals on battery management techniques, such as conserving power by limiting radio use during non-critical times.
4. Encourage participation in community training programs or CERT (Community Emergency Response Team) initiatives for hands-on experience.
FRS radio training, GMRS radio training, CTCSS disable, DCS disable, emergency communication skills
Train for Radio Direction Finding in Emergencies
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Radio direction finding techniques can help locate individuals who are trapped or in dangerous locations:

1. Train local responders and volunteers in basic radio direction finding methods, such as using signal strength indicators and...
Radio direction finding techniques can help locate individuals who are trapped or in dangerous locations:

1. Train local responders and volunteers in basic radio direction finding methods, such as using signal strength indicators and triangulation.
2. Coordinate with experienced groups like the Civil Air Patrol or Amateur Radio Service volunteers for assistance during emergencies.
3. These techniques are especially useful when individuals are trapped inside buildings or other structures where visual identification is not possible.
4. Ensure that all personnel involved in rescue operations have access to direction finding equipment and training.
radio direction finding, trapped individuals, emergency training, Civil Air Patrol, Amateur Radio Service
Understand The Limitations Of Two-Way Radios In Emergencies
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Do Not Expect Immediate Help: Possession of a two-way radio does not guarantee an instant or reliable response to a call for help.

2. Avoid False Assumptions: Some families may believe that having a radio ensures...
Procedure:

1. Do Not Expect Immediate Help: Possession of a two-way radio does not guarantee an instant or reliable response to a call for help.

2. Avoid False Assumptions: Some families may believe that having a radio ensures immediate assistance, but this is not always the case.

3. Prepare For Isolation: Emergency aid may be unavailable due to impassable conditions, flooding, power outages, or lack of public safety resources.
radio limitations, emergency response, isolation risks
Understand The Physics Of Radio Wave Propagation For Effective Communication
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Recognize Limitations Based On Physics: The range of a radio is determined by the laws of physics, particularly how UHF radio waves propagate.

2. Ignore Marketing Claims: Be aware that marketing claims about radios do not...
Procedure:

1. Recognize Limitations Based On Physics: The range of a radio is determined by the laws of physics, particularly how UHF radio waves propagate.

2. Ignore Marketing Claims: Be aware that marketing claims about radios do not override the physical limitations of signal propagation.

3. Plan Accordingly: Understand that even with the best equipment, communication may be limited based on environmental factors.
radio physics, uhf propagation, marketing claims
Understand The Role Of FRS And GMRS In Disaster Communication
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Explain Capabilities And Limitations: Emergency OES/OEM officials must inform the public about what FRS and GMRS can and cannot do during a disaster.

2. Assess Available Resources: Officials should evaluate:
- Are...
Procedure:

1. Explain Capabilities And Limitations: Emergency OES/OEM officials must inform the public about what FRS and GMRS can and cannot do during a disaster.

2. Assess Available Resources: Officials should evaluate:
- Are there GMRS repeaters in your community? What are their coverage areas?
- How many families have access to these systems?
- Where are the base stations located? Are they connected to emergency power?
- Will the owners make the repeaters available for use during emergencies?

3. Identify Local GMRS Licensees: Determine if there are GMRS licensees in your community who may be able to assist with communication during a disaster.

4. Ensure EOC Readiness: The Emergency Operations Center (EOC) should have licensed GMRS operators available to man a base station during emergencies.
frs, gmrs, disaster communication, repeaters, eoc readiness
Use Conventional Communication Methods As Primary Options
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Prioritize Cellular And Landline Phones: Use conventional methods like cellular or landline telephones for communication during emergencies, as they are familiar to the public.

2. Accept Possible Outages: Be prepared for...
Procedure:

1. Prioritize Cellular And Landline Phones: Use conventional methods like cellular or landline telephones for communication during emergencies, as they are familiar to the public.

2. Accept Possible Outages: Be prepared for these systems to be temporarily unavailable after a disaster.

3. Use Two-Way Radios As A Backup: In the time period immediately following a disaster and before conventional systems are restored, two-way radios can be valuable for local communication.
conventional communication, cellular phones, landline phones, backup radio use
Use Ham Repeater Frequencies for Emergency Monitoring
High Priority Source: Survival Communications Primer
To monitor ham repeaters and stay informed during emergencies:

1. Access a ham repeater database: Visit a website such as [http://www.artscipub.com/repeaters/](http://www.artscipub.com/repeaters/) or a similar resource. This site lists ham...
To monitor ham repeaters and stay informed during emergencies:

1. Access a ham repeater database: Visit a website such as [http://www.artscipub.com/repeaters/](http://www.artscipub.com/repeaters/) or a similar resource. This site lists ham repeaters across the US.

2. Identify emergency-related repeaters: Pay particular attention to any repeaters that are listed as OEM (Office of Emergency Management), EMA (Emergency Management Agency), ARES (Amateur Radio Emergency Service), or RACES (Radio Amateur Civil Emergency Service) affiliated.

3. Monitor the frequencies: Even if you are not a ham radio operator, listening to ham operators can provide valuable information during emergencies. These repeaters often serve as communication hubs for emergency management and response teams.
ham repeater,frequencies,emergency monitoring,artscipub.com
Use Plain Language for Radio Communication
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. When using a radio to communicate with neighbors, use plain language instead of codes.

2. Avoid the use of ten codes or other shorthand that may lead to misunderstandings.

3. This ensures clear and effective communication during...
Procedure:

1. When using a radio to communicate with neighbors, use plain language instead of codes.

2. Avoid the use of ten codes or other shorthand that may lead to misunderstandings.

3. This ensures clear and effective communication during emergencies.
plain language, radio communication, emergency clarity
Use Radio Codes Only When Necessary
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Use radio codes instead of plain language only if you can guarantee there will be no misunderstandings.

2. This is particularly important in high-stress or chaotic situations where clarity is essential.

3. Ensure that all users...
Procedure:

1. Use radio codes instead of plain language only if you can guarantee there will be no misunderstandings.

2. This is particularly important in high-stress or chaotic situations where clarity is essential.

3. Ensure that all users understand the specific codes being used and their meanings.
radio code, communication protocol, emergency use
Use Radios with Coded-Squelch Disabled
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Action: Encourage neighbors to use radios with coded-squelch disabled.

Details:
• This allows for broader communication during emergencies.
coded-squelch disable, radio accessibility, emergency communication
Use Two-Way Radios For Local Neighbor Communication During Disasters
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Primary Use For Neighbors: Two-way radios are primarily intended for communication between neighbors during and after a disaster.

2. Support CERT Volunteers: These systems can help CERT volunteers or others provide...
Procedure:

1. Primary Use For Neighbors: Two-way radios are primarily intended for communication between neighbors during and after a disaster.

2. Support CERT Volunteers: These systems can help CERT volunteers or others provide immediate search and rescue assistance or allow neighbors to assist each other.

3. Do Not Rely On Radios For Emergency Aid: Understand that emergency aid may not be available to everyone, even if they have a radio.
neighbor communication, cert volunteers, disaster support
Use Two-Way Radios To Communicate With Isolated Individuals During Emergencies
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Enable Communication In Remote Areas: While not guaranteed, having a two-way radio may allow officials to communicate with individuals in isolated areas during emergencies.

2. Coordinate With Emergency Management:...
Procedure:

1. Enable Communication In Remote Areas: While not guaranteed, having a two-way radio may allow officials to communicate with individuals in isolated areas during emergencies.

2. Coordinate With Emergency Management: Officials should be prepared to use these systems to reach people who are cut off from other forms of communication.
isolated individuals, emergency communication, remote areas
Avoid Use of Call Tones on Radios
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Procedure:

1. Avoid using call tones on FRS radios as they serve no useful purpose during emergencies.

2. These tones can make the radio seem like a toy, which may reduce its effectiveness in serious situations.

3. Encourage users to...
Procedure:

1. Avoid using call tones on FRS radios as they serve no useful purpose during emergencies.

2. These tones can make the radio seem like a toy, which may reduce its effectiveness in serious situations.

3. Encourage users to communicate directly and clearly without relying on call tones.
call tone, FRS radio, communication clarity
Avoiding Common FRS Radio Pitfalls in Emergencies
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Prevent common mistakes that can hinder communication during emergencies.

Procedure:
1. Do not rely on complex radios with multiple features, as they are harder to use under stress.
2. Avoid using CTCSS or DCS tones,...
Objective: Prevent common mistakes that can hinder communication during emergencies.

Procedure:
1. Do not rely on complex radios with multiple features, as they are harder to use under stress.
2. Avoid using CTCSS or DCS tones, which:
- Are difficult for most users to set up
- Are marketed as privacy features but do not provide real privacy
- Often cause interference with other users if not used correctly
3. Never assume others will listen before transmitting; this can lead to accidental interference.
4. Avoid using radios that require menus or push-button functions, as these are confusing and hard to master in an emergency.
5. Do not use FRS radios with tone-coded squelch features during emergencies, as they may prevent communication entirely.

Notes:
• Radios should be intuitive and easy to operate without instruction manuals or prior knowledge.
• Feature-rich radios are often less reliable in high-stress situations.
FRS radio pitfalls, emergency communication mistakes, CTCSS DCS tones, radio interference
Communications Planning for Collective-Protection Shelters
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Communications Planning for Collective-Protection Shelters

1. Use wire communication systems to communicate with those in adjacent shelters or immediately outside the shelter.
2. If radios are required for communications with adjacent units at...
Communications Planning for Collective-Protection Shelters

1. Use wire communication systems to communicate with those in adjacent shelters or immediately outside the shelter.
2. If radios are required for communications with adjacent units at a distance:
- Remote the antennas away from the shelter.
- Place antennas so that a hill or other obstacle is between the antennas and the enemy, if possible.
wire communication, radio usage, antenna placement
Selecting Appropriate FRS Radios for Emergency Use
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Choose FRS radios that are simple and effective for emergency communication.

Procedure:
1. Choose FRS-only radios to avoid the need for a license.
2. Avoid tone-coded squelch options, as they:
- Complicate setup
-...
Objective: Choose FRS radios that are simple and effective for emergency communication.

Procedure:
1. Choose FRS-only radios to avoid the need for a license.
2. Avoid tone-coded squelch options, as they:
- Complicate setup
- Cause compatibility issues between different manufacturers
- Interfere with GMRS repeaters when used on channels 8 through 14
- Make it harder to communicate quickly during an emergency
3. Remove call tone features from radios, as they are rarely useful and often used to annoy others.
4. Opt for a simple one-button scan function for ease of use.
5. Avoid feature-rich models, as they tend to be more complex and less reliable in emergencies.
6. Prioritize simplicity and voice communication over advanced features.

Notes:
• Manufacturers should focus on developing radios that are easy to use, especially for non-experts.
• Radios with reduced complexity are better suited for emergency situations like CERT teams or neighborhood crime prevention groups.
FRS radios, emergency communication, radio setup, CERT teams, neighborhood communication
Selecting a Two-Way Radio for Hunting or Outdoor Use
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Procedure:

1. Determine the communication needs of your group:
- Consider the distance between users (e.g., hunting in large areas may require greater range).
- Decide if you need additional features such as privacy codes, multiple...
Procedure:

1. Determine the communication needs of your group:
- Consider the distance between users (e.g., hunting in large areas may require greater range).
- Decide if you need additional features such as privacy codes, multiple channels, or detachable antennas.
2. Choose between FRS-only and FRS/GMRS radios:
- FRS-only radios are ideal for short-range communication (up to 2 miles) and do not require a license. They are typically less expensive but offer fewer features.
- FRS/GMRS radios provide greater range (up to 10 miles under ideal conditions), more channels, and may include advanced features like privacy codes. However, they require a GMRS license from the FCC.
3. Ensure all radios in your group are of the same model or compatible models:
- Radios from different brands may not support shared features such as privacy codes.
4. Consider channel availability:
- FRS offers 14 channels; GMRS offers 23 channels.
- More channels increase the likelihood of finding a clear frequency, especially in urban areas.
5. Check for additional features:
- Look for radios with durable construction, long battery life, and weather resistance if used outdoors.
6. Verify local regulations:
- Some states may prohibit the use of two-way radios during deer hunting or other activities.
two-way radio selection, FRS radios, GMRS radios, hunting communication, outdoor radio use
Set Up Family Radio Service (FRS) Communications
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Overview: The Family Radio Service (FRS) is a license-free very-short-range radio service suitable for local communication.

Steps:
1. Use channels one through seven, which are shared with the General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS). GMRS...
Overview: The Family Radio Service (FRS) is a license-free very-short-range radio service suitable for local communication.

Steps:
1. Use channels one through seven, which are shared with the General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS). GMRS licensees may use up to 5 watts effective radiated power on these channels, allowing FRS radios in a neighborhood to be useful if one licensed resident communicates with many local hand-held units.
2. Use channels eight through fourteen exclusively for FRS communication.
3. Be aware that FRS is limited to one-half watt output, and manufacturer claims of long-range communication are exaggerated.
4. Antenna height significantly affects range, as FRS radio waves travel line of sight. Communication over tens of miles may be possible from high altitudes (e.g., on a mountain or building roof).
5. Be prepared for severe interference in urban areas, especially due to intentional call tones used to annoy others.
6. Avoid using FRS radios as children’s toys, and ensure that parents know who their children are communicating with, particularly with text-messaging models like Hasbro radios.
7. Use FRS radios for very-local communication around a neighborhood or home, especially before, during, and after disasters.
8. Be aware of FCC rules: FRS radios cannot be connected to external antennas, telephone systems, or store-and-forward repeaters.
FRS radio, line of sight, urban interference, children’s use, FCC rules, local communication
Understand Standardized Alphabets Used by Emergency Services
Standard Source: Survival Communications Primer
Emergency services often use standardized alphabets for clear communication. Here are two common systems:

### Law Enforcement Alphabet (NATO/Military/International)
| Letter | Word |
|--------|------|
| A | Adam |
| B | Boy |
| C ...
Emergency services often use standardized alphabets for clear communication. Here are two common systems:

### Law Enforcement Alphabet (NATO/Military/International)
| Letter | Word |
|--------|------|
| A | Adam |
| B | Boy |
| C | Charles|
| D | David|
| E | Edward|
| F | Frank|
| G | George|
| H | Henry|
| I | Ida |
| J | John |
| K | King |
| L | Lincoln|
| M | Mary |
| N | Nora |
| O | Ocean|
| P | Paul |
| Q | Queen|
| R | Robert|
| S | Sam |
| T | Tom |
| U | Union|
| V | Victor|
| W | William|
| X | X-ray|
| Y | Young|
| Z | Zebra|

### NATO/Military/International Alphabet
| Letter | Word |
|--------|----------|
| A | Alpha |
| B | Bravo |
| C | Charlie |
| D | Delta |
| E | Echo |
| F | Foxtrot |
| G | Golf |
| H | Hotel |
| I | India |
| J | Juliet |
| K | Kilo |
| L | Lima |
| M | Mike |
| N | November |
| O | Oscar |
| P | Papa |
| Q | Quebec |
| R | Romeo |
| S | Sierra |
| T | Tango |
| U | Uniform |
| V | Victor |
| W | Whiskey |
| X | X-ray |
| Y | Yankee |
| Z | Zulu |

Use: These alphabets are used to avoid confusion when spelling out names, locations, or other words over radio communication. Always use the correct alphabet based on the context (e.g., law enforcement vs. military).
alphabet,emergency services,NATO,military,communication standards
Using Two-Way Radios in the Field or During Outdoor Activities
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Procedure:

1. Choose a channel:
- Select an unused frequency (channel) that is not being used by others outside your group.
- In crowded areas, use one of the additional channels available on FRS/GMRS radios to avoid interference.
2....
Procedure:

1. Choose a channel:
- Select an unused frequency (channel) that is not being used by others outside your group.
- In crowded areas, use one of the additional channels available on FRS/GMRS radios to avoid interference.
2. Establish communication protocols:
- Agree on a schedule for check-ins or updates with your group (e.g., every 30 minutes during hunting).
- Set up specific codes or phrases for emergencies (e.g., 'Deer down' or 'Need help').
3. Maintain radio battery life:
- Use radios with long-lasting batteries, and carry spare batteries if needed.
- Turn off the radio when not in use to conserve power.
4. Monitor for interference:
- If communication is unclear or interrupted, switch to a different channel.
5. Keep the radio accessible:
- Store the radio in a waterproof case or pouch if used in wet environments (e.g., hunting near water).
6. Practice using the radio before heading into the field:
- Ensure all users are familiar with the controls and features of the radios.
two-way radio usage, outdoor communication, hunting with radios, radio protocols
Avoid Illegal Use of FRS Radios Outside the US and Canada
Reference Source: Disaster Communications
Overview: The Family Radio Service (FRS) is only legal in the United States and Canada.

Steps:
1. Do not use FRS radios outside the U.S. or Canada, as it is illegal in most other countries.
2. If you need to use FRS frequencies abroad,...
Overview: The Family Radio Service (FRS) is only legal in the United States and Canada.

Steps:
1. Do not use FRS radios outside the U.S. or Canada, as it is illegal in most other countries.
2. If you need to use FRS frequencies abroad, obtain prior permission from local authorities, though this is rare and usually only granted for specific purposes.
3. Be aware that some individuals have used FRS radios illegally in foreign countries without authorization.
FRS radio, international use, illegal usage, FCC regulations
Daytime Shortwave Listening Procedure
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio 101
Objective: Optimize shortwave listening during daylight hours.

Procedure:
1. Avoid peak daytime hours (10 A.M. to 3 P.M.) as signal quality is typically poorest during this time due to reduced broadcasting activity.
2. **Use the...
Objective: Optimize shortwave listening during daylight hours.

Procedure:
1. Avoid peak daytime hours (10 A.M. to 3 P.M.) as signal quality is typically poorest during this time due to reduced broadcasting activity.
2. Use the following bands for daytime listening:
- 13m band: Results vary, but worth trying. Sometimes extremely good around sunrise and sunset.
- 16m band: Similar to 19m band.
- 19m band: Best overall daytime band. May also be good at night in summer months. Sometimes extremely good around sunrise and sunset. Sometimes good at night in the summer.
- 22m band: Similar to 19m band.
- 25m band: Best around sunrise and sunset. May be good mid-day in some areas.
- 31m band: Similar to 25m band.
3. Experiment with local conditions as results may vary by region and time of day.
shortwave radio, daytime listening, frequency bands, signal quality
Nighttime Shortwave Listening Procedure
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio 101
Objective: Optimize shortwave listening during nighttime hours.

Procedure:
1. Use the following bands for nighttime listening:
- 25m band: Similar to 31m band.
- 31m band: Good all night everywhere. Often extremely good at...
Objective: Optimize shortwave listening during nighttime hours.

Procedure:
1. Use the following bands for nighttime listening:
- 25m band: Similar to 31m band.
- 31m band: Good all night everywhere. Often extremely good at sunrise and sunset. Good results often start about an hour before sunset.
- 49m band: Best overall night band.
- 41m band: Similar to 49m band. Good all night worldwide.
2. Optimal times for listening: Around sunset and sunrise, as signal quality is typically best during these periods.
3. Be prepared for variability in signal strength depending on location and atmospheric conditions.
shortwave radio, nighttime listening, frequency bands, signal quality
Use FRS Radios for Hunting Coordination (Where Legal)
Reference Source: Disaster Communications
Overview: Use of FRS radios by hunters may be regulated in some states.

Steps:
1. Check local hunting regulations to determine whether the use of two-way radios is allowed for coordinating hunts.
2. In some states, using FRS radios to...
Overview: Use of FRS radios by hunters may be regulated in some states.

Steps:
1. Check local hunting regulations to determine whether the use of two-way radios is allowed for coordinating hunts.
2. In some states, using FRS radios to coordinate hunting is considered unsportsmanlike conduct, and may result in penalties or disqualification from hunting activities.
3. If permitted, ensure that all users are aware of the rules and use radios responsibly.
hunting regulations, FRS radio, unsportsmanlike conduct
