2. Connect the plate of the vacuum tube to the carbon terminal (positive pole) of the B or dry cell battery.
3. Connect the zinc terminal (negative pole) of the B or dry cell battery to binding post a.
4. Connect post b to the other side of the variable condenser.
5. Connect the terminals of the head phones to the binding posts a and b.
6. Ensure that the plate connections of the battery are not reversed.

7. Connect one of the posts of the rheostat to one terminal of the filament.
8. Connect the other terminal of the filament to the negative terminal of the A or storage battery.
9. Connect the positive terminal of the A or storage battery to the other post of the rheostat.
10. Finally, connect the positive terminal of the A or storage battery with the wire that runs from the head phones to the variable condenser.
vacuum tube connection, battery setup, radio wiring, rheostat configuration, head phone attachment
Construct a Grid Leak Amplifier Receiving Set with Crystal Detector
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:
1. A loose coupled tuning coil
2. A variable condenser
3. Two fixed condensers
4. A crystal detector or better, a vacuum tube detector
5. An A battery (6 volt storage battery)
6. A rheostat
7. A B battery (22-1/2 volt dry...
Materials Required:
1. A loose coupled tuning coil
2. A variable condenser
3. Two fixed condensers
4. A crystal detector or better, a vacuum tube detector
5. An A battery (6 volt storage battery)
6. A rheostat
7. A B battery (22-1/2 volt dry cell battery)
8. A fixed resistance unit, also known as a leak grid
9. A pair of head-phones

Procedure:
1. Assemble the tuning circuit: Connect the loose coupled tuning coil in series with the variable condenser and one of the fixed condensers to form the antenna circuit.
2. Connect the detector circuit: Attach the crystal detector or vacuum tube detector to the output side of the tuning circuit. If using a vacuum tube detector, ensure it is properly biased with the A battery.
3. Set up the power supply: Connect the 6-volt storage battery (A battery) to the grid leak resistor and the rheostat in series. This provides biasing for the detector and controls signal strength.
4. Connect the audio output: Link the output of the detector circuit to the head-phones through the second fixed condenser, which acts as a coupling capacitor to block DC current while allowing AC signals (audio frequencies) to pass.
5. Add shielding: If using multiple amplifier tubes or vacuum tube detectors, ensure they are properly shielded from each other to avoid feedback and howling noise in the head-phones.
6. Test the circuit: Adjust the rheostat to control sensitivity and tune the variable condenser to receive signals. Listen through the head-phones for incoming radio transmissions.

Notes:
• The grid leak resistor must be carefully selected based on testing, as it determines the operating point of the detector tube.
• If using a vacuum tube detector instead of a crystal detector, additional biasing and amplification stages may be required.
• Ensure all components are properly insulated to avoid short circuits or interference.
grid leak amplifier, crystal detector, radio receiving set, vacuum tube, tuning coil, variable condenser, head-phones
Construct a Six Step Amplifier Receiving Set with Loop Aerial
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure for Constructing a Six Step Amplifier Receiving Set with Loop Aerial

1. Use three-step radio and audio frequency amplifiers: Use three amplifying tubes coupled with radio frequency transformers and three amplifying tubes coupled...
Procedure for Constructing a Six Step Amplifier Receiving Set with Loop Aerial

1. Use three-step radio and audio frequency amplifiers: Use three amplifying tubes coupled with radio frequency transformers and three amplifying tubes coupled with audio frequency transformers, as described in the section on _A Radio Frequency Transformer Receiving Set_.

2. Construct loop aerial:
- Wind 10 turns of No. 14 or 16 copper wire about a wooden frame that is two feet on each side, spacing the wires 1/16 inch apart (as shown in Fig. 47).
- This forms an efficient indoor loop aerial, eliminating the need for an outdoor aerial.

3. Receiving range: With this six-step amplifier set and loop aerial, you can receive wavelengths of 150 to 600 meters from high-power stations at considerable distances.

4. Wiring diagram: Follow the wiring diagram shown in Fig. 47 for connecting the components.
loop aerial,six-step amplifier,wiring diagram,cascade connection
Construct a Tuning Coil (Transmitting Inductance)
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To construct a tuning coil, also known as a transmitting inductance:

1. Materials: Use copper wire.
2. Winding: Wind 25 or 30 turns of the copper wire on an insulating tube or frame.
3. Purpose: This simple design is suitable for basic...
To construct a tuning coil, also known as a transmitting inductance:

1. Materials: Use copper wire.
2. Winding: Wind 25 or 30 turns of the copper wire on an insulating tube or frame.
3. Purpose: This simple design is suitable for basic applications and provides the necessary inductance for tuning purposes.
tuning coil,transmitting inductance,copper wire,winding,insulating tube
Construct a Wireless Key for Amateur Radio Use
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To construct a wireless key suitable for amateur radio use:

1. Design the Key: Build it like an ordinary telegraph key, but ensure that it is somewhat heavier if large currents are to be used.
2. Contact Points: Provide large silver contact...
To construct a wireless key suitable for amateur radio use:

1. Design the Key: Build it like an ordinary telegraph key, but ensure that it is somewhat heavier if large currents are to be used.
2. Contact Points: Provide large silver contact points to handle high current loads effectively.
3. Spark Gap Types: Choose from three types of spark gaps:
- The _plain_ or _stationary type_: Suitable for small spark-coil sets.
- The _rotating type_: Prevents arcing and ensures a quicker, cleaner break with less dragging of the spark.
- The _quenched gap_ type: More efficient than both plain and rotating types, and is noiseless.
wireless key,telegraph key,silver contact points,spark gaps,rotating spark gap,quenched gap
Construct an Oscillation Transformer for Radio Transmitters
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To construct an oscillation transformer suitable for radio transmitters:

1. Primary Coil: Wind a number of turns of copper wire on a fixed insulating support to form the primary coil.
2. Secondary Coil: Wind approximately twice as many...
To construct an oscillation transformer suitable for radio transmitters:

1. Primary Coil: Wind a number of turns of copper wire on a fixed insulating support to form the primary coil.
2. Secondary Coil: Wind approximately twice as many turns of copper wire on another fixed insulating support to form the secondary coil.
3. Relative Positioning: Ensure that the coils are relatively movable, allowing for tuning and adjustment of the transformer's inductance.
4. Regulatory Requirement: An oscillation transformer is required by government regulations unless _inductively coupled_ is used instead.
oscillation transformer,primary coil,secondary coil,copper wire,insulating support
How to Begin Wireless Communication
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Overview

To begin wireless communication, you need to understand the basic components of a wireless system and how to obtain necessary licenses.

### Steps:
1. Understand the types of wireless systems: There are two main types: *wireless...
Overview

To begin wireless communication, you need to understand the basic components of a wireless system and how to obtain necessary licenses.

### Steps:
1. Understand the types of wireless systems: There are two main types: *wireless telegraph* (using a telegraph key) and *wireless telephone* (using a microphone transmitter).
2. Identify the parts of a wireless system:
- The *aerial wire system* or *antenna*
- The *transmitter* or *sender*
- The *receiver* or *receptor*
3. Obtain necessary information: If you live near an electrical supply store, visit it for equipment and advice. Otherwise, contact makers of wireless apparatus for catalogues and price lists.
4. Know what you need before buying: Understand the components required to achieve your goals (e.g., receiving vs. transmitting).
5. Get a government license:
- Required for *sending* messages but not for *receiving*.
- To apply, contact the *Radio Inspector* in your district: Boston, New York City, Baltimore, Norfolk, New Orleans, San Francisco, Seattle, Detroit, or Chicago.
6. Start with a receiving set: This is the easiest way to begin. You can listen-in to broadcasts without needing a license.
7. Consider aerial wire systems:
- If you live in an apartment or city, string your aerial on the roof (with permission).
- If not feasible, use wires along the building or even connect to an iron/brass bed for basic reception.
8. Ground connection is essential:
- Connect your receiving set to a wire that leads to moist earth.
- Use existing pipes if available; otherwise, bury copper or zinc sheets in the ground.
9. Choose between crystal and vacuum tube detectors:
- *Crystal detector* is cheaper but less sensitive and requires frequent adjustment.
- *Vacuum tube detector* is more sensitive but requires two small batteries.
10. Set up a simple receiving set: Consists of (a) a detector, (b) a tuning coil, and (c) a telephone receiver connected to the aerial and ground.
11. Use amplifiers for distant stations: Vacuum tubes can be used in *cascade* to amplify signals many times over.
12. Select appropriate receivers:
- A single receiver or a pair with a head-band gives better results.
- Higher resistance (e.g., 3,200 ohms) improves sensitivity.
13. Use a loudspeaker for group listening: A metal cone fitted with a telephone receiver can project sound to a room full of people.
wireless communication, radio amateur, starting wireless, aerial wire system, receiving set, government license, crystal detector, vacuum tube detector
How to Set Up a Simple Receiving Set
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Overview

A simple receiving set consists of three main components: a detector, a tuning coil, and a telephone receiver.

### Steps:
1. Select the detector: Choose between *crystal* or *vacuum tube* detectors. Crystal is cheaper but less...
Overview

A simple receiving set consists of three main components: a detector, a tuning coil, and a telephone receiver.

### Steps:
1. Select the detector: Choose between *crystal* or *vacuum tube* detectors. Crystal is cheaper but less sensitive; vacuum tube requires batteries but is more reliable.
2. Set up the tuning coil:
- A simple tuning coil consists of a single layer of copper wire wound on a cylinder with an adjustable contact.
- For sharp tuning, use a *loose coupled tuning coil* and connect a variable condenser across the closed oscillation circuit.
3. Connect to aerial and ground: Ensure your receiving set is connected to both the aerial wire and a good ground connection (e.g., through pipes or buried copper/zinc sheets).
4. Use fixed condensers:
- Connect a *fixed condenser* across the telephone receivers when using a single coil tuner.
5. Add amplification if needed: Use vacuum tubes in *cascade* to amplify signals for distant stations.
6. Select appropriate receivers:
- A single receiver or a pair with a head-band gives better results.
- Higher resistance (e.g., 3,200 ohms) improves sensitivity.
7. Use a loudspeaker: For group listening, use a metal cone fitted with a telephone receiver to project sound.

Notes: The setup is ideal for beginners and can be assembled from separate components or purchased as a pre-assembled set.
simple receiving set, radio setup, tuning coil, detector selection, ground connection, amplification, loudspeaker
Prevent Howling in Amplifier Systems
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure for Preventing Howling in Radio Frequency or Audio Frequency Amplifiers

1. Shield amplifier tubes: Enclose each amplifier tube in a metal box and place them at least six inches apart from one another to prevent feedback.

2....
Procedure for Preventing Howling in Radio Frequency or Audio Frequency Amplifiers

1. Shield amplifier tubes: Enclose each amplifier tube in a metal box and place them at least six inches apart from one another to prevent feedback.

2. Position transformers: Arrange the transformers so that their cores are at right angles to each other, and ensure they are placed no less than six inches apart.

3. Feedback prevention:
- Feedback through amplifiers can cause head phones or loudspeakers to howl.
- Proper shielding and spacing of components is essential for eliminating this issue in cascade amplifier systems.
howling prevention,feedback suppression,cascade amplifier,wiring diagram
Tune Receiving Set No. 1 with Tuning Coil
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Objective: Tune the receiving set using the tuning coil to receive signals.

Steps:
1. Move slide A of the double-slide tuner (see B in Fig. 15) over to the end of the coil connected with the ground wire.
2. Move slide B near the opposite...
Objective: Tune the receiving set using the tuning coil to receive signals.

Steps:
1. Move slide A of the double-slide tuner (see B in Fig. 15) over to the end of the coil connected with the ground wire.
2. Move slide B near the opposite end of the coil (the one that has the free end).
3. Move slide A toward slide B until you hear dots and dashes, speech, or music coming in as loud as possible.
4. Continue moving slide B toward slide A until the sounds become even louder.

Notes:
• With a few trials, you will be able to tune in or out any station within range.
• This method works best for stations that are not too close or powerful.
tuning coil,double slide tuner,receiving set adjustment,signal tuning
Understand the Differences Between Spark Gap and Vacuum Tube Transmitters
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To understand the differences between spark gap and vacuum tube transmitters:

1. Spark Gap Set: Produces _periodic_ oscillations, which are discontinuous.
2. Limitation for Telephony: Cannot be used for wireless telephony due to the...
To understand the differences between spark gap and vacuum tube transmitters:

1. Spark Gap Set: Produces _periodic_ oscillations, which are discontinuous.
2. Limitation for Telephony: Cannot be used for wireless telephony due to the discontinuity of the oscillations.
3. Vacuum Tube Transmitter: Produces _sustained_ oscillations, which are continuous and ideal for wireless telephony.
4. Advantage of Vacuum Tubes: Easier to maintain sustained oscillations compared to electric arcs, making vacuum tubes nearly universal in wireless telephone transmitters.
spark gap,vacuum tube,sustained oscillations,wireless telephony,periodic oscillations
Understand the Differences Between Two and Three Electrode Vacuum Tubes
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Two-Electrode Vacuum Tube:
• Consists of a wire filament and a metal plate, sealed in a glass bulb with a vacuum or nitrogen gas.
• The wire filament is heated red-hot, while the metal plate remains cold and charged with positive...
Two-Electrode Vacuum Tube:
• Consists of a wire filament and a metal plate, sealed in a glass bulb with a vacuum or nitrogen gas.
• The wire filament is heated red-hot, while the metal plate remains cold and charged with positive electricity.
• Used as a detector but lacks amplification capabilities.

Three-Electrode Vacuum Tube (Triode):
• Includes an additional grid electrode, placed between the filament and the metal plate.
• Allows for greater control over current flow, enabling amplification of signals.
• More sensitive than two-electrode tubes and has largely replaced them in modern applications.

Connection Differences:
• In a three-electrode tube, the grid is connected to one post of the variable condenser.
• The plate is connected to the positive terminal of the B battery (dry cell), while the filament is connected to the A battery (storage battery) through the rheostat.

Advantages of Triodes:
• Greater sensitivity and amplification power.
• Can drive a loud speaker, making it suitable for public address systems or larger listening areas.
two electrode tube, three electrode tube, triode function, grid connection, amplification capability
Choosing a Spark Gap for Transmitting Sets
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select either a fixed, rotary, or quenched spark gap.

2. Fixed spark gaps are rarely used with large currents due to arcing issues.

3. Rotary spark gaps consist of:
- A wheel driven by an electric motor
-...
Procedure:

1. Select either a fixed, rotary, or quenched spark gap.

2. Fixed spark gaps are rarely used with large currents due to arcing issues.

3. Rotary spark gaps consist of:
- A wheel driven by an electric motor
- Projecting plugs (electrodes) on the wheel
- Stationary plugs on each side of the wheel

4. The number of sparks per second can be adjusted by changing the speed of the wheel.

5. Quenched spark gaps are preferred for induction coil sets:
- Eliminates noise from ordinary gaps
- Increases range by 200% when properly designed

6. Cost examples:
- Rotary gap with 110-volt motor: $25.00
- 1/4 kilowatt quenched gap: $10.00
spark gap, rotary spark gap, quenched spark gap, induction coil set
Connecting Apparatus for Alternating Current Use
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Layout and placement:
- Screw the key to the table near the front edge.
- Place the high tension condenser behind the key.
- Position the oscillation transformer behind the condenser.
- Set the alternating current...
Procedure:

1. Layout and placement:
- Screw the key to the table near the front edge.
- Place the high tension condenser behind the key.
- Position the oscillation transformer behind the condenser.
- Set the alternating current transformer to the left of the oscillation transformer.
- Place the rotary or quenched spark gap in front of the transformer.

2. Wiring steps:
- Bring No. 12 or 14 insulated wires from the 110-volt lighting leads.
- Connect them with a single-throw, double-pole switch.
- Connect one pole of the switch to the primary coil post of the alternating power transformer.
- Connect the other post of the transformer to one post of the key and the other post of the key to the other pole of the switch.

3. Motor connection:
- Connect the motor of the rotary spark gap to the power circuit.
- Use a single-pole, single-throw switch in the motor circuit.

4. Spark gap and condenser connections:
- Connect secondary coil posts to the rotary or quenched spark gap.
- Connect one post of the spark gap to one post of the condenser.
- Connect the other post of the condenser to the primary coil of the oscillation transformer (inside coil).

5. Ground and aerial connection:
- Connect the secondary coil post of the oscillation transformer to ground.
- Connect the clip of the secondary coil to the wire leading to the aerial when tuning.
alternating current setup, wiring diagram, radio equipment layout
Connecting Apparatus for Direct Current Use
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. For direct current use (110 volts), an electrolytic interrupter is required.

2. Construction of the electrolytic interrupter:
- A jar filled with a solution of 1 part sulfuric acid and 9 parts water.
- A lead electrode...
Procedure:

1. For direct current use (110 volts), an electrolytic interrupter is required.

2. Construction of the electrolytic interrupter:
- A jar filled with a solution of 1 part sulfuric acid and 9 parts water.
- A lead electrode with a large surface area, fastened to the cover and set in a porcelain sleeve.
- The end of the electrode rests on the bottom of the jar.

3. Connection steps:
- Connect the interrupter in series with the primary coil of a spark coil or alternating current transformer.
- Apply direct current from 40 to 110 volts through it.
- The interrupter will make and break the circuit between 1,000 to 10,000 times per minute.

4. Adjusting interruptions:
- Raise or lower the porcelain sleeve to expose more or less of the platinum or alloy point, varying the number of interruptions per minute.

5. Polarity connection:
- Connect the platinum or alloy anode to the + (positive) power lead.
- Connect the lead cathode to the - (negative) power lead.

6. Cost: As low as $3.00.
direct current setup, electrolytic interrupter, sulfuric acid solution
Connecting the Lightning Switch for Aerial Grounding
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Connect the aerial wire to the middle post of the lightning switch.

2. One end post of the lightning switch connects to one of the middle posts of the aerial switch.

3. The other end post of the lightning switch leads to a...
Procedure:

1. Connect the aerial wire to the middle post of the lightning switch.

2. One end post of the lightning switch connects to one of the middle posts of the aerial switch.

3. The other end post of the lightning switch leads to a separate ground outside the building, ensuring proper grounding and protection from electrical surges.

4. This setup is shown in wiring diagrams (Figs. 26 and 27) for complete sending and receiving sets.
lightning switch, aerial grounding, radio safety
Installing an Aerial Switch for a Complete Sending and Receiving Set
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Use a double-pole, double-throw switch (or a specialized aerial switch) to install both a transmitter and receiver.

2. Mount the switch on a porcelain base for durability.

3. Connect one of the middle posts of the switch to...
Procedure:

1. Use a double-pole, double-throw switch (or a specialized aerial switch) to install both a transmitter and receiver.

2. Mount the switch on a porcelain base for durability.

3. Connect one of the middle posts of the switch to the ground wire.

4. Connect the other middle post to the lightning switch, which connects with the aerial.

5. Connect the post of the tuning coil to one end post of the switch and the clip of the tuning coil to the complementary post.

6. Connect one opposite end post of the switch to the post of the receiving tuning coil, and connect the sliding contact of the latter to the other complementary post of the switch.

7. This setup allows switching between sending and receiving modes seamlessly.
aerial switch, double-pole switch, radio installation
Selecting a High Tension Condenser for Transmitting Sets
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. For amateur use, only condensers with a capacitance of 0.007 microfarad are allowed.

2. A sectional high tension condenser can be used if it has the correct capacitance.

3. Cost examples based on transformer power:
-...
Procedure:

1. For amateur use, only condensers with a capacitance of 0.007 microfarad are allowed.

2. A sectional high tension condenser can be used if it has the correct capacitance.

3. Cost examples based on transformer power:
- 1/4-kilowatt transformer: $7.00
- 1/2-kilowatt transformer: $14.00
- 1-kilowatt transformer: $21.00
high tension condenser, capacitance, microfarad, amateur radio
Selecting a Wireless Key for Transmitting Sets
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Choose a standard wireless key as shown in the text.

2. The key should be heavier than a regular telegraph key with larger contact points.

3. Current is carried by heavy conductors directly to the contact points, not through...
Procedure:

1. Choose a standard wireless key as shown in the text.

2. The key should be heavier than a regular telegraph key with larger contact points.

3. Current is carried by heavy conductors directly to the contact points, not through bearings.

4. Available sizes and specifications:
- Size 1: Carries 5 amperes, costs $4.00
- Size 2: Carries 10 amperes, costs $6.50
- Size 3: Carries 20 amperes, costs $7.50
wireless key, transmitting set, telegraph key, current capacity
Tuning a Transmitter Using a Hot Wire Ammeter
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To adjust the transmitter so that it sends out waves of a specific length (e.g., 200 meters).

2. Use a hot wire ammeter (also called a radiation ammeter) to tune the transmitter.

3. The hot wire ammeter consists...
Procedure:

1. Purpose: To adjust the transmitter so that it sends out waves of a specific length (e.g., 200 meters).

2. Use a hot wire ammeter (also called a radiation ammeter) to tune the transmitter.

3. The hot wire ammeter consists of:
- A thin platinum wire through which high-frequency currents pass, heating it.
- Expansion and contraction of the wire moves a needle over a scale marked in fractions of an ampere.

4. Tuning steps:
- Adjust the spark gap and tuning coil until the ammeter shows the maximum energy flow (needle swings farthest to the right).
- This indicates that the aerial system and closed oscillation circuit are in tune, radiating the greatest amount of energy.

5. If using a condenser with 0.007 microfarad capacitance, tuning for 200-meter waves is achieved when the ammeter shows maximum energy flow.
hot wire ammeter, transmitter tuning, radiation ammeter, 200 meter wave
Using a Wave-Meter for Tuning to Different Wavelengths
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. To tune the transmitter to different wavelengths (other than 200 meters), you must have a wave-meter.

2. The wave-meter is used in conjunction with the hot wire ammeter and other tuning components.

3. It allows for precise...
Procedure:

1. To tune the transmitter to different wavelengths (other than 200 meters), you must have a wave-meter.

2. The wave-meter is used in conjunction with the hot wire ammeter and other tuning components.

3. It allows for precise adjustment of the transmitter's frequency output, enabling communication over various distances.

4. The wave-meter should be connected to the oscillation circuit or tuning coil as needed.
wave-meter, frequency adjustment, radio tuning
Consult Appendix for Electrical Terminology and Definitions
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To gain a thorough understanding of electrical terminology and definitions:

1. Reference Material: Turn to the Appendix beginning on page 301 of this book.
2. Abbreviations Table: Find abbreviations of electrical terms commonly used in all...
To gain a thorough understanding of electrical terminology and definitions:

1. Reference Material: Turn to the Appendix beginning on page 301 of this book.
2. Abbreviations Table: Find abbreviations of electrical terms commonly used in all works dealing with the subject.
3. Definitions Section: Review brief definitions of electric and magnetic units, which are essential for understanding technical discussions.
4. Memorization Recommendation: Commit these terms to memory or at least become thoroughly familiar with them so that you can instantly recognize their meanings when encountered.
appendix,electrical terminology,abbreviations,electric units,magnetic units

Radio Maintenance (27) -

Maintaining Radio Receivers and Transmitters in Cold Weather
High Priority Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
### Maintaining Radio Receivers and Transmitters in Cold Weather

• Stress the vital importance of communications during northern operations.
• Place radio sets in sheltered locations whenever possible. Erect lean-tos, snowcaves, windbreaks, or any...
### Maintaining Radio Receivers and Transmitters in Cold Weather

• Stress the vital importance of communications during northern operations.
• Place radio sets in sheltered locations whenever possible. Erect lean-tos, snowcaves, windbreaks, or any other appropriate type of shelter to protect equipment from direct exposure to extreme climatic conditions.
• Install a complete antenna system such as long wire antenna, elevated ground plane antenna, single wire inverted L-antenna, doublet antenna, which are more efficient than fractional wavelength whip antennas.
• Cut doublet antennas to operating frequency. The length of this antenna is determined by the following formula: _L_ = 468 / _F_, where _L_ is length in feet and _F_ is operating frequency in megacycles (468 is a constant factor derived from the basic formula). This formula does not apply to antennas longer than half-wave.
• Keep in mind that the radiation pattern of a doublet antenna is maximum at right angles to the plane of the antenna.
• Elevate radio frequency cables above the surface to ensure they will not freeze to the ground.
• Construct a counterpoise system in locations where frozen ground prevents installation of a ground rod or where a good earth ground is not available.
• Use radio retransmission stations to extend communications beyond the distance normally covered by one radio set.
• Use arctic lubricants on Radar and Beam antennas.
• Warm up radio sets for at least one-half hour before applying plate voltage to transmitter tubes. The sets may be turned on but do not transmit for at least one-half hour.
• Frequently check antenna system on mobile units and remove snow, ice, or slush formations.
radio maintenance, cold weather, antenna installation, retransmission stations
Follow Breakdown Procedure for Radio Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure all stations have a breakdown procedure in place based on their network and location.
2. If repair facilities are available nearby, operators should be aware of them and have access to the technical manual for...
Procedure:

1. Ensure all stations have a breakdown procedure in place based on their network and location.
2. If repair facilities are available nearby, operators should be aware of them and have access to the technical manual for technicians.
3. In areas without repair facilities, operators must await instructions from HQ.
4. HQ operators should monitor stations that do not respond when called and report this to the network maintenance person.
5. If a station remains off the air longer than normal, assume it has broken down and investigate further.
6. Even if a station cannot transmit, it may still be able to receive messages; continue calling them in and sending instructions.
7. Use alternative communication methods (e.g., couriers, other radio networks) to send critical instructions if a complete breakdown is suspected.
radio breakdown procedure, HQ communication, station failure, emergency repair
Implement a Radio Network Maintenance Plan
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Purpose: Ensure continuous operation of a radio network by addressing potential failures and implementing maintenance procedures.

Procedure:
1. Preventive Measures:
- Choose, install, and operate equipment according to best practices...
Purpose: Ensure continuous operation of a radio network by addressing potential failures and implementing maintenance procedures.

Procedure:
1. Preventive Measures:
- Choose, install, and operate equipment according to best practices (see para. 5.2.5 for details).
- Safeguard transceivers from external damage such as lightning by disconnecting aerials during storms.
- Ensure proper installation by a competent radio technician.

2. Maintenance Philosophy:
- Maintain spare parts, manuals, and circuit diagrams on-site to enable quick repairs.
- Avoid relying solely on sending faulty equipment to a central workshop for repair, especially in developing countries with limited transport options.
- Consider local training of nationals as radio technicians for long-term solutions.

3. Interim Solutions:
- Cooperate with other organizations to rotate a technician among them for 2 years at a time.
- Ensure compliance with visa and work permit regulations before sending expatriate personnel.

4. Financial Planning:
- Define financial arrangements early, including who will pay for:
- Repair of transceivers in the workshop (including parts and labor).
- Travel and accommodation costs for technicians visiting distant stations.
- Recurring replacements such as batteries.
- Consider payment methods that are practical in regions where bank cheques or transfers may be unreliable, including physical movement of money with appropriate safeguards.

5. Cost Estimation:
- Estimate costs at an early stage (see para. 5.1.6 for guidance).
- Be aware that the cost of providing a radio maintenance facility or using another’s can be significant and must not be underestimated.

Notes:
• In Zaire, it was common to find no spare parts, manuals, or circuit diagrams available when equipment failed, leading to prolonged downtime.
• A technician may charge $15 per hour for repairs, while a commercial firm might send a technician from Britain at the cost of travel, accommodation, and $300 per day.
radio maintenance, spare parts, manual, circuit diagrams, technician training, financial planning, cost estimation
Perform Fault Finding Before DIY Repair in Radio Communications
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Before attempting any Do It Yourself (DIY) repair on radio communications equipment, follow this procedure:

1. Preparation:
- Ensure that you have a clear understanding of the system and its components.
- Review all relevant...
Before attempting any Do It Yourself (DIY) repair on radio communications equipment, follow this procedure:

1. Preparation:
- Ensure that you have a clear understanding of the system and its components.
- Review all relevant documentation or manuals for the equipment being repaired.

2. Fault Finding Procedure:
- Perform the Fault Finding procedure as outlined in section 7.2.3 of this handbook before attempting any DIY repairs. This step is crucial to identify the exact nature and location of the fault.

3. DIY Considerations:
- Be aware that some jobs may turn into disasters if not performed correctly, especially in radio communications engineering where improper handling can lead to further damage or safety risks.
- Follow instructions carefully and ensure they are sufficiently well defined and adhered to.

4. Important Warnings:
- "A little knowledge is a dangerous thing" – be cautious when performing DIY repairs, as incorrect actions may cause more harm than good.
- Only proceed with DIY repairs if you have the necessary skills and tools.
fault finding,diy repair,radiocommunications,maintenance,safety
Radio Equipment Inspection and Maintenance Checklist
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

• Verify that the power light or indicator is at its usual brightness; if dull, recharge or replace the battery.
• Ensure the power light remains bright during transmission. If it dims, the battery may not be sufficient for...
Procedure:

• Verify that the power light or indicator is at its usual brightness; if dull, recharge or replace the battery.
• Ensure the power light remains bright during transmission. If it dims, the battery may not be sufficient for sending signals.
• Check that the TRANSMIT light flashes when speaking into the microphone. If it does not:
- Try using a spare microphone to determine if the issue is with the original one.
- If the TRANSMIT light still fails to function, there may be an internal fault in the transceiver.
• Repeat checks on all channels to identify whether the problem affects specific frequencies or all channels.
• Inspect aerials for damage, ensuring they are intact and not obstructed by branches or other objects. If damaged, attempt to restore them or request replacement parts from HQ.
• Examine aerial cables and connections for visible damage. Perform a temporary repair if possible and inform maintenance personnel immediately.
power light check, transmit light test, aerial inspection, cable damage, temporary repair
Replace Fuses in Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify fuse type:
- Most modern radio equipment uses non-repairable fuses (e.g., glass or ceramic tube encased fuse wire).

2. Handle blown fuses properly:
- If a fuse has blown, do not use a piece of wire to...
Procedure:

1. Identify fuse type:
- Most modern radio equipment uses non-repairable fuses (e.g., glass or ceramic tube encased fuse wire).

2. Handle blown fuses properly:
- If a fuse has blown, do not use a piece of wire to bypass it.
- The added wire should not exceed the fuse rating in current capacity.

3. Determine correct replacement fuse size:
- A current twice the "fuse rating" will blow the fuse.

4. Use Table 8.1 for reference:
- Use the following table to determine appropriate copper wire sizes and their corresponding fuse ratings:

|SWG|Diameter in mm.|Fuse rating in amps.|
|---|---|---|
|38|.152|3|
|35|.213|5|
|29|.345|10|
|25|.508|15|
|23|.610|20|
|21|.813|30|

5. Avoid improper fuse replacements:
- Using a wire with a fusing value in excess of the rated current can cause damage or safety hazards.
fuses, radio equipment repair, fuse replacement, electrical safety
Troubleshoot Power Failure in Transceiver
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Check (1): For a battery-operated set, ensure that the battery leads are not disconnected and that the battery is still present.
- For a mains-operated transceiver, confirm there is power available in the radio room.
2....
Procedure:

1. Check (1): For a battery-operated set, ensure that the battery leads are not disconnected and that the battery is still present.
- For a mains-operated transceiver, confirm there is power available in the radio room.
2. Check (2): Ensure that the fuse has not blown. Replace it with a good one if necessary.
- If the fuse blows again, there is likely a fault inside the transceiver. Do not replace the fuse again but inform HQ by some other means of the problem.
3. Check (3): Inspect plugs and cables for any visual damage.
- If a faulty cable is suspected, inform maintenance and request a replacement.
4. Check (4): Look for simple explanations such as:
- The power lead may be disconnected at both ends, with the next user only plugging into the wall socket without realizing the other end is not connected to the transceiver.

Notes:
• Always check the obvious and ordinary when troubleshooting a power failure.
power failure,transceiver,fuse replacement,cable damage,obvious checks
Conduct Preliminary Fault Checks Before Calling Maintenance
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Initial Fault Diagnosis by the Operator:

1. Review and follow all operating instructions provided with the radio equipment.
2. Check that controls such as RF Gain or Squelch are set to their correct positions.
3. **Verify...
Procedure for Initial Fault Diagnosis by the Operator:

1. Review and follow all operating instructions provided with the radio equipment.
2. Check that controls such as RF Gain or Squelch are set to their correct positions.
3. Verify that all connections (power, antenna, etc.) are secure and properly made.
4. Test basic functionality of the transceiver:
- Ensure that power is applied correctly.
- Check for any visible damage or loose components.
5. If no obvious issues are found, proceed to perform tests outlined in para. 8.6.2.1.

Notes:
• This step helps avoid unnecessary service calls and ensures that the operator is familiar with basic troubleshooting procedures.
• It also reduces downtime by identifying simple fixes before involving a technician.
fault diagnosis, radio checks, preliminary maintenance
Daily Maintenance of Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

• Remove power and aerial cables at the end of each contact session.
• Check battery acid level weekly, topping up with water as needed.
• Keep battery lugs free from corrosion, applying grease regularly to prevent...
Procedure:

• Remove power and aerial cables at the end of each contact session.
• Check battery acid level weekly, topping up with water as needed.
• Keep battery lugs free from corrosion, applying grease regularly to prevent damage.
• Maintain a clean environment for the radio: keep the cabinet, desk, or table free of dust and unnecessary paper.
• Ensure proper ventilation around the radio during use to avoid overheating.
• Remove dust sheets completely before using the radio after storage.
• Keep the radio room clean and secure, limiting access to authorized personnel only.
• Avoid excessive handling of controls, especially twisting knobs unnecessarily while waiting for contact.
• Prevent unauthorized use of the radio by restricting access to trained operators.
• Inspect aerials and cables regularly, particularly after storms or adverse weather conditions.
• Maintain solar panels by keeping them clean and ensuring they are not shaded by overgrowth.
• Assign a primary operator with a trained standby person in case of emergency.
• Keep operating instructions accessible for quick reference during troubleshooting.
• Report any breakdowns immediately to HQ, providing as much detail as possible about the issue.
radio maintenance, battery care, dust removal, ventilation, solar panel maintenance, unauthorized use, emergency standby
Determine Maintenance Strategy Before Equipment Purchase
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Decide on your maintenance strategy before purchasing radio equipment.
2. Consider factors that influence annual maintenance costs, such as:
- Quality control during manufacturing
- Safeguards built into the equipment
-...
Procedure:

1. Decide on your maintenance strategy before purchasing radio equipment.
2. Consider factors that influence annual maintenance costs, such as:
- Quality control during manufacturing
- Safeguards built into the equipment
- Good design of installation at each station
- Implementation of good operating procedures
3. If your network has 25 or more stations, consider having an in-house radio technician and workshop unless reliable repair facilities are available locally.
4. Evaluate the cost of setting up a workshop, including:
- Basic test equipment (starting at £1,000)
- Building, work benches, seating, lighting, fans, or air conditioning
5. Factor in annual costs for employing a technician.
6. Note that church missions and aid agencies may have cost advantages over commercial organizations when hiring technicians.
7. Once established, your repair facility can generate income by providing services to other groups.
maintenance strategy, radio technician, workshop setup, repair facility, equipment costs
Maintain Shortwave Radio System Regularly
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Schedule regular inspections and maintenance by competent technicians to ensure the mean time between failures (MTBF) of your system is maximized.
2. Ensure proper installation of all components to prevent faults from...
Procedure:

1. Schedule regular inspections and maintenance by competent technicians to ensure the mean time between failures (MTBF) of your system is maximized.
2. Ensure proper installation of all components to prevent faults from occurring in the first place.
3. Have highly skilled technicians on call for immediate response during a disaster situation. These individuals should be well briefed, drilled, and equipped with the necessary tools for any repair or installation work.
4. Maintain a stock of spare parts that are specific to your equipment so that repairs can be made quickly without delays.
5. Establish long-term contracts with reliable maintenance companies. Keep them informed about the type of equipment you have and how it is maintained.
6. Do not rely solely on technology; always plan for human intervention, as no system is completely fail-safe.
7. Document all maintenance activities to track the condition of your equipment over time and identify patterns or recurring issues.
shortwave radio,maintenance,technicians,spares,MTBF,disaster preparedness
Maintain a Spare Parts Inventory for Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine spare parts needs:
- Hold a range of spare parts at the central maintenance facility rather than having a full set at each station.
- This reduces capital cost (e.g., 1 spare item for every 10 transceivers...
Procedure:

1. Determine spare parts needs:
- Hold a range of spare parts at the central maintenance facility rather than having a full set at each station.
- This reduces capital cost (e.g., 1 spare item for every 10 transceivers instead of 1 per transceiver).

2. Prioritize spare parts selection:
- Plug-in circuit boards or modules should be the highest priority.
- Mechanical parts liable to wear out, such as switches, press buttons, relays, and variable resistors (e.g., volume controls), are next in priority.
- Hot filament devices, like thermionic valves and indicator lights (not LEDs), follow.
- Plugs and sockets that are unplugged daily, such as aerial and power connections, should also be included.
- High power transistors used in the transmitter come next.
- Loudspeaker and microphone parts should be stocked.
- Non-standard components specific to a particular transceiver must be kept.
- A selection of transistors, resistors, and capacitors, chosen by the person responsible for maintenance, is also recommended.

3. Keep an up-to-date list:
- Maintain a detailed inventory of all spare parts held.
- Use identifying numbers where available to ensure correct part identification.

4. Consider keeping a spare transceiver:
- A completely spare transceiver can be kept at the central workshop for immediate replacement.
- It is best to wait until the faulty transceiver arrives at the workshop before dispatching the spare.
- The existence of a spare may cause issues in some cultures, especially if there is a long waiting list for radio equipment.
- Highlight that additional components (e.g., aerial, battery, solar charger, microphone) would be needed to make the spare transceiver functional as a complete station.
spare parts, radio equipment maintenance, inventory management, radio repair
Monitor Battery Charge State Daily
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Monitoring Battery Charge State:

1. Use a voltage indicator or hydrometer to measure the battery's state of charge daily before using the transceiver.
2. Record the readings in a logbook or digital tracking system.
3....
Procedure for Monitoring Battery Charge State:

1. Use a voltage indicator or hydrometer to measure the battery's state of charge daily before using the transceiver.
2. Record the readings in a logbook or digital tracking system.
3. Analyze the recorded data to detect trends that may indicate overuse, undercharging, or end-of-life issues.
4. Take corrective action if any of these conditions are detected:
- If overuse is suspected, reduce usage and ensure proper charging.
- If undercharging is identified, check the charger and power source.
- If battery life is nearing its end, replace it with a new one.

Notes:
• Regular monitoring prevents unexpected failures due to discharged batteries.
• This procedure helps in planning maintenance schedules and ensuring continuous operation of the radio equipment.
battery charge monitoring, voltage indicator, hydrometer use
Perform Physical Tests on Radio Cables for Faults
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Inspect cables for signs of damage, such as broken or disconnected wires.
2. Gently move the cables, especially where they enter a plug or socket, to check if movement causes the transceiver to start working again.
3. If...
Procedure:

1. Inspect cables for signs of damage, such as broken or disconnected wires.
2. Gently move the cables, especially where they enter a plug or socket, to check if movement causes the transceiver to start working again.
3. If the transceiver works when the cable is moved or twisted, this indicates a potential fault in the wiring.
4. Report the issue by sending a message requesting a replacement cable or technician assistance.
5. Check battery acid levels regularly as instructed, since low voltage can cause problems with the radio and is often overlooked until it's too late.
6. Inspect wires connected to the battery for corrosion that may break the circuit.
7. Move these wires gently to see if they affect the transceiver’s operation, indicating a possible fault.
radio cable test, physical tests, battery acid check, corrosion inspection, fault detection
Perform Weekly Battery Acid Level Check and Refill
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Weekly Battery Maintenance:

1. Check the acid level in a vented battery each week.
- Use a hydrometer or visual inspection to determine if the acid level is low.
2. Refill with distilled or rain water as needed.
- Do...
Procedure for Weekly Battery Maintenance:

1. Check the acid level in a vented battery each week.
- Use a hydrometer or visual inspection to determine if the acid level is low.
2. Refill with distilled or rain water as needed.
- Do not use tap water, as it contains minerals that can damage the battery.
3. Ensure proper sealing of the battery after refilling.
- Prevent spillage and exposure to air.

Notes:
• This procedure is essential for maintaining battery longevity and performance.
• If the battery is not vented, this step may not be necessary.
battery maintenance, acid level check, distilled water, radio maintenance
Prevent Battery Terminal Corrosion
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Preventing Battery Terminal Corrosion:

1. Inspect battery terminals regularly for signs of corrosion.
- Look for white, green, or blue deposits on the terminals.
2. **Clean corroded terminals using a wire brush or a...
Procedure for Preventing Battery Terminal Corrosion:

1. Inspect battery terminals regularly for signs of corrosion.
- Look for white, green, or blue deposits on the terminals.
2. Clean corroded terminals using a wire brush or a solution of baking soda and water.
- Apply the solution with a cloth or brush to remove corrosion.
3. Apply petroleum grease (Vaseline) to the cleaned terminals.
- This creates a protective barrier against future corrosion.
4. Ensure that the battery connections are tight and secure.
- Loose connections can lead to increased corrosion over time.

Notes:
• Corrosion can cause poor electrical contact, leading to reduced performance or failure of the radio equipment.
• Regular cleaning and greasing will significantly extend the life of the battery terminals.
battery corrosion, terminal maintenance, petroleum grease, radio maintenance
Prevent Dust Build-Up on Transceiver and Microphone
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Preventing Dust Accumulation on Radio Equipment:

1. Cover the transceiver and microphone with a dust cover when not in use.
- This prevents dust from settling on sensitive components.
2. **Store the equipment inside a desk...
Procedure for Preventing Dust Accumulation on Radio Equipment:

1. Cover the transceiver and microphone with a dust cover when not in use.
- This prevents dust from settling on sensitive components.
2. Store the equipment inside a desk or cabinet when not being used.
3. Clean the exterior of the transceiver and microphone regularly using a soft, dry cloth.
4. Avoid using liquids or abrasive materials for cleaning.
- These can damage sensitive electronic components.

Notes:
• Dust accumulation can interfere with the performance of the radio equipment.
• Regular cleaning helps maintain optimal signal quality and longevity of the device.
dust prevention, transceiver maintenance, microphone care
Provide Appropriate Tools for Radio Equipment Maintenance
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Assess the need for tools:
- The decision on what tools to provide should be based on expectations of personnel involvement in radio equipment maintenance.

2. Restrict access if necessary:
- In some locations, it...
Procedure:

1. Assess the need for tools:
- The decision on what tools to provide should be based on expectations of personnel involvement in radio equipment maintenance.

2. Restrict access if necessary:
- In some locations, it may be necessary to prevent unauthorized tampering with transceivers.
- Paint the instruction "Do not open" in white paint on the cover of the transceiver in three languages.
- Seal the screws securing the cover with white paint to prevent access.

3. Limit tool provision:
- In some cases, no tools may be supplied to certain locations if maintenance is not expected to be performed locally.
- This applies to all personnel, including expatriates and local staff.
tool provision, radio equipment security, maintenance restrictions
Trim Vegetation Around Antenna and Cables
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Maintaining Antenna Integrity in Tropical Areas:

1. Inspect the area around the antenna regularly for overgrowth of vegetation.
2. Cut down any branches that have grown over or are touching the aerial and its cable.
3....
Procedure for Maintaining Antenna Integrity in Tropical Areas:

1. Inspect the area around the antenna regularly for overgrowth of vegetation.
2. Cut down any branches that have grown over or are touching the aerial and its cable.
3. Remove creepers or vines that are growing up the aerial mast or cables.
4. Ensure that no part of the vegetation is in contact with the antenna or its supporting structures.

Notes:
• Vegetation can cause signal interference, damage to equipment, and even pose a safety hazard.
• Regular trimming helps maintain clear communication and prevents potential failures.
vegetation control, antenna maintenance, tropical radio setup
Troubleshoot Battery Voltage Problems with Other Charging Methods
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Check (1): Ensure that the generator or mains battery charger is providing the necessary charging current.
2. Allow sufficient time for recharging: If the battery does not recover, suspect it may be too old or damaged in...
Procedure:

1. Check (1): Ensure that the generator or mains battery charger is providing the necessary charging current.
2. Allow sufficient time for recharging: If the battery does not recover, suspect it may be too old or damaged in some way.
3. Refer to para 3.2.3.3 on battery life and related topics for further guidance.

Notes:
• This procedure applies when the battery is charged by means other than solar panels.
battery voltage,generator charger,mains charger,battery age,damage
Troubleshoot Battery Voltage Problems with Solar Panels
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Check (1): Ensure that the surface of the solar panel is clean.
2. Check (2): Observe any charging indicator light or meter where fitted.
3. Check (3): Confirm that the duration of daily use has not exceeded the...
Procedure:

1. Check (1): Ensure that the surface of the solar panel is clean.
2. Check (2): Observe any charging indicator light or meter where fitted.
3. Check (3): Confirm that the duration of daily use has not exceeded the recommended time.
4. Check (4): Suspect lightning damage possibly to diode.
5. Allow battery time to recharge: Refer to para. 3.2.3.4 for guidance.
6. If the battery does not recover, inform a maintenance person.

Notes:
• This procedure applies specifically when the battery is charged by solar panels.
battery voltage,solar panel,charging indicator,lightning damage,battery recharge
Troubleshoot Unusual Noises from Loudspeaker
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Check (1): Disconnect the aerial from the transceiver to see if the noise stops.
- If it does, these are strong external signals and there is nothing wrong with the transceiver.
2. Check (2): If there is still a whistle...
Procedure:

1. Check (1): Disconnect the aerial from the transceiver to see if the noise stops.
- If it does, these are strong external signals and there is nothing wrong with the transceiver.
2. Check (2): If there is still a whistle or warbling tones even with no aerial connected:
- Reconnect the aerial and try reducing RF and AF Gain controls to stop the noise.
- Inform radio maintenance of the problem.
3. Check (3): If there is a loud hum during receive:
- (a) Where the radio battery is charged off the mains, ensure that the mains battery charger is switched off while operating.
- (b) Where there is a hum on a mains operated transceiver, suspect a possible fault in the transceivers own mains power unit.
unusual noise,loudspeaker,aerial disconnection,RF gain,AF gain,mains charger,faulty power unit
Understand False Indications on Transceiver Panels
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Interpreting Indicator Lights Correctly:

1. Recognize that indicator lights may not always reflect the true status of internal circuits.
2. **Refer to technical documentation or circuit diagrams to understand how each light...
Procedure for Interpreting Indicator Lights Correctly:

1. Recognize that indicator lights may not always reflect the true status of internal circuits.
2. Refer to technical documentation or circuit diagrams to understand how each light functions within the transceiver's system.
3. If an indicator light fails, investigate further by checking related components and power supply lines.
4. Use additional diagnostic tools (e.g., multimeter) to verify the actual status of circuits if necessary.

Notes:
• Indicator lights can provide misleading information if their circuit connections are not fully understood.
• Proper training on how these indicators work is essential for accurate fault diagnosis.
indicator light interpretation, false indications, transceiver diagnostics
Verify Power Light Functionality in Case of Fuse Failure
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Diagnosing Power Light Issues:

1. If the power light is not functioning, check if a fuse has blown.
- Specifically, test the 5 amp fuse in the DC power protection circuit.
2. **If the 5 amp fuse is blown, this will be...
Procedure for Diagnosing Power Light Issues:

1. If the power light is not functioning, check if a fuse has blown.
- Specifically, test the 5 amp fuse in the DC power protection circuit.
2. If the 5 amp fuse is blown, this will be indicated by the absence of the Power light.
3. If only the 15 amp fuse has blown, the Power light may still appear to be on.
- This can give a false indication that power is being supplied correctly.
4. To verify actual power supply status:
- Speak into the microphone to activate the transmitter.
- Observe if the Transmit light flashes, confirming that power is reaching the rest of the transceiver.

Notes:
• This procedure helps identify fuse failures and false indications from the Power light.
• It ensures that operators can accurately diagnose issues without unnecessary service calls.
power light diagnosis, fuse failure, transceiver testing
Considering Contract Maintenance for Radio Equipment
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Contract maintenance should be considered when:

• There is a reliable commercial radio repair facility (e.g., in large cities like Nairobi).
• There are **means of transporting equipment to and from the facility at reasonably frequent...
Contract maintenance should be considered when:

• There is a reliable commercial radio repair facility (e.g., in large cities like Nairobi).
• There are means of transporting equipment to and from the facility at reasonably frequent intervals.

When evaluating contract maintenance providers, consider the following factors:

1. Retaining fee.
2. Labour cost per hour.
3. Overheads involved in obtaining and handling spares.
4. Equipment transportation arrangements.
5. Technicians' travel and accommodation costs.
6. Maximum time from reporting a fault to completion of repairs (if definable).

This evaluation helps determine the most cost-effective and reliable maintenance solution for an organization.
contract maintenance, radio repair, transportation, costs, reliability
Establishing a Radio Workshop for Equipment Repair
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To perform satisfactory repairs in a workshop, it is necessary to have:

1. A qualified and capable radio technician.
2. Adequate test equipment, tools, and working space.
3. A reliable source of sufficient electrical power.
4. **A...
To perform satisfactory repairs in a workshop, it is necessary to have:

1. A qualified and capable radio technician.
2. Adequate test equipment, tools, and working space.
3. A reliable source of sufficient electrical power.
4. A secure and dust-free room with adequate lighting and ventilation.
5. A store of sufficient spare components and parts.
6. Technical manuals for each type or model of transceiver to be repaired.

These requirements ensure that the workshop is equipped to handle repairs effectively and efficiently.
workshop, radio repair, technician, test equipment, tools, power supply, spare parts, technical manuals
Preparing Equipment for Workshop Repair
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Before sending equipment to the workshop, radio operators should:

1. Perform a test at their station to identify any issues.
2. Send the microphone with the transceiver.
3. Include any separate mains or other power unit used.
4. **Pack...
Before sending equipment to the workshop, radio operators should:

1. Perform a test at their station to identify any issues.
2. Send the microphone with the transceiver.
3. Include any separate mains or other power unit used.
4. Pack the power cable and plug, aerial switch, and its connecting cable if used.
5. Ensure all items are packed in a way suitable for the mode of transport.

This preparation helps ensure that the workshop has all necessary components to perform repairs effectively.
equipment repair, packing, microphone, power unit, cable, transport

Emergency Alerting (27) -

Document and Implement AWN Guidance for Nuclear Detonation Incidents
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Planners must ensure that Alert, Warning, and Notification (AWN) guidance is properly documented, applied, tested, and exercised for nuclear detonation incidents.

• Document all AWN procedures in response plans, standard operating procedures...
Planners must ensure that Alert, Warning, and Notification (AWN) guidance is properly documented, applied, tested, and exercised for nuclear detonation incidents.

• Document all AWN procedures in response plans, standard operating procedures (SOPs), and checklists related to nuclear detonation scenarios.

• Ensure that these procedures are tested and exercised regularly to maintain readiness and effectiveness.

• Incorporate key planning factors from this chapter into all relevant documents to increase awareness of the importance of AWN in nuclear detonation response.

• Use FEMA’s Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS) as a critical tool for delivering timely, actionable warnings to affected communities.

• Understand that timely alerts are essential to protect people from radiation exposure and other hazards following a nuclear detonation.
alert system, warning procedures, IPAWS, emergency communication, nuclear detonation response
Include AWN Messaging in ESF-15 SOP for Nuclear Detonations
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action Item: Ensure that AWN messaging is included in ESF-15 SOP (Emergency Support Function 15 Standard Operating Procedures) as part of the response to a nuclear detonation.

Key provisions:
• Annex N - Radiological of **FEMA’s ESF-15...
Action Item: Ensure that AWN messaging is included in ESF-15 SOP (Emergency Support Function 15 Standard Operating Procedures) as part of the response to a nuclear detonation.

Key provisions:
• Annex N - Radiological of FEMA’s ESF-15 SOP prescribes specific AWN and public messaging actions in response to nuclear detonations.
• The document is available at: [www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/2020 10/fema_esf 15_sop_2019.pdf](www.fema.gov/sites/default/files/2020 10/fema_esf 15_sop_2019.pdf).
• Messaging must include the directive: 'Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned' until the fallout threat picture can be clarified.

Purpose: This messaging is critical to saving thousands of lives by avoiding exposure to radioactive fallout.
ESF-15 SOP,AWN messaging,nuclear detonation,fallout zone directives
Ensure Minimum Essential Non-subscription WEA Capability in Urban Areas
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Maintain a minimum essential non-subscription WEA capability to provide immediate public AWN after a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Identify and prioritize the integration of WEA capabilities into AWN plans for urban areas.
2....
Objective: Maintain a minimum essential non-subscription WEA capability to provide immediate public AWN after a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Identify and prioritize the integration of WEA capabilities into AWN plans for urban areas.
2. Ensure that alerting authorities are prepared to send alerts even if internet services are partially disrupted.
3. Collaborate with technical experts to validate assumptions about internet availability during a nuclear detonation.
4. Verify that non-subscription WEA is available as an alternative means of communication in the event of internet outages.
WEA capability, urban planning, non-subscription alerting, internet availability, AWN plans
Ensure Resilient AWN Capabilities for ESF-15 Mission
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Maintain resilient All-Warnings Notification (AWN) capabilities to support the ESF-15 mission in the aftermath of a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that both state and local Joint Information Centers (JICs) have access...
Objective: Maintain resilient All-Warnings Notification (AWN) capabilities to support the ESF-15 mission in the aftermath of a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that both state and local Joint Information Centers (JICs) have access to multiple dissemination channels for AWN.
2. Plan comprehensively to meet critical public AWN needs, including fallout warnings and orders to shelter in place.
3. Coordinate with the Secretary of Homeland Security who may appoint ESF-15 deputies to affected states and regions as described in Annex C of the Response FIOP.
4. Ensure that minimum essential non-subscription WEA capability is included in AWN plans to provide immediate public alerts after a nuclear detonation.
5. Review and update planning regularly based on current capabilities, threats, and technical advancements.
resilient AWN, ESF-15 mission, JICs, emergency communication, nuclear detonation
Identify Number of IPAWS Alerting Authorities Needed in Urban Planning Area
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Determine the number of IPAWS alerting authorities required to maintain post-nuclear detonation WEA alerting capability.

Procedure:
1. Conduct a risk assessment for your urban planning area, focusing on potential nuclear...
Objective: Determine the number of IPAWS alerting authorities required to maintain post-nuclear detonation WEA alerting capability.

Procedure:
1. Conduct a risk assessment for your urban planning area, focusing on potential nuclear detonation scenarios.
2. Identify how many IPAWS-capable Emergency Operations Centers (EOCs) are needed in the planning area to ensure redundancy and resiliency of the Alert Warning Network (AWN).
3. Ensure that at least one EOC with an IPAWS WEA alerting capability is located in or near the planning area and can remain operational after a nuclear detonation.
4. Analyze potential impacts on cell tower functionality, which may degrade cellphone/mobile device coverage during a nuclear event.
5. Plan for resilient alternative transport mechanisms and connectivity for EOCs, especially those responsible for sending alerts.

Additional Information:
• Planners should leverage IPAWS’ advanced AWN interoperability technology to ensure redundancy.
• Establishing redundant IPAWS-capable EOCs increases the likelihood that at least one will survive a nuclear detonation and remain operational to broadcast “Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned.”
IPAWS, EOCs, WEA, nuclear detonation, alerting authorities
Include WEA Capabilities in AWN Plans
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure that Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) are included in All-Warnings Notification (AWN) plans.

Procedure:
1. Review the complexities of subscription versus non-subscription public AWN for mobile devices as outlined by Bean...
Objective: Ensure that Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) are included in All-Warnings Notification (AWN) plans.

Procedure:
1. Review the complexities of subscription versus non-subscription public AWN for mobile devices as outlined by Bean (2019).
2. Include WEA capabilities in all AWN planning to ensure immediate public notification after a nuclear detonation.
3. Recognize that there is no alternative to near-immediate public AWN for fallout warning and orders to shelter in place, which can save thousands from initial radiation exposure.
4. Ensure that the internet is not assumed to be down across the entire country when sending WEA alerts, as recent technical discussions suggest otherwise.
WEA alerts, AWN plans, emergency communication, nuclear detonation, fallout warning
Operationalize AWN with Regular AOT Tools
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action Item: Ensure Alert and Warning Notification (AWN) is operationalized using regular All-Hazards Alerting Technologies (AOT) tools and emerging technologies.

• Verify that current alert systems are fully functional and capable of...
Action Item: Ensure Alert and Warning Notification (AWN) is operationalized using regular All-Hazards Alerting Technologies (AOT) tools and emerging technologies.

• Verify that current alert systems are fully functional and capable of delivering warnings to the public in a nuclear detonation scenario.
• Incorporate both traditional AOT tools, such as AM/FM radio broadcasts, and modern technologies for effective communication.
• Ensure redundancy in AWN delivery methods to account for potential failures in any single system.
alert warning notification,AOT tools,nuclear detonation,radio broadcast
Operationalize Alert and Warning Systems (AWN)
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:
1. Ensure that Alert and Warning Systems (AWN) are fully operationalized using regular Alerting Operations Tools (AOT) and emerging technologies.
2. Regularly test and maintain AWN systems to ensure they function effectively during a...
Procedure:
1. Ensure that Alert and Warning Systems (AWN) are fully operationalized using regular Alerting Operations Tools (AOT) and emerging technologies.
2. Regularly test and maintain AWN systems to ensure they function effectively during a nuclear detonation or other emergency scenarios.
3. Incorporate diverse communication methods, such as AM/FM radio broadcasts, into the alerting strategy for wide-area notification (AWN).
4. Maintain awareness of the status of all critical infrastructure that could be impacted by a nuclear event and integrate this information into AWN planning.
alert warning systems, AOT, emergency alerting, radio broadcast, critical infrastructure
Plan for Pre-Detonation Alerting and WEA/EAS Messaging
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Prepare for the pre-detonation alerting process to ensure timely warning dissemination.

Steps:
1. Issue a Presidential message from both NAWAS and NPWS within tens of minutes before detonation.
2. Follow with local alerts to...
Objective: Prepare for the pre-detonation alerting process to ensure timely warning dissemination.

Steps:
1. Issue a Presidential message from both NAWAS and NPWS within tens of minutes before detonation.
2. Follow with local alerts to deliver 90- and 360-character WEAs and EAS alerts as soon as possible using pre-scripted messages.
3. Ensure that alerting authorities are trained, proficient, and have up-to-date IPAWS PMO certificates.
4. Coordinate with FEMA workshops on WEA/EAS message content for future use.
pre-detonation alerting, WEA/EAS messaging, NAWAS, NPWS, IPAWS PMO
Activate EAS System
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Identify the process to activate the EAS.
- Determine what protocol or system was used (e.g., software, NWS, radio station, IPAWS).
- Record how long the activation process took.
- If NWS or a radio station was used,...
Procedure:

1. Identify the process to activate the EAS.
- Determine what protocol or system was used (e.g., software, NWS, radio station, IPAWS).
- Record how long the activation process took.
- If NWS or a radio station was used, verify that there was communication between the ORO and the broadcast station to confirm the EAS message prior to broadcast.
EAS activation, emergency alert system, NWS verification, ORO communication
Complete Route/Alternate Alerting for Emergency Situations
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Complete route/alternate alerting when necessary due to system failure or exception areas.
- Focus on the most challenging routes, which may be lengthy or affected by conditions such as traffic patterns, road conditions, etc.
...
Procedure:

1. Complete route/alternate alerting when necessary due to system failure or exception areas.
- Focus on the most challenging routes, which may be lengthy or affected by conditions such as traffic patterns, road conditions, etc.
- Vary the demonstration of these routes across different assessment activities.
- Document why route/alternate alerting was initiated.
- Confirm whether it was a FEMA-approved exception area.
- Identify what organization(s) are responsible for providing route/alternate alerting.
- Determine under what conditions route/alternate alerting was initiated.
- Identify who notified the resources to begin route/alternate alerting and how they were notified.
- List what resources provided route/alternate alerting.
- Record how long it took to complete the route/alternate alerting.
- Document how the message was announced and its content.
- For exception areas, confirm whether notification was completed within 45 minutes of the initial decision by authorized offsite emergency officials.
- Identify what system was used for exception areas.
- Determine who approves the use of the system for alerting exception areas.
- Confirm who deployed the system and what process was followed.
- Verify whether individual sub-areas can be activated using the system to alert FEMA-approved exception areas.
- Document whether a test was conducted or if a previous test report was used as confirmation of operation in alerting exception areas.
route alerting, alternate alerting, exception areas, system failure
Coordinate with Guiding Authorities During Emergencies
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure effective coordination between EOCs and guiding authorities during emergencies.

Procedure:
1. Establish communication channels with all relevant state and local EOC public alert initiators.
2. Coordinate with nearly 6,000...
Objective: Ensure effective coordination between EOCs and guiding authorities during emergencies.

Procedure:
1. Establish communication channels with all relevant state and local EOC public alert initiators.
2. Coordinate with nearly 6,000 state and local EOC public alert initiators to ensure seamless information sharing.
3. Collaborate with the growing number of IPAWS alerting authorities (currently over 1,600) across the U.S. and territories.
4. Ensure coordination is in line with Federal Communications Commission (FCC) regulations that only allow IPAWS alerting authorities to send WEA alerts without subscription.
5. Develop pre-scripted messages for public alerting as outlined in Annex N of the ESF-15 SOP, including variations of 'Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned.'
6. Prepare to rapidly deploy alerts before and immediately after a nuclear detonation.
7. Continue public messaging about fallout and other hazards as described in Chapter 6 of this guidance.
coordination, emergency communication, EOCs, IPAWS, WEA alerts, public alerting
Disseminate Updated Emergency Information
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Ensure that updated emergency information is disseminated in a timely manner.
- Confirm whether messages were updated to reflect the most current information about the incident.
updated emergency messages, timely dissemination, incident information
Ensure 24-Hour EAS Broadcasting Capability
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Broadcast the message on a 24-hour basis.
- Determine what the 24-hour capability of this location is.
- Confirm whether there is backup power supply or an alternate station used for broadcasting.
24-hour EAS broadcasting, backup power, alternate station
Ensure Communications Infrastructure Resilience for AWN Delivery
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure that communications infrastructure is resilient and capable of delivering alerts during a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Identify critical communications infrastructure (e.g., AM/FM transmission towers, cell towers,...
Objective: Ensure that communications infrastructure is resilient and capable of delivering alerts during a nuclear detonation.

Steps:
1. Identify critical communications infrastructure (e.g., AM/FM transmission towers, cell towers, internet service providers, etc.).
2. Assess whether these systems may be impacted by the detonation or fallout.
3. Prioritize sending alerts into an area rather than failing to attempt communication.
4. Use IPAWS architecture to leverage multiple pathways for alert delivery (e.g., WEA, EAS, broadcast radio).
5. Prepare alternative networks (e.g., satellite) in case of infrastructure damage.
communications resilience, AWN delivery, IPAWS architecture, alternative networks
Ensure Consistent Alert Origination Tests and Exercises for AWN
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Action Item: Ensure plans include consistent alert origination tests and exercises.

Planners need to:
1. Mandate increases in the frequency of tests, trainings, and exercises for all individuals allowed to operate AOTs (Alert Originating...
Action Item: Ensure plans include consistent alert origination tests and exercises.

Planners need to:
1. Mandate increases in the frequency of tests, trainings, and exercises for all individuals allowed to operate AOTs (Alert Originating Terminals).
2. Ensure high levels of proficiency among personnel to lessen or eliminate delays and false alerts, which can detract from public confidence.
3. Include proficiency in AWN (All-Warnings Network) for nuclear detonation scenarios in readiness training.

Purpose: Sustaining AWN capabilities without interruption post-detonation enables state and local JICs (Joint Information Centers) to become the leading sources of unified public information.
alert origination, AWN, exercises, training, nuclear detonation
Ensure Planning Area Has MOU with FEMA and IPAWS-Compliant Tools
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
