1. Take the H-250/U handset or handheld remote control radio device (HRCRD) C-12493/U.
2. Connect the selected device to the radio set.
3. Ensure all...
Objective: Connect the handset correctly to the SINCGARS manpack radio.

Steps:
1. Take the H-250/U handset or handheld remote control radio device (HRCRD) C-12493/U.
2. Connect the selected device to the radio set.
3. Ensure all connections are secure and functional.

References: TM 11-5820-890-10-8, page 3-12.
handset connection, H-250/U, C-12493/U, SINCGARS radio setup
Connect Solar Panel to Transceiver System Safely
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure that the solar panel is connected using cables with a consistent color code (e.g., red for positive, black for negative) throughout your system.
2. Verify the polarity of the solar panel's output and match it to the...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that the solar panel is connected using cables with a consistent color code (e.g., red for positive, black for negative) throughout your system.
2. Verify the polarity of the solar panel's output and match it to the transceiver's input requirements (positive to positive, negative to negative).
3. Use non-interchangeable connectors or clearly label cables if using direct wire connections to avoid polarity errors.
4. Ensure that all components in your system—solar panel, regulator, battery, and transceiver—are wired with a standardized color code for consistency.
5. If necessary, replace existing cables to ensure compatibility and reduce the risk of future connection errors.
6. Always double-check connections before powering on any equipment to prevent damage from incorrect polarity or voltage mismatches.
solar panel connection, DC wiring, polarity verification, system consistency
Preset Function Controls on SINCGARS Manpack Radio
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Preset the function controls for operation.

Steps:
1. Access the control panel of the SINCGARS manpack radio.
2. Adjust and preset the function controls according to operational requirements.
3. Verify that all settings are...
Objective: Preset the function controls for operation.

Steps:
1. Access the control panel of the SINCGARS manpack radio.
2. Adjust and preset the function controls according to operational requirements.
3. Verify that all settings are correct and ready for use.

References: TM 11-5820-890-10-8, page 5-1.
preset function controls, SINCGARS radio setup, control monitor
Install a Magmount Antenna on a Vehicle for Emergency Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To install a Magmount antenna on a vehicle:

1. Select a suitable antenna: Choose a quarter wave or three-quarter wave antenna with a powerful magnet at the base.
2. Position the antenna: Mount it in the center of the vehicle's roof to...
To install a Magmount antenna on a vehicle:

1. Select a suitable antenna: Choose a quarter wave or three-quarter wave antenna with a powerful magnet at the base.
2. Position the antenna: Mount it in the center of the vehicle's roof to ensure optimal signal transmission and reception.
3. Secure the antenna: The magnet will hold the antenna firmly in place, but avoid direct contact with trees or other objects that may dislodge it.
4. Connect the feeder cable: Route the feeder cable through an open window into the vehicle.
5. Attach a handheld radio: Connect the handheld radio to the magmount antenna for use while in the vehicle.
6. Power the radio: Use the cigarette lighter of the vehicle to power the radio during operation.
7. Disconnect and store: When exiting the vehicle, disconnect the magmount antenna and lock it inside the car.
8. Switch to portable mode: Connect a rubber duck antenna to the handheld radio for portable use outside the vehicle.

Important Notes: This setup is highly effective for emergency communication and provides flexibility in both mobile and portable modes.
magmount antenna, vehicle radio, emergency communication, mobile station, shortwave radio
Install and Use Correct Plugs and Sockets for Transceiver Power Supply
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure that plugs and sockets are designed to fit together in only one orientation (with a locating lug and key-way) to prevent incorrect connections.
2. If the power supply plug is frequently removed, paint matching halves of the...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that plugs and sockets are designed to fit together in only one orientation (with a locating lug and key-way) to prevent incorrect connections.
2. If the power supply plug is frequently removed, paint matching halves of the plug and socket with the same color that contrasts with the rest of the radio.
3. This visual aid will help users correctly align the plug and reduce attempts to force it in incorrectly.
4. Always use plugs and sockets compatible with the voltage and current requirements of your transceiver (e.g., 12V DC or AC mains).
5. Ensure that all connections are secure and not prone to accidental disconnection during operation.
plug and socket installation, power supply safety, plug orientation, visual identification
Optimize Antenna Positioning for Mobile Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To maximize signal strength and range on a vehicle-mounted radio system:

1. Install the antenna in a central position on the roof of the vehicle to ensure optimal signal propagation.
2. Ensure proper bonding to the vehicle's earth:...
To maximize signal strength and range on a vehicle-mounted radio system:

1. Install the antenna in a central position on the roof of the vehicle to ensure optimal signal propagation.
2. Ensure proper bonding to the vehicle's earth: This is crucial for grounding and improving signal performance.
3. Use a larger, heavier antenna: Vehicles can support larger antennas due to less weight constraints compared to handheld devices.
4. Avoid obstructions: Ensure that no objects block the line of sight from the antenna to improve range.

Why it matters: A well-positioned and properly grounded antenna significantly increases signal strength and communication range, especially in emergency situations.
antenna positioning, vehicle radio, mobile station, signal optimization
Select and Request a Suitable Frequency for Radio Operation
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Choose an appropriate frequency based on operational needs.

Steps:
1. When applying for your radio licence, provide the following information:
- Time of day you plan to operate
- Distance to be covered by the radio link
2....
Objective: Choose an appropriate frequency based on operational needs.

Steps:
1. When applying for your radio licence, provide the following information:
- Time of day you plan to operate
- Distance to be covered by the radio link
2. Suggest a suitable frequency range (e.g., 2–30 MHz) based on these factors.
3. Authorities may allocate a frequency based on your request or assign one and require you to schedule operating times for optimal ionospheric propagation.
4. Refer to Chapter 1 of this handbook for detailed information on radio wave propagation.
frequency selection, radio propagation, licence application
Setting Up Message Interruption with Coast Radio Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with your coast radio station to set up message interruption at a specified time or charge level.
2. Ensure that the system is configured correctly to interrupt communications as needed during disaster scenarios.
3....
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with your coast radio station to set up message interruption at a specified time or charge level.
2. Ensure that the system is configured correctly to interrupt communications as needed during disaster scenarios.
3. Verify that this feature is enabled and tested periodically for reliability.
message interruption, coast radio station, communication control
Understanding Full Duplex and Simplex Communication Modes
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize that 'Full Duplex' communication allows both ends to transmit simultaneously.
2. Understand that 'Simplex' communication requires alternating transmission with the use of 'over' to signal turn-taking.
3. Choose the...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that 'Full Duplex' communication allows both ends to transmit simultaneously.
2. Understand that 'Simplex' communication requires alternating transmission with the use of 'over' to signal turn-taking.
3. Choose the appropriate mode based on equipment capabilities and operational needs during disaster scenarios.
full duplex, simplex, radio communication modes
Verify Power and Aerial Connections on a Transceiver
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Locate the power and aerial connections, usually found on the rear of the transceiver.
2. Ensure these connections are made as accessible as possible for disconnecting cables or consider fitting another plug and socket in a more...
Procedure:

1. Locate the power and aerial connections, usually found on the rear of the transceiver.
2. Ensure these connections are made as accessible as possible for disconnecting cables or consider fitting another plug and socket in a more convenient location.
3. Check if there are internal connections or switches that change the transceiver to work from 12 volt DC, 110 volt AC, or 220 volt AC.
4. Follow the manufacturer's instructions carefully when making appropriate power connections.
5. Some models may have switches on the rear of the transceiver which require adjustment by a screwdriver.
6. Verify if two power leads are provided and ensure they plug into the correct power socket on the transceiver: one for 12 volt DC, and another for AC mains volts (e.g., 110, 220, or 240 volts).
7. For AC operation, set the transceiver to operate at the required voltage and fit a suitable mains plug of the type used in your area.
8. For DC operation, ensure that the positive and negative wires are terminated in non-interchangeable connectors clearly labeled as such.
9. If removing insulation from wires for battery terminals, label them clearly as positive or negative to avoid polarity errors.
10. Ensure transceivers are safeguarded against incorrect polarity connections (refer to para. 5.2.5.1).
power connections, aerial connections, transceiver setup, voltage settings, polarity errors, DC/AC operation
Assemble Manpack Radio
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Assemble a Manpack Radio:

1. Check and Install Battery:
- (a) Inspect the battery box for dirt or damage.
- (b) Stand RT on front panel guards.
- (c) Check battery life condition (you will be using the rechargeable BB-390...
Steps to Assemble a Manpack Radio:

1. Check and Install Battery:
- (a) Inspect the battery box for dirt or damage.
- (b) Stand RT on front panel guards.
- (c) Check battery life condition (you will be using the rechargeable BB-390 batteries).
- (d) Place battery in box.
- (e) Close battery cover, and secure using latches.
- (f) Return radio to upright position.

2. Enter Battery Life Condition into Radio (if used battery was installed):
- (a) Set FCTN to LD.
- (b) Press BAT, then CLR.
- (c) Enter number recorded on side of battery.
- (d) Press STO.
- (e) Set FCTN to SQ ON.
manpack radio, battery installation, assembly, military FM radio

Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Install Handset:

1. Inspect the Handset for Damage:
- (a) Inspect the handset for damage.

2. Attach Handset to RT:
- (b) Push handset on AUD/DATA and twist clockwise to lock in place.
handset installation, military FM radio
Install and Inspect Antenna on Manpack Radio
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Install and Inspect Antenna:

1. Inspect Whip Antenna Connector:
- (a) Check whip antenna connector on antenna and on radio for damage.

2. Screw Whip Antenna into Base:
- (b) Screw whip antenna into base.
- (c) Hand...
Steps to Install and Inspect Antenna:

1. Inspect Whip Antenna Connector:
- (a) Check whip antenna connector on antenna and on radio for damage.

2. Screw Whip Antenna into Base:
- (b) Screw whip antenna into base.
- (c) Hand tighten.

3. Mate Antenna Base with RT ANT Connector:
- (d) Carefully mate antenna base with RT ANT connector.
- (e) Hand tighten.

4. Position Antenna as Needed:
- (f) Position antenna as needed by bending goose neck.

Note: Keep antenna straight, if possible. If the antenna is bent to a horizontal position, it may be necessary to turn the radio in order to receive and transmit messages.
antenna installation, antenna inspection, military FM radio
Load Frequencies on Manpack Radio (Single Channel)
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Load Frequencies Using Single Channel Mode:

1. Obtain Ranger SOI:
- (a) Obtain Ranger Signal Operating Instructions (SOI).

2. Set Controls for Frequency Loading:
- (b) Set FCTN to LD.
- (c) Set MODE to...
Steps to Load Frequencies Using Single Channel Mode:

1. Obtain Ranger SOI:
- (a) Obtain Ranger Signal Operating Instructions (SOI).

2. Set Controls for Frequency Loading:
- (b) Set FCTN to LD.
- (c) Set MODE to SC.
- (d) Set CHAN to MAN, Cue, or desired channel (1 to 6) where frequency is to be stored.

3. Enter Frequency:
- (e) Press FREQ (display will show 00000, or the frequency the RT is currently set to).
- (f) Press CLR (display will show five lines).
- (g) Enter the number of the new frequency.

4. Save Frequency:
- (h) If you make a mistake with a number, press CLR.
- (i) Press STO (display will blink).
- (j) Set FCTN to SQ ON.
frequency loading, single channel mode, military FM radio
Pack Manpack Radio into Field Pack
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Pack Manpack Radio into Field Pack:

1. Place Radio in Field Pack:
- (a) Place radio transmitter (RT) in field pack with antenna on the left shoulder.

2. Secure Field Pack Flap:
- (b) Fold top flap of field over RT and...
Steps to Pack Manpack Radio into Field Pack:

1. Place Radio in Field Pack:
- (a) Place radio transmitter (RT) in field pack with antenna on the left shoulder.

2. Secure Field Pack Flap:
- (b) Fold top flap of field over RT and secure flap to field pack using straps and buckles.
field pack installation, military FM radio
Retrieve Signal Operating Instructions (SOI) from ANCD

3....
Steps to Retrieve SOI from Automated Net Control Device (ANCD):

1. Power On the ANCD:
- (a) Press the ON button on the ANCD keypad.

2. Unlock the Keypad:
- (b) Press the letter lock button to unlock the keys on the ANCD.

3. Access Main Menu:
- (c) Press Main Menu key.

4. View SOI Information:
- (d) Once you press the main menu key, this is what should show on the screen.
soi retrieval, ancd operation, signal operating instructions
Scan Multiple Frequencies on Manpack Radio
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Scan Multiple Frequencies:

1. Load All Desired Frequencies:
- (a) Load all desired frequencies using "Single Channel Loading Frequencies" instructions.

2. Set Controls for Scanning:
- (b) Set CHAN to CUE.
-...
Steps to Scan Multiple Frequencies:

1. Load All Desired Frequencies:
- (a) Load all desired frequencies using "Single Channel Loading Frequencies" instructions.

2. Set Controls for Scanning:
- (b) Set CHAN to CUE.
- (c) Set SC to FH.
- (d) Set FCTN to SQ ON.

3. Initiate Scan:
- (e) Press STO (display will say SCAN).
- (f) Press 8. You will now be able to scan more than one frequency.
frequency scanning, multiple channels, military FM radio
Set Presets on Manpack Radio
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Steps to Set Presets:

1. Adjust Controls:
- (a) Set CHAN to 1.
- (b) Set MODE to SC.
- (c) Set RF PWR to HI.
- (d) Set VOL to mid range.
- (e) Set DIM full clockwise.
- (f) Set FCTN to...
Steps to Set Presets:

1. Adjust Controls:
- (a) Set CHAN to 1.
- (b) Set MODE to SC.
- (c) Set RF PWR to HI.
- (d) Set VOL to mid range.
- (e) Set DIM full clockwise.
- (f) Set FCTN to LD.
- (g) Set DATA RATE to OFF.
preset configuration, military FM radio
Understand Power Output and Battery Management for Handheld Radios
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To manage power output and battery life effectively on handheld radios:

1. Recognize the impact of power on range: While antennas are more important than power in handheld devices, increasing transmitter power can improve signal...
To manage power output and battery life effectively on handheld radios:

1. Recognize the impact of power on range: While antennas are more important than power in handheld devices, increasing transmitter power can improve signal strength.
2. Battery limitations: The majority of a handheld radio's size and weight is due to its battery, which must last for 8 hours of use and be recharged overnight.
3. Replace batteries regularly: To avoid unexpected failure, change the battery halfway through the day (e.g., during lunch) using a spare that was pre-charged.
4. Battery life metrics: Handheld radios typically offer:
- 8 hours of standby time (listening without speaking)
- 30 minutes of talk time
5. Reduce power consumption for longer battery life: Lowering the transmitter power can significantly extend battery life, though it may reduce range.
6. Use vehicle batteries for mobile stations: Mobile radios benefit from higher power output because vehicle batteries have greater capacity than handheld ones.

Important Notes: Always carry spare batteries and chargers to ensure uninterrupted communication during extended operations.
handheld radio battery, power output, mobile station, battery management
Understand and Avoid Color Coding Errors in Cables
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize that there is no world-wide standard for cable color coding.
2. Cable colors depend on the manufacturer or country of origin, leading to potential confusion.
3. Be aware of common examples of color codes used:
- **AC...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that there is no world-wide standard for cable color coding.
2. Cable colors depend on the manufacturer or country of origin, leading to potential confusion.
3. Be aware of common examples of color codes used:
- AC Mains Examples:
- Black (Phase), White (Neutral), Green (Earth)
- Brown (Phase), Blue (Neutral), Grey/Yellow (Earth)
- Brown (Phase), Blue (Neutral), Green/Yellow (Earth)
- 12 Volt DC Transceiver Cables:
- White (Positive), Black (Negative)
- Brown (Positive), Green (Negative)
- Red (Positive), Black (Negative)
- Solar Panel Cables:
- Brown (Positive), Blue (Negative)
- Black (Positive), Blue (Negative)
- Red (Positive), Black (Negative)
4. Avoid assuming that color codes from one system apply to another (e.g., AC mains black = line, white = neutral; this may not match DC systems).
5. Label cables clearly with their voltage and polarity if different colors are used for the same voltage connections.
6. Provide a wiring diagram for reference, but be aware that labels can come off and diagrams can be lost.
7. Consider tying a knot in the positive cable to identify it visually.
8. Establish a consistent color code policy across your network (e.g., traditional UK DC: red = positive, black = negative) from solar panel through regulator, battery, and transceiver.
9. Replace existing cables if necessary to ensure consistency and avoid future errors.
cable color coding, voltage identification, polarity confusion, wiring standards, DC/AC cable colors
Use of GMRS Radios and Power Limits
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Proper Use of GMRS Radios:
• Base stations and mobile units: May use external antennas and power outputs up to fifty watts.
• Interstial channels (Family Radio Service channels 1 through 7): May be used with an effective radiated power...
Proper Use of GMRS Radios:
• Base stations and mobile units: May use external antennas and power outputs up to fifty watts.
• Interstial channels (Family Radio Service channels 1 through 7): May be used with an effective radiated power limit of up to five watts.
• Repeaters: GMRS repeaters are considered private property. Sharing agreements must be in writing and kept with station records.
• Higher power outputs: Increase the reliability of communications over greater distances.
GMRS power limits,repeaters,private property,higher power

Signaling (21) -

Create Ground-to-Air Emergency Code Symbols
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Procedure:

1. Use the Ground-to-Air Emergency Code (Figure 19-6) to communicate with aircraft.
2. Create five definite, meaningful symbols that are:
- A minimum of 1 meter wide and 6 meters long
- Maintain a 1:6 ratio...
Procedure:

1. Use the Ground-to-Air Emergency Code (Figure 19-6) to communicate with aircraft.
2. Create five definite, meaningful symbols that are:
- A minimum of 1 meter wide and 6 meters long
- Maintain a 1:6 ratio between width and length
3. Ensure the signal contrasts greatly with the ground it is on (e.g., use bright colors or reflective materials).
4. Place the symbol in an open area easily spotted from the air.

Notes:
• These symbols are internationally recognized by aircraft pilots.
• Use large, clear markers for visibility.
ground-to-air code, emergency signal, aircraft communication
Recognize Aircraft Acknowledgment Signals
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Procedure:

1. Once the pilot of a fixed-wing aircraft has sighted you, he will normally indicate that he has seen you by:
- Flying low
- Moving the plane
- Flashing lights (as shown in Figure 19-9)
2. Be ready to relay other...
Procedure:

1. Once the pilot of a fixed-wing aircraft has sighted you, he will normally indicate that he has seen you by:
- Flying low
- Moving the plane
- Flashing lights (as shown in Figure 19-9)
2. Be ready to relay other messages to the pilot once he acknowledges that he received and understood your first message.
3. Use a radio, if possible, to relay further messages.
4. If no radio is available, use the codes covered in previous sections (e.g., SOS or Ground-to-Air Emergency Code).

Notes:
• Aircraft acknowledgment signals are standardized for communication with survivors on the ground.
• Always be prepared to provide additional information once contact has been made.
aircraft acknowledgment, survival signaling, radio communication
Send an SOS Signal Using Lights or Flags
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Procedure:

1. Use lights or flags to send an SOS signal, which consists of:
- Three short pulses (dots)
- Three long pulses (dashes)
- Three short pulses (dots)
2. Repeat the sequence continuously until assistance arrives.
3. When...
Procedure:

1. Use lights or flags to send an SOS signal, which consists of:
- Three short pulses (dots)
- Three long pulses (dashes)
- Three short pulses (dots)
2. Repeat the sequence continuously until assistance arrives.
3. When using flags, hold them on the left side for dashes and on the right side for dots.

Notes:
• The SOS is the internationally recognized distress signal in radio Morse code.
• A dot is a short, sharp pulse; a dash is a longer pulse.
SOS, signaling, flags, lights, distress signal
Use Body Signals to Convey Messages to Aircraft Pilots
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Procedure:

1. When an aircraft is close enough for the pilot to see you clearly, use body movements or positions (Figure 19-7) to convey a message.
2. Use clear and deliberate gestures that are easily recognizable from the air.
3. Ensure...
Procedure:

1. When an aircraft is close enough for the pilot to see you clearly, use body movements or positions (Figure 19-7) to convey a message.
2. Use clear and deliberate gestures that are easily recognizable from the air.
3. Ensure your body signals are visible against the background and not obscured by terrain or vegetation.

Notes:
• Body signals should be used only when the pilot is close enough for visual recognition.
• These signals can indicate distress, direction, or other critical information.
body signal, aircraft communication, survival signaling
Use Panel Signals to Convey Messages with a Life Raft Cover or Sail
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Procedure:

1. If you have a life raft cover, sail, or a suitable substitute, use the symbols shown in Figure 19-8 to convey a message.
2. Create the symbols using the available material (e.g., paint, reflective tape, or fabric).
3....
Procedure:

1. If you have a life raft cover, sail, or a suitable substitute, use the symbols shown in Figure 19-8 to convey a message.
2. Create the symbols using the available material (e.g., paint, reflective tape, or fabric).
3. Ensure the symbols are large and clearly visible from the air.

Notes:
• These panel signals are designed for use with life rafts or sails in survival situations.
• Use high-contrast colors to maximize visibility.
panel signal, life raft, sail signaling
Using Mirrors or Shiny Objects for Signaling
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Purpose: To use mirrors or shiny objects to reflect sunlight and signal rescuers.

Procedure:
1. On a sunny day, a mirror is your best signaling device.
2. If you don't have a mirror, polish your canteen cup, belt buckle, or any similar...
Purpose: To use mirrors or shiny objects to reflect sunlight and signal rescuers.

Procedure:
1. On a sunny day, a mirror is your best signaling device.
2. If you don't have a mirror, polish your canteen cup, belt buckle, or any similar object that will reflect the sun's rays.
3. Direct the flashes in one area so they are secure from enemy observation.
4. Practice using a mirror or shiny object for signaling now; do not wait until you need it.
5. If you have an MK-3 signal mirror, follow the instructions on its back (see Figure 19-3).
6. Wear the signal mirror on a cord or chain around your neck so that it is ready for immediate use.
7. Ensure the glass side of the mirror is against your body to prevent it from flashing and being seen by enemies.
mirror signaling, shiny object, sun signal, MK-3 mirror, visual distress
Using Pen Flares for Distress Signaling
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Purpose: To use pen flares as a visual distress signal to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Pen flares are part of an aviator's survival vest and consist of a pen-shaped gun with a flare attached by a nylon cord.
2. When fired, the pen...
Purpose: To use pen flares as a visual distress signal to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Pen flares are part of an aviator's survival vest and consist of a pen-shaped gun with a flare attached by a nylon cord.
2. When fired, the pen flare sounds like a pistol shot and fires the flare about 150 meters high.
3. The device is approximately 3 centimeters in diameter.
4. To have the pen flare ready for immediate use:
- Take it out of its wrapper.
- Attach the flare.
- Leave the gun un-cocked.
- Wear it on a cord or chain around your neck.
5. Be ready to fire it in front of search aircraft and have a secondary signal prepared.
6. Be ready to take cover in case the pilot mistakes the flare for enemy fire.
pen flare, distress signal, aviator survival gear, visual signaling
Using Star Clusters for Distress Signaling
Critical Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Purpose: To use star clusters as a visual distress signal to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Use red star clusters whenever possible, as red is the international distress color.
2. Any color will still let your rescuers know where you...
Purpose: To use star clusters as a visual distress signal to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Use red star clusters whenever possible, as red is the international distress color.
2. Any color will still let your rescuers know where you are.
3. Star clusters reach a height of 200 to 215 meters, burn an average of 6 to 10 seconds, and descend at a rate of 14 meters per second.
star cluster, distress signal, red star, visual signaling
Constructing Signal Fires for Nighttime Distress
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Purpose: To create visible signals during darkness to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Build three fires in a triangle (the international distress signal) or in a straight line with about 25 meters between the fires.
2. Construct the...
Purpose: To create visible signals during darkness to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Build three fires in a triangle (the international distress signal) or in a straight line with about 25 meters between the fires.
2. Construct the fires as soon as time and the situation permit, and protect them until they are needed.
3. If you are alone, maintaining three fires may be difficult; maintain one signal fire instead.
4. Consider your geographic location:
- In a jungle, find a natural clearing or the edge of a stream where fires will not be hidden by foliage. You may need to clear an area.
- In a snow-covered area, clear the ground of snow or create a platform on which to build the fire so that melting snow does not extinguish it.
5. Use a burning tree (tree torch) as another signal method:
- Select an isolated tree to avoid starting a forest fire.
- Set pitch-bearing trees afire, even when green.
- For other types of trees, place dry wood in the lower branches and ignite it so that flames flare up and ignite the foliage.
- Before the primary tree is consumed, cut and add more small green trees to the fire to produce more smoke.
signal fire, triangle fire, jungle fire, snow fire, tree torch, visual signal, distress signal
Creating Smoke Signals for Daytime Distress
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Purpose: To create visible smoke signals during daylight to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Build a smoke generator and use it to produce smoke.
2. The international distress signal is three columns of smoke.
3. Create a color of...
Purpose: To create visible smoke signals during daylight to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Build a smoke generator and use it to produce smoke.
2. The international distress signal is three columns of smoke.
3. Create a color of smoke that contrasts with the background:
- Use dark smoke against a light background, or light smoke against a dark background for maximum visibility.
4. To create white smoke, smother a large fire with green leaves, moss, or a little water.
5. To create black smoke, add rubber or oil-soaked rags to the fire.
6. In a desert environment, smoke hangs close to the ground and can be spotted by pilots in open terrain.
7. Smoke signals are effective only on calm, clear days; high winds, rain, or snow disperse smoke and reduce visibility.
smoke signal, white smoke, black smoke, desert signaling, distress smoke
Using Tracer Ammunition for Signaling
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
Purpose: To use tracer ammunition as a visual signal to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Use rifle or pistol tracer ammunition to signal search aircraft.
2. Do not fire the ammunition in front of the aircraft.
3. Be ready to take...
Purpose: To use tracer ammunition as a visual signal to attract rescuers.

Procedure:
1. Use rifle or pistol tracer ammunition to signal search aircraft.
2. Do not fire the ammunition in front of the aircraft.
3. Be ready to take cover if the pilot mistakes your tracers for enemy fire.
tracer ammunition, signaling aircraft, visual distress signal
Air to Ground Communication Signals
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
To communicate with aircraft using ground signals:

When signals are seen and understood:

• During the day or in bright moonlight, signal by rocking from side to side.
• During dark nights, use a signal lamp to make **green...
To communicate with aircraft using ground signals:

When signals are seen and understood:

• During the day or in bright moonlight, signal by rocking from side to side.
• During dark nights, use a signal lamp to make green flashes.

When signals are seen but not understood:

• During the day or in bright moonlight, signal by making a complete circle to the left.
• During dark nights, use a signal lamp to make red flashes.

These signals help establish communication with aircraft and ensure that your message is received.
air to ground,signal recognition,communication signals,survival signaling
Constructing Letter Symbols with Contrast
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
To construct letter symbols with contrast:

1. Use materials that stand out against the surrounding vegetation and terrain. Ideally, bring material from another location such as clothing, air panels, or a space blanket.

2. On snow, use pine...
To construct letter symbols with contrast:

1. Use materials that stand out against the surrounding vegetation and terrain. Ideally, bring material from another location such as clothing, air panels, or a space blanket.

2. On snow, use pine boughs to create the letters, or utilize sea dye from a life vest for visibility.

3. Ensure the letter is clearly visible and distinguishable from its surroundings.
contrast,letter symbols,signaling,visibility,survival signaling
Use Hand Signals to Acknowledge Orders or Messages
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. If a scout hears an order or message from a leader, hold your hand level with your head throughout the communication.
2. If you do not hear the message, remain still and make no sign.
3. The leader will then repeat the message...
Procedure:

1. If a scout hears an order or message from a leader, hold your hand level with your head throughout the communication.
2. If you do not hear the message, remain still and make no sign.
3. The leader will then repeat the message louder or beckon to the scout to come closer.

Purpose: This ensures clear communication between scouts and leaders.
hand signals, acknowledgment, orders, messages
Use Hand Signals to Indicate 'Stop' or 'Halt'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Hold your hand straight up over your head with the palm facing forward.

Meaning: This signal means "Stop," "Halt."
hand signals, stop, halt
Use Hand or Flag Signals for 'Close In' or 'Come Here'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Hold your hand or flag high and wave it rapidly from side to side at full extent of arm.
2. Alternatively, whistle a succession of short, quick blasts.

Meaning: This signal means "Close in," "Rally," "Come here," "Danger," or...
Procedure:

1. Hold your hand or flag high and wave it rapidly from side to side at full extent of arm.
2. Alternatively, whistle a succession of short, quick blasts.

Meaning: This signal means "Close in," "Rally," "Come here," "Danger," or "Cattle on track."
hand signals, flag signals, close in, come here
Use Indian Signs to Indicate 'Good Water' or 'Camp Here Because of Illness'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Mark a symbol indicating good water and the direction to it.
2. If one of the group was sick, mark a symbol showing where you camped due to illness.

Purpose: These signs help indicate safe water sources or reasons for camping...
Procedure:

1. Mark a symbol indicating good water and the direction to it.
2. If one of the group was sick, mark a symbol showing where you camped due to illness.

Purpose: These signs help indicate safe water sources or reasons for camping in specific locations.
indian signs, good water, camp because of illness
Use Indian Signs to Indicate 'I Am Ready to Fight'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Hold up a weapon.

Meaning: This signal means "I am ready to fight."
indian signs, i am ready to fight
Use Indian Signs to Indicate 'I Have Found Something'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Hold up a pole horizontally with your hands on it.

Meaning: This signal means "I have found something."
indian signs, i have found something
Use Indian Signs to Indicate 'War or Trouble' and 'Peace'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Use specific signs to indicate "War or trouble about" or "Peace."
indian signs, war, peace
Use Staff Signals for 'I Have Found'
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Hold your staff horizontally (level) with both hands above your head.
2. If the object is far away, move the staff up and down slowly.
3. If the object is close by, move the staff up and down rapidly.

Meaning: This signal...
Procedure:

1. Hold your staff horizontally (level) with both hands above your head.
2. If the object is far away, move the staff up and down slowly.
3. If the object is close by, move the staff up and down rapidly.

Meaning: This signal means "I have found."
staff signals, i have found

Emergency Communication (19) -

Implement a Reliable Communication System
Critical Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Reliable Communication System Setup Procedure:

1. Select Communication Devices: Choose reliable communication systems such as two-way radios for short-range, group communication during emergencies.
2. Ensure Backup Systems: Have backup...
Reliable Communication System Setup Procedure:

1. Select Communication Devices: Choose reliable communication systems such as two-way radios for short-range, group communication during emergencies.
2. Ensure Backup Systems: Have backup communication devices available in case of failure or damage to primary equipment.
3. Train Personnel: Train all personnel involved in flood fighting on the proper use and maintenance of communication systems.
4. Coordinate with Local Agencies: Establish communication protocols with local emergency management, public safety organizations, and other relevant agencies.
5. Upgrade Systems if Needed: Consider upgrading to newer communication technologies that allow multiple systems to communicate seamlessly without requiring separate radios for each system.
two-way radios,communication systems,flood fighting,backup communication
Prepare for Evolving Safety Guidance and Instructions
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure the public is prepared to receive updated safety instructions during a nuclear emergency.

Steps:
1. Anticipate that safety messages will be updated frequently, as conditions may change rapidly after a detonation.
2....
Objective: Ensure the public is prepared to receive updated safety instructions during a nuclear emergency.

Steps:
1. Anticipate that safety messages will be updated frequently, as conditions may change rapidly after a detonation.
2. Use clear language such as “instructions will be updated” rather than “instructions may change,” to avoid confusion and maintain public trust.
3. Educate the public on the importance of following evolving guidance, emphasizing that updates are based on new information, not errors in previous instructions.
4. Train communicators to deliver these messages consistently across all platforms and audiences.
5. Conduct messaging studies with local populations to determine which language and phrasing is most effective for different groups.
evolving instructions,message clarity,public trust,safety guidance
Select Appropriate Communication Equipment for Outback Travel
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
For communication during outback travel, consider the following:

• Citizen band UHF or VHF radios are suitable for short-range communication between vehicles.
• HF radios are essential for long-range communication and can be hired from...
For communication during outback travel, consider the following:

• Citizen band UHF or VHF radios are suitable for short-range communication between vehicles.
• HF radios are essential for long-range communication and can be hired from communications suppliers at reasonable rates.
• Satellite/digital telephones allow instant voice communication with no time delay, even in areas not covered by a digital network. These systems use a saturation of satellites to ensure connectivity.

Note: Establish a communication schedule with the Royal Flying Doctor Service and contact them daily to advise of your location.
radio communication, satellite phone, outback travel
Prepare for Communication with Central Communications (Central Comms.)
High Priority Source: US Antarctic Continental Field Manual
Procedure for preparing to communicate with Central Comms.:

1. Be prepared prior to calling Central Comms.: Iridium® phones can drop calls or cut out momentarily, so have a concise message ready.
2. Prepare focused questions:
- Be...
Procedure for preparing to communicate with Central Comms.:

1. Be prepared prior to calling Central Comms.: Iridium® phones can drop calls or cut out momentarily, so have a concise message ready.
2. Prepare focused questions:
- Be clear about what you need from the EOC or other support teams.
3. Have someone ready to take notes during communication with Central Comms.
4. Have your documentation ready: Use of the Incident worksheet is helpful for reference.
5. Be prepared with an evacuation plan, including:
- Urgency of the situation
- Your location
- Weather and terrain factors
- Timetable for evacuation
6. State requests for additional resources if needed (e.g., gear, food, support).
central comms, communication preparation, evacuation plan, resource request
Assemble Communication Kit
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Items to include in a communication kit:

• Pocket radio with battery/solar power
• Cell phone
• Two way radio: CB, GMRS, FRS
• Spare NiCad batteries
• Solar battery charger
communication, emergency radio, survival gear
Communicate with Team Leader During Emergencies
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Communicate with Team Leader During Emergencies

Purpose: Ensure effective communication and coordination during emergencies.

Steps:
1. Maintain regular contact with your team leader, especially when there are changes in the...
### Communicate with Team Leader During Emergencies

Purpose: Ensure effective communication and coordination during emergencies.

Steps:
1. Maintain regular contact with your team leader, especially when there are changes in the following:
- Security conditions (e.g., spread of fighting)
- Impact on you and others (e.g., need for additional help or evacuation)
- Condition of casualties that may require new measures or a change in evacuation destination
- Weather, access route, and traffic conditions
2. Issue an alert as soon as possible, but only when manageable and after gathering sufficient information.
3. Consider the following before issuing an alert:
- Is there a standard alert procedure in place?
- What means of communication are available?
4. Keep messages clear and concise, ensuring they are factual and not subjective.
5. Avoid sharing names of casualties or military information.
6. Be brief and go straight to the point when communicating.

Important Notes:
• Any transmitted or shared information can be intercepted, so ensure it is accurate and minimal.
• Information that _can_ be misunderstood _will_ be misunderstood, so clarity is essential.
communication, team leader, emergency, alert message, security conditions, casualty condition, evacuation needs
Ensure Communication Checks with Military Police Desk
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Steps:

1. Verify that initial communication checks are conducted using both telephonic (Class C line and direct line) and radio systems.
2. Ensure communication is made with the military police desk.
communication checks, military police desk, Class C line, direct line, radio communication
Establish Communication Protocols for Levee Emergencies
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Objective: Ensure effective communication during levee-related emergencies.

Steps:
1. Define Communication Channels: Establish clear protocols for communicating hazardous conditions to public agencies and the general public, including:
...
Objective: Ensure effective communication during levee-related emergencies.

Steps:
1. Define Communication Channels: Establish clear protocols for communicating hazardous conditions to public agencies and the general public, including:
- Emergency operations centers;
- Local government officials;
- Residents and businesses in at-risk areas.
2. Designate Responsible Parties: Identify who is responsible for initiating communication during an emergency (e.g., levee operators, local emergency management).
3. Use Multiple Communication Methods: Ensure that communication can be conducted through various means such as:
- Radio;
- Telephone;
- Email;
- Public alerts;
- Social media.
4. Coordinate with Local Agencies: Maintain regular contact with State and local emergency management agencies to ensure consistency in messaging and response efforts.
5. Train Personnel: Ensure that all personnel involved in communication protocols are trained on how to relay information effectively during emergencies.
communication protocols,levee emergencies,emergency management,public alerts
Establish Emergency Communication with Staff and Volunteers
High Priority Source: Pandemic Planning Guidance
Objective: Maintain communication with employees, volunteers, and members during an emergency.

Procedure:
1. Develop a comprehensive communication plan that includes multiple channels (e.g., email, phone, social media).
2. Ensure all staff...
Objective: Maintain communication with employees, volunteers, and members during an emergency.

Procedure:
1. Develop a comprehensive communication plan that includes multiple channels (e.g., email, phone, social media).
2. Ensure all staff and volunteers have access to the latest information about organizational policies and updates.
3. Train leadership on how to disseminate critical messages quickly and effectively during an emergency.
4. Regularly test communication systems to ensure they are functional in a crisis.
communication plan, emergency communication, staff updates, pandemic preparedness
Establish and Maintain Communication During Emergencies
High Priority Source: Basic Emergency Plan
Procedure:
1. Ensure access to emergency weather radios and other local emergency information sources.
2. Keep a list of all family members' contact information, including phone numbers, email addresses, and any special needs or medical...
Procedure:
1. Ensure access to emergency weather radios and other local emergency information sources.
2. Keep a list of all family members' contact information, including phone numbers, email addresses, and any special needs or medical requirements.
3. Designate a primary communication method (e.g., radio, mobile phone, landline) for relaying updates during an emergency.
4. Establish a backup communication plan in case the primary method fails (e.g., using text messages, social media, or prearranged meeting points).
5. Train all family members on how to use the communication tools and what information to relay in different types of emergencies.
6. Keep emergency contact lists updated and accessible at all times.
emergency communication,weather radio,contact list,family communication plan
Implement Systematic Emergency Communications During a Pandemic
High Priority Source: Pandemic Planning Guidance
Purpose: To ensure timely and effective communication with staff and families during a pandemic.

Steps:
1. Use systematic emergency communications methods such as:
- Telephone calling tree
- E-mail alerts
- Call-in voice...
Purpose: To ensure timely and effective communication with staff and families during a pandemic.

Steps:
1. Use systematic emergency communications methods such as:
- Telephone calling tree
- E-mail alerts
- Call-in voice recording
2. Communicate the current status of the pandemic in the community and the status of childcare program activities.
3. Ensure that messages for staff and families are targeted, clear, and provided in different languages reflecting the community's linguistic diversity.
pandemic communication, emergency alert systems, multilingual alerts
Maintain Communication Between Medical Transport Provider and Receiving Facility
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Ensure continuous communication between the medical transport provider and the receiving facility to coordinate patient care.

Procedure:
1. Establish Communication Channels
- Use reliable communication methods (e.g.,...
Objective: Ensure continuous communication between the medical transport provider and the receiving facility to coordinate patient care.

Procedure:
1. Establish Communication Channels
- Use reliable communication methods (e.g., radio, phone) before departure to establish contact with the receiving facility.
2. Coordinate Arrival Details
- Share information about expected arrival time, patient condition, and any special requirements for handling the individual.
3. Update During Transport
- Provide periodic updates on the status of the patient during transport (e.g., changes in condition, delays).
4. Confirm Receiving Facility Readiness
- Confirm with the receiving facility that they are prepared to accept the patient and have appropriate resources available.
5. Document Communication
- Keep a log of all communications for record-keeping and evaluation purposes.

Key Considerations:
• Ensure communication equipment is tested and operational before transport begins.
• If communication fails, follow established backup protocols (e.g., relay through other agencies or use alternative methods).
communication, medical transport, receiving facility, emergency coordination, radiological emergency
Set Up a Temporary Antenna System for Emergency Communication
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify a suitable location: Find an area with minimal obstructions and clear line-of-sight to the communication target (e.g., a football ground or disaster site).
2. Attach antenna to a stable structure: Use a small whip...
Procedure:

1. Identify a suitable location: Find an area with minimal obstructions and clear line-of-sight to the communication target (e.g., a football ground or disaster site).
2. Attach antenna to a stable structure: Use a small whip antenna with a helical counterpoise and clip it to a position clear of metalwork, such as a pipe or veranda rail.
3. Run coaxial cable to a central location: Connect the antenna to a length of coax cable that is led into a first aid post or communication hub located under a metal stand or structure.
4. Connect a handheld radio: Plug an ordinary handheld radio into the coax cable and power it using its own battery.
5. Test signal strength: Ensure that the setup provides satisfactory reception and transmission, which can be significantly better than using a handheld alone.
6. Document procedures for quick deployment: With practice, this setup can be rigged up in about half an hour, making it ideal for temporary or emergency communication needs.

Notes:
• This method is used by organizations like the St. John Ambulance Brigade to overcome signal interference from metal structures.
• The cost of this temporary system is much lower than building a permanent base station.
temporary antenna, emergency communication, coax cable setup, first aid post, signal interference
Use Plain Language and Message Mapping Tools for Effective Communication
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure that emergency messages are understood by all members of the community.

Steps:
1. Avoid technical jargon, acronyms, and complex terms, such as “shelter-in-place,” which may not be understood by some members of the...
Objective: Ensure that emergency messages are understood by all members of the community.

Steps:
1. Avoid technical jargon, acronyms, and complex terms, such as “shelter-in-place,” which may not be understood by some members of the public.
2. Use plain language to convey information clearly and directly.
3. Utilize message mapping tools to structure messages for different audiences and ensure consistency across all communication channels.
4. Test messages with community members, especially those from vulnerable populations, to confirm understanding.
5. Tailor messages for specific groups, such as non-English speakers or individuals in healthcare facilities, using culturally appropriate language and formats.
plain language,message mapping,audience tailoring,cultural appropriateness
Utilize CB for Emergency and Disaster Communication
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. In areas affected by disaster or emergency, use the CB band to connect with local users who may be willing to assist.
2. Many countries have disaster relief networks (e.g., 'React') that operate on CB frequencies.
3. Be aware that...
Procedure:
1. In areas affected by disaster or emergency, use the CB band to connect with local users who may be willing to assist.
2. Many countries have disaster relief networks (e.g., 'React') that operate on CB frequencies.
3. Be aware that CB is often used as a common communication channel across multiple agencies during emergencies.

Notes:
• CB can be a valuable tool in emergency situations where other communication systems may fail.
• Repeater use is not allowed on the CB band, so all communication must be simplex (one-way at a time).
CB disaster,react network,simplex communication
Develop a Detailed Household Communication Plan
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Identify primary and secondary communication methods for all household members (e.g., cell phones, landlines, radios).

2. Establish a designated meeting point in case of separation during an emergency.

3. Create a list of...
Procedure:

1. Identify primary and secondary communication methods for all household members (e.g., cell phones, landlines, radios).

2. Establish a designated meeting point in case of separation during an emergency.

3. Create a list of emergency contacts, including family members, neighbors, local authorities, and community leaders.

4. Ensure that all household members have access to this information and know how to use it in an emergency.

5. Practice the communication plan periodically to ensure everyone is familiar with the procedures.
communication plan,homeland security,emergency contacts
Outback Travel Code of Ethics - Communication Equipment
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Have the ability to communicate with the outside world either by radio, satellite phone or global positioning system e-mail.
communication equipment,outback travel code of ethics,satellite phone
Preparing for Communication System Failures
Standard Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Have Backup Communication Devices: Keep a two-way radio, satellite phone, or ham radio on hand in case of cellular network failures.
2. Stay Informed Through Alternative Channels: Monitor local news, emergency broadcasts,...
Procedure:

1. Have Backup Communication Devices: Keep a two-way radio, satellite phone, or ham radio on hand in case of cellular network failures.
2. Stay Informed Through Alternative Channels: Monitor local news, emergency broadcasts, and social media through battery-powered radios or internet-connected devices.
3. Avoid Overloading Networks During Emergencies: Limit non-essential calls during large-scale emergencies to prevent overloading communication systems.
4. Prepare for Power Outages in Communication Devices: Keep backup batteries or power sources for radios, phones, and other communication equipment.
5. Practice Emergency Drills: Regularly practice using backup communication devices with family members or emergency contacts.
6. Install Surge Protectors for Electronics: Use surge protectors to safeguard communication devices from potential power surges or fluctuations.
7. Keep a List of Emergency Contacts: Maintain an updated list of emergency contacts, including local authorities, hospitals, and out-of-area family members.
communication failure,emergency preparedness,two way radio,satellite phone,backup communication
Develop Communication for Pandemic Recovery
Standard Source: Pandemic Planning Guidance
Objective: Create clear and effective communication strategies to inform employees, students, and families about the resumption of activities.

Procedure:
1. Draft a communication plan that includes multiple channels (e.g., email, social...
Objective: Create clear and effective communication strategies to inform employees, students, and families about the resumption of activities.

Procedure:
1. Draft a communication plan that includes multiple channels (e.g., email, social media, campus announcements).
2. Prepare messages that outline key information such as dates for returning to campus, health protocols, and available support services.
3. Ensure all communications are consistent with public health guidelines and institutional policies.
4. Conduct test runs of the communication plan before a pandemic to identify any gaps or issues.
pandemic communication, resumption messaging, institutional updates

Radio Equipment (17) -

Assemble a Short Wave Regenerative Receiver with Two Variometers and Two Variable Condensers
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:
1. One variocoupler
2. Two variometers
3. One .001 microfarad variable condenser
4. One .0005 microfarad variable condenser
5. One 2 megohm grid leak resistance
6. One vacuum tube detector
7. One 6 volt A battery
8. One 200...
Materials Required:
1. One variocoupler
2. Two variometers
3. One .001 microfarad variable condenser
4. One .0005 microfarad variable condenser
5. One 2 megohm grid leak resistance
6. One vacuum tube detector
7. One 6 volt A battery
8. One 200 ohm potentiometer
9. One 22-1/2 volt B battery
10. One .001 microfarad fixed condenser
11. One pair of 2,000 ohm headphones

Assembly Steps:
1. Screw the variocoupler to the middle of the base board or panel.
2. Secure the two variometers on either side of the variocoupler so that the distance between them is 9 or 10 inches.
3. Use copper sheets for shielding: place a sheet of copper on the back of the panel and between the components, or enclose the variometers and detector in copper boxes to prevent interference.
4. Ensure the stators of the variometers are at right angles to each other to avoid mutual induction that can cause the headphones or loudspeaker to howl.
5. Connect the leading-in wire to the fixed end of the primary coil (stator) of the variocoupler.
6. Solder a short length of wire to the tap on the stator that gives the longest wave you want to receive and connect this to one post of the .001 mfd. variable condenser.
7. Connect one end of the secondary coil (rotor) of the variocoupler with one post of the .0005 mfd. variable condenser and the other part to the grid of the detector tube.
8. Connect the other end of the rotor of the variocoupler to one of the posts of the first variometer and the other post of this to one of the terminals of the detector filament.
9. Connect this filament terminal with the - or negative electrode of the A battery and the + or positive electrode of this with one post of the rheostat.
10. Lead a wire from the other post of the rheostat to the free terminal of the filament.
11. Shunt the potential around the A battery and connect the sliding contact to the - or zinc pole of the B battery and the + or carbon pole with one terminal of the headphones.
12. Connect the other terminal of the headphone to one of the posts of the second variometer, and the other post of this to the plate of the detector tube.

Optional Amplification:
If you want to add an amplifier tube, connect it to the posts instead of the headphones as described in the previous set.
short wave receiver, variocoupler, variometer, radio assembly, regenerative set, variable condenser
Extend Battery Life with a BaoFeng Extended Battery
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Purpose: Increase talk time and usability of your BaoFeng radio.

Procedure:
1. Purchase a BaoFeng Extended Battery.
2. Replace the standard battery in your BaoFeng radio with the extended battery.
3. Ensure that your radio is compatible...
Purpose: Increase talk time and usability of your BaoFeng radio.

Procedure:
1. Purchase a BaoFeng Extended Battery.
2. Replace the standard battery in your BaoFeng radio with the extended battery.
3. Ensure that your radio is compatible with the extended battery model before installation.
4. Monitor battery life during use and recharge as needed.
BaoFeng Extended Battery, radio battery upgrade, BaoFeng power solution
Prepare for Shipping Shortwave Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Pack Equipment Properly:
- Ensure the manufacturer or shipping agency packs equipment in shock-absorbent material inside a cardboard box.
- Enclose the cardboard boxes in a strong wooden box to provide additional...
Procedure:

1. Pack Equipment Properly:
- Ensure the manufacturer or shipping agency packs equipment in shock-absorbent material inside a cardboard box.
- Enclose the cardboard boxes in a strong wooden box to provide additional mechanical protection and reduce the risk of theft.

2. Prepare Documentation:
- Include all necessary documentation with the shipment (e.g., airway bill, customs forms).

3. Consider Shipping Method:
- Air freight is more expensive but faster and reduces the risk of damage or loss during transit.
- Sea freight may be cheaper but takes longer and increases the risk of damage or theft.

4. Estimate Shipping Costs (Example):
- Wooden box, packing band, and marking: £35.00
- Delivery to airport: £8.00
- Air freight charges on 147 kilos at £2.25 per kilo: £330.75
- Airway bill charges, documentation, etc.: £20.00
- Insurance: £10.00
- Telex with flight details: £6.00
- Total estimated cost: £409.75 (example from 1986; update for current rates)

5. Alternative Shipping Method:
- For small quantities, consider having someone bring the equipment as personal luggage, even if it means paying excess baggage fees.
- Ensure they carry all necessary documentation with them to avoid delays at customs.

Notes:
• Always verify up-to-date shipping rates and methods for your specific location and destination.
• Proper packaging and documentation are essential to prevent damage, theft, or delays.
shipping radio equipment, shortwave radio logistics, radio setup transportation
Program Your BaoFeng Radio with a Programming Cable
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Purpose: Customize your radio settings using a computer.

Procedure:
1. Purchase a BaoFeng Radio Programming Cable.
2. Connect the cable to your BaoFeng radio and a computer.
3. Use compatible software (e.g., CHIRP) to program your radio...
Purpose: Customize your radio settings using a computer.

Procedure:
1. Purchase a BaoFeng Radio Programming Cable.
2. Connect the cable to your BaoFeng radio and a computer.
3. Use compatible software (e.g., CHIRP) to program your radio with desired frequencies, channels, and settings.
4. Verify that the programmed settings are working correctly on your radio.
BaoFeng Radio Programming Cable, radio programming, BaoFeng radio setup
Select and Purchase CB Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Choose a CB transceiver that complies with local regulations (e.g., AM modulation in the USA or FM modulation in the UK).
2. Consider purchasing equipment on the 27MHz frequency band, which is standard for CB use in many...
Procedure:
1. Choose a CB transceiver that complies with local regulations (e.g., AM modulation in the USA or FM modulation in the UK).
2. Consider purchasing equipment on the 27MHz frequency band, which is standard for CB use in many countries.
3. Be aware that prices may vary depending on regional frequency allocations and modulation requirements.

Notes:
• Equipment designed for one region (e.g., USA) may not work in another due to differences in frequency or modulation standards.
• Cheaper equipment may be less effective if it does not conform to local regulations.
CB transceiver,27MHz,AM,FM,modulation
Understand and Use a Variocoupler for Short Wave Reception
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
What is a Variocoupler?
A variocoupler consists of a primary coil wound on the outside of an insulating material tube. Taps are connected to certain turns of this coil, allowing you to fix the wavelength your aerial system will...
What is a Variocoupler?
A variocoupler consists of a primary coil wound on the outside of an insulating material tube. Taps are connected to certain turns of this coil, allowing you to fix the wavelength your aerial system will receive.

Components:
• Stator: The primary coil and fixed part of the variocoupler.
• Rotor: The secondary coil wound on a ball mounted on a shaft that can be turned within the stator for fine tuning.

How It Works:
1. The rotor is mounted in bearings on the stator so it can rotate freely inside it.
2. By turning the rotor, you adjust the coupling between the primary and secondary coils to tune into different wavelengths (from 150 meters up to 600 meters).
3. This allows for sharp tuning, where all wavelengths are tuned out except the one your set is designed to receive.

Connection:
• Connect the leading-in wire to one end of the primary coil (stator) of the variocoupler.
• Solder a wire to a tap on the stator that corresponds to the desired wavelength and connect it to a variable condenser.
variocoupler, short wave tuning, radio components, stator, rotor

Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Purpose: Increase the usable range of your radio.

Compatibility: Works with BTECH, BaoFeng, and Yaesu radios.

2. Attach it to your compatible radio according to the manufacturer's...

Procedure:

2. Attach it to your compatible radio according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Construct a Bell Telephone Receiver
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To construct a Bell telephone receiver, follow these steps:

1. Gather materials: A hard-rubber or composition shell and cap, a permanent steel bar magnet, fine insulated copper wire, and a soft iron disk (diaphragm).
2. **Assemble the...
To construct a Bell telephone receiver, follow these steps:

1. Gather materials: A hard-rubber or composition shell and cap, a permanent steel bar magnet, fine insulated copper wire, and a soft iron disk (diaphragm).
2. Assemble the components:
- Securely fix the bar magnet inside the handle of the receiver.
- Wind a coil of fine insulated copper wire around one end of the bar magnet.
- Place the soft iron diaphragm over the end of the magnet, ensuring it is positioned so that it comes within about 1/32 of an inch of the magnet when the cap is screwed on.
3. Connect wiring: Attach the ends of the coil to two binding posts located in the end of the shell (or shown at the sides for clarity).
4. Final assembly: Screw the cap onto the shell, ensuring it has a small opening to allow sound waves to pass through.

Notes: The coil typically has a resistance of about 75 ohms. This resistance affects the sensitivity and performance of the receiver.
bell telephone receiver, construction, components, assembly, coil winding
Construct a Loud Speaker Using a Telephone Receiver and Megaphone Horn
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Components required:

• A telephone receiver (as described earlier)
• A megaphone horn or acoustic horn

Assembly steps:
1. Connect the telephone receiver to the megaphone horn.
2. Ensure that the loud speaker is connected to a...
Components required:

• A telephone receiver (as described earlier)
• A megaphone horn or acoustic horn

Assembly steps:
1. Connect the telephone receiver to the megaphone horn.
2. Ensure that the loud speaker is connected to a receiving set with a vacuum tube detector and at least one-step amplifier for basic use, or two- or three-step amplifiers for better performance.
3. Use a 100-volt B battery to energize the plate of the second or third amplifier tube if using multi-step amplification.
4. Ensure that amplifying tubes are rated to handle at least 100 volts on the plate.

Directional current flow: When current flows through the coils in one direction, it produces better results than when flowing in the opposite direction. Test both directions by reversing connections and selecting the best-performing configuration.
loud speaker construction, telephone receiver, megaphone horn, amplifier setup
Construct a Wireless Headphone (Watch-case Type)
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
To construct a wireless headphone of the watch-case type, follow these steps:

1. Gather materials: A permanent bar magnet bent to fit into the shell of the receiver, fine insulated wire, and a soft iron diaphragm.
2. **Assemble the...
To construct a wireless headphone of the watch-case type, follow these steps:

1. Gather materials: A permanent bar magnet bent to fit into the shell of the receiver, fine insulated wire, and a soft iron diaphragm.
2. Assemble the components:
- Bend the bar magnet so that it fits into the shell as shown in Fig. 63.
- The ends of the magnet (called poles) are bent up to form a _bipolar_ receiver.
- Wind fine insulated wire around the magnet, ensuring close proximity to the core for maximum sensitivity.
- Securely hold the diaphragm over the magnet by screwing on the cap.
3. Connect wiring: Ensure that the ends of the coil are connected properly and that the headphone is ready for use.

Notes: Two such receivers are typically connected with a headband to form a headset. Each receiver should be wound to the same resistance, and they must be connected in series.
wireless headphone, construction, bipolar receiver, assembly
Construct the Simplest Type of Loud Speaker (Arkay Model)
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Components required:

• A brass horn with a curve, and an adapter or frame with a set screw.

Assembly steps:
1. Fit one of your existing headphones into the adapter or frame using the set screw to secure it in place.
2. Attach the...
Components required:

• A brass horn with a curve, and an adapter or frame with a set screw.

Assembly steps:
1. Fit one of your existing headphones into the adapter or frame using the set screw to secure it in place.
2. Attach the adapter to the bottom of the brass horn, ensuring that the headphone is properly aligned within the curved horn.
3. Ensure the construction is rigid enough to prevent overtones or distortion of speech and music.

Usage: This model works with one- or two-step amplifiers and allows a large group of people (in a room, auditorium, or outdoors) to hear signals from a distant station without relying on headphones.
arkay loud speaker, construction, brass horn, headphone integration
Estimate Initial Costs for a Basic Shortwave Radio Setup
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine Base Cost of Transceiver:
- A basic 150 watt PEP SSB HF transceiver costs approximately £1,000.

2. Calculate Additional Item Costs (based on Table 5.1):
- Each additional channel: £50
- Spare parts:...
Procedure:

1. Determine Base Cost of Transceiver:
- A basic 150 watt PEP SSB HF transceiver costs approximately £1,000.

2. Calculate Additional Item Costs (based on Table 5.1):
- Each additional channel: £50
- Spare parts: £100 (allow about 10% of the basic transceiver price for spares)
- AC power supply: £40
- USB and LSB: £80
- Hand-held microphone: £25
- Desk microphone: £80
- Earphones: £15
- Single dipole aerial: £150
- Broadband aerial: £300
- Aerial selector switch: £25
- Modifications (as specified in para. 5.2.5): £50
- Technical manuals: £10 per manual
- 12-volt battery: £80
- Solar battery charger: £250
- Mains battery charger: £50

3. Consider Additional Costs:
- Petrol or diesel generator (if required)
- Aerial masts (if required)

4. Check for Discounts:
- Investigate the possibility of obtaining discounts, especially if purchasing in bulk.
- Some manufacturers offer concessionary prices to missions and aid groups, including gifts of equipment.
- Discounts can range from 10% to 40%.

5. Account for Taxes:
- Check if there is a tax on equipment (e.g., VAT in Great Britain) that may need to be paid or reclaimed upon export.

6. Calculate Final Cost After Discount:
- Example: A £1,000 radio with 40% discount costs £600; a £700 radio with 20% discount costs £560.

Notes:
• Ensure you have the correct number of technical manuals based on your maintenance plan (one per set if each station is responsible for its own equipment).
• Consider purchasing spare parts as recommended by the manufacturer.
shortwave radio, cost estimation, transceiver setup, radio equipment budgeting
Understand How Headphones Work with Rectified Oscillations and Current Flow
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
How headphones work:

• No electrical or mechanical connection: The diaphragm is not directly connected to other parts of the receiver.
• Current flow through coils: When rectified oscillations (from a detector) or small currents from a...
How headphones work:

• No electrical or mechanical connection: The diaphragm is not directly connected to other parts of the receiver.
• Current flow through coils: When rectified oscillations (from a detector) or small currents from a B battery pass through the magnet coils, they energize the permanent steel magnet more than when no current flows.
• Magnetic attraction: This increased magnetic energy causes the magnet to attract the diaphragm more strongly than it would on its own.
• Current cutoff and vibration: When the current is cut off, the reduced magnetic pull allows the diaphragm to spring back. As varying currents flow through the coils, the diaphragm vibrates, producing sound waves.

Key takeaway: The movement of the diaphragm in response to changing currents is what produces audible sound.
headphone operation, rectified oscillations, current flow, diaphragm vibration
Understand Impedance in Headphones and Its Effects on Current Flow
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Impedance is the total opposition to alternating current (AC) flow in a circuit, which includes both ohmic resistance and inductive reactance. Key points include:

1. Ohmic Resistance: The material of the wire opposes current flow...
Impedance is the total opposition to alternating current (AC) flow in a circuit, which includes both ohmic resistance and inductive reactance. Key points include:

1. Ohmic Resistance: The material of the wire opposes current flow directly.
2. Inductive Reactance: When a wire is wound into a coil, it increases impedance due to inductive effects. This effect becomes more pronounced with higher frequency AC signals.
3. Impedance and Frequency: For high-frequency oscillations, the impedance of magnet coils can be so great that they effectively block (or _choke off_) these frequencies from passing through.

Conclusion: Impedance plays a critical role in determining how well a headphone or receiver will perform with different types of signals.
impedance, ohmic resistance, inductive reactance, frequency effects
Understand Resistance, Turns of Wire and Sensitivity in Headphones
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Understanding the relationship between resistance, turns of wire, and sensitivity:

• Resistance: The higher the ohmic resistance of a receiver (e.g., 75 ohms, 1000 ohms), the more sensitive it is to weak signals. However, this is not...
Understanding the relationship between resistance, turns of wire, and sensitivity:

• Resistance: The higher the ohmic resistance of a receiver (e.g., 75 ohms, 1000 ohms), the more sensitive it is to weak signals. However, this is not directly due to resistance itself but rather the number of turns in the coil.
• Turns of wire: A greater number of turns increases sensitivity because the same current flows around the core more times, enhancing magnetic effects.
• Wire size and resistance: Smaller gauge wires (e.g., No. 40) allow for more turns but increase ohmic resistance.

Sensitivity requirements:
• For wireless work, a receiver should have at least 1,000 ohms of resistance per earphone.
• High-grade headsets may be wound to as high as 3,000 ohms per earphone.

Connection: When using multiple headsets with one receiving set, ensure all are of the same resistance and connected in series.
resistance, sensitivity, turns of wire, headphone construction

Radio Antennas (16) -

Selecting and Installing an HF Antenna
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Selecting and Installing an HF Antenna:

1. Determine Frequency Requirements:
- Identify the frequency or frequencies you are allocated by the host government.
- If multiple frequencies are assigned, specify this to your...
Procedure for Selecting and Installing an HF Antenna:

1. Determine Frequency Requirements:
- Identify the frequency or frequencies you are allocated by the host government.
- If multiple frequencies are assigned, specify this to your antenna supplier.

2. Choose Appropriate Antenna Type:
- Select an antenna compatible with the chosen frequency(s). Options include:
- Broadband antennas
- Long Wire antennas with an Automatic or Manual Antenna Tuning Unit (ATU)
- Temporary 'bobbin type' antennas for mobility
- Consider physical space and installation requirements. Antennas may be approximately 50 meters long.

3. Directional Considerations:
- If communicating to a known location, use a beam antenna to focus power in that direction.
- For mobile stations, an 'inverted V' antenna is preferable for both vertical and horizontal polarization.

4. Installation Best Practices:
- Ensure proper installation with attention to height, bearing, and earthing.
- Provide installers with manufacturer instruction manuals and access to technical advice.

5. Radio Selection Considerations:
- While radio make/model is less critical than frequency and antenna choice, select a reliable model that:
- Is understood by your team
- Has good maintenance support in the area
- Is type approved for use in the country you are working in
- Consider buying locally to simplify logistics.

6. Scanners for Multiple Frequencies:
- If using multiple frequencies, a scanner is recommended to listen across all channels at intervals (e.g., 1 second).
- Program scanners to switch between frequencies automatically.
- Ensure that the calling station transmits for at least 15 seconds so the scanner can detect and alert users.
- Connect scanners to an external RX antenna socket on the back of the transceiver when not transmitting.

7. Use of HF Amateur Radio in Emergencies:
- If permitted by the host government, use amateur radio bands (e.g., 3.5, 7, and 10 MHz) for local and long-distance communication.
- Coordinate frequency usage with other groups to avoid interference.

8. Advanced Data Communication Modes:
- Amateur bands support data communication modes beyond voice, which can be used for more complex operations.
HF antenna, antenna installation, radio frequency selection, scanner use, amateur radio in emergencies
Adjusting Aerials Using Halyards and Pulleys
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Aerial adjustment: The aerial will have been cut either to the correct length or if you have so specified to some 10 cms. too long to allow for tuning after erection.

• Ideally, it should be possible to raise and lower the aerial by means of...
Aerial adjustment: The aerial will have been cut either to the correct length or if you have so specified to some 10 cms. too long to allow for tuning after erection.

• Ideally, it should be possible to raise and lower the aerial by means of halyards of nylon rope and pulleys.
• One disadvantage of nylon rope was that after several years of exposure to the sun in equatorial Africa the rope broke easily.
