• Also in some places a rope halyard is an attractive item and could well be stolen.
• Hard-drawn wire has been used instead as it is more durable and less noticeable than white rope.
aerial tuning, halyard setup, radio antenna adjustment
Guidelines for Aerial Placement and Support
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Guidelines: The following should be aimed for but may not be achieved:

• Do not put the aerial parallel to electric power lines or other long pieces of metal wire.
• Do not suspend the aerial above a tin roof.
• Have some 20 metres of clear...
Guidelines: The following should be aimed for but may not be achieved:

• Do not put the aerial parallel to electric power lines or other long pieces of metal wire.
• Do not suspend the aerial above a tin roof.
• Have some 20 metres of clear ground in front of the aerial, that is in the direction the power in it is to be transmitted.
• A horizontal dipole can be supported at each end.
• If thick co-axial cable is used then an extra pole can be used to support the centre of the aerial.
• Do not let either of the aerial wires touch this pole, it should be attached to the central insulator.
• A central support is necessary for inverted-vee aerials.
aerial placement, radio antenna guidelines, installation tips
Install and Configure a Broadband Aerial System
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Select the aerial type: Choose a broadband aerial that covers the desired frequency range (e.g., 3 to 30 MHz) as specified by the manufacturer.
2. Prepare installation site:
- Ensure the aerial is erected horizontally or...
Procedure:
1. Select the aerial type: Choose a broadband aerial that covers the desired frequency range (e.g., 3 to 30 MHz) as specified by the manufacturer.
2. Prepare installation site:
- Ensure the aerial is erected horizontally or in an inverted vee form.
- The height above ground should be at least 5 metres, preferably 10 metres for optimal performance.
3. Connect to coaxial cable: Use a single coaxial cable to connect the aerial to the radio.
4. Check SWR specifications:
- Ensure that the aerial has an SWR of less than 2.0 over at least 90% of its frequency range.
- Some manufacturers may require an SWR of 1.5 or less for compatibility with their transceivers.
5. Test performance: Verify that the aerial receives all transmissions (wanted and unwanted) equally well, but be aware that strong signals can cause interference when listening to weak stations.
6. Monitor and adjust: Regularly check SWR and make adjustments if necessary to maintain optimal performance.
broadband aerial, SWR, frequency range, interference
Install and Configure a Multi-dipole Aerial System
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Select the number of dipoles: Choose up to 4 individual dipoles, each tuned to a different frequency (differences should be at least 15% between frequencies).
2. Construct the aerial system:
- Connect all dipoles to one...
Procedure:
1. Select the number of dipoles: Choose up to 4 individual dipoles, each tuned to a different frequency (differences should be at least 15% between frequencies).
2. Construct the aerial system:
- Connect all dipoles to one piece of coaxial cable as shown in Fig 2.8.
3. Install the aerial: Mount the multi-dipole system in a location that allows for optimal signal transmission and reception.
4. Check for interference: Ensure that the frequencies used by each dipole do not cause significant interaction or interference with one another.
5. Test performance:
- Verify that all dipoles are functioning correctly at their respective frequencies.
6. Adjust as needed: If tuning is difficult due to interaction between dipoles, consider repositioning or adjusting the spacing between them.
multi-dipole, coaxial cable, frequency tuning, interference
Install and Tune a Helical Whip Aerial
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Select the aerial type: Choose a helical whip aerial, which consists of a wire twisted around and sealed into a fibreglass rod.
2. Determine tuning method:
- If pre-tuned to a specific frequency, it can be used at other...
Procedure:
1. Select the aerial type: Choose a helical whip aerial, which consists of a wire twisted around and sealed into a fibreglass rod.
2. Determine tuning method:
- If pre-tuned to a specific frequency, it can be used at other frequencies with an ATU.
3. Install the aerial on the vehicle: Secure the aerial in place, ensuring it is not obstructed by other components.
4. Connect the ATU (if used): Ensure the ATU is properly connected between the aerial and the transmitter if tuning to multiple frequencies.
5. Tune the aerial:
- If using an ATU, adjust the controls on the ATU to achieve a low Standing Wave Ratio (SWR).
6. Verify performance: Test the aerial at various frequencies and ensure it operates efficiently with minimal SWR.
helical whip, mobile aerial, ATU, tuning, SWR
Install and Tune a Long Wire Aerial with an ATU
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Select the wire: Use a long piece of insulated wire (longer than 10 metres) for the aerial.
2. Ensure insulation: Make sure the wire is properly insulated from any supports or structures it is attached to.
3. **Install the...
Procedure:
1. Select the wire: Use a long piece of insulated wire (longer than 10 metres) for the aerial.
2. Ensure insulation: Make sure the wire is properly insulated from any supports or structures it is attached to.
3. Install the aerial: Hang the wire in an open area, away from obstructions and sources of interference.
4. Connect the ATU:
- Use an Aerial Tuning Unit (ATU) between the wire and the transmitter.
- The ATU can be integrated into the transceiver or used as a separate unit.
5. Tune the aerial: Adjust the controls on the ATU to achieve a low SWR. Some modern ATUs will automatically tune the aerial for frequencies from 1.5 to 30 MHz and reduce the SWR to 1.5.
6. Verify performance:
- Test the aerial at various frequencies and ensure it operates efficiently with minimal SWR.
7. Monitor power handling: Ensure that the ATU can handle the transmitter output power (up to 1KW for modern units).
long wire aerial, ATU, SWR, tuning, insulation
Install and Tune a Vertical Mobile Aerial with an ATU
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Select the aerial length: Choose a vertical mobile aerial that is approximately 2 or 3 metres long, as full 1/4 wavelength aerials (e.g., 15 metres at 5 MHz) are impractical for mobile use.
2. Determine tuning method:
-...
Procedure:
1. Select the aerial length: Choose a vertical mobile aerial that is approximately 2 or 3 metres long, as full 1/4 wavelength aerials (e.g., 15 metres at 5 MHz) are impractical for mobile use.
2. Determine tuning method:
- If using an Aerial Tuning Unit (ATU), the aerial can be tuned to any frequency.
- If pre-tuned, a coil is used either at the base or halfway up the aerial. The further the coil is from the base, the more efficient the aerial.
3. Install the aerial on the vehicle:
- Secure the aerial in place, ensuring it is not obstructed by other components.
4. Connect the ATU (if used):
- Ensure the ATU is properly connected between the aerial and the transmitter.
5. Tune the aerial: Adjust the tuning controls on the ATU to achieve a low Standing Wave Ratio (SWR). This should be done after installation on the vehicle, as per para 6.4.3.
6. Verify performance:
- Test the aerial at various frequencies and ensure it operates efficiently with minimal SWR.
vertical aerial, mobile aerial, ATU, tuning, SWR
Select and Install Appropriate Aerials for Transceiver Use
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine Frequency Range Needs:
- For a single channel set, a single aerial may be sufficient.
- If you need directional coverage or multiple frequency ranges, consider using multiple dipoles or a broadband aerial.

2....
Procedure:

1. Determine Frequency Range Needs:
- For a single channel set, a single aerial may be sufficient.
- If you need directional coverage or multiple frequency ranges, consider using multiple dipoles or a broadband aerial.

2. Choose the Right Aerial Type:
- A single dipole can cover all frequencies within +2% or -2% of its center frequency (see para. 2.2.1).
- Up to four dipoles can be combined in a multipurpose configuration to cover multiple frequencies (see para. 2.6.1).
- A broadband aerial is recommended for use with more than two frequencies, as it may offer cost-effective coverage (see para. 2.6.2).

3. Check Power Rating Compatibility:
- Ensure the aerial's all-weather power rating is sufficient for your transmitted power level.
- Aerials rated for at least 1Kw PEP are common and suitable for most transceivers, as transmitted power typically does not exceed 150w PEP.

4. Install Aerial Correctly:
- Follow the manufacturer's guidelines for aerial installation to ensure optimal performance and safety.
- Secure the aerial in a location that minimizes interference and allows for proper signal transmission.
aerial selection, dipole antenna, broadband aerial, transceiver frequency range, aerial power rating, radio antenna installation
Selecting and Preparing Anchor Points on Buildings for Aerial Supports
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Survey the site and decide the best location for the aerial and what sort of support you are going to use, such as existing buildings or trees.

1. Anchor points on buildings: Ensure that they are strong and secure enough to hold the weight...
Survey the site and decide the best location for the aerial and what sort of support you are going to use, such as existing buildings or trees.

1. Anchor points on buildings: Ensure that they are strong and secure enough to hold the weight of the aerial. Facia boards are not normally satisfactory.
- If metal hooks are fitted to wooden beams, be aware that some woods rot rapidly in the tropics.
- Ensure that the aerial or its associated wire or rope will not rub against adjacent walls or overhanging roofs while swinging about in strong winds.
anchor points, buildings, aerial supports, radio antenna installation
Using Aerial Clamps for Adjusting and Securing Aerials
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Aerial clamp: An aerial clamp of the type shown in Fig. 6.4 has been developed by apprentices in Britain to serve the special requirements of being able to raise and lower for initial tuning then to leave the aerial erected whilst being able to...
Aerial clamp: An aerial clamp of the type shown in Fig. 6.4 has been developed by apprentices in Britain to serve the special requirements of being able to raise and lower for initial tuning then to leave the aerial erected whilst being able to recover the ropes.

• The aerial can be pulled up and lowered by the rope loop A.
• When adjustments are complete, the aerial is pulled up and the eyelet will automatically be above the hole in the base.
• The rope loop B is now pulled down causing the metal rod to come down through the eyelet and to lock into the hole in the base.
• The ropes A and B are no longer needed and the knots in them can be untied enabling both of them to be pulled down to earth.
aerial clamp, radio antenna setup, adjustment tool
Using Metal Masts for Aerial Supports
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Metal masts: Where no suitably located buildings or trees are available then aerial masts can be erected.

• Masts can be made from metal tubing, e.g., galvanised steel, aluminium, etc., depending on what is available.
• They will require guy...
Metal masts: Where no suitably located buildings or trees are available then aerial masts can be erected.

• Masts can be made from metal tubing, e.g., galvanised steel, aluminium, etc., depending on what is available.
• They will require guy wires about every 4 metres, depending on the strength of the pole material used.
• At least 3 guy wires are necessary at each point to give all-round support.
• Barbed wire has been successfully used for aerial guys.
• If it is thought that the aerial will remain in the same place for many years then the mast and the anchor points for the guy wires can be set in concrete.
metal masts, aerial support, radio antenna installation
Using Movable Bases for Aerial Supports
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Movable bases: A mission in Zaire has found that they have had to relocate aerials because the radio room has had to be changed to different buildings.

• Hence, they are trying out a movable aerial base made from pieces of steel e.g., two 1...
Movable bases: A mission in Zaire has found that they have had to relocate aerials because the radio room has had to be changed to different buildings.

• Hence, they are trying out a movable aerial base made from pieces of steel e.g., two 1 metre steel bars in a cross and also old vehicle wheel hubs as anchor points for guys.
• These bases are all buried several feet underground but can be dug up when necessary.
movable base, aerial support, radio antenna installation
Using Trees as Aerial Supports
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Trees: Palm trees can frequently be found in convenient positions to support aerials but the fronds need to be kept cut so that they do not foul the aerial.

• Allow the aerial to hang a little slack because of the movement of the palm tree in...
Trees: Palm trees can frequently be found in convenient positions to support aerials but the fronds need to be kept cut so that they do not foul the aerial.

• Allow the aerial to hang a little slack because of the movement of the palm tree in storms could break a tight aerial.
• The same considerations apply to other trees used as supports.
• Fix the aerial to a rigid part of the tree, not on thin branches which wave about in storms.
tree support, aerial installation, radio antenna setup
Using Wooden Poles as Aerial Supports
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Wooden poles: In Zaire, 12 metre wooden poles set into 2 metre holes in the ground have been used as aerial supports.

• Although the poles were painted with used engine oil some rotted after 3 to 5 years and had to be replaced.
• If set firmly...
Wooden poles: In Zaire, 12 metre wooden poles set into 2 metre holes in the ground have been used as aerial supports.

• Although the poles were painted with used engine oil some rotted after 3 to 5 years and had to be replaced.
• If set firmly into the ground these should be self-supporting and not need guys.
wooden poles, aerial support, radio antenna installation
Understand the Directivity of a Vertical Aerial
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Recognize directivity characteristics: Understand that a vertical aerial transmits power equally in all horizontal directions and at low vertical angles (below 45 degrees).
2. Determine suitability: Use this type of aerial...
Procedure:
1. Recognize directivity characteristics: Understand that a vertical aerial transmits power equally in all horizontal directions and at low vertical angles (below 45 degrees).
2. Determine suitability: Use this type of aerial for a central station with a network spread out over various angles and long distances.
3. Consider limitations: Be aware that the aerial is not directional, making it less suitable for situations requiring focused signal transmission in specific directions.
vertical aerial, directivity, signal transmission

Radio Installation (14) -

Lightning Protection for Radio Equipment
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Be aware of the risk of lightning damage to shortwave transceivers and take necessary precautions.
2. Lightning can reach your radio equipment through the aerial cable or, in some cases, the power supply cable,...
Procedure:

1. Be aware of the risk of lightning damage to shortwave transceivers and take necessary precautions.
2. Lightning can reach your radio equipment through the aerial cable or, in some cases, the power supply cable, potentially damaging internal components or causing injury.
3. In areas with frequent thunderstorms (e.g., Zaire, Kenya), it is especially important to implement lightning protection measures.
4. If no official lightning protection services are available, inform users of the dangers and how to protect their equipment.
5. Consider installing lightning arrestors as part of your installation process.
lightning protection, radio equipment, safety precautions, storm preparedness
Install Fail-to-Battery Relay Circuit
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify the 6-pin input power socket on your transceiver that accepts a cable and plug from either the AC mains or a 12-volt battery.
2. Check in detail what is connected to each pin of the transceiver's power socket, as the...
Procedure:

1. Identify the 6-pin input power socket on your transceiver that accepts a cable and plug from either the AC mains or a 12-volt battery.
2. Check in detail what is connected to each pin of the transceiver's power socket, as the mains plug may serve an additional purpose by completing another electrical circuit (two pins are shorted).
3. Install a fail-to-battery relay and circuit to ensure that if the AC mains fail, the radio automatically switches to battery power.
4. Refer to section 3.2.1.2 for details on how to construct or install a simple fail-to-battery circuit.
fail-to-battery relay, radio installation, power backup, electrical circuit
Install and Tune Mobile HF Aerial on a Vehicle
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Installing and Tuning an HF Aerial on a Vehicle:

1. Aerial Placement:
- Avoid mounting the aerial near the rear bumper or fender due to interference from the vehicle body.
- The best location is the center of the roof,...
Procedure for Installing and Tuning an HF Aerial on a Vehicle:

1. Aerial Placement:
- Avoid mounting the aerial near the rear bumper or fender due to interference from the vehicle body.
- The best location is the center of the roof, as it provides optimal signal transmission.
2. Installation Steps:
- Ensure the aerial base is securely mounted on a strong part of the vehicle body (e.g., frame).
- Avoid using thin metal parts like wings or roofs unless reinforced to prevent distortion during travel.
3. Tuning Process: Follow manufacturer instructions for tuning your specific aerial type.
- Example: A single frequency aerial may have a coil in the middle and a stainless steel rod (2 mm diameter) that can be adjusted by sliding 2–5 cm into a housing and locking it with a screw.
4. Adjusting Length:
- If the aerial is too long, cut off small sections (1 cm at a time) until the lowest SWR (Standing Wave Ratio) is achieved.
5. Transport Considerations: Remove the aerial for journeys through dense foliage or jungle environments to avoid damage from overhanging branches or bamboo.
HF aerial installation, mobile radio tuning, vehicle antenna setup
Power Supply Installation and Management
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Step 1: Ensure your power supply is reliable and properly connected to the transceiver.

Step 2: Pay close attention to cable color coding to avoid misconnections that could render equipment inoperable.

Step 3: Verify that both the...
Step 1: Ensure your power supply is reliable and properly connected to the transceiver.

Step 2: Pay close attention to cable color coding to avoid misconnections that could render equipment inoperable.

Step 3: Verify that both the power source and connecting cables are compatible with the transceiver’s requirements for optimal performance.

Note: A faulty or improperly connected power supply can leave your transceiver non-functional, especially during emergencies when mains power is unavailable.
power supply,cable color coding,equipment reliability
Proper Installation of Radio Equipment by Competent Personnel
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure that the radio equipment is installed by the most competent person available.
- Refer to para. 5.1.4 for options regarding technician selection.
2. If using a technician unfamiliar with the site, provide them with as...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that the radio equipment is installed by the most competent person available.
- Refer to para. 5.1.4 for options regarding technician selection.
2. If using a technician unfamiliar with the site, provide them with as much information about each location as possible before their visit.
3. Allow the technician to discuss the site with someone who is familiar with it if possible.
4. Ensure that all necessary equipment is ready for the technician to take with them during installation.
5. If aerial poles or other equipment need to be obtained locally, advise those in charge at each location regarding the size and specifications of required items so they are ready upon arrival.
6. Avoid unsuitable temporary arrangements (e.g., wiring) that may compromise long-term performance.
- Example: A long thin wire used as a power lead from battery to transceiver can cause voltage loss, leading to poor transmission quality.
7. Allow adequate time for installation, factoring in delays based on your experience and the cultural context of the area (e.g., welcoming sessions that may delay work).
8. If equipment arrives at HQ directly from manufacturers in large packages:
- Repack it into suitable sizes for transport by small aircraft, Land Rover, or canoe.
- Ensure new packing is shock-absorbent and waterproof.
9. Before dispatching equipment to installation sites, verify that all items are present and functioning if possible.
10. It is advisable for the installer to carry spare parts in case of faults during transport or installation.
11. Check whether documentation (e.g., customs receipts, radio licenses, written authority) is required based on local regulations.
- Example: In Zaire, you may need to prove authorization and that customs have been paid when entering the country.
radio installation, technician selection, equipment preparation, documentation, spare parts
Selecting Optimal Locations for Radio Equipment Installation
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Allow the technician sufficient time at each location to supervise all installation steps.
2. Include time for site inspection and decision-making regarding the best placement of equipment.
3. If no technician is available or time...
Procedure:

1. Allow the technician sufficient time at each location to supervise all installation steps.
2. Include time for site inspection and decision-making regarding the best placement of equipment.
3. If no technician is available or time is limited, assess the location yourself and determine where to place the equipment.
4. Ideally:
- Select or build a dedicated room to house the radio equipment.
- Clear a large area of ground of trees and other obstructions.
- Erect aerial poles of the required height.
5. Position the solar panel in an unshaded location.
6. Place the battery adjacent to the transceiver.
7. Ensure that the transceiver is located within 25 metres of the aerial and as close as possible to the battery.
8. If ideal conditions are not achievable:
- Consider existing building complexes for housing equipment.
- Avoid clearing valuable food-producing trees (e.g., mangoes) unless necessary.
9. Keep the transceiver near the battery due to high current flow (e.g., a peak of 20 amps) in the connecting cable.
10. The solar panel can be placed further from the battery, as the charging current is typically only 1 or 2 amps.
equipment placement, site selection, solar panel positioning, battery location
Cooling Setup for Transceiver
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Step 1: If the transceiver is housed in an enclosed space (e.g., radio desk), ensure proper cooling.

Step 2: Use a quiet axial flow fan to cool regulator transistors, which can produce significant heat.

Step 3: Mount the fan on a...
Step 1: If the transceiver is housed in an enclosed space (e.g., radio desk), ensure proper cooling.

Step 2: Use a quiet axial flow fan to cool regulator transistors, which can produce significant heat.

Step 3: Mount the fan on a sound-absorbing base if necessary to reduce noise.

Step 4: Improve airflow by mounting the transceiver on two pieces of wood (approximately 2 inches square) with holes drilled for any feet on the transceiver. Attach this assembly to the desk as shown in Fig. 6.2.
cooling,transceiver,fan,mounting,airflow
Install Mobile HF SSB Transceiver in a Vehicle
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Installing a Mobile HF SSB Transceiver:

1. Select Mounting Location: Place the transceiver below the dashboard, in front of the passenger seat.
- This location provides protection from direct sunlight and allows the...
Procedure for Installing a Mobile HF SSB Transceiver:

1. Select Mounting Location: Place the transceiver below the dashboard, in front of the passenger seat.
- This location provides protection from direct sunlight and allows the passenger to operate it.
- Ensure sufficient legroom is left if long journeys are planned.
2. Mounting Security:
- Use the manufacturer's special mobile mount.
- The mounting should allow easy fitting and removal by authorized personnel.
- Consider installing a lock for security purposes.
3. Microphone Setup: Use a fist or hand microphone with a clip fitted on or near the transceiver to secure it during use.
mobile installation, HF SSB transceiver, vehicle radio setup
Install Transceiver in Chosen Position
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Step 1: Determine the optimal position for the transceiver based on your setup requirements.

Step 2: Secure the transceiver in this position as the first step of installation, as it will influence cable length and routing.

Note: Ensure...
Step 1: Determine the optimal position for the transceiver based on your setup requirements.

Step 2: Secure the transceiver in this position as the first step of installation, as it will influence cable length and routing.

Note: Ensure that the transceiver is connected to its power source and functioning before proceeding with aerial installation. However, if time constraints or logistical challenges require, you may install the aerial beforehand, provided there is someone on-site capable of following instructions for erecting it.
transceiver,installation,cable routing,power source,aerial setup
Microphone Clip Installation on Transceiver
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Step 1: Attach the microphone clip to a convenient location (e.g., dashboard or desk side) when not in use.

Step 2: Avoid attaching the clip directly to the transceiver case unless absolutely necessary, as this can cause damage.

**Step...
Step 1: Attach the microphone clip to a convenient location (e.g., dashboard or desk side) when not in use.

Step 2: Avoid attaching the clip directly to the transceiver case unless absolutely necessary, as this can cause damage.

Step 3: If attaching the clip to the transceiver, ensure that screws do not protrude inside and risk damaging internal components.

Step 4: When removing or reattaching the microphone, consider the spring tension in the clip. Adjust the angle of the metal piece if needed to achieve proper retention.

Warning: Improper installation can lead to broken indicator lights or damage to internal circuits.
microphone clip,installation,spring tension,damage prevention
Proper Battery Connection for Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Connect the cables to the battery using proper battery connectors, not crocodile clips or other types of clips.
2. Ensure that the connectors provide a good electrical connection and do not wear away the battery lugs over...
Procedure:

1. Connect the cables to the battery using proper battery connectors, not crocodile clips or other types of clips.
2. Ensure that the connectors provide a good electrical connection and do not wear away the battery lugs over time.
3. Check the position of the positive and negative lugs on the battery, as they may be reversed depending on how the battery is oriented.
4. Do not assume the positive terminal is always on your right; carefully verify the correct lug before connecting.
5. Make sure the power leads are long enough to accommodate different sizes or shapes of batteries that may need replacement in the future.
6. Apply a good layer of anti-corrosion grease to all metal parts involved in the connection to prevent corrosion and ensure longevity.
battery connection, radio equipment, anti-corrosion grease, proper connectors, electrical safety
Proper Coaxial Cable Installation in the Radio Room
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Proper Coaxial Cable Installation in the Radio Room:

1. Route the coaxial cable carefully: Ensure that it does not rub on any rough surface or sharp edge, such as the edge of a tin roof.
2. **Avoid excessive pressure when...
Procedure for Proper Coaxial Cable Installation in the Radio Room:

1. Route the coaxial cable carefully: Ensure that it does not rub on any rough surface or sharp edge, such as the edge of a tin roof.
2. Avoid excessive pressure when bending the cable: Let the cable be in a gentle curve rather than forcing it into tight bends.
3. Retain excess aerial cable: If there are several metres of spare aerial cable when it reaches the transceiver, do not cut it off. It may be required if the radio is moved in the future.
4. Lay out excess cable straight: Do not coil up the excess cable. Instead, lay it out straight in an area such as the roof space.
5. Label multiple coaxial cables immediately: If the installation involves more than one coaxial cable, label each one as soon as you bring it into the room to avoid future confusion.
6. Route cables to the radio desk: Route the cables from their respective positions to the radio desk and then either to the aerial switch or directly to the transceiver.
7. Number aerial switch positions: Number the aerial switch positions (e.g., 1 to 4) and connect the aerials so that they correspond with the channel number they serve on the transceiver. For example, Aerial position 3 should serve Channel 3.
coaxial cable installation,radio room setup,aerial switch labeling,cable routing
Set Up Power Supply for Mobile HF Transceiver
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Setting Up a Power Supply for a Mobile HF SSB Transceiver:

1. Power Requirements: A 150 watt PEP transceiver requires an average current of 12 amps with a peak demand of 18 amps.
2. Connection Point:
- Connect the power...
Procedure for Setting Up a Power Supply for a Mobile HF SSB Transceiver:

1. Power Requirements: A 150 watt PEP transceiver requires an average current of 12 amps with a peak demand of 18 amps.
2. Connection Point:
- Connect the power cable directly to the vehicle battery, not through existing fuses or cigarette lighter sockets (which are only suitable for low-power CB sets).
3. Fuse Installation: Install a 20 amp fuse in the power cable to protect against overcurrent.
4. Battery Terminal Preparation:
- Check that battery lugs are secure and not worn thin.
- Use packing material if necessary to ensure reliable contact between the terminal and the lug.
HF transceiver power supply, vehicle radio installation, battery connection
Erecting an Aerial for Shortwave Radio Installation
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Safety First: Do not attempt to erect an aerial if lightning can be seen or thunder heard.
2. Determine Aerial Specifications: Refer to Chapter 2 to determine the length, direction, and height of the aerial based on...
Procedure:

1. Safety First: Do not attempt to erect an aerial if lightning can be seen or thunder heard.
2. Determine Aerial Specifications: Refer to Chapter 2 to determine the length, direction, and height of the aerial based on theoretical calculations.
3. Check for Permanent Installation: If the aerial is not going to be lowered for tuning, ensure it is at the calculated theoretical length before erection (see para. 2.2.1).
4. Uncoil Aerial and Coaxial Cable: Carefully uncoil the aerial wire and coaxial cable, ensuring there are no twists or kinks.
5. Lay Out on Ground: If possible, lay out the aerial on the ground below where it will be raised to confirm that the supports are sufficiently far apart.
6. Measure Aerial Length: Measure each piece of aerial wire as a final confirmation that the length is correct (e.g., one wire may be 12.3 metres while another may be 10.1 metres, which could indicate an error in manufacturing or installation).
7. Check Coaxial Cable Length: Confirm the coaxial cable length is between 25 to 30 metres unless a special length was specified.
8. Secure Coaxial Cable: Ensure that the coaxial cable is secured to the centre insulator and not left hanging only from its coaxial plug.
9. Use Supporting Wire or Rope: Use wire or nylon rope between the aerial insulators and the supporting structure (tree, pole, building). If using wire:
- Ensure it does not touch the aerial wire.
- Avoid using pieces of wire that are the same length as or within 1 metre of any piece of aerial wire or its multiples. For example, for a dipole at 7.305 MHz (each wire is 9.76 metres), avoid using supporting wires between 8.76 to 10.76 metres, 18.52 to 20.52 metres, etc.
- This prevents the wire from acting as a resonant parasitic element that could negatively affect performance.
10. Secure Insulators: Securely fasten the aerial insulators to their supporting wire or rope before raising the aerial.
11. Raise Aerial into Position:
- Use excess length in the supporting wire or rope initially, as it will be cut once the aerial is firmly attached.
- Raise and secure one end of the aerial first, ensuring no twists or kinks are introduced.
- If a pole is used to support the centre insulator, erect this next, checking that the aerial wires do not touch anything.
- Raise and secure the other end of the aerial in the same manner.
12. Adjust Sag: For aerials supported between solid buildings or rigid metal poles, pull the supporting wire until the aerial is reasonably straight. For aerials supported by trees, allow it to sag up to 1 metre in the middle.
- A 20-metre aerial with a 1-metre sag will only permit an outward movement of 5 cm in each tree before becoming horizontal and tight.
- Consider wind direction when allowing sag, as it may reduce strain on the aerial.
- For thin wooden poles without guy ropes, allow some sag to avoid breaking the pole in strong winds.
13. Route Coaxial Cable: Once the aerial is in position, route the coaxial cable to the radio room.
14. Protect Coaxial Cable: Protect the coaxial cable from damage by suspending it on poles approximately 3 or 4 metres above the ground.
- Avoid using thin wire to attach the coaxial cable to the poles as this can cut into the cable.
- Use solid material between the wire and cable, or use cord or other broader material without sharp edges.
aerial erection, shortwave radio installation, coaxial cable, aerial length, supporting rope, sag adjustment

Emergency Alerts (13) -

Distribute Emergency WEA Messages via IPAWS Network
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Access FEMA’s Integrated Public Alert & Warning System (IPAWS) network.
2. Utilize the city's opt-in emergency communication service to reach all individuals within a specified radius (e.g., 50 miles of Metropolis).
3. Send an...
Procedure:

1. Access FEMA’s Integrated Public Alert & Warning System (IPAWS) network.
2. Utilize the city's opt-in emergency communication service to reach all individuals within a specified radius (e.g., 50 miles of Metropolis).
3. Send an Emergency SIP message through IPAWS, ensuring it reaches all registered users in the designated area.
4. Compose and send a Wireless Emergency Alert (WEA) message to cellphones with the following content:
- *“This is a message from [Agency Name]: a nuclear detonation has occurred. To protect yourself and your family, get inside, stay inside, stay tuned for more information. Move to the lowest level/most interior portion of the building if possible. Follow instruction from officials—this can save your life.”*
5. Ensure that all messages are consistent across platforms and agencies (e.g., city watch office, neighboring counties, state EOC, White House).
6. Repeat the message as needed to reinforce instructions: *“Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned.”*
IPAWS, WEA, emergency message, nuclear detonation, alert dissemination
Understand the Emergency Alert System in Norway
Critical Source: Norway One Week Preparedness Guide
The Emergency Alert System is a service operated by Norwegian authorities to warn citizens of serious situations that pose an acute risk to life and health.

Steps:
1. Ensure your mobile phone has 4G or 5G coverage: This is required to...
The Emergency Alert System is a service operated by Norwegian authorities to warn citizens of serious situations that pose an acute risk to life and health.

Steps:
1. Ensure your mobile phone has 4G or 5G coverage: This is required to receive emergency alerts.
2. Recognize the warning siren patterns:
- Three series with one minute in between each series: «Important notice – seek information». Listen to NRK P1 for details.
- Short intervals for approximately one minute: «Danger, risk of attack – seek cover».
- Continuous sound for 30 seconds: Indicates that the danger is over.
3. Be aware of public warning systems in larger cities and conurbations: These are located to provide alerts to densely populated areas.
emergency alert system, warning sirens, Norway, mobile alerts
Implement Geo-Targeted Communications Strategy
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Use geographically targeted messaging to improve emergency response effectiveness.

Steps:
1. Incorporate Geo-Targeting: Include geo-targeting as a key component of your communications strategy.
2. **Plan for Zone-Specific...
Objective: Use geographically targeted messaging to improve emergency response effectiveness.

Steps:
1. Incorporate Geo-Targeting: Include geo-targeting as a key component of your communications strategy.
2. Plan for Zone-Specific Messages: Develop different messages tailored to each impact area within the affected region to ensure appropriate actions are taken in each zone.
3. Use Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA): Utilize WEA systems, which send geographically targeted text-like alerts to compatible mobile devices, to notify people of imminent threats and specific instructions (e.g., evacuation orders for those in the DRZ).
4. Coordinate Targeted Messaging: Practice coordinating and delivering targeted messages through various platforms. This can be tested during preparedness activities such as National Preparedness Month.
5. Test WEA Capabilities: Conduct regular tests of WEA systems within your community to identify gaps, assess capabilities, and engage in preparedness efforts.
geo-targeting, WEA, emergency alerts, communication strategy
Respond to Emergency Alerts Received via NOAA Weather Radio (NWR)
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Purpose: Know how to respond effectively when receiving an emergency alert through NWR.

Steps to Take When Receiving an Alert:
1. Listen carefully to the message: Pay attention to the type of warning or hazard being broadcast, such as a...
Purpose: Know how to respond effectively when receiving an emergency alert through NWR.

Steps to Take When Receiving an Alert:
1. Listen carefully to the message: Pay attention to the type of warning or hazard being broadcast, such as a tornado watch, flood warning, or chemical release alert.
2. Take immediate action based on the information provided: Follow instructions from NWS forecasters and local emergency management officials. For example:
- If it is a severe weather warning, seek shelter immediately.
- If it is an AMBER alert, contact local law enforcement if you have any information about the missing person.
3. Use the special alarm tone feature (if available): This will sound an alert and provide immediate information about lifethreatening situations.
4. Notify others in your household or community: Ensure that everyone is aware of the emergency and knows what actions to take.
5. Stay tuned for updates: NWR will continue to broadcast updates until the situation is resolved.

Notes:
• During a nuclear detonation, NWR can be used to receive warnings such as the NAWAS Attack Warning, which will be broadcast as an NWEM over NWR and NOAA’s Weather Wire Service.
• Always verify the information received from NWR with local emergency management or official sources.
NOAA Weather Radio, emergency response, NWR alerts, nuclear detonation warning, AMBER alert, HazCollect system
Benefits of WEA Technology in Emergency Situations
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) offer several key advantages during emergencies:

1. Faster Delivery: WEAs use SMS-CB technology, which avoids mobile device congestion and ensures faster message delivery compared to traditional SMS-PP...
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) offer several key advantages during emergencies:

1. Faster Delivery: WEAs use SMS-CB technology, which avoids mobile device congestion and ensures faster message delivery compared to traditional SMS-PP services.
2. Comprehensive Coverage: WEAs are broadcast simultaneously to all WEA-capable devices within a targeted cell tower’s coverage area, ensuring that 100% of the target population receives the alert with minimal overshoot (no more than 1/10th mile).
3. No Subscription Required: Users do not need to sign up or pay for WEAs; they are automatically delivered to all WEA-capable devices in an affected area.
4. Accessibility Features: The unique ring tone and vibration of WEAs make them particularly helpful for individuals with hearing disabilities, ensuring that alerts are received even if the user cannot hear a traditional alert sound.
5. No Cost to Recipients: Wireless customers are not charged for receiving WEA messages, making this an accessible emergency communication tool for all users.
WEA benefits,SMS-CB,faster delivery,accessibility features
Ensure Emergency Messaging Includes Directions for Ambulatory Victims
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Provide clear instructions to ambulatory victims on how to reach medical facilities during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Develop emergency messaging protocols that include directions or instructions for ambulatory...
Objective: Provide clear instructions to ambulatory victims on how to reach medical facilities during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Develop emergency messaging protocols that include directions or instructions for ambulatory victims.
2. Include specific guidance in messages about transporting themselves to evacuation centers (ECs) or medical centers (MCs).
3. Use clear and concise language in all communication, ensuring it is easily understood by the public.
4. Broadcast information through multiple channels, such as radio, television, mobile alerts, and social media platforms.
5. Update messaging dynamically based on real-time conditions, including radiation zones or changes to medical facility availability.

Notes:
• Ambulatory victims may be at risk of acute radiation syndrome (ARS) and need timely access to medical care.
• Clear directions help reduce confusion and ensure efficient movement of patients toward appropriate treatment locations.
emergency messaging, ambulatory victims, evacuation centers, medical centers, nuclear detonation
How WEAs Are Delivered to Recipients
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) are delivered through a cell tower-based system that ensures alerts reach all WEA-capable devices within the targeted area:

1. Location-Based Delivery: WEAs are sent to all WEA-capable mobile devices in the...
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) are delivered through a cell tower-based system that ensures alerts reach all WEA-capable devices within the targeted area:

1. Location-Based Delivery: WEAs are sent to all WEA-capable mobile devices in the coverage area of a specific cell tower, ensuring that only those physically located in the affected geographic area receive the alert.
2. No Database Required: Alerts are not sent to a database of phone numbers but are broadcast simultaneously to all eligible devices within range.
3. SMS-CB Technology: WEAs use SMS-CB (Cell Broadcast), which allows for one-to-many delivery and avoids mobile device congestion, ensuring faster and more comprehensive message delivery during emergencies.
4. No Overshoot Beyond Target Area: WEAs are broadcast to ensure 100% coverage of the target area with a maximum overshoot of no more than 1/10th mile, minimizing unnecessary alerts outside the affected zone.
WEA delivery,cell tower coverage,SMS-CB,location-based alerting
Understanding Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) and Their Functionality
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) are designed to provide immediate alerts to mobile devices in specific geographic areas during emergencies. These alerts:

1. Automatically display on the mobile device screen, regardless of whether the phone...
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) are designed to provide immediate alerts to mobile devices in specific geographic areas during emergencies. These alerts:

1. Automatically display on the mobile device screen, regardless of whether the phone is on or off.
2. Use a unique ring tone and vibration pattern, making them distinguishable from regular text messages and particularly helpful for individuals with hearing disabilities.
3. Can include a URL/web link that allows recipients to access more detailed information about the emergency.
4. Are targeted to specific geographic areas based on cell tower coverage, ensuring alerts are sent only to devices located in the affected area.
5. Are not subscription-based, meaning users do not need to sign up or download any app to receive them.
6. Are free of charge for wireless customers and are automatically delivered if a WEA is active in their location.
7. Use SMS-CB (Cell Broadcast) technology, which allows messages to be sent simultaneously to all devices within the targeted cell tower’s coverage area, avoiding mobile device congestion issues.
8. Are supported by all major U.S. wireless providers on a voluntary basis and are included in many modern WEA-capable handsets.
9. Can be turned off by individual users through their device settings if desired.
10. Reach 100% of the target area, with no more than a 1/10th mile overshoot, ensuring comprehensive coverage during emergencies.

Important Notes:
• WEAs are not sent to a database of phone numbers; instead, they are broadcasted based on location.
• Users should check with their wireless provider to confirm if their mobile device is WEA-capable.
Wireless Emergency Alerts,WEA,functionality,geographic targeting,SMS-CB,cell broadcast,alert delivery
How to Determine if a Mobile Device is WEA-Capable
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
To determine whether a mobile device can receive Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA), users should:

1. Check with their wireless carrier to confirm that the device is WEA-capable.
2. Note that **not all handsets on the market are capable of...
To determine whether a mobile device can receive Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA), users should:

1. Check with their wireless carrier to confirm that the device is WEA-capable.
2. Note that not all handsets on the market are capable of receiving WEAs, even though major U.S. wireless providers participate in WEA voluntarily.
3. Understand that wireless carriers sell mobile devices with WEA capability included, but users should verify this information directly with their provider.
4. Be aware that WEA functionality is dependent on the type of mobile device used, and some older or non-standard devices may not support WEA.
WEA-capable devices,wireless carrier verification,mobile device compatibility
Monitor Pager for Flight Updates
Standard Source: US Antarctic Field Manual
Procedure:

1. If the team has a pager, monitor it for updates.

Details:
• This ensures that all team members are promptly informed of any changes to the flight schedule or other critical information.
pager, communication
Opt-In and Opt-Out Settings for WEAs
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) are enabled by default on WEA-capable devices but can be managed by users:

1. Automatic Receipt: Users automatically receive WEAs if they are located in an area where a WEA is active, without the need to...
Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) are enabled by default on WEA-capable devices but can be managed by users:

1. Automatic Receipt: Users automatically receive WEAs if they are located in an area where a WEA is active, without the need to sign up or download any applications.
2. Opt-Out Option: Users may choose to turn off their ability to receive WEAs through the settings menu of their mobile device.
3. No Subscription Required: Unlike subscription-based alert systems, users do not have to register for WEAs, and no tracking or delivery information is collected.
4. User Control: The opt-in setting can be adjusted at any time by individual handset users based on personal preference.
WEA opt-out,wireless alert settings,user control
Understand the Functionality of NOAA Weather Radio (NWR)
Standard Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Purpose: Understand how NWR operates and its role in emergency communication.

Functionality Overview:
• Coverage Area: NWR provides nationwide coverage, including all 50 states, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and U.S. Pacific...
Purpose: Understand how NWR operates and its role in emergency communication.

Functionality Overview:
• Coverage Area: NWR provides nationwide coverage, including all 50 states, Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands, and U.S. Pacific Territories.
• Signal Transmission: Broadcasts are transmitted via over 1,000 narrowband transmitters operating in the VHF public service band at specific frequencies (see Table 7 for details).
• Content Provided: NWR broadcasts official Weather Service warnings, watches, forecasts, and other hazard information 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
• Emergency Activation: During an emergency, NWS forecasters interrupt routine weather programming and send out a special tone that activates weather radios in the listening area.
• Additional Uses: NWR also broadcasts Non-Weather Emergency Messages (NWEM) through the All-Hazards Emergency Message Collection System (HazCollect), including alerts for natural disasters, environmental hazards, and public safety issues such as AMBER alerts or 911 outages.

Notes:
• The system can be used by institutions like schools and hospitals for continuous monitoring.
• NWR is a public service provided by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) under the Department of Commerce.
NOAA Weather Radio, NWR functionality, emergency communication, VHF public service band, HazCollect system, NWEM broadcasting
Utilize NIXLE Alerts for Community Communication
Standard Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
Procedure for Utilizing NIXLE Alerts in Emergency Planning:

1. **Access NIXLE CONNECT Services:
- Register with NIXLE CONNECT, a communication platform that allows public safety agencies to send alerts via text, web, and email.
-...
Procedure for Utilizing NIXLE Alerts in Emergency Planning:

1. **Access NIXLE CONNECT Services:
- Register with NIXLE CONNECT, a communication platform that allows public safety agencies to send alerts via text, web, and email.
- Ensure all relevant staff and community partners are subscribed to receive these notifications.

2. **Use Alerts for Emergency Mitigation:
- Use NIXLE alerts not only for emergency response but also for community outreach and public relations efforts.
- Include information on preparedness tips, shelter locations, and other critical updates during emergencies.

3. **Train Staff to Respond to Alerts:
- Provide training to all staff on how to interpret and act upon NIXLE alerts.
- Ensure that the agency has a clear protocol for responding to different types of messages (e.g., evacuation orders, shelter-in-place instructions).
NIXLE alerts,community communication,public safety,text alerts,emergency notifications

Emergency Alert Systems (13) -

Assess and Mitigate Delays in Alert Issuance
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Conduct a thorough assessment of potential delays in alert issuance during nuclear detonation scenarios.
2. Identify the causes of delays, such as:
- System malfunctions
- Human error or lack of training
- Inadequate...
Procedure:

1. Conduct a thorough assessment of potential delays in alert issuance during nuclear detonation scenarios.
2. Identify the causes of delays, such as:
- System malfunctions
- Human error or lack of training
- Inadequate pre-planning
3. Develop strategies to mitigate these delays, including:
- Regular system maintenance and testing
- Training for alert originators on rapid response protocols
- Ensuring redundancy in communication systems
4. Implement a process to track the results of tests, training, and exercises related to alert issuance.
5. Use this data to improve preparedness and ensure that delays are minimized or eliminated wherever possible.
6. Ensure that all personnel involved in alert dissemination understand the additive effects of delays, which can significantly impact response effectiveness.
7. Incorporate delay mitigation strategies into certification, specialization, or qualification processes for roles such as public warning specialists.

Important Considerations:
• Delays can be exacerbated by multiple factors and may have a cumulative effect on the overall response time.
• Senior leaders must be aware of these delays to make informed decisions during emergencies.
delay mitigation,alert issuance,nuclear detonation,response time
Ensure Continuous and Reliable Emergency Alert System (EAS) Capabilities
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Verify that all EAS stations have the capability to:
- Interrupt broadcasts 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, whether streaming live or previously aired programming.
- Broadcast emergency instructions without...
Procedure:

1. Verify that all EAS stations have the capability to:
- Interrupt broadcasts 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, whether streaming live or previously aired programming.
- Broadcast emergency instructions without interruption during incidents.

2. Ensure systems remain unaffected by adverse environmental conditions such as floods or power outages.

3. Confirm that the EAS can repeat messages multiple times to ensure maximum coverage and reach.

4. Establish a set of intervals for message repetition based on the nature of the emergency and expected response time.

5. Maintain a list of broadcast stations and other systems used for emergency communication within the plume exposure pathway (EPZ).

6. Ensure that all EAS stations are equipped with backup power sources to maintain functionality during outages.
EAS, emergency broadcasting, message repetition, backup power, environmental resilience
Evaluate Siren System Resiliency in Nuclear Detonation Scenarios
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Conduct a comprehensive evaluation of siren systems used for public alerts.
2. Determine how often the siren system is tested and ensure that testing frequency meets or exceeds recommended standards.
3. Assess factors that may...
Procedure:

1. Conduct a comprehensive evaluation of siren systems used for public alerts.
2. Determine how often the siren system is tested and ensure that testing frequency meets or exceeds recommended standards.
3. Assess factors that may impact siren resiliency, including:
- Power supply reliability (e.g., backup generators)
- Physical damage resistance
- Coverage area and signal strength
4. Ensure that sirens are integrated with other alert systems for redundancy in case of system failure.
5. Train personnel on how to test and maintain siren systems, including during nuclear detonation scenarios.
6. Document all evaluations and testing results for use in planning and response activities.
7. Regularly update the siren system based on findings from evaluations and real-world events.

Important Considerations:
• Sirens may be less effective in urban areas due to noise pollution or physical obstructions.
• Backup power sources must be tested regularly to ensure they function during a nuclear detonation.
siren system,nuclear detonation,resiliency testing,alert systems
Implement Pre-Scripted Messages for Emergency Alerts
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Prepare pre-scripted messages in advance for use during nuclear detonation scenarios.
2. Save these messages to desktops or secure storage within the EOC for rapid access.
3. Ensure that all messages are **reviewed and...
Procedure:

1. Prepare pre-scripted messages in advance for use during nuclear detonation scenarios.
2. Save these messages to desktops or secure storage within the EOC for rapid access.
3. Ensure that all messages are reviewed and approved by senior leadership prior to being stored.
4. Messages should be tailored for different alert types, such as immediate evacuation, shelter-in-place orders, and radiation exposure warnings.
5. Test these pre-scripted messages regularly during training exercises to ensure they function correctly within the AOT software.
6. Ensure that all personnel who may need to send alerts are familiar with the location and use of stored messages.
7. Maintain a log of all tests, training, and exercises related to message preparation and delivery for certification purposes.

Important Considerations:
• Messages must be compatible with AOT software and dissemination platforms.
• Some systems may require sending alerts within a prescribed time window, so timing is critical.
• Pre-scripted messages should be updated regularly to reflect changes in protocols, locations, or threats.
pre-scripted messages,nuclear detonation,alert systems,AOT software,EOC training
Understand Components of the Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS)
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Familiarize yourself with the components of IPAWS, which includes:
- The Common Alerting Protocol (CAP)
- The National Emergency Management Information System (NEMIS)
- The Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) system
-...
Procedure:

1. Familiarize yourself with the components of IPAWS, which includes:
- The Common Alerting Protocol (CAP)
- The National Emergency Management Information System (NEMIS)
- The Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA) system
- The Emergency Alert System (EAS)
2. Ensure that all EOC personnel understand how IPAWS functions and its role in alert dissemination during nuclear detonation scenarios.
3. Train personnel on the use of IPAWS components, including how to send alerts through various channels such as radio, television, mobile devices, and public address systems.
4. Regularly test IPAWS components to ensure they function correctly under different conditions, including during a nuclear detonation.
5. Maintain an updated inventory of all IPAWS components for use in planning and response activities.
6. Ensure that all personnel involved in alert dissemination understand the importance of timely responses when using IPAWS.

Important Considerations:
• IPAWS is designed to provide a unified approach to public alerting across multiple platforms.
• Components must be maintained and tested regularly to ensure they are operational during emergencies.
IPAWS components,nuclear detonation,alert systems,EOC training
Utilize Local Jurisdiction Emergency Notification Systems (AWN)
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Identify all local jurisdiction emergency notification systems available within the area of responsibility.
2. Determine the types of AWN systems in use, such as:
- Text messages
- Social media alerts
- Email...
Procedure:

1. Identify all local jurisdiction emergency notification systems available within the area of responsibility.
2. Determine the types of AWN systems in use, such as:
- Text messages
- Social media alerts
- Email notifications
- Reverse dialback systems (public databases or subscription-based)
3. Map the locations and capabilities of each system, including language support, media type compatibility, and special features.
4. Ensure that all local systems are integrated with the EOC for rapid alert dissemination.
5. Train personnel on how to use these systems, including how to access them during a nuclear detonation scenario.
6. Conduct regular testing of AWN systems to ensure they function correctly under various conditions, including during a nuclear detonation.
7. Maintain an updated inventory of all local jurisdiction AWN systems for use in planning and response activities.

Important Considerations:
• Some jurisdictions may have pre-existing opt-in or sign-up systems that can be used immediately.
• Systems with limited language support may require additional resources to ensure effective communication with diverse populations.
AWN systems,local jurisdiction,nuclear detonation,alert dissemination
Utilize the Emergency Alert System (EAS) During a Nuclear Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Understand EAS Functionality: The EAS is the primary communication system for national alert and warning (A&W). It operates even when other communication systems fail, making it essential during emergencies like nuclear...
Procedure:

1. Understand EAS Functionality: The EAS is the primary communication system for national alert and warning (A&W). It operates even when other communication systems fail, making it essential during emergencies like nuclear detonations.

2. EAS Coverage: The EAS includes:
- NPWS PEP stations
- All radio and television broadcasters
- Cable television providers
- Wireless cable systems
- SDARS (Satellite Digital Audio Radio Service)
- DBS (Direct Broadcast Satellite) providers

3. Message Delivery: Emergency messages sent via IPAWS to EAS can include:
- Full message text for screen crawl/display
- Audio attachments such as MP3 files
- Additional languages, depending on local broadcast station capabilities

4. Activation Process: An audio announcement and text display interrupt programming on TVs and radios. The emergency message is repeated twice before regular programming resumes.

5. Television Display Format: The format for displaying EAS messages varies by station; however, the content will be visible to viewers.

6. Internet Connection Requirement: EAS participants must have an internet connection to poll IPAWS-OPEN and receive CAP (Common Alerting Protocol) alerts.

7. Redundancy Requirements: EAS participants are required by the FCC to monitor both IPAWS-OPEN and over-the-air broadcast systems for redundancy, as well as other radio/television station sources in accordance with state EAS plans.
EAS, emergency alert system, nuclear detonation, IPAWS-OPEN, CAP alerts, FCC, radio, television, communication redundancy
Coordinate with EAS Radio Stations for Local Alerting
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Collaborate with Broadcast Stations: Local coordination and partnership are necessary to enable local EAS alerting, as broadcast stations are not required by law to air local emergency messages.

2. **Understand the Role of...
Procedure:

1. Collaborate with Broadcast Stations: Local coordination and partnership are necessary to enable local EAS alerting, as broadcast stations are not required by law to air local emergency messages.

2. Understand the Role of EAS in Local Emergencies: The EAS is particularly important for rural communities and urban areas affected by nuclear detonations or other post-disaster situations.

3. Leverage Existing Partnerships: NOAA’s NWR (National Weather Radio) and SLTT (State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial) governments regularly use the EAS to disseminate emergency alerts.

4. Consider Public Communication Preferences: While some question the effectiveness of AM/FM radio and satellite radio for AWN dissemination compared to digital media, research data from 2019 indicates that:
- Over 90% of Americans are reached weekly by radio
- Time spent listening (TSL) to AM/FM radio is 10 times greater than TSL to streaming platforms
- Radio reaches 60% of listeners in connected cars
- Total AM/FM listeners reached 250 million in 2018

5. Use Radios During Disruptions: In the aftermath of a nuclear detonation, communication disruptions may increase reliance on radios in cars and trucks for seeking emergency AWN broadcasts.

6. Avoid Exaggerating Radio Access: Caution is needed to avoid overstating the access provided by AM/FM radio for purposes of AWN.
EAS coordination, local alerting, radio usage, public communication, AM/FM radio, nuclear detonation, IPAWS-OPEN
Implement IPAWS Capabilities in Urban Area Nuclear Detonation Response Plans
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Review Executive Order (EO) 13407: This order, signed in June 2006, provides the foundational guidance for the development and implementation of national alerting systems.

2. **Understand the IPAWS Modernization Act of 2015...
Procedure:

1. Review Executive Order (EO) 13407: This order, signed in June 2006, provides the foundational guidance for the development and implementation of national alerting systems.

2. Understand the IPAWS Modernization Act of 2015 (PL 114-143): This legislation directed FEMA to:
- Establish common alerting and warning protocols, standards, terminology, and operating procedures for the system;
- Include the capability to adapt the distribution and content of communications based on geographic location, risks, and multiple communication technologies;
- Alert, warn, and provide equivalent information to individuals with disabilities, access and functional needs, or limited English proficiency;
- Ensure that specified training, tests, and exercises for such a system are conducted and that the system is resilient, secure, and can withstand external attacks.

3. Incorporate IPAWS capabilities into urban area nuclear detonation response plans: Planners should ensure that the operational capabilities of IPAWS—such as geographic-based alerting, multi-channel communication support, accessibility features, and system resilience—are included in their planning for responding to a nuclear detonation.

Important Considerations:
• The system must be tested regularly through training, exercises, and drills.
• Resilience against external attacks is essential to ensure the system remains functional during emergencies.
IPAWS capabilities, urban area planning, nuclear detonation response, alerting protocols
Integrate Local Alerting Systems with IPAWS Infrastructure
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Understand the IPAWS infrastructure: The Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS) is a national alert and warning (A&W) infrastructure used by Federal, State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial (FSLTT) public alerting...
Procedure:

1. Understand the IPAWS infrastructure: The Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS) is a national alert and warning (A&W) infrastructure used by Federal, State, Local, Tribal, and Territorial (FSLTT) public alerting authorities to send emergency alerts to citizens.

2. Use IPAWS-OPEN for message transmission: The IPAWS-OPEN component receives and authenticates messages transmitted by alerting authorities before routing them through the IPAWS communications pathways.

3. Transition to cloud provider facilities: As of April 2021, IPAWS-OPEN has transitioned to cloud provider facilities, enhancing system resiliency and scalability.

4. Integrate local Alert Warning Network (AWN) systems: FSLTT alerting authorities may choose to integrate their local AWN systems that use Common Alerting Protocol (CAP) standards with the IPAWS infrastructure for interoperability.

5. Ensure compliance with technical requirements: There are no fees associated with connecting to IPAWS-OPEN, but alerting authorities must meet specific technical requirements and obtain a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with FEMA.

6. Utilize multiple alert distribution channels: The system supports sending alerts through the Emergency Alert System (EAS), Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA), National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Weather Radio All Hazards (NWR), and other public alerting systems, all from a single interface.

Important Considerations:
• CAP is an XML standard adopted by OASIS for structured information exchange.
• IPAWS provides a gateway to send alerts using multiple distribution channels based on geographic location, risks, and communication technologies.
IPAWS, alert system integration, CAP standards, emergency alerts, public warning systems
Integrate Local Alerting Systems with IPAWS-OPEN
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Verify eligibility: Ensure that your local alerting authority is designated as a Federal, State, Local, Tribal, or Territorial (FSLTT) public alerting authority.
2. Obtain MOA with FEMA: Secure a Memorandum of Agreement...
Procedure:

1. Verify eligibility: Ensure that your local alerting authority is designated as a Federal, State, Local, Tribal, or Territorial (FSLTT) public alerting authority.
2. Obtain MOA with FEMA: Secure a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) to connect with IPAWS-OPEN.
3. Adhere to technical requirements: Meet all technical specifications required for integration with IPAWS-OPEN, including compatibility with Common Alerting Protocol (CAP) standards.
4. Integrate local AWN systems: Connect your local Alert Warning Network (AWN) systems that use CAP standards to the IPAWS infrastructure.
5. Utilize multiple alert channels: Leverage IPAWS capabilities to send alerts through various distribution channels, such as Emergency Alert System (EAS), Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA), and NOAA Weather Radio All Hazards (NWR).
6. Ensure accessibility: Ensure that alerts are accessible to individuals with disabilities, access and functional needs, or limited English proficiency.
7. Maintain system resilience: Implement measures to ensure the security and resiliency of your alerting systems against external threats.
IPAWS, IPAWS-OPEN, CAP standards, EAS, WEA, NWR, alert integration, FEMA MOA
Understand the IPAWS Architecture and Functionality
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Review system components: Understand that IPAWS-OPEN is a centralized platform responsible for receiving, authenticating, and routing emergency alerts from alerting authorities to various communication pathways.
2. **Recognize...
Procedure:

1. Review system components: Understand that IPAWS-OPEN is a centralized platform responsible for receiving, authenticating, and routing emergency alerts from alerting authorities to various communication pathways.
2. Recognize infrastructure transition: Note that IPAWS-OPEN has transitioned to cloud provider facilities as of April 2021, enhancing its scalability and resiliency.
3. Familiarize with alert distribution channels: Recognize that IPAWS supports multiple alerting systems including EAS, WEA, NWR, and other public alerting systems from a single interface.
4. Understand interoperability standards: Be aware of the Common Alerting Protocol (CAP) standards used for enabling interoperability between dissimilar alert originators and distribution channels.
5. Review system capabilities: Understand that IPAWS provides advanced technologies, resiliency, and capabilities to public safety officials for sending alerts effectively.
6. Study system architecture: Review Figure 41 (IPAWS-OPEN and National Public Warning System [NPWS] Architecture) to understand the structure of the IPAWS infrastructure, including how messages are processed and distributed.
7. Follow legal guidance: Be aware that Executive Order (EO) 13407 from June 2006 provides fundamental guidance for the system's operation.
IPAWS architecture, CAP standards, EAS, WEA, NWR, alert distribution, IPAWS-OPEN, NPWS
Qualify Alert and Notification Systems Against NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Review the requirements outlined in NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1 to ensure alert and notification systems meet federal standards.
2. Conduct a technical evaluation of existing alert systems, including their reach, accuracy, and...
Procedure:

1. Review the requirements outlined in NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1 to ensure alert and notification systems meet federal standards.
2. Conduct a technical evaluation of existing alert systems, including their reach, accuracy, and reliability.
3. Identify any gaps or deficiencies in current systems that may hinder effective emergency communication.
4. Implement corrective actions based on the findings from the evaluation.
5. Document all steps taken to qualify the system against NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1 for audit and compliance purposes.
alert systems, FEMA, NUREG-0654, qualification

Emergency Broadcasting (12) -

Establish Pre-incident Social Media Presence for Emergency Communication
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure critical life safety messages are disseminated during a nuclear detonation scenario.

Procedure:
1. Invest in social media development now. Cultivate a verified social media presence and following before an incident to...
Objective: Ensure critical life safety messages are disseminated during a nuclear detonation scenario.

Procedure:
1. Invest in social media development now. Cultivate a verified social media presence and following before an incident to build credibility for that channel.
2. Predetermine communication channel options based on expected nuclear detonation impacts and preidentified preferred message channels.
3. Ensure your jurisdiction’s plans include a standard operating procedure (SOP) regarding safety instruction communication methods.
4. Prioritize platforms your constituents visit and use frequently. Make sure to publish safety messages across all the platforms your jurisdiction uses.
5. Publish alternate communication methods in preparedness campaigns, so people know where to find information if certain systems are down.
6. Structure specific communication pathway priorities around estimated impact zones (e.g., deployable cell towers or flyover messaging may be prioritized in the MDZ).
7. Incorporate procedures for communications staff to monitor and quickly correct conflicting or inaccurate information.
8. Engage subject matter experts to assess questionable messages and assist with drafting messages to counter or augment others.
social media, emergency communication, nuclear detonation, message dissemination, SOP, alternate communication methods
Implement Pre-Detonation A&W Planning and Alerting Procedures
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Issue a warning within tens of minutes before the detonation using Presidential messages from both the National Wireless Emergency Alert System (NAWAS) and the National Public Warning System (NPWS).
2. Follow with local alerting...
Procedure:

1. Issue a warning within tens of minutes before the detonation using Presidential messages from both the National Wireless Emergency Alert System (NAWAS) and the National Public Warning System (NPWS).
2. Follow with local alerting to send 90- and 360-character WEAs and EAS alerts as soon as possible.
3. Use pre-scripted message templates validated by technical experts and the IPAWS Program Management Office (PMO).

Details:
• Alerting authorities must be trained, proficient, and have up-to-date certificates with the IPAWS PMO.
• Workshops are held to refine WEA and EAS alert messages for future use.
pre-detonation,A&W planning,WEA,EAS,IPAWS PMO
Monitor and Correct Conflicting or Inaccurate Information During Emergencies
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure accurate information is disseminated during emergencies by monitoring and correcting conflicting messages.

Procedure:
1. Assign communications staff to monitor all public channels for misinformation, rumors, or...
Objective: Ensure accurate information is disseminated during emergencies by monitoring and correcting conflicting messages.

Procedure:
1. Assign communications staff to monitor all public channels for misinformation, rumors, or conflicting information.
2. Establish a rapid response team that includes subject matter experts (e.g., medical professionals, radiation specialists) who can assess questionable messages quickly.
3. Develop a process for drafting and disseminating corrective messages that counter inaccurate information or augment existing messages with additional details.
4. Ensure all communications are consistent across platforms to avoid confusion among the public.
5. Use verified sources and subject matter experts to validate all messages before they are released to ensure accuracy and credibility.
misinformation, subject matter experts, rapid response team, accurate communication, emergency messaging
Using NAWAS for AWN Messaging in Post-Detonation Scenarios
Critical Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Procedure:

1. Activate the AWN messaging system: Once a nuclear detonation has occurred, initiate the AWN messaging process through the FOC/FAOC.
2. Use NAWAS as the primary communication channel for transmitting AWN messages to...
Procedure:

1. Activate the AWN messaging system: Once a nuclear detonation has occurred, initiate the AWN messaging process through the FOC/FAOC.
2. Use NAWAS as the primary communication channel for transmitting AWN messages to SWPs.
3. SWPs relay the message to localities or the public using available communications according to their state EAS plan and the operational AWN distribution channels in local EOCs.
4. Ensure continuous messaging: During the Recovery phase, maintain ongoing communication with affected areas by continuously providing critical information through AWN.
5. Use technical factors for targeting:
- Analyze fallout wind vectors
- Use forecast plots to determine appropriate messaging areas
6. Prioritize public safety and awareness in all messages sent through the system.

Important Notes:
• NAWAS is essential for ensuring that AWN reaches affected populations quickly and effectively.
• Local EOCs must be prepared with operational distribution channels to ensure message delivery.
NAWAS,AWN,EAS,EOC,NORAD,FOC/FAOC
