Establish Methodology for EAS Message Rebroadcast
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Determine Frequency and Timing of Rebroadcasts:
- Establish the frequency (how many times) and interval (e.g., every 15 minutes) at which EAS messages will be rebroadcast.
- Ensure that rebroadcast intervals are...
Procedure:

1. Determine Frequency and Timing of Rebroadcasts:
- Establish the frequency (how many times) and interval (e.g., every 15 minutes) at which EAS messages will be rebroadcast.
- Ensure that rebroadcast intervals are appropriate for the nature of the incident and the urgency of the information being conveyed.

2. Define Procedures for Rebroadcasting:
- Develop clear procedures for rebroadcasting EAS messages, including who is responsible for initiating the rebroadcast and how it will be executed.
- Ensure that all relevant communication channels (e.g., radio, television) are prepared to rebroadcast messages as needed.

3. Coordinate with EAS Stations:
- Work closely with EAS stations to ensure they understand their role in rebroadcasting messages and have the necessary equipment and procedures in place.

4. Maintain Records of Rebroadcasts:
- Keep detailed records of all rebroadcasts, including the date, time, message content, and communication channels used.
EAS messages, emergency broadcasting, rebroadcast frequency, coordination with stations
Establish Process for Selecting and Modifying EAS Messages
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Define the Purpose of EAS Messages:
- Ensure that EAS messages are used to communicate critical information during radiological emergencies.
- Include instructions for protective actions as required by evaluation...
Procedure:

1. Define the Purpose of EAS Messages:
- Ensure that EAS messages are used to communicate critical information during radiological emergencies.
- Include instructions for protective actions as required by evaluation criterion E.4.

2. Develop a Process for Selecting and Modifying EAS Messages:
- Establish criteria for selecting which EAS messages will be sent based on the nature of the incident, urgency, and impact.
- Define procedures for modifying templates to reflect updated information (e.g., changes in ECL, dose rates, or protective actions).

3. Obtain Approval for Modified Messages:
- Ensure that all modified messages are reviewed and approved by relevant stakeholders before being sent to the EAS station(s) for broadcast.

4. Maintain a Record of Modifications:
- Keep detailed records of all modifications made to EAS message templates, including the date, reason for modification, and approving authority.
EAS messages, emergency broadcasting, message selection, approval process
Establish Provisions for Follow-Up Messages in EAS Communications
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Define the Purpose of Follow-Up Messages:
- Ensure that follow-up messages are used to provide updated information on incidents, including changes to the ECL, dose rates, or protective actions.

2. **Develop a Process for...
Procedure:

1. Define the Purpose of Follow-Up Messages:
- Ensure that follow-up messages are used to provide updated information on incidents, including changes to the ECL, dose rates, or protective actions.

2. Develop a Process for Generating and Sending Follow-Up Messages:
- Establish procedures for generating follow-up messages based on new or updated information from the incident.
- Define how these messages will be sent to EAS stations for broadcast (e.g., through direct communication, automated systems).

3. Coordinate with EAS Stations:
- Ensure that EAS stations are prepared to receive and broadcast follow-up messages as needed.
- Provide training or guidance to EAS station personnel on how to handle follow-up messages during emergencies.

4. Maintain Records of Follow-Up Messages:
- Keep detailed records of all follow-up messages, including the date, time, content, and communication channels used.
follow-up messages, EAS communications, emergency broadcasting, message records
Establish Provisions for Foreign Language Translations and Special News Broadcasts in EAS Communications
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Identify Target Languages and Communities:
- Determine which languages are required for translation based on the demographics of the affected population.
- Identify any special news broadcasts that may be necessary...
Procedure:

1. Identify Target Languages and Communities:
- Determine which languages are required for translation based on the demographics of the affected population.
- Identify any special news broadcasts that may be necessary (e.g., for individuals with disabilities or limited English proficiency).

2. Develop Translation Procedures:
- Establish procedures for translating EAS messages into foreign languages, including who is responsible for translations and how they will be reviewed and approved.
- Ensure that translations are accurate and convey the same critical information as the original message.

3. Coordinate with EAS Stations:
- Work with EAS stations to ensure they have the capability to broadcast translated messages in multiple languages.
- Provide training or guidance to EAS station personnel on how to handle multilingual broadcasts during emergencies.

4. Maintain Records of Translations and Broadcasts:
- Keep detailed records of all foreign language translations and special news broadcasts, including the date, time, content, and communication channels used.
foreign language translation, EAS communications, emergency broadcasting, accessibility
Leverage FEMA IPAWS WEAs for Emergency Alerts
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Use advanced alerting systems to deliver critical messages efficiently during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Utilize the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS) Wireless...
Objective: Use advanced alerting systems to deliver critical messages efficiently during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Utilize the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s Integrated Public Alert and Warning System (IPAWS) Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs).
2. Deliver alerts using Short Message Service-Cell Broadcast (SMS-CB), which sends one-to-many messages simultaneously to all devices in a specified area.
3. Ensure that WEAs are pre-configured with key safety instructions, such as shelter-in-place orders or evacuation routes.
4. Coordinate with local authorities and emergency management to ensure accurate targeting of alerts based on geographic zones.

Notes:
• SMS-CB avoids network congestion by not relying on individual device connections.
• WEAs should be tested regularly to ensure functionality during emergencies.
FEMA IPAWS, emergency alerts, SMS-CB
Predetermine Communication Channel Options for Nuclear Detonation Scenarios
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Identify and prepare communication channels based on expected nuclear detonation impacts.

Procedure:
1. Assess the impact zones (e.g., MDZ - Major Damage Zone) to understand which communication modes may be impaired.
2....
Objective: Identify and prepare communication channels based on expected nuclear detonation impacts.

Procedure:
1. Assess the impact zones (e.g., MDZ - Major Damage Zone) to understand which communication modes may be impaired.
2. Preidentify preferred message channels that are most likely to remain functional in each zone.
3. Develop a standard operating procedure (SOP) for safety instruction communication methods, tailored to the expected impact zones.
4. Ensure that all platforms used by your jurisdiction have been tested and verified before an incident occurs.
5. Include alternate communication methods in preparedness campaigns so the public knows where to find information if primary systems are down.
6. For areas with impaired communication (e.g., MDZ), prioritize deployable cell towers or flyover messaging as backup options.
nuclear detonation, communication channels, impact zones, deployable cell towers, flyover messaging
Utilize Alternative Communication Pathways in Post-Detonation Scenarios
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Identify and leverage alternative communication networks when primary infrastructure is damaged or unavailable.
2. Use satellite, broadcast (AM/FM radio), and other non-cellular pathways for sending alerts and warnings.
3....
Procedure:

1. Identify and leverage alternative communication networks when primary infrastructure is damaged or unavailable.
2. Use satellite, broadcast (AM/FM radio), and other non-cellular pathways for sending alerts and warnings.
3. Coordinate with state, regional, or national assets outside the HZ to perform A&W functions based on prior Memoranda of Agreement (MOA) or multi-jurisdictional agreements.

Details:
• Cell towers may be destroyed, leading to loss of internet and electrical power.
• IPAWS OPEN primary and cloud-based servers can still be used if internet access is available.
• Emphasize AM/FM radio broadcasts as a reliable communication method.
alternative networks,satellite,broadcast,IPAWS OPEN,MOA
Ensure Communication During Emergencies
Standard Source: Sweden In Case of Crisis or War
Procedure:

1. Keep a radio powered by batteries, solar panels, or a winding mechanism at home.
2. Store extra batteries for the radio.
3. Have a mobile phone and a charged power bank available.
4. Keep a car phone charger on...
Procedure:

1. Keep a radio powered by batteries, solar panels, or a winding mechanism at home.
2. Store extra batteries for the radio.
3. Have a mobile phone and a charged power bank available.
4. Keep a car phone charger on hand in case of power outages.
5. Write down important phone numbers on paper.
6. Use Sveriges Radio, channel P4, which is the official emergency information channel in Sweden.
7. Find the frequency for your local P4 channel at [krisinformation.se](https://www.krisinformation.se).
emergency communication,radio preparation,Sveriges Radio,P4 channel

Radio (11) -

Preparing for Pager System Failures in Emergencies
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Preparing for Pager System Failures During Emergencies:

1. Identify Vulnerabilities: Understand that pagers are vulnerable to power outages, network failures, and physical damage (e.g., storm damage to masts or antenna...
Procedure for Preparing for Pager System Failures During Emergencies:

1. Identify Vulnerabilities: Understand that pagers are vulnerable to power outages, network failures, and physical damage (e.g., storm damage to masts or antenna obstructions).
2. Have Backup Power Sources: Ensure that critical paging stations have backup battery systems capable of operating for 8-16 hours without mains power.
3. Maintain Redundant Communication Channels: Use pagers in conjunction with other communication methods (e.g., mobile phones, satellite systems) to ensure redundancy during emergencies.
4. Test Pager Systems Regularly: Conduct regular tests of the paging system and its components (transmitter, antenna, power supply) to identify potential failures before they occur.
5. Train Users on Alternative Procedures: Train users to follow alternative procedures if pagers fail, such as using mobile phones or other backup communication devices.
pager failure,backup power,emergency redundancy,test pager system
Understanding Pager Limitations in Emergencies
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Understanding Pager Limitations During Emergencies:

1. Recognize Coverage Restrictions: Understand that pagers rely on the coverage of a radio base station network. If you are outside the subscription area or in an area with...
Procedure for Understanding Pager Limitations During Emergencies:

1. Recognize Coverage Restrictions: Understand that pagers rely on the coverage of a radio base station network. If you are outside the subscription area or in an area with poor signal (e.g., underground, inside buildings), the pager may not receive alerts.
2. Be Aware of Power Outages: Pagers depend on the power and line services at the paging station. In case of a disaster that disrupts these services (e.g., storm damage to masts), pagers will not function until power is restored.
3. Acknowledge One-Way Communication: Understand that pagers are one-way devices. The sender cannot confirm whether the message was received, and repeated paging may be necessary if no response is received.
4. Prepare for Paging List Failures: Recognize that the paging station relies on a 'Paging list' from a control computer, which must be transmitted via telephone line. If lines are down, the station will not have the necessary information to send alerts.
5. Plan for Backup Communication: Since pagers may fail during emergencies (e.g., due to power outages or network failures), consider using them in conjunction with other communication systems like mobile phones or satellite networks.
pager limitations,emergency,power outage,coversagerestrictions,one way communication
Using Pagers for Callout Alerts
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Using Pagers in Callout Alert Situations:

1. Pre-arrange Alert Codes: Agree on specific alert codes or tones with the user to indicate different types of emergencies or actions required (e.g., a loud tone for urgent alerts,...
Procedure for Using Pagers in Callout Alert Situations:

1. Pre-arrange Alert Codes: Agree on specific alert codes or tones with the user to indicate different types of emergencies or actions required (e.g., a loud tone for urgent alerts, vibration for silent environments).
2. Set Up Meeting Points or Contact Numbers: Ensure that users know where to go or what phone number to call in response to an alert.
3. Use Multi-Tone Pagers if Available: If using advanced pagers with multiple tones, assign different tones to different types of alerts for quick identification.
4. Utilize Screen-Based Pagers: For models with screens, configure the system to display messages (up to 255 characters) that include phone numbers, radio callsigns, or detailed instructions.
5. Store Messages: Ensure that pagers are set up to store the last 10 messages in case the user misses an alert.
6. Respond Promptly: When alerted, users should respond according to pre-arranged protocols (e.g., go to a meeting point, call a number, or check the pager screen for instructions).
callout alert,pager usage,message storage,multitone pagers
Setting Up and Maintaining Communications in Cold Weather
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
### Setting Up and Maintaining Communications in Cold Weather

1. Select appropriate communication equipment that is rated for cold weather operations, including radios, antennas, and power supplies.
2. Install radio communication systems...
### Setting Up and Maintaining Communications in Cold Weather

1. Select appropriate communication equipment that is rated for cold weather operations, including radios, antennas, and power supplies.
2. Install radio communication systems with proper antenna placement to ensure optimal signal propagation despite environmental challenges such as auroral effects or ionospheric disturbances.
3. Use insulated cables and connectors for line communications to prevent freezing and signal degradation in cold environments.
4. Ensure batteries and power supplies are rated for low temperatures, and maintain backup power sources, such as generators or solar panels, if necessary.
5. Implement visual communication systems, such as signal flares or flags, as a backup during radio failure or extreme weather conditions.
6. Train personnel in peculiar operating procedures for cold weather communications, including handling mechanical malfunctions, microphone assemblies, and equipment affected by breathing and sweating.
7. Regularly inspect and maintain all communication systems, ensuring they are operational and free from damage caused by cold temperatures or environmental factors.
cold weather radio, antenna placement, battery maintenance, backup power sources
Setting Up a Stand-Alone Private Paging System
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Setting Up a Stand-Alone Private Paging System:

1. Select the Location: Choose a suitable location for the transmitter, ensuring that the antenna can cover the desired area (typically within a 10KM radius).
2. **Install...
Procedure for Setting Up a Stand-Alone Private Paging System:

1. Select the Location: Choose a suitable location for the transmitter, ensuring that the antenna can cover the desired area (typically within a 10KM radius).
2. Install Antenna: Mount the antenna in an elevated position to maximize coverage. Ensure it is clear of obstructions.
3. Connect Transmitter: Connect the transmitter to the antenna and power source. Use a reliable power supply, such as a battery or mains electricity.
4. Configure System: Set up the paging system to operate on a designated frequency. If required, obtain a radio licence from the appropriate authority.
5. Test Coverage: Test the coverage area by sending test pagers to users within the intended range. Adjust antenna position or transmitter settings as needed for optimal performance.
6. Ensure Independence: Confirm that the system is independent of local telecom networks and does not rely on external services for operation.
7. Maintain System: Regularly check the equipment for faults, ensure batteries are charged, and keep the antenna clear of obstructions.
pager,stand alone,private paging system,radiolicense,transmitter,antenna
Future Trends in Paging and Mobile Communication Integration
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Understanding Future Trends in Paging and Mobile Communication Integration:

1. Recognize Convergence of Technologies: Understand that future mobile phones may integrate paging capabilities, reducing the need for separate...
Procedure for Understanding Future Trends in Paging and Mobile Communication Integration:

1. Recognize Convergence of Technologies: Understand that future mobile phones may integrate paging capabilities, reducing the need for separate pagers.
2. Utilize SMS Services: Use Short Message Service (SMS) on GSM networks to send text-based alerts similar to pagers but with confirmation of message receipt and response capability.
3. Plan for Satellite-Based Paging Systems: Prepare for future satellite-based paging systems that offer global coverage, eliminating the need for local telecom services.
4. Ensure Compatibility with New Systems: Stay informed about new technologies (e.g., digital mobile phones) and ensure compatibility with existing communication protocols.
5. Maintain Dual Communication Systems: Continue to use pagers alongside other communication methods until satellite-based systems are fully implemented.
future paging,smartphone integration,satellite paging,SMS
Assemble a Low-Cost Radio Receiving Set for $5.00 or Less
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:

• A _crystal detector_ (e.g., the _Rasco baby_ detector from Radio Specialty Company)
• A _tuning coil_
• An _earphone_ (preferably a 1000 ohm resistance model like Mesco No. 470)
• A cardboard tube for the tuning coil
-...
Materials Required:

• A _crystal detector_ (e.g., the _Rasco baby_ detector from Radio Specialty Company)
• A _tuning coil_
• An _earphone_ (preferably a 1000 ohm resistance model like Mesco No. 470)
• A cardboard tube for the tuning coil
• _No. 40 Brown and Sharpe gauge plain enameled magnet wire_
• A _round wood-base switch_ with 7 contact points
• A thin, non-enameled diaphragm (5 cents)
• Binding posts
• A wooden base (1/2 or 1 inch thick, 3-1/2 inches wide and 5-1/2 inches long)
• A _ground connection_ (e.g., a water pipe, iron pipe driven into the ground, or a hydrant)
• An aerial wire or alternative (rain pipe not grounded, steel frame of an umbrella)

Steps to Assemble:

1. Assemble the Tuning Coil:
- Use a cardboard tube 2 inches in diameter and 3 inches long.
- Wind 250 turns of _No. 40 Brown and Sharpe gauge plain enameled magnet wire_ around the tube.
- At every 15th turn, scrape the wire and solder on a piece about 7 inches long (create 6 taps total).
- Bring the wires to the inside of the tube and out through one end.

2. Mount the Tuning Coil:
- Mount the coil on a wooden base as described above.
- Connect one end of the coil to one end point on the switch, and connect each tap to successive contact points on the switch (see Figs. 99 and 100 for reference).

3. Install the Crystal Detector:
- Mount the _Rasco baby_ detector on the wooden base.
- Ensure the cat-whisker wire is adjusted to rest lightly on the exposed part of the galena crystal.

4. Attach Earphone and Binding Posts:
- Screw two binding posts in front of the coil for connecting the earphone.

5. Connect Aerial and Ground:
- Connect the aerial wire (or alternative) to the lever on the switch.
- Connect the free end of the tuning coil to a ground connection.

6. Test the Set:
- Put on your earphone, adjust the detector, and move the lever over the switch contacts until it is in adjustment.
- If all connections are properly made, you should be able to pick up messages, music, or broadcasts from nearby stations.

Notes:
• For improved sensitivity, consider rewinding a Bell telephone receiver with _No. 40_ single covered silk magnet wire and using a thin, non-enameled diaphragm.
• If you use an ordinary earphone, it may not be as sensitive; consider purchasing a wireless-specific model like the Mesco No. 470 for better performance.
low-cost radio, crystal detector, tuning coil, earphone assembly, wireless set, $5.00 receiver
Assemble and Adjust a Crystal Detector for a Low-Cost Radio Receiving Set
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:

• A _Rasco baby_ crystal detector (or similar model)
• Galena crystal
• Phosphor-bronze wire (cat-whisker)
• Binding posts
• Screwdriver or small tools for adjustment

Steps to Assemble the Crystal Detector:

1. **Mount...
Materials Required:

• A _Rasco baby_ crystal detector (or similar model)
• Galena crystal
• Phosphor-bronze wire (cat-whisker)
• Binding posts
• Screwdriver or small tools for adjustment

Steps to Assemble the Crystal Detector:

1. Mount the Detector Base:
- The base is made of black composition and has a standard in which a rod slides.
- On one end of the rod, there is a hard rubber adjusting knob; on the other end, a thin piece of _phosphor-bronze wire_ (cat-whisker) is fixed.

2. Insert Galena Crystal:
- Unscrew the knurled cap at the base and place the galena crystal in the cavity of the post.
- Screw the cap back on securely to hold the crystal in place.

3. Adjust Cat-Whisker Wire:
- Adjust the free end of the cat-whisker wire so that it rests lightly on the exposed part of the galena crystal.
- This adjustment is crucial for proper signal reception and should be fine-tuned as needed.

Notes:
• If you are constructing your own detector, you can use a couple of binding posts, a bit of galena, and a piece of brass wire to create a simple version.
• Ensure that the cat-whisker is not too tight or loose on the crystal; it should make light contact for optimal performance.
crystal detector assembly, rasco baby detector, galena crystal setup
Connect and Test a Low-Cost Radio Receiving Set
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:

• Completed radio receiving set (assembled from previous steps)
• Aerial wire or alternative (rain pipe not grounded, steel frame of an umbrella)
• Ground connection (water pipe, iron pipe driven into the ground, hydrant)
-...
Materials Required:

• Completed radio receiving set (assembled from previous steps)
• Aerial wire or alternative (rain pipe not grounded, steel frame of an umbrella)
• Ground connection (water pipe, iron pipe driven into the ground, hydrant)
• Earphone (prepared as per earlier instructions)

Steps to Connect and Test the Set:

1. Connect Aerial Wire:
- Attach the aerial wire or alternative (e.g., a rain pipe not grounded or an umbrella frame) to the lever on the switch.

2. Ground Connection:
- Connect the free end of the tuning coil to a ground connection, such as a water pipe, iron pipe driven into the ground, or a hydrant.

3. Attach Earphone:
- Connect the earphone to the binding posts on the wooden base.

4. Adjust and Test the Set:
- Put on your earphone and adjust the crystal detector by moving the lever over the switch contacts until it is in adjustment.
- If all connections are properly made, you should be able to pick up Morse code messages, music, or broadcasts from nearby stations.

Notes:
• Ensure that all connections are secure and properly insulated to avoid interference or short circuits.
• For best results, test the set in an area with minimal electrical noise and good signal strength.
radio testing, aerial connection, ground setup, wireless set tuning
Select and Prepare an Earphone for a Low-Cost Radio Receiving Set
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:

• An earphone (preferably a wireless-specific model like Mesco No. 470)
• Thin, non-enameled diaphragm (5 cents)
• _No. 40_ single covered silk magnet wire or enameled wire of the same size
• Soldering tools and wire...
Materials Required:

• An earphone (preferably a wireless-specific model like Mesco No. 470)
• Thin, non-enameled diaphragm (5 cents)
• _No. 40_ single covered silk magnet wire or enameled wire of the same size
• Soldering tools and wire stripper

Steps to Prepare the Earphone:

1. Choose an Appropriate Earphone:
- An ordinary Bell telephone receiver is not ideal for wireless work due to its low resistance and thick diaphragm.
- If you have a Bell phone, consider rewinding it with _No. 40_ single covered silk magnet wire or enameled wire of the same size to improve sensitivity.

2. Replace Diaphragm:
- Replace the existing diaphragm with a thin, non-enameled one (5 cents) for better vibration and sound quality.

3. Purchase a Wireless-Specific Earphone:
- Consider buying a model like Mesco No. 470, which is wound to 1000 ohms resistance and includes a cord.
- For an additional $1.00, you can purchase a head-band for the earphone, making it more comfortable and practical (see Fig. 98).

Notes:
• Ensure that the diaphragm is not enameled, as this may dampen vibrations.
• If using a rewound Bell phone, ensure all connections are properly insulated to avoid short circuits.
earphone selection, wireless earphone setup, mesco no. 470
Use a Condenser to Improve Receiving Quality in a Low-Cost Radio Set
Reference Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Materials Required:

• A condenser (capacitor)
• Soldering tools and wire stripper

Steps to Use the Condenser:

1. Connect the Condenser:
- You are not required to connect a condenser across the earphone, but doing so will improve...
Materials Required:

• A condenser (capacitor)
• Soldering tools and wire stripper

Steps to Use the Condenser:

1. Connect the Condenser:
- You are not required to connect a condenser across the earphone, but doing so will improve the receiving qualities of the receptor.
- Connect the condenser in parallel with the earphone for optimal performance.

2. Ensure Proper Wiring:
- Ensure that all connections are secure and properly insulated to avoid interference or short circuits.

Notes:
• A condenser helps filter out noise and improve signal clarity, making it easier to receive weak signals.
• If you do not have a condenser available, the set will still function but may be less sensitive.
condenser use, capacitor for radio, signal improvement

Radio Licensing (10) -

Obtain Licenses for Radio Communication Equipment
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Step 1: Contact the national post and telecommunications office of the country where you plan to operate your radio equipment. For example, in Zaire, this would be the Office National des Postes et Telecommunication du Zaire (ONPTZ).

**Step...
Step 1: Contact the national post and telecommunications office of the country where you plan to operate your radio equipment. For example, in Zaire, this would be the Office National des Postes et Telecommunication du Zaire (ONPTZ).

Step 2: Apply for a licence through the appropriate government body, which handles technical aspects of radio communication.

Step 3: Seek approval from state security if required. This is an additional step that may be necessary depending on the country's regulations.

Step 4: Ensure you have obtained all necessary licences before using your radio equipment. Failure to do so can result in legal consequences or interference with your operations.
radio licensing,state security approval,telecom office,application process
Obtain Type Approval for Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure radio equipment meets local technical standards before import.

Steps:
1. Inquire if your country requires 'type approval' for specific radios.
2. Check which types of radios have already received type approval to avoid...
Objective: Ensure radio equipment meets local technical standards before import.

Steps:
1. Inquire if your country requires 'type approval' for specific radios.
2. Check which types of radios have already received type approval to avoid delays.
3. If required, submit the complete technical specification of the radio to the authorities for review.
4. Note that previously approved models may expedite the process.
type approval, radio equipment standards, technical specifications
Research Licensing Conditions Before Purchasing a Radio
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Gather information about radio licensing requirements before purchasing equipment.

Steps:
1. Contact local authorities or licensed groups for details on licensing conditions.
2. Identify if there is a central body that assists...
Objective: Gather information about radio licensing requirements before purchasing equipment.

Steps:
1. Contact local authorities or licensed groups for details on licensing conditions.
2. Identify if there is a central body that assists with licence applications (e.g., L'Eglise de Christ du Zaire in Zaire).
3. Be prepared to fill out multiple forms and provide detailed information, including:
- Technical specifications of the radio
- Circuit diagrams
- Maps and plans for your network
4. Do not purchase or import radios until you have obtained a licence.
5. Note that authorities may deny permission or impose restrictions on the type of equipment used.
radio licence, licensing conditions, radio regulations, communications authority
Understand Frequency and Network Limitations Imposed by Licensing Authorities
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Be aware of potential restrictions on radio equipment and network configurations.

Steps:
1. If you plan to use a synthesised transceiver (capable of operating from 2–30 MHz), be prepared for possible limitations:
- You may...
Objective: Be aware of potential restrictions on radio equipment and network configurations.

Steps:
1. If you plan to use a synthesised transceiver (capable of operating from 2–30 MHz), be prepared for possible limitations:
- You may only be allowed to use frequencies explicitly approved by the licensing authority.
2. For large networks, such as one with twenty stations, expect potential restrictions:
- Authorities may require dividing the network into smaller groups (e.g., four groups of five stations).
- Each group must have a principal station that communicates directly with HQ.
3. Subsidiary stations in each group may be limited to lower power outputs (e.g., 25–40 watts) depending on local conditions.
frequency limitations, network configuration, radio power restrictions
Understand Licence Fee Requirements
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Be aware of the financial obligations associated with radio licensing.

Steps:
1. Research the annual licence fee for transmitting stations in your country.
2. Note that fees vary significantly (e.g., £25 to £75 per station in...
Objective: Be aware of the financial obligations associated with radio licensing.

Steps:
1. Research the annual licence fee for transmitting stations in your country.
2. Note that fees vary significantly (e.g., £25 to £75 per station in some African countries as of 1987).
3. Understand that operating a transmitter without a current, valid licence is illegal and may result in penalties.
radio licence fee, annual fees, radio regulations
Obtain Amateur Radio Operator License - Element 2 Exam
Standard Source: Survival Communications Primer
Procedure:
1. Register for the amateur radio operator license exam session.
2. Pay the single session fee of $14.00 to take as many tests (elements) as desired during that session.
3. Pass Element 2 to be licensed as an amateur radio...
Procedure:
1. Register for the amateur radio operator license exam session.
2. Pay the single session fee of $14.00 to take as many tests (elements) as desired during that session.
3. Pass Element 2 to be licensed as an amateur radio operator.
4. If any test is failed, retake it by paying the $14.00 fee again for a new session.

Details:
• Element 2 is required for obtaining a technician license.
• Additional elements (elements 3 and 4) are available for higher-level licenses (General and Extra).
• Study resources include free materials from [FRRL Education](http://www.frrl.org/education/) and the July 1, 2006 Element 2 question pool at [NCVEC Question Pool](http://www.ncvec.org/downloads/2006tech.pdf).
amateur radio license, element 2 exam, technician license, radio operator, communications license
Obtain a CB Radio Licence
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Contact your local Post Office or telecommunications authority.
2. Apply for a Citizens Band (CB) radio licence, which is typically renewable annually.
3. Ensure the licence allows you to set up a radio transmitter that conforms to...
Procedure:
1. Contact your local Post Office or telecommunications authority.
2. Apply for a Citizens Band (CB) radio licence, which is typically renewable annually.
3. Ensure the licence allows you to set up a radio transmitter that conforms to national regulations.

Notes:
• This procedure applies primarily in countries with established CB bands such as the USA and UK.
• In some regions, using CB may be illegal due to lack of allocated frequencies.
CB licence,application,post office,telecom authority
Obtain a GMRS License for Personal Use
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Steps to Obtain a GMRS License:
1. Apply as an individual: Only personal licensees are eligible for a GMRS license. Organizations, clubs, and businesses cannot obtain a GMRS license.
2. Pay the license fee: A renewable license is valid...
Steps to Obtain a GMRS License:
1. Apply as an individual: Only personal licensees are eligible for a GMRS license. Organizations, clubs, and businesses cannot obtain a GMRS license.
2. Pay the license fee: A renewable license is valid for five years upon payment of the required fee.
3. License covers immediate family members: The GMRS license extends to all members of the licensee’s immediate family, even if they do not live in the same household.
4. Responsibility for equipment operation: The licensee is responsible for ensuring that all radio equipment used as part of their system is operated properly and in compliance with FCC regulations.
GMRS license,application,individual,family coverage,equipment responsibility
Study Resources for Amateur Radio License Exams
Standard Source: Survival Communications Primer
Procedure:
1. Access free study resources from [FRRL Education](http://www.frrl.org/education/) to prepare for elements 1, 2, and 3.
2. Review the July 1, 2006 Element 2 test question pool at [NCVEC Question...
Procedure:
1. Access free study resources from [FRRL Education](http://www.frrl.org/education/) to prepare for elements 1, 2, and 3.
2. Review the July 1, 2006 Element 2 test question pool at [NCVEC Question Pool](http://www.ncvec.org/downloads/2006tech.pdf) for practice.
3. For General and Extra exams, use the ARRL License Manuals available at [ARRL Catalog](http://www.arrl.org/catalog/lm/).
4. Consider using Q&A books that cover question pools (available for free online), though they may not fully explain theory behind operating modes and rules.
5. Take practice tests from [QRZ Testing Page](http://www.qrz.com/p/testing.pl) for elements 2, 3, and/or 4.

Details:
• The ARRL License Manuals are recommended for in-depth understanding of radio theory and regulations.
• Practice tests on QRZ can help simulate real exam conditions.
study materials, radio license exams, ARRL manuals, practice tests, amateur radio study
Obtaining a GMRS License for Two-Way Radios
Reference Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Procedure:

1. Determine if you need a license:
- If using an FRS-only radio, no license is required.
- If using an FRS/GMRS radio, a GMRS license from the FCC is required.
2. Apply for a GMRS license:
- Visit the FCC's Universal...
Procedure:

1. Determine if you need a license:
- If using an FRS-only radio, no license is required.
- If using an FRS/GMRS radio, a GMRS license from the FCC is required.
2. Apply for a GMRS license:
- Visit the FCC's Universal Licensing System (http://wireless.fcc.gov/uls/) to apply online.
- Alternatively, manually file FCC Form 605 with the FCC.
3. Pay the licensing fee:
- As of 2006, the fee for a five-year renewable GMRS license is $80.
4. No test is required:
- Unlike some other radio licenses, no exam or test is needed to obtain a GMRS license.
5. Keep your license on file:
- Ensure all users of GMRS radios have access to the license documentation for compliance with FCC regulations.
GMRS license application, FCC licensing, radio regulations, two-way radio compliance

Radio Communication (10) -

Erect Antennae in Arctic Conditions
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand that erecting antennae is difficult in the north due to frozen ground, which makes it hard to drive antenna ground, guy pegs, and ground plate rods.
2. Exercise care when handling lead-in and metal mounts as they...
Procedure:

1. Understand that erecting antennae is difficult in the north due to frozen ground, which makes it hard to drive antenna ground, guy pegs, and ground plate rods.
2. Exercise care when handling lead-in and metal mounts as they become brittle in cold temperatures.
3. Prefer vertical antennae for ground-wave propagation on HF bands.
4. Avoid using fractional wavelength whip antennae except for short distances.
5. Utilize antenna towers installed on lake or sea ice with toggles deployed through holes drilled in the ice for guy anchors.
6. Use underlying fresh or sea water as a predictable electrical ground.

Warnings:
• Frozen ground and brittle materials increase risk of damage during installation.
• Ensure proper insulation and handling to prevent breakage.
arctic antenna installation, frozen ground, vertical antenna, HF propagation
Maintain and Protect Antennae from Environmental Factors in Arctic Conditions
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Equip all large horizontal antennae with counterweights to prevent wires or poles from breaking due to pressure from ice or wind.
2. Remove wet snow and sleet freezing to the antennae by jarring the supports.
3. Understand...
Procedure:

1. Equip all large horizontal antennae with counterweights to prevent wires or poles from breaking due to pressure from ice or wind.
2. Remove wet snow and sleet freezing to the antennae by jarring the supports.
3. Understand that ground conductivity in northern areas is generally low, especially over ice caps, permafrost, and snow-covered terrain.
4. Recognize that HF and below frequencies have reduced effective groundwave ranges over poor conductive terrain.
5. Ensure antennae efficiency by securing the best available ground or building an artificial grounding system if necessary.
6. Never connect more than one transmitter to a single ground or counterpoise.
7. Avoid connecting electrical noise-producing items (e.g., DC battery-charging generators, metal-walled huts) to a receiver ground system.

Warnings:
• Poor conductivity and environmental factors can severely impact antenna performance.
• Improper grounding may lead to equipment failure or interference.
antenna maintenance, arctic conductivity, groundwave propagation, counterweights
Respond to Ionospheric Disturbances in Arctic Areas
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand that solar storms and disturbances affect the ionosphere globally, but are especially severe in arctic areas.
2. Recognize that polar cap absorption, caused by solar flare activity, can lead to blackouts on sky wave...
Procedure:

1. Understand that solar storms and disturbances affect the ionosphere globally, but are especially severe in arctic areas.
2. Recognize that polar cap absorption, caused by solar flare activity, can lead to blackouts on sky wave and scatter systems for several hours, and on HF during severe disturbances for several days.
3. Be aware that these effects may be magnified when they coincide with periods of maximum auroral activity.
4. Note that predictions of disturbed conditions in polar areas are only possible in general terms, not specific to time or location.

Warnings:
• Communication systems relying on HF and sky wave propagation should have backup plans for blackouts.
• During solar storms, expect reduced or no communication capabilities over HF frequencies.
ionospheric disturbance, solar storm, polar cap absorption, HF communication, arctic radio
Select and Deploy Ground-Plane or Dipole Antennae in Arctic Conditions
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Consider the use of ground-plane antennae and dipoles for tactical radio in every operational plan.
2. Use telescopic type ground-plane antenna masts to increase the range of tactical radio sets.
3. Ensure that the...
Procedure:

1. Consider the use of ground-plane antennae and dipoles for tactical radio in every operational plan.
2. Use telescopic type ground-plane antenna masts to increase the range of tactical radio sets.
3. Ensure that the antenna is positioned at a minimum height of 6 meters (20 feet) above ground level to counter the effects of highly charged particles from blowing snow in arctic conditions.
4. Command vehicles equipped with tactical radios should have facilities for using either a ground-plane antenna or a dipole antenna mounted on an elevated mast or tower.

Warnings:
• Failure to elevate antennas may result in reduced communication range and interference from charged particles.
• Ensure that all antenna systems are compatible with the environment and mission requirements.
ground-plane antenna, dipole antenna, arctic communication range, tactical radio
Identify and Mitigate Atmospheric Static in Arctic Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand that continuous high-level static is rare in arctic latitudes but sporadic noise is common.
2. Recognize that irregularly occurring and steady rushes of increasing noise may indicate auroral disturbances on the...
Procedure:

1. Understand that continuous high-level static is rare in arctic latitudes but sporadic noise is common.
2. Recognize that irregularly occurring and steady rushes of increasing noise may indicate auroral disturbances on the frequency being used.
3. Note that low frequency (LF) is less affected by atmospheric noise than HF.
4. Be aware that precipitation static can occur due to highly charged snow flakes or pellets during high winds, especially in northern areas.
5. Understand that charged particles of snow driven against metal vehicles, masts, and antennae may discharge with a high-pitched static sound audible on all frequencies.
6. Recognize that this form of noise is more commonly encountered on aircraft radio and vehicle-mounted stations than ground stations.
7. Be alert to the possibility of very heavy shocks from the ground lead in antennae subject to high winds or blowing snow, even if audible static is not great.

Warnings:
• Static can interfere with communication clarity and may indicate underlying atmospheric disturbances.
• Ensure equipment is shielded or grounded properly to mitigate effects.
atmospheric static, precipitation static, arctic radio noise, auroral disturbance
Understand Normal Working Range for Vehicle-Mounted Tactical Radio Sets in Arctic Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Recognize that the normal working range for vehicle-mounted tactical radio sets is 32–40 km (20–25 miles).
2. Be aware that this range may be affected by environmental factors such as ionospheric disturbances, atmospheric...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that the normal working range for vehicle-mounted tactical radio sets is 32–40 km (20–25 miles).
2. Be aware that this range may be affected by environmental factors such as ionospheric disturbances, atmospheric static, and poor ground conductivity in arctic regions.
3. Ensure that communication systems have backup plans for reduced or no communication capabilities during severe weather or solar storms.

Warnings:
• Relying solely on normal working ranges can lead to unexpected communication failures in extreme conditions.
• Always carry alternative communication methods and equipment.
tactical radio range, arctic communication distance, vehicle-mounted radio
Implement Pactor Communication System for Humanitarian Aid
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
### Implement Pactor Communication System for Humanitarian Aid

Overview:
The Pactor system is a highly successful development from the TOR systems, combining features of both TOR and Packet systems. It was invented by German radio amateurs who...
### Implement Pactor Communication System for Humanitarian Aid

Overview:
The Pactor system is a highly successful development from the TOR systems, combining features of both TOR and Packet systems. It was invented by German radio amateurs who wanted to exchange computer data files and found the limited character set of AMTOR too restrictive.

Advantages of Pactor:
• Full ASCII Character Set Support: Pactor can transmit the entire ASCII character set, including upper case and lower case letters, as well as all symbols on a computer keyboard. This allows for the transmission of files generated by electronic mailing systems, spreadsheets, or even programs themselves.
• Faster Data Transfer Rates: Pactor transfers data four times faster than TOR at a rate of 200 Bauds (with a version also available at 800 Bauds). This is still relatively slow compared to PSTN lines and modems but significantly faster than TOR.
• Advanced Error Correction Systems: More advanced error correction systems are used, ensuring that messages arrive without corruption even over poor circuits.

Special Version for Humanitarian Aid:
A special version of Pactor was developed for humanitarian aid organizations, known as Swiss-PTC (Pactor Controller). This version is designed to prevent unintended listeners from intercepting information not intended for them. It became a big hit and generated high demand for traffic.

Implementation Steps:
1. Select the Appropriate Version of Pactor: Choose between standard Pactor or the Swiss-PTC version depending on whether you are part of a humanitarian aid organization or not.
2. Ensure Compatibility with Existing Systems: The system is compatible with ordinary SITOR and can automatically recognize and select the correct system to use.
3. Use Private Frequencies: Humanitarian aid organizations, such as the ICRC, IFRC, and UNHCR, use private frequencies for Pactor communication to prevent unauthorized interception of information.
4. Integrate into Automated Electronic Mail Systems: Pactor is part of the IFRC automated electronic mail system, allowing Red Cross offices in areas without data services to access the electronic mailing system in Geneva without the aid of radio operators.

Important Considerations:
• Security and Privacy: Ensure that messages are transmitted securely to prevent unauthorized interception. This is especially important for humanitarian aid organizations operating in tense environments.
• Cost Efficiency: Using private frequencies eliminates the need to pay for data services, making Pactor a cost-effective solution for communication in remote areas.

Example of Unauthorized Interception:
In one case, a journalist intercepted private remarks from a Red Cross field team using AMTOR equipment. The contents of the message were used to write a newspaper report that caused a diplomatic incident and resulted in significant costs due to the need to relocate individuals mentioned in the article.

Conclusion:
Pactor is now the preferred system for humanitarian aid organizations, offering fast, secure, and reliable communication over radio frequencies.
pactor, swiss-ptc, humanitarian aid, ascii character set, error correction, data transfer rates, private frequencies, red cross, icrc, ifrc, unhcr
Set Up VHF Communication Network
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Setting Up a VHF Communication Network

1. Assess Requirements: Determine the communication needs of your network, including range, number of users, and geographic coverage.
2. Consult a Radio Engineer: Work with a...
Procedure for Setting Up a VHF Communication Network

1. Assess Requirements: Determine the communication needs of your network, including range, number of users, and geographic coverage.
2. Consult a Radio Engineer: Work with a qualified radio engineer to design a plan that meets your specific requirements and selects appropriate equipment.
3. Select VHF Frequencies: Choose frequencies within the 70-150 MHz region, which are commonly used for VHF communication.
4. Install Transmitters and Receivers: Set up transceivers with 25 watt power output to achieve a range of up to 80 km in open terrain.
5. Use Line-of-Sight Principle: Ensure that there is a clear line of sight between transmitting and receiving antennas for optimal communication.
6. Install Relay Stations (Repeaters): Place unattended automatic relay stations on elevated positions such as hills or tall buildings to extend the range beyond the horizon and around obstacles.
7. Test Communication: Verify that all devices can communicate within an 80 km radius of the repeater, allowing for extended coverage even if direct line-of-sight is not possible between distant stations.

Notes:
• VHF communication is limited to line-of-sight or relay-assisted range.
• Relay stations significantly increase the effective coverage area.
VHF communication, radio network setup, line of sight, relay station, VHF repeater
Understand Amateur Radio Capabilities and Limitations
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Understanding Amateur Radio Usage

1. Identify Frequency Range: Amateur radio operates on frequencies from 1.8 MHz to 10,000 MHz, allowing global communication including satellite use.
2. Determine Communication Types:...
Procedure for Understanding Amateur Radio Usage

1. Identify Frequency Range: Amateur radio operates on frequencies from 1.8 MHz to 10,000 MHz, allowing global communication including satellite use.
2. Determine Communication Types: Supports various modes such as voice (normal speech), television, teletype, and computer-to-computer communication.
3. Check Licensing Requirements: Research the licensing process in your country. Most countries require passing examinations before obtaining a license.
4. Understand Legal Restrictions: Be aware of legal limitations on what communications can be made. Some countries restrict amateurs from speaking directly to non-amateurs or connecting others via radio.
5. Cooperate During Emergencies: Amateurs are expected to assist in disaster and relief operations, working with the International Red Cross and government departments when needed.
6. Consider Global Use: Many expatriates use amateur radio for regular communication with friends and family abroad.
7. Be Aware of Country-Specific Rules: Some countries impose strict restrictions or even ban amateur radio entirely.

Notes:
• Licensing requirements vary significantly by country.
• Amateur radio is not intended for commercial use.
amateur radio, licensing, communication modes, emergency response, international regulations
Evaluate Costs for Establishing a Radio Network
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Evaluating the Cost of Setting Up a Radio Network

1. Estimate Capital Costs: Include the cost of transceivers, aerials, power sources, and other equipment.
2. Account for Shipping and Customs: Factor in shipping costs,...
Procedure for Evaluating the Cost of Setting Up a Radio Network

1. Estimate Capital Costs: Include the cost of transceivers, aerials, power sources, and other equipment.
2. Account for Shipping and Customs: Factor in shipping costs, insurance, and customs duties when importing equipment.
3. Include Installation Expenses: Budget for professional installation services to ensure proper setup and functionality.
4. Calculate Recurring Costs: Consider ongoing expenses such as operating the network, maintenance of equipment, and licensing fees.
5. Assess Long-Term Financial Impact: Understand that initial costs may be higher than expected due to additional components like test equipment or modifications.
6. Review Equipment Specifications: Choose transceivers with appropriate power requirements, ease of use, and future availability for long-term reliability.

Notes:
• Initial costs can exceed the price of a single transceiver due to associated expenses.
• Recurring costs should be considered when planning for long-term network operation.
radio network cost, capital expenditure, recurring expenses, equipment budgeting

Equipment Maintenance (10) -

Handle Insulated Wire and Cable in Subzero Temperatures
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the effects of subzero temperatures on insulated wire and cable:
- Insulation tends to become stiff and brittle, increasing the risk of cracking.
2. Use special cold-weather type electrical insulating tape for...
Procedure:

1. Understand the effects of subzero temperatures on insulated wire and cable:
- Insulation tends to become stiff and brittle, increasing the risk of cracking.
2. Use special cold-weather type electrical insulating tape for splicing field wire and cables.
3. Avoid using standard friction and rubber tapes in extreme cold as they may lose adhesiveness.
4. Protect hands with mittens or gloves when handling insulated wire and cable, even though this may restrict dexterity.
insulated cable, cold weather maintenance, splicing, insulation
Maintain Radio Equipment in Extreme Cold Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the impact of extreme cold on radio equipment:
- Receivers and transmitters adjusted for operations in a relatively warm area may exhibit changes in their frequency determining circuits, impairing operation.
2....
Procedure:

1. Understand the impact of extreme cold on radio equipment:
- Receivers and transmitters adjusted for operations in a relatively warm area may exhibit changes in their frequency determining circuits, impairing operation.
2. Ensure that radio operators are trained to make frequent checks for proper frequency setting (if the type of equipment allows).
3. Monitor battery voltage regularly, as low battery voltage can have a detrimental effect on performance.
4. Keep radios operational whenever possible to avoid temperature-related malfunctions.
radio maintenance, extreme cold, frequency settings, battery voltage
Maintain Rubber and Rubber-Like Compounds in Extreme Cold Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the effects of cold on rubber and rubber-like compounds:
- These materials become increasingly stiff and brittle at low temperatures.
2. When handling cordage, flex it slowly and carefully to minimize breakage.
3....
Procedure:

1. Understand the effects of cold on rubber and rubber-like compounds:
- These materials become increasingly stiff and brittle at low temperatures.
2. When handling cordage, flex it slowly and carefully to minimize breakage.
3. Warm rubber items before flexing them if feasible.
4. Power cables and co-axial cable transmission lines should be warmed before being laid in the cold.
5. Avoid contact of rubber items with fuel and lubricants where possible.
rubber maintenance, cold weather, cable handling, insulation
Maintain and Store Batteries in Cold Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the effects of cold on batteries:
- Dry batteries should be stored for issue at temperatures between -12°C and 2°C.
2. Warm batteries slowly to 21°C before use if they have been exposed to lower ambient...
Procedure:

1. Understand the effects of cold on batteries:
- Dry batteries should be stored for issue at temperatures between -12°C and 2°C.
2. Warm batteries slowly to 21°C before use if they have been exposed to lower ambient temperatures.
3. Batteries that have been permitted to cold soak require a longer heating period before being ready for use.
4. Avoid exposing batteries to extreme cold for prolonged periods to prevent damage and ensure optimal performance.
battery storage, cold weather maintenance, battery warming
Maintenance and Care of Communications Equipment in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
### Maintenance and Care of Communications Equipment in Cold Weather

• Keep equipment in the best possible operating condition by organizing a thorough and comprehensive preventive maintenance program.
• Take precautions to prevent damage to...
### Maintenance and Care of Communications Equipment in Cold Weather

• Keep equipment in the best possible operating condition by organizing a thorough and comprehensive preventive maintenance program.
• Take precautions to prevent damage to equipment due to moisture condensation. Cold equipment should be wrapped in a blanket or parka before being brought into a heated shelter and allowed to warm gradually.
cold weather, communications equipment, maintenance, moisture condensation
Prevent Mechanical Malfunctions in Communication Equipment Due to Contraction
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the causes of mechanical malfunctions due to contraction:
- Different metals contract at different rates when temperature falls.
- Components such as plugs, keys, jacks, valves, shafts, bearings, and dials are...
Procedure:

1. Understand the causes of mechanical malfunctions due to contraction:
- Different metals contract at different rates when temperature falls.
- Components such as plugs, keys, jacks, valves, shafts, bearings, and dials are subject to malfunctioning because of differences in rates of contraction.
2. Take preventive measures by ensuring that equipment is designed or maintained with materials that have similar thermal expansion coefficients where possible.
3. Regularly inspect and maintain components prone to contraction issues.
mechanical malfunctions, contraction, communication equipment, cold weather maintenance
Prevent Temperature-Induced Malfunctions in Radio Equipment
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the impact of rapid temperature changes on radio equipment:
- Rapid changes in temperature can result in malfunctions or failures of most electrical or electronic equipment.
2. Avoid shutting down radio sets whenever...
Procedure:

1. Understand the impact of rapid temperature changes on radio equipment:
- Rapid changes in temperature can result in malfunctions or failures of most electrical or electronic equipment.
2. Avoid shutting down radio sets whenever possible to prevent sudden temperature changes.
3. If shutdown is necessary, ensure that radios are enclosed and insulated to guard against sudden changes in temperature.
4. When moving cold equipment into warm environments, wrap it in blankets or sleeping bags to avoid moisture condensation and frost formation.
temperature changes, radio maintenance, insulation, moisture prevention
Protect Microphone Assemblies from Freezing Moisture
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Understand the impact of moisture on microphone assemblies:
- Moisture from breath freezes on presser switches and perforated cover plates, rendering equipment inoperative.
2. Use microphone covers to prevent moisture...
Procedure:

1. Understand the impact of moisture on microphone assemblies:
- Moisture from breath freezes on presser switches and perforated cover plates, rendering equipment inoperative.
2. Use microphone covers to prevent moisture ingress.
3. If microphone covers are unavailable, carry the microphone inside the parka close to the body for insulation.
4. Consider using prophylactics as an expedient solution for microphone covers.
microphone protection, moisture freezing, cold weather maintenance
Operating Communication Equipment in Extreme Cold
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Use Standard Communication Equipment with Precautions:
- Standard types of communication equipment can function effectively at very low temperatures if properly winterized and maintained.

2. **Include Winterization in...
Procedure:

1. Use Standard Communication Equipment with Precautions:
- Standard types of communication equipment can function effectively at very low temperatures if properly winterized and maintained.

2. Include Winterization in Operational Plans:
- Ensure that operational plans include special maintenance requirements for cold weather operations.

3. Install and Operate Equipment in Warm Shelters:
- Signal communications equipment should be installed and operated in warm shelters to ensure optimal performance.
- Warm shelters are essential for maintenance personnel working on communication systems.

4. Keep Equipment Warm and Dry:
- Follow winterization instructions closely to maintain the best possible performance of communication equipment.
- Ensure that all equipment remains warm and dry at all times.
cold weather communications, equipment maintenance, winterization
Proper Handling and Maintenance of Communication Equipment
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Proper Handling and Maintenance of Communication Equipment

Operators must be made aware that the equipment they are using needs to be handled carefully. It is often expensive and sensitive but used properly, most modern communication equipment...
Proper Handling and Maintenance of Communication Equipment

Operators must be made aware that the equipment they are using needs to be handled carefully. It is often expensive and sensitive but used properly, most modern communication equipment should stand up to daily use for a long time. In addition, the operator may be asked to do some routine maintenance (see para. 8.6).

Controls
• Controls and knobs should only be adjusted when necessary.
• Operators waiting their turn to transmit should not fiddle with the controls turning them first one way then another.
• Knobs and indicator lights have worked loose and been broken because of this kind of treatment.
• The rule must be to touch the controls only when necessary, e.g., to hear more clearly or to change channels.

Microphones
• Microphones are delicate instruments and have often been treated badly.
• They should not be pulled to reach across a room for someone else to speak, which can result in broken connections.
• Only the operator or the person sitting in his chair does the speaking.
• In the case of a desk mike, it must be left on the desk or the table close to the transceiver.
• It may be wisest to have it fixed in position on the desk or table and it should always be left connected to the transceiver.
• Hand held mikes are very often passed from hand to hand or left swinging loose on the end of their cables, which can cause wear and breakage.
• They are best kept for use with mobile equipment or as a spare microphone.

Cables and Aerials
• Cables and leads such as those coming from the power source and from the aerial should not be allowed to become tangled up or twisted into knots.
• They should be handled with care, the same as the rest of the equipment.
• Watch the plugs or connections on the ends.
• When disconnecting an aerial cable, make sure that the coupling ring (see Fig. 6.5.) has been fully unscrewed before pulling it from the socket.
• Never remove a cable by pulling it; hold on to the connector on its end while removing it.
• Be careful not to cross thread the connectors.
• If there is more than one aerial, label each aerial cable with the frequency and channel number it is used with.
• If there is an aerial switch in use, leave the aerials connected to the switch all the time.
• Disconnect the transceiver from the aerials by using the connecting cable between the transceiver and the switch.
• This cable is best connected and disconnected at the aerial switch rather than from the transceiver socket.
• The actual aerial should be checked visually regularly but particularly after storms and high winds.
• No tree branches should be allowed to obstruct it.

Battery Care
• Never switch on a transceiver when it is only connected to a solar charger (i.e., when the battery has been disconnected).
• The acid level of a vented battery must be checked regularly at least once a week.
• If the level is down, it must be filled up with distilled water or other substitute (e.g., rain-water collected in a plastic sheet).
• The battery voltage should be measured regularly if there is a voltmeter available to do this.
• If voltage is too low, the transceiver must not be used until the battery is fully charged again.
• It has been known for an operator whose battery was flat to get a second battery and connect it in series with the radio battery, resulting in 20 volts being applied to the 12-volt transceiver causing serious damage.
• When transmitting, the battery voltage is low if it goes down below 11 volts.
• Receiving does not require so much power, so if the voltage is too low to transmit, it may be possible to listen and receive messages.

Lightning Danger
• In areas where thunderstorms occur frequently, it is a good practice to disconnect the aerial when the transceiver is not in use.
• Where thunderstorms only occur in certain seasons, some operators will only remove the aerial during these seasons but this is not an entirely reliable scheme as the start and finish of a season is not always well defined.
• Therefore, the practice of always removing the aerial at the end of every contact is the recommended practice.
• The HQ station of one radio network in Kenya periodically reminds the operators of the other stations to disconnect their aerials after using their radios.
• It is also good practice to disconnect power cables, especially mains cables and battery cables where the battery is being charged by solar panels on the roof.

Aerial Tuning Units (ATU)
• The operator should be warned that high voltages occur at the aerial terminal of transceivers which incorporate an aerial tuning unit (ATU).
• RF burns can be received if the terminal is touched during transmission.
• With these units, when the frequency in use is changed, the ATU will need retuning as per instructions for that particular unit.
communication equipment,maintenance,controls,microphones,cables,aerials,battery care,lightning danger,aerial tuning units

Two Way Radios (8) -

Purchasing Multiple Radios for a Group
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Ensure compatibility and consistency when buying multiple radios for your group.

1. Buy the same model from the same manufacturer: This ensures that all optional features, such as privacy codes, work correctly between devices.
2....
Objective: Ensure compatibility and consistency when buying multiple radios for your group.

1. Buy the same model from the same manufacturer: This ensures that all optional features, such as privacy codes, work correctly between devices.
2. Verify that all radios operate on the same frequency band (FRS or GMRS): Radios on different bands may not be able to communicate with each other.
3. Confirm that all radios support the same channels and features: This ensures full functionality of any advanced features like eavesdrop reducer, VOX, or weather channels.
group radios, FRS, GMRS, radio compatibility
Selecting a Two-Way Radio with Additional Features for Outdoor Use
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Choose a radio with features that enhance outdoor experiences.

1. Look for radios with GPS capabilities: These can help you navigate in remote areas or track your location during outdoor activities.
2. **Consider models with...
Objective: Choose a radio with features that enhance outdoor experiences.

1. Look for radios with GPS capabilities: These can help you navigate in remote areas or track your location during outdoor activities.
2. Consider models with digital compasses, thermometers, barometers, and altimeters: These tools provide valuable information about your surroundings and weather conditions.
3. Choose a radio with a backlit LCD display: This makes it easier to read the screen in low-light conditions.
4. Opt for radios with auto channel scan features: These automatically search for signals on all channels, helping you find active frequencies quickly.
outdoor radios, GPS, digital compass, backlit display, auto channel scan
Selecting a Two-Way Radio with Privacy Codes
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Choose a two-way radio with privacy codes to reduce unwanted interference.

1. Choose a channel and privacy code (CTCSS or squelch code): Select a predetermined channel and privacy code for your group. This ensures that only...
Objective: Choose a two-way radio with privacy codes to reduce unwanted interference.

1. Choose a channel and privacy code (CTCSS or squelch code): Select a predetermined channel and privacy code for your group. This ensures that only radios using the same channel and code can communicate with each other.
2. Ensure all users have matching settings: All members of your group must use the exact same channel and privacy code to maintain communication.
3. Be aware of limitations: Even with a privacy code, others on the same channel who do not have their privacy-code feature enabled will still be able to hear your transmissions. Additionally, anyone using the same channel and code can transmit to your radio.
privacy codes, CTCSS, squelch codes, two-way radios, communication settings
Using Weather Channels on Two-Way Radios
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Access real-time weather information using NOAA channels.

1. Select a radio with NOAA weather channels: Ensure your radio has access to channels that provide the latest weather updates from the National Oceanic Atmospheric...
Objective: Access real-time weather information using NOAA channels.

1. Select a radio with NOAA weather channels: Ensure your radio has access to channels that provide the latest weather updates from the National Oceanic Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).
2. Tune into the appropriate channel: Use the radio's menu or channel selection feature to switch to a NOAA weather channel.
3. Monitor for severe weather alerts: Stay informed about potential storms, floods, and other hazardous conditions that may affect your area.
NOAA weather channels, two-way radios, weather updates, emergency preparedness
Using the Eavesdrop Reducer Feature on Two-Way Radios
Standard Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Use voice-inversion technology to prevent eavesdropping on FRS radio conversations.

1. Select a channel and privacy code: Choose a specific channel and privacy code for your group, just like with standard privacy codes.
2....
Objective: Use voice-inversion technology to prevent eavesdropping on FRS radio conversations.

1. Select a channel and privacy code: Choose a specific channel and privacy code for your group, just like with standard privacy codes.
2. Enable the eavesdrop reducer feature: This feature scrambles transmissions between users on the same channel and code, preventing others from listening in on your conversations.
3. Ensure all users have matching settings: All members of your group must use the exact same channel, privacy code, and eavesdrop reducer setting to maintain secure communication.
eavesdrop reducer, voice-inversion technology, FRS radios, secure communication
Choosing a Two-Way Radio with Call Alerts
Reference Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Select a radio with call alerts to receive notifications of incoming messages.

1. Select a radio with customizable call alerts: Look for radios that offer different types of alerts, such as tones or vibrations.
2. **Choose the...
Objective: Select a radio with call alerts to receive notifications of incoming messages.

1. Select a radio with customizable call alerts: Look for radios that offer different types of alerts, such as tones or vibrations.
2. Choose the appropriate alert type based on usage: For example, if you're hunting, select a radio with a vibrate alert to avoid spooking wildlife.
3. Test the alert system before use: Ensure that the call alerts work properly in your intended environment.
call alerts, two-way radios, vibration alerts, hunting communication
Selecting the Right Battery Type for Two-Way Radios
Reference Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Choose a battery type that suits your usage and budget.

1. Consider AA or AAA alkaline batteries: Most radios use these types of batteries, but they can be expensive if you're using the radio frequently.
2. **Choose rechargeable...
Objective: Choose a battery type that suits your usage and budget.

1. Consider AA or AAA alkaline batteries: Most radios use these types of batteries, but they can be expensive if you're using the radio frequently.
2. Choose rechargeable NiMH or NiCad batteries for frequent use: If you plan to use your radio often, opt for rechargeable batteries to save money in the long run.
3. Use a 120-volt or 12-volt power source for recharging: Depending on your radio model and accessories, you can recharge your batteries using either a 120-volt (home) or 12-volt (vehicle) power source.
battery type, two-way radios, NiMH, NiCad, rechargeable batteries
Understanding the Limitations of Two-Way Radio Range Claims
Reference Source: Bass Pro Shop Radio Guide
Objective: Be aware that actual two-way radio range may be less than advertised.

1. Recognize that environmental factors affect range: Walls, hills, trees, and weather can significantly reduce the effective range of your radio.
2. **Expect...
Objective: Be aware that actual two-way radio range may be less than advertised.

1. Recognize that environmental factors affect range: Walls, hills, trees, and weather can significantly reduce the effective range of your radio.
2. Expect maximum range only in ideal conditions: The best performance is achieved on flat, open ground with no obstructions and clear skies.
3. Do not rely solely on manufacturer claims for range: Always test your radios in real-world conditions to understand their limitations.
radio range, FRS, GMRS, environmental factors

Signaling Techniques (8) -

Proper Use of Signal Mirror When Signaling Aircraft
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
CAUTION:

• Do not flash a signal mirror rapidly because a pilot may mistake the flashes for enemy fire.
• Do not direct the beam in the aircraft's cockpit for more than a few seconds as it may blind the pilot.
• Haze, ground fog, and mirages...
CAUTION:

• Do not flash a signal mirror rapidly because a pilot may mistake the flashes for enemy fire.
• Do not direct the beam in the aircraft's cockpit for more than a few seconds as it may blind the pilot.
• Haze, ground fog, and mirages may make it hard for a pilot to spot signals from a flashing object. If possible, get to the highest point in your area when signaling.
• If you can't determine the aircraft's location, flash your signal in the direction of the aircraft noise.

Note:
• Pilots have reported seeing mirror flashes up to 160 kilometers away under ideal conditions.
• Figures 19-4 and 19-5 show methods of aiming a signal mirror for signaling.
signal mirror, aircraft signaling, survival signals, emergency signaling
Using Flashlight or Strobe Light to Signal Aircraft at Night
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- At night you can use a flashlight or a strobe light to send an SOS to an aircraft.
• When using a strobe light, take care to prevent the pilot from mistaking it for incoming ground fire.
• The strobe light flashes 60 times per minute.
• Some...
- At night you can use a flashlight or a strobe light to send an SOS to an aircraft.
• When using a strobe light, take care to prevent the pilot from mistaking it for incoming ground fire.
• The strobe light flashes 60 times per minute.
• Some strobe lights have infrared covers and lenses.
• Blue flash collimators are also available for strobe lights.
flashlight signaling, strobe light, aircraft signal, night survival
Using Gunshots as a Distress Signal
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- In some situations you can use firearms for signaling. Three shots fired at distinct intervals usually indicate a distress signal.
• Do not use this technique in enemy territory. The enemy will surely come to investigate shots.
gunshot signaling, survival firearm, emergency gunshot
Using Sea Dye Markers in Water Survival Situations
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- All military aircraft involved in operations near or over water will normally carry a water survival kit that contains sea dye markers.
• If you are in a water survival situation, use sea dye markers during daylight to indicate your location.
-...
- All military aircraft involved in operations near or over water will normally carry a water survival kit that contains sea dye markers.
• If you are in a water survival situation, use sea dye markers during daylight to indicate your location.
• These spots of dye stay conspicuous for about 3 hours, except in very rough seas.
• Use them only if you are in a friendly area.
• Keep the markers wrapped until you are ready to use them.
• Use them only when you hear or sight an aircraft.
• Sea dye markers are also very effective on snow-covered ground; use them to write distress code letters.
sea dye marker, water survival, distress signal, emergency signaling
Using Audio Signals for Rescuers
Standard Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- Radios, whistles, and gunshots are some of the methods you can use to signal your presence to rescuers.
audio signals, survival audio, emergency signaling
Using Clothing to Signal Aircraft
Standard Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
