- Spreading clothing on the ground or in the top of a tree is another way to signal.
• Select articles whose color will contrast with the natural surroundings.
• Arrange them in a large geometric pattern to make them more likely to attract attention.
clothing signal, survival signaling, emergency clothing use
Using Natural Materials to Signal from the Air
Standard Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- If you lack other means, you can use natural materials to form a symbol or message that can be seen from the air.
• Build mounds that cast shadows; you can use brush, foliage of any type, rocks, or snow blocks.
• In snowcovered areas, tramp the...
- If you lack other means, you can use natural materials to form a symbol or message that can be seen from the air.
• Build mounds that cast shadows; you can use brush, foliage of any type, rocks, or snow blocks.
• In snowcovered areas, tramp the snow to form letters or symbols and fill the depression with contrasting material (twigs or branches).
• In sand, use boulders, vegetation, or seaweed to form a symbol or message.
• In brush-covered areas, cut out patterns in the vegetation or sear the ground.
• In tundra, dig trenches or turn the sod upside down.
• In any terrain, use contrasting materials that will make the symbols visible to the aircrews.
natural signaling, survival signals, ground signals
Using Whistles for Close-Range Signaling
Standard Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- Whistles provide an excellent way for close up signaling.
• In some documented cases, they have been heard up to 1.6 kilometers away.
• Manufactured whistles have more range than a human whistle.
whistle signaling, survival whistle, emergency whistle use

Radio Transmission (7) -

Operation of a C.W. Telegraph Transmitter with Alternating Current Using One Oscillator Tube
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Use an alternating current (AC) as the initial source of energy for the oscillator tube.
2. Connect the AC power supply to the plate of the oscillator tube, ensuring that only the positive half of the AC waveform energizes the...
Procedure:

1. Use an alternating current (AC) as the initial source of energy for the oscillator tube.
2. Connect the AC power supply to the plate of the oscillator tube, ensuring that only the positive half of the AC waveform energizes the plate.
3. This causes oscillations to be set up in the circuit when the positive voltage is applied, resulting in an intermittent musical tone heard in the headphones at the receiving end.
4. No buzzer, chopper, or interruptor is needed in the key circuit because the oscillations are naturally interrupted by the alternating current itself.
5. The transmitter is referred to as a tone transmitter due to this characteristic.

Notes:
• This type of transmitter does not produce continuous waves (C.W.) but rather intermittent tones based on the AC waveform.
• The wave form and oscillations are shown in Figure 95, where only the positive half of the AC voltage sets up oscillations.
alternating current, tone transmitter, oscillator tube, intermittent wave, headphone reception
Understanding the Role of the Grid in a Vacuum Tube Oscillator Circuit
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. The grid in a vacuum tube oscillator circuit acts as an amplifier for the oscillations set up by the plate.
2. A small change in the grid potential causes a larger change in the current flowing from the plate to the filament,...
Procedure:

1. The grid in a vacuum tube oscillator circuit acts as an amplifier for the oscillations set up by the plate.
2. A small change in the grid potential causes a larger change in the current flowing from the plate to the filament, amplifying the signal.
3. When the grid is connected into the tuning coil, aerial wire, and clip 2, it forms an amplifying circuit that corresponds to the regenerative circuit of a receiving set.
4. The oscillations surge through this circuit, causing the grid to be alternately charged with positive and negative voltages, which in turn amplifies the plate current further.
5. This process continues until the tube reaches its output limit or until the signal is modulated by an external source (e.g., a telegraph key or microphone).

Notes:
• The grid must be properly connected to ensure effective amplification and modulation of signals.
• This setup is essential for both C.W. and wireless telephone transmitters.
vacuum tube, grid circuit, amplification, tuning coil, regenerative circuit
Heating the Filament with Alternating Current
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Use an alternating current power transformer to develop the necessary plate voltage.
2. Provide a second secondary coil on the transformer specifically for heating the filament of the oscillation tube.
3. Connect the filament...
Procedure:

1. Use an alternating current power transformer to develop the necessary plate voltage.
2. Provide a second secondary coil on the transformer specifically for heating the filament of the oscillation tube.
3. Connect the filament terminals across a voltmeter instead of using an ammeter in series with it.
4. Regulate the voltage applied to the filament using a rheostat, ensuring that the filament is kept at a constant voltage rather than a constant current strength.

Notes:
• This method increases the life of the filament compared to direct current heating.
• Always ensure proper insulation and grounding for safety.
alternating current, filament heating, power transformer, rheostat, voltmeter
How Transmitting Set No. 2 Works with Alternating Current
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Overview: This procedure explains how a transmitting set that uses an alternating current power transformer operates, including the use of a spark gap and oscillation circuit.

### Steps:
1. Alternating Current Operation:
- The current...
Overview: This procedure explains how a transmitting set that uses an alternating current power transformer operates, including the use of a spark gap and oscillation circuit.

### Steps:
1. Alternating Current Operation:
- The current from a generator flows through the primary coil of the power transformer (also called an alternating current transformer).
- The alternations of the usual lighting current are 60 cycles per second, setting up an alternating magnetic field in the core.
- These magnetic lines of force expand and contract, producing alternating currents at the secondary coil with much higher voltage according to the ratio of primary and secondary turns of wire (as explained under the sub-caption _Ratio of Transformation_ ).

2. Direct Current Operation:
- When a 110 volt direct current is used to energize the power transformer, an electrolytic interruptor is needed to make and break the primary circuit.
- Electrodes are connected in series with the primary coil and a source of direct current (40 to 110 volts).
- Bubbles of gas form on the end of the platinum or alloy anode, preventing current flow until the bubbles break, allowing the current to flow again rapidly and repeatedly.

3. Electrolytic Capacitance:
- The electrolytic interrupter has inherent capacitance due to electrolytic action, called _electrolytic capacitance_ , which is large enough to balance the self-induction of the circuit.
- The greater the number of breaks per minute, the smaller the required capacitance.

4. Rotary Spark Gap:
- In this type of spark gap, two fixed electrodes are connected with the terminals of the secondary coil of the power transformer and also with the condenser and primary of the oscillation transformer.
- When any pair of rotating disk electrodes aligns with the fixed ones, a spark occurs.
- The pitch of the note depends on the motor speed driving the disk.

5. Quenched Spark Gap:
- A quenched spark gap sets up oscillations in a coupled closed and open circuit by making only three or four sharp swings in the primary circuit, transferring all energy to the secondary circuit where it oscillates many times before damping out.
- This results in radiated waves that approach continuous waves, allowing sharper tuning.

6. Oscillation Transformer:
- In this set, a condenser is charged and discharged in the closed circuit, setting up oscillations that surge through the closed circuit as in Set No. 1.
- An oscillation transformer is used, with its primary coil included in the closed circuit to produce strong oscillating magnetic lines of force.
- These magnetic fields set up electric oscillations in the secondary coil of the oscillation transformer, which surge through the aerial wire system and are radiated as electric waves.

### Notes:
• The advantage of using an oscillation transformer is that it allows a much larger capacitance in the closed circuit than in the aerial wire system, enabling more energy to be stored by the condenser and impressed on the aerial when radiated as electric waves.
alternating current,power transformer,rotary spark gap,quenched spark gap,oscillation transformer
How a Wireless Transmitter Sends Out Electric Waves
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Overview: This procedure explains how a wireless transmitter sends out electric waves by transforming low voltage direct or alternating current into high potential alternating currents, charging a condenser, discharging through a spark gap, and...
Overview: This procedure explains how a wireless transmitter sends out electric waves by transforming low voltage direct or alternating current into high potential alternating currents, charging a condenser, discharging through a spark gap, and setting up high-frequency currents in the oscillation circuits.

### Steps:
1. Battery and Spark Coil Circuit:
- Press down on the key to make contact between silver points, closing the circuit.
- Low voltage direct current from the battery flows through the primary coil of the spark coil, magnetizing the soft iron core.
- The magnetic core pulls the spring of the vibrator over to it, breaking the circuit when contact is made.
- When the circuit breaks, the core loses its magnetism, and the spring returns to make contact with the adjusting screw, repeating the cycle.

2. Condenser Function:
- A condenser is connected across the contact points of the vibrator.
- This increases the voltage at the ends of the secondary coil compared to when it's used without a condenser.
- The self-induction of the primary coil causes the pressure of the current to rise, and when the circuit closes again, the current discharges through the primary coil.
- When the break occurs, the current flows into the condenser instead of arcing across the contact points.

3. Transformation of Primary Spark Coil Current Into Secondary Currents:
- Every time the vibrator contact points close the primary circuit, the electric current in the primary coil is changed into closed magnetic lines of force that cut through the secondary coil, setting up a momentary current in one direction.
- When the vibrator points break apart, the primary circuit opens, and the magnetic lines of force contract, cutting the turns of wire in the secondary coil in the opposite direction, setting up another momentary current in the other direction.
- This transforms low voltage direct current into alternating currents with a frequency equal to that of the spring vibrator, but with much higher potential (voltage).

4. Ratio of Transformation:
- The primary coil is wound with thick insulated copper wire and has fewer turns than the secondary coil, which is wound with very fine insulated copper wire and has many more turns.
- If the primary and secondary coils have the same number of turns, the voltage at the secondary would be the same as that of the current in the primary (ratio of transformation = 1).
- The ratio of transformation is directly proportional to the number of turns on the primary and secondary coils. For example, if the primary has 10 turns and the secondary has 1,000 turns, the voltage at the secondary will be 100 times higher than that of the current in the primary.

5. Secondary Spark Coil Circuit:
- The secondary coil is connected to a spark gap (electrodes).
- Alternating high potential currents from the secondary coil charge the electrodes alternately positively and negatively.
- When the potential is high enough, the air gap between the electrodes breaks down, allowing charges to rush together, forming a spark.
- The heated gap becomes a good conductor, causing the current to surge forth and back with high frequency before the air replaces what has burned out.

6. Oscillations and Electric Waves:
- The electric oscillations produced by the spark are in the order of a million or more per second.
- If an aerial and ground wire are connected to the electrodes, the oscillations surge up and down the aerial, converting into electric waves that travel out into space.

7. Closed Oscillation Circuit:
- A closed oscillation circuit includes a spark gap, condenser, and oscillation transformer.
- The high potential alternating current from the secondary coil charges both the spark gap electrodes (with small capacitance) and the condenser (with large capacitance that can be adjusted).
- When the condenser is fully charged, it discharges through the spark gap, setting up electric oscillations that surge to and fro in the closed circuit.
- The aerial wire acts as a good radiator, converting nearly all of the energy from the oscillations into electric waves.

### Notes:
• An open circuit transmitter sends out waves four times as long as the aerial itself but is strongly damped and not permitted for use by the Government.
• A closed oscillation circuit allows tuning to send out waves of a given length with less damping, allowing more current to be sent into the aerial wire system.
transmitter,spark coil,circuit,condenser,oscillation,electric wave,ratio of transformation
Operation of a Wireless Telephone Transmitter with Direct Current (Long Distance)
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Use the same basic setup as the short-distance wireless telephone transmitter, but include a battery to energize the microphone transmitter.
2. Connect the microphone and battery in series with the rest of the circuit,...
Procedure:

1. Use the same basic setup as the short-distance wireless telephone transmitter, but include a battery to energize the microphone transmitter.
2. Connect the microphone and battery in series with the rest of the circuit, ensuring that the current from the battery is sufficient to power both the microphone and the oscillator tube.
3. The microphone modulates the sustained oscillations set up by the oscillator tube based on the sound waves it receives.
4. These modulated oscillations are then transmitted through the aerial wire as a wireless telephone signal.
5. Ensure that all components, including the battery, microphone, and oscillator tube, are properly connected for long-distance transmission.

Notes:
• This setup is suitable for long-distance communication due to the use of a dedicated power source (battery) for the microphone.
• Regular maintenance and checking of connections are essential for reliable performance.
wireless telephone, direct current, long distance, microphone transmitter, battery power
Setting Up Oscillations with a Vacuum Tube Using Direct Current
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Connect the positive part of the 110-volt direct current to the plate of the oscillator tube via the lamp socket, panel cut-out, and choke coil.
2. Connect the negative part of the 110-volt direct current to the filament of the...
Procedure:

1. Connect the positive part of the 110-volt direct current to the plate of the oscillator tube via the lamp socket, panel cut-out, and choke coil.
2. Connect the negative part of the 110-volt direct current to the filament of the oscillator tube.
3. Apply a 6-volt battery current to heat the filament until it reaches brilliancy, allowing electrons to be emitted from the filament to form a conducting path between the filament and plate.
4. Connect the plate of the oscillator tube through a blocking condenser to clip 3 of the tuning coil.
5. Connect the other end of the tuning coil (clip 2) back to the filament, forming a closed oscillation circuit.
6. When the grid is connected to the tuning coil, aerial wire, and clip 2, it forms an amplifying circuit that corresponds to the regenerative circuit in a receiving set.
7. Oscillations are generated when the grid voltage alternates between positive and negative, causing stronger oscillations in the plate circuit.
8. These oscillations travel through the closed circuits and radiate as electric waves along the aerial wire.
9. The telegraph key is placed in the grid circuit to modulate the oscillations by varying their amplitude.

Notes:
• This setup is used for short-distance C.W. (Continuous Wave) transmitters.
• Ensure that all connections are secure and insulated to prevent interference or signal loss.
vacuum tube, direct current, oscillation circuit, telegraph key, blocking condenser, tuning coil

Antennas (7) -

Install an External Antenna for Improved Radio Reception
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Unplug or use extension lead: If the antenna can be unplugged from the radio, do so. Alternatively, use an extension lead made from 50 ohm coax cable with appropriate connectors.
2. Extend the antenna: Plug the extension...
Procedure:

1. Unplug or use extension lead: If the antenna can be unplugged from the radio, do so. Alternatively, use an extension lead made from 50 ohm coax cable with appropriate connectors.
2. Extend the antenna: Plug the extension lead into the socket where the original antenna was connected. Lead the extension out of the room to the highest point possible or a location with the clearest view towards your communication target.
3. Limit cable length: Keep the extension lead no longer than 100 meters unless using 'Low Loss' coaxial cable, which is more expensive but reduces signal loss over long distances.
4. Use appropriate antenna design: Do not use the original antenna from the radio. Instead, choose a specialized external antenna such as:
- Dipole
- Base loaded whip
- Ground plane radial
- Slim Jim
- Helical counterpoise whip
5. Select lightweight and portable options: For portability, choose a light, easy-to-carry quarter wave whip with a helical counterpoise. These should ideally come with a large, strong clip for quick attachment to pipes or rails.
6. Secure the antenna: If no clip is available, use tape or string to secure the antenna in position.

Notes:
• The improvement in signal strength from even modest height gains can be dramatic.
• Sacrificing mobility may be necessary for optimal performance.
• Ensure that the antenna is positioned away from obstructions and metal structures that could interfere with signal transmission.
external antenna, radio reception, coax cable, portable antenna, base station setup
Calculate Dipole Aerial Length for a Given Frequency
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To calculate the length of a dipole aerial for a given frequency, use the following formulas:

• Total length in feet = 468 / Frequency in MHz
• Total length in metres = 142.6 / Frequency in MHz

For example, if using a frequency of 6...
To calculate the length of a dipole aerial for a given frequency, use the following formulas:

• Total length in feet = 468 / Frequency in MHz
• Total length in metres = 142.6 / Frequency in MHz

For example, if using a frequency of 6 MHz:

• Total length in feet = 468 / 6 = 78 feet
• Total length in metres = 142.6 / 6 ≈ 23.77 metres

The dipole aerial is designed to be half a wavelength long at the frequency of use, and it can be used effectively within ±2% of this frequency.
dipole aerial, antenna length calculation, HF communication, shortwave radio
Construct a 1/4 Wavelength Vertical Aerial for HF Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To construct a 1/4 wavelength vertical aerial:

1. Select materials: Use a single conductor that is 1/4 wavelength long at the frequency of operation.
2. Insulate from ground: Ensure the base of the aerial is well insulated from the...
To construct a 1/4 wavelength vertical aerial:

1. Select materials: Use a single conductor that is 1/4 wavelength long at the frequency of operation.
2. Insulate from ground: Ensure the base of the aerial is well insulated from the ground.
3. Connect to coaxial cable: Connect the inner conductor of a coaxial cable to the vertical aerial at its base.
4. Provide good earthing:
- Attach 3 or more pieces of wire, each 1/4 wavelength long, to the outer braid of the coaxial cable at the base of the aerial.
- Lay these wires radially and spaced equally apart (as shown in Fig. 2.7).
- Bury them approximately 25 cm underground to provide a good earth connection.

This configuration is suitable for HF communication and requires proper earthing for optimal performance.
vertical aerial construction, 1/4 wavelength antenna, HF radio setup
Construct a Horizontal Half-Wave Dipole Aerial
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To construct a horizontal half-wave dipole aerial:

1. Select materials: Use pre-stressed aerial wire to prevent stretching.
2. Cut the wire: Divide the total length of the dipole into two equal parts, each being one-quarter wavelength...
To construct a horizontal half-wave dipole aerial:

1. Select materials: Use pre-stressed aerial wire to prevent stretching.
2. Cut the wire: Divide the total length of the dipole into two equal parts, each being one-quarter wavelength long.
3. Connect the wires:
- Connect one piece of wire to the centre conductor of a coaxial cable.
- Connect the other piece of wire to the outer conductor of the same coaxial cable.
4. Attach insulators: Secure each end of the wires to their own insulator.
5. Suspend horizontally: Mount the aerial at a certain height above the ground, ensuring it is horizontal.
6. Ensure waterproof and mechanical strength: Use ready-made units for connecting the coaxial cable to the aerial wires. These may include a BALUN for improved performance.
7. Avoid earth system: No additional circuits or grounding systems are required.
dipole construction, HF antenna setup, shortwave radio installation
Construct an Inverted Vee Aerial for All-Round Coverage
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To construct an inverted vee aerial:

1. Use a dipole: Start with a horizontal half-wave dipole as described in the previous procedure.
2. Support in the middle: Secure the center of the dipole to a support point (e.g., a pole or tree) at a...
To construct an inverted vee aerial:

1. Use a dipole: Start with a horizontal half-wave dipole as described in the previous procedure.
2. Support in the middle: Secure the center of the dipole to a support point (e.g., a pole or tree) at a height above ground level.
3. Slope each side down: Allow each end of the dipole to slope downward until they are approximately 2 metres above the ground, forming an inverted V shape.
4. Benefits:
- This configuration transmits and receives energy in most directions, making it ideal for all-round coverage.
- It supports both horizontal and vertical polarization, allowing communication with fixed stations using horizontal dipoles as well as mobile units using vertical aerials.
inverted vee antenna, HF all-round coverage, shortwave radio setup
Design a Single Dipole for Two Frequencies
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand the design requirements: It is possible to design a single dipole and its coaxial feeder cable to operate at two frequencies.
2. Purpose of the design: The main reason for using this design is economy, i.e., to...
Procedure:

1. Understand the design requirements: It is possible to design a single dipole and its coaxial feeder cable to operate at two frequencies.
2. Purpose of the design: The main reason for using this design is economy, i.e., to obtain 'two aerials for the price of one'.
3. Expert assistance required: To understand the design you may require the aid of a radio communications engineer.
4. Design considerations:
- The dipole is designed for use normally at one frequency.
- At the second frequency, there will be standing waves on the coaxial cable.
5. Adjust the length of the coaxial cable: Cut the length of the coaxial cable to present a low impedance at the transceiver.
6. Measure SWR:
- Measure the Standing Wave Ratio (SWR) at the transceiver output socket.
7. Optimize SWR:
- Reduce or increase the length of the coaxial cable until an acceptable SWR is achieved, e.g., less than 2.
dipole,coaxial cable,frequencies,SWR,radio design
Determine Optimal Height for a Dipole Aerial Based on Communication Range
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
To determine the optimal height of a dipole aerial based on communication range:

• For short-range communication (up to 150 km): The dipole should be suspended at a height of 1/4 wavelength above the ground. This allows for a steep angle of...
To determine the optimal height of a dipole aerial based on communication range:

• For short-range communication (up to 150 km): The dipole should be suspended at a height of 1/4 wavelength above the ground. This allows for a steep angle of radiation toward the ionosphere, which is ideal for short-distance communication.
• For long-range communication (e.g., 1000–1500 km): The dipole should be suspended at a height of 1/2 wavelength above the ground. This allows for a shallower angle of radiation toward the ionosphere, which is more suitable for long-distance communication.

Example using a frequency of 7 MHz:
• 1/4 wavelength = 10.7 metres
• 1/2 wavelength = 21.4 metres

In practice, if constructing special towers is not feasible, the aerial should be suspended as high as possible between trees, poles, or buildings to achieve the best compromise between theoretical ideal and practical limitations.
dipole height calculation, HF antenna positioning, shortwave radio setup

Disaster Planning (6) -

Determine Communication Needs and Personnel Deployment
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Identify who needs to communicate, when, how long, and what type of communication is required.

Steps:
1. Evaluate the deployment of personnel and determine if radios are necessary for each role.
2. Consider the following...
Objective: Identify who needs to communicate, when, how long, and what type of communication is required.

Steps:
1. Evaluate the deployment of personnel and determine if radios are necessary for each role.
2. Consider the following scenarios:
- Field disaster worker to EOC (Emergency Operations Center)
- Field disaster worker to other field disaster workers
- Field disaster worker to on-scene public safety personnel
3. Determine if communication needs are limited to short-range or long-range.
4. Consider the need for relay systems via radio or telephone.
5. Identify available relay resources (e.g., backup radios, telephones, repeaters).
6. Assess whether citizen volunteers will be required to carry communications equipment or if on-scene public safety personnel can serve as relays.
7. Determine how many people and which individuals in your community need radios (not everyone may require one).
communication planning, personnel deployment, radio usage, relay systems
Develop a Neighborhood Emergency Communications Plan
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Create and maintain a comprehensive communication plan for your neighborhood during emergencies.

Steps:
1. Identify key personnel (e.g., Block Captains, Amateur Radio operators) who should be part of the plan.
2. Ensure that...
Objective: Create and maintain a comprehensive communication plan for your neighborhood during emergencies.

Steps:
1. Identify key personnel (e.g., Block Captains, Amateur Radio operators) who should be part of the plan.
2. Ensure that local OEM (Office of Emergency Management) is aware of your group and has a copy of your communications plan.
3. Coordinate with local public safety officials to ensure they have access to your communication strategy.
4. Confirm that your neighborhood has trained individuals (e.g., CERT program participants) who can operate radios during emergencies.
5. Ensure that the OEM has provided basic emergency planning training for all involved parties.
emergency communication plan, OEM coordination, CERT program
Establish Neighborhood Communication Testing and Reliability
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Ensure that your neighborhood’s communication network is reliable and functional during a disaster.

Steps:
1. Test the range of radios from residences to key access points (e.g., main roads, intersections).
2. Verify whether...
Objective: Ensure that your neighborhood’s communication network is reliable and functional during a disaster.

Steps:
1. Test the range of radios from residences to key access points (e.g., main roads, intersections).
2. Verify whether radios can communicate between all critical locations in the neighborhood (e.g., water pumping plants, clubhouses, guard shacks, major intersections).
3. Confirm that your network can relay requests for help beyond FRS and GMRS ranges if necessary.
4. Ensure that communication with other neighborhoods is possible when needed.
radio testing, range verification, communication reliability
Create and Maintain Communication Calling Trees for Welfare Checks
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Develop a calling tree system to alert neighbors during emergencies and check on vulnerable individuals.

Steps:
1. Create an alerting calling tree to notify neighbors of potential threats (e.g., tornado, flood) and direct...
Objective: Develop a calling tree system to alert neighbors during emergencies and check on vulnerable individuals.

Steps:
1. Create an alerting calling tree to notify neighbors of potential threats (e.g., tornado, flood) and direct them to take cover.
2. Establish a welfare calling tree to prioritize checking in with elderly residents and other high-risk individuals after a disaster.
3. Ensure that all community members are aware of the calling trees and their roles within them.
4. Regularly test and update the calling trees to ensure they remain functional.
calling tree, welfare check, alert system
Commit to Supplemental Communications Planning
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Ensure OES/OEMs and Public Safety agencies commit to supplemental communications planning.

Steps:
1. Identify the supplemental communication needs of every neighborhood, public safety agency, major utility, etc.
2. Encourage and...
Objective: Ensure OES/OEMs and Public Safety agencies commit to supplemental communications planning.

Steps:
1. Identify the supplemental communication needs of every neighborhood, public safety agency, major utility, etc.
2. Encourage and train volunteers through drills and organized training sessions.
3. Work with Amateur Radio RACES and ARES as primary supplemental civilian communications resources.
4. Ensure that Public Safety agencies use Amateurs as RACES volunteers rather than ARES volunteers when assigned to a specific agency.
5. Fingerprint RACES volunteers for quality control, integrity assurance, and eligibility for workman’s compensation insurance benefits if injured in the line of duty.
6. Avoid using communications volunteers in roles outside their expertise (e.g., search and rescue).
7. Encourage firefighters, dispatchers, OES/OEM staff, medical personnel, and police officers to become Amateur Radio operators.
8. Identify volunteers who should not be part of a program before an emergency occurs.
9. Set clear standards and expectations for volunteers to ensure a better program.
10. Make sure OES/OEM leaders are the right people—supervisory, managerial, organizational, and politically skilled individuals.

Notes:
• This is not just about FRS/GMRS but also includes broader disaster planning considerations.
• The diversity of neighborhoods requires localized approaches to supplemental communications or CERT programs.
supplemental communication, OES/OEM, RACES, ARES, volunteer training, fingerprinting, disaster planning
Identify and Prepare Meeting Spaces for Training and Planning
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Secure a location for neighborhood meetings to train on emergency communication procedures.

Steps:
1. Identify an existing meeting place in the neighborhood (e.g., community center, school) that can be used for training...
Objective: Secure a location for neighborhood meetings to train on emergency communication procedures.

Steps:
1. Identify an existing meeting place in the neighborhood (e.g., community center, school) that can be used for training sessions.
2. If no suitable space is available, coordinate with local officials or organizations to arrange one.
3. Ensure that the selected location is accessible and equipped with necessary resources (e.g., tables, chairs, radios).
4. Schedule regular meetings to discuss communication planning and emergency preparedness.
training space, meeting location, emergency drills

Wireless Telegraphy (6) -

Assembling the Sending Apparatus for Wireless Telegraphy
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Construct a sending apparatus for wireless telegraphy.

Components Required:
• A two-inch induction coil
• Heavy spark gap (preferably zinc)
• A wireless key with heavy contacts
• A plate condenser (can be homemade using good...
Objective: Construct a sending apparatus for wireless telegraphy.

Components Required:
• A two-inch induction coil
• Heavy spark gap (preferably zinc)
• A wireless key with heavy contacts
• A plate condenser (can be homemade using good glass)
• A triple pole, double throw aerial switch (can be made by scouts)

Steps:
1. Acquire or construct the induction coil, ensuring it is of proper size and quality.
2. Install a heavy spark gap (zinc recommended) to handle high voltage currents.
3. Use a wireless key with heavy contacts for sending Morse code signals.
4. Construct a plate condenser using good glass, ensuring it can hold sufficient charge.
5. Assemble the triple pole, double throw aerial switch, which allows switching between different circuits or antennas.

Notes:
• All components must be connected securely to prevent electrical failures.
• Ensure that the key and spark gap are properly insulated from the ground.
sending apparatus,wireless telegraphy,induction coil,spark gap,key,condenser,aerial switch
Building a Spark Gap for a Wireless Telegraph Set
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
To build a spark gap for a wireless telegraph set:

1. Prepare the base: Use a hard-wood base with two uprights.
2. Attach brass strips: Fasten strips of brass to the uprights.
3. Insert battery zincs: Place two pieces of battery zincs...
To build a spark gap for a wireless telegraph set:

1. Prepare the base: Use a hard-wood base with two uprights.
2. Attach brass strips: Fasten strips of brass to the uprights.
3. Insert battery zincs: Place two pieces of battery zincs under the brass strips so that the gap between their ends is variable.
4. Add binding posts: Fasten binding posts to the brass strips for contact.

Details:
• The spark gap allows for controlled electrical discharges, which are essential for transmitting signals.
• Adjusting the gap can help fine-tune the performance of the telegraph set.
spark gap,wireless telegraph set,construction,communications
Building an Aerial for Wireless Telegraphy
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Construct a properly insulated aerial for wireless telegraphy.

Materials Needed:
• Wire (sufficient length and strength)
• Insulating materials (e.g., rubber, glass)
• Soldering equipment
• Support structures (poles, trees,...
Objective: Construct a properly insulated aerial for wireless telegraphy.

Materials Needed:
• Wire (sufficient length and strength)
• Insulating materials (e.g., rubber, glass)
• Soldering equipment
• Support structures (poles, trees, etc.)

Steps:
1. Determine the maximum height and length of aerial based on available space.
2. The aerial should be at least 50 to 60 feet high and 70 to 100 feet long.
3. Ensure that the aerial is well insulated from the ground using appropriate materials.
4. Use soldered connections for all wire joints to ensure solid electrical contact.
5. Make sure the aerial is strong and durable, as it plays a critical role in the station's performance.

Notes:
• Avoid sharp bends or kinks in the wire, which can cause signal loss.
• Regularly inspect the aerial for wear or damage.
aerial,wireless telegraphy,insulation,soldering,wire connections
Connecting Components for a Wireless Telegraph Station
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Properly connect all components of the wireless telegraph station.

Steps:
1. Refer to the provided diagram (not included in this text) to understand how to wire the components together.
2. Ensure that the **aerial is properly...
Objective: Properly connect all components of the wireless telegraph station.

Steps:
1. Refer to the provided diagram (not included in this text) to understand how to wire the components together.
2. Ensure that the aerial is properly connected to the sending and receiving circuits.
3. Connect the induction coil to the spark gap and key for signal transmission.
4. Wire the receiving apparatus (watch-case receivers, tuning coil, condensers, metal detector) in a way that allows signals to be detected clearly.
5. Ensure all connections are soldered and secure, with no exposed wires or loose joints.

Notes:
• The aerial must be of the correct height for optimal signal transmission (8–10 miles range if properly set up).
• Test the entire system after assembly to confirm that it can send and receive signals effectively.
connecting components,wireless telegraphy,circuit assembly,aerial connection,test system
Constructing a Sending Condenser for a Wireless Telegraph Set
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
To construct a sending condenser for a wireless telegraph set:

1. Use glass as dielectric: The dielectric material is glass.
2. Use tin-foil conducting surfaces: Arrange sheets of tin-foil in a pile, alternating with sheets of glass.
3....
To construct a sending condenser for a wireless telegraph set:

1. Use glass as dielectric: The dielectric material is glass.
2. Use tin-foil conducting surfaces: Arrange sheets of tin-foil in a pile, alternating with sheets of glass.
3. Shape the foil: Shape the tin-foil as shown in the drawing (not included here).
4. Connect lugs: Alternate sheets have their lugs projecting on opposite sides; all lugs on the same side are connected together.
5. Adjust number of plates: For a one-inch coil, only a few plates are needed, but for higher power, more plates are necessary.

Details:
• The sending condenser is similar in construction to the receiving condenser but differs in materials used.
• This component helps store and release electrical energy efficiently during transmission.
sending condenser,wireless telegraph set,construction,condenser
Setting Up a Wireless Telegraph Set for Sending and Receiving
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
To set up a wireless telegraph set for both sending and receiving:

1. Arrange the instruments: Set them up as shown in the drawing (not included here).
2. Use a double-point, double-throw switch: This switch allows you to toggle between...
To set up a wireless telegraph set for both sending and receiving:

1. Arrange the instruments: Set them up as shown in the drawing (not included here).
2. Use a double-point, double-throw switch: This switch allows you to toggle between sending and receiving modes.
3. Connect the receiving instruments: Ensure that all components are properly connected for receiving signals.
4. Test the receiver: Place the receiver at the ear and move the detector point over the mineral until clear signals are heard.
5. Adjust tuning coil: Fine-tune the tuning coil to maximize signal strength.
6. Set up sending apparatus: Ensure that the key, batteries, and other components for sending are properly connected.
7. Test communication: Use the key and batteries to send a signal to another station and confirm that it is received clearly.
8. Adjust helix clips: Place the clip on various turns of the helix until the receiving station confirms that signals are loudest.

Details:
• This setup allows for both sending and receiving signals using the same aerial and ground system.
• A spark coil is recommended for sending, as it is more cost-effective than building one from scratch.
setting up,wireless telegraph set,communications,equipment setup

Emergency Information Dissemination (6) -

Coordinate and Authorize Message Release During a Radiological Emergency
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Ensure that message content is approved by the appropriate authority before release.
2. Coordinate messaging with Federal, state, local, and tribal stakeholders prior to dissemination.
3. Confirm that communication methods are...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that message content is approved by the appropriate authority before release.
2. Coordinate messaging with Federal, state, local, and tribal stakeholders prior to dissemination.
3. Confirm that communication methods are consistent with an established Joint Information System (JIS).
4. Repeat emergency information at regular intervals as needed for clarity and impact.
message release, coordination, approval, JIS, stakeholders
Deliver Clear and Accessible Emergency Messaging
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Use plain language to ensure messages are clear, concise, accurate, and delivered in a timely manner.
2. Ensure the Protective Action Decision (PAD) is correctly reflected in all messaging.
3. Disclose and explain the Estimated...
Procedure:

1. Use plain language to ensure messages are clear, concise, accurate, and delivered in a timely manner.
2. Ensure the Protective Action Decision (PAD) is correctly reflected in all messaging.
3. Disclose and explain the Estimated Center of Landfall (ECL) clearly and adequately.
4. When necessary, use familiar landmarks and boundaries to describe protective action areas.
5. Include a closing statement in all messages directed at affected or potentially affected populations.
6. Ensure that the closing statement is communicated effectively through appropriate channels.
clear messaging, PAD, ECL, landmarks, closing statement
Ensure Messaging Consistency with Protective Actions
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Provide all necessary instructions to assist the public in carrying out Protective Action Decisions (PADs), including:
- Evacuation instructions and routes.
- Reception center locations.
- What to take when evacuating.
...
Procedure:

1. Provide all necessary instructions to assist the public in carrying out Protective Action Decisions (PADs), including:
- Evacuation instructions and routes.
- Reception center locations.
- What to take when evacuating.
- Shelter-in-place instructions.
- Information for schools and persons with access and/or functional needs.
- Public inquiry hotline telephone numbers.
2. Confirm that all messaging is consistent with the protective actions being implemented.
protective actions, evacuation instructions, reception centers, functional needs
Respond to Media and Public Inquiries During an Emergency
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Ensure that appropriate Public Information Officers (PIOs) or subject matter experts (SMEs) are available to respond to inquiries.
2. Gather and verify information through reliable sources before disseminating it.
3. Coordinate...
Procedure:

1. Ensure that appropriate Public Information Officers (PIOs) or subject matter experts (SMEs) are available to respond to inquiries.
2. Gather and verify information through reliable sources before disseminating it.
3. Coordinate information with appropriate personnel for approval prior to release.
4. Conduct regular exchanges, discussions, and coordination among PIOs and SMEs to ensure consistency in messaging.
5. Conduct media briefings that are frequent, timely, and accurate.
6. Address all media and public inquiries appropriately and professionally.
7. Monitor trends and rumors and address them effectively through media releases.
media response, PIOs, SMEs, rumor control, public inquiries
Update Emergency Information as the Incident Progresses
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Continuously update information on the incident progression.
2. Validate previously identified protective action areas and clearly identify any new ones.
3. Clearly communicate any changes or invalidations to previously provided...
Procedure:

1. Continuously update information on the incident progression.
2. Validate previously identified protective action areas and clearly identify any new ones.
3. Clearly communicate any changes or invalidations to previously provided information, such as:
- Rerouting of evacuation routes due to impediments.
4. Ensure that all updates are coordinated and delivered effectively through appropriate channels.
5. Provide follow-up messaging when necessary to address changes or updates.
incident update, protective areas, evacuation routes, communication channels
Address Cultural and Linguistic Considerations in Messaging
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Determine if public information needs to be available in non-English languages at the location/site.
2. Translate messages or provide them in alternative formats as needed for non-English speakers.
3. Ensure that individuals with...
Procedure:

1. Determine if public information needs to be available in non-English languages at the location/site.
2. Translate messages or provide them in alternative formats as needed for non-English speakers.
3. Ensure that individuals with access and/or functional needs receive messages and actionable information through appropriate means.
4. Identify any cultural or linguistic considerations relevant to the area and implement strategies to address them effectively.
cultural considerations, non-English languages, functional needs

Information Management (6) -

Incorporate Geo-Targeting in Emergency Communications Strategy
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Use geographically targeted alerts to deliver precise emergency messages.

Steps:
1. Understand Geo-Targeting Tools: Learn about systems such as Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) that can send text-like messages to mobile...
Objective: Use geographically targeted alerts to deliver precise emergency messages.

Steps:
1. Understand Geo-Targeting Tools: Learn about systems such as Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEAs) that can send text-like messages to mobile devices within a specific geographic area.
2. Develop Zone-Specific Messages: Create tailored messages for different impact zones, such as evacuation orders for the DRZ or shelter-in-place instructions for other areas.
3. Coordinate with Local Agencies: Work with local emergency management and public safety agencies to ensure that geo-targeting systems are properly configured and tested.
4. Practice Message Delivery: Conduct regular drills to test WEA capabilities and practice sending targeted messages during National Preparedness Month or other preparedness events.
5. Engage Local Businesses and Organizations: Partner with local businesses, schools, and community groups to help amplify emergency messages through their networks.
geo-targeting, wireless emergency alerts, zone-specific messaging, emergency communication strategy
Practice Geotargeted Message Deployment
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure that geotargeted emergency messages can be effectively deployed during a crisis.

Steps:
1. Conduct Regular Tests: Simulate sending targeted alerts to different zones using systems like WEAs to identify any technical...
Objective: Ensure that geotargeted emergency messages can be effectively deployed during a crisis.

Steps:
1. Conduct Regular Tests: Simulate sending targeted alerts to different zones using systems like WEAs to identify any technical issues or gaps in coverage.
2. Coordinate with Local Agencies and Businesses: Involve local government, public safety agencies, and community organizations in testing scenarios to ensure a coordinated response.
3. Evaluate Message Clarity and Reach: After tests, assess whether messages were received by the intended audience and if they were understood correctly.
4. Refine Communication Protocols: Use test results to improve message content, delivery timing, and targeting accuracy for future emergencies.
5. Engage the Public in Preparedness Activities: Encourage community participation in preparedness events such as National Preparedness Month to build awareness of geotargeted communication tools.
emergency drill, message testing, geotargeted alerts, communication preparedness
Prepare a Strategy for Prioritizing Information Requests
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Action: Prepare a strategy to prioritize requests and catalog approved answers to questions from the press and public.

Details:
• Anticipate communications staff being overwhelmed by information requests, press inquiries, and public...
Action: Prepare a strategy to prioritize requests and catalog approved answers to questions from the press and public.

Details:
• Anticipate communications staff being overwhelmed by information requests, press inquiries, and public inquiries.
• Prioritizing information needs is critical for response efficacy.
• Create a bank of approved messages to answer common and repeated questions. Approve responses for the press and public, and catalog received questions and answers sent.
• Reusing responses increases timeliness of delivery.
• Store approved messages in an accessible format across jurisdictions.
• Identify appropriate cataloging methods (e.g., shared documents, databases) that allow the entire public affairs team and anyone staffing a public affairs position to access them.
• Establish a message-sharing strategy before an incident happens, exercise it regularly, and troubleshoot challenges.
message prioritization,cataloging responses,nuclear communication strategy
Implement Frequent Emergency Updates
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Ensure the public receives consistent and accurate information from official sources.

Steps:
1. Establish a Communication Schedule: Set specific times for updates, even if no new information is available, to maintain trust...
Objective: Ensure the public receives consistent and accurate information from official sources.

Steps:
1. Establish a Communication Schedule: Set specific times for updates, even if no new information is available, to maintain trust and encourage reliance on official channels.
2. Provide Contextual Information: Explain ongoing efforts, their purpose, and how they relate to public safety. This helps reassure the public that actions are being taken to protect them.
3. Use Simple Language: Avoid technical jargon or complex explanations. Keep updates clear and easy to understand for all audiences.
4. Repeat Key Messages: Reinforce critical information such as sheltering instructions, evacuation orders, or radiation protection measures in every update.
5. Monitor Feedback: Pay attention to public reactions and concerns raised during updates to adjust messaging as needed.
emergency communication, public updates, information management, crisis communication
Leverage Family and Social Networks for Emergency Messaging
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Use personal connections to spread emergency information effectively.

Steps:
1. Identify Key Contacts: Determine which family members, friends, or community leaders are likely to be reached first in an emergency and can act...
Objective: Use personal connections to spread emergency information effectively.

Steps:
1. Identify Key Contacts: Determine which family members, friends, or community leaders are likely to be reached first in an emergency and can act as messengers.
2. Train Contacts on Emergency Protocols: Ensure they understand the correct actions (e.g., sheltering, evacuation) and how to relay instructions clearly to others.
3. Encourage Proactive Communication: Ask contacts to check in with loved ones regularly during a crisis and share updates from official sources.
4. Provide Clear Instructions for Relaying Messages: Give specific guidance on what messages should be shared (e.g., “Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned”) and how they should be communicated (e.g., via phone calls, text messages, or social media).
5. Use Social Media Strategically: Encourage contacts to post updates from official sources on their personal accounts to reach a wider audience.
community communication, social networks, emergency messaging, family preparedness
Confront Radiation Protection Rumors
Standard Source: Planning Guidance Nuclear Detonation FEMA
Objective: Address false information about radiation protection.

Steps:
1. Verify Information: Confirm whether the rumor (e.g., drinking iodine for radiation protection) is accurate or misleading by consulting official sources such as...
Objective: Address false information about radiation protection.

Steps:
1. Verify Information: Confirm whether the rumor (e.g., drinking iodine for radiation protection) is accurate or misleading by consulting official sources such as FEMA, CDC, or local public health authorities.
2. Educate Yourself: Understand the correct methods of radiation protection, including sheltering in place, using potassium iodide (if appropriate and prescribed), and following official guidance.
3. Disseminate Accurate Information: Share verified information with others through trusted channels such as family, community groups, or social media posts that are clearly labeled as coming from authoritative sources.
4. Encourage Skepticism of Unverified Claims: Teach others to question the source and validity of health-related claims found online, especially those promoting unproven remedies or actions.
5. Report False Information: If you encounter misinformation on social media or other platforms, report it to the platform’s moderators or official emergency management agencies.
radiation protection, iodine rumor, misinformation, emergency communication, public health

Disaster Communication (5) -

Licensing Neighbors for GMRS and FRS Communication
High Priority Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Use General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS) and Family Radio Service (FRS) to support local communication when amateur radio resources are insufficient.

Procedure:
1. If there is a lack of Amateur Radio operators in isolated...
Objective: Use General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS) and Family Radio Service (FRS) to support local communication when amateur radio resources are insufficient.

Procedure:
1. If there is a lack of Amateur Radio operators in isolated neighborhoods, consider licensing neighbors for GMRS.
2. A GMRS license can be obtained online without taking an exam and allows the use of base or mobile stations up to 50 watts of power.
3. FRS can be used for very-local short-distance communication within the same vicinity.
4. A GMRS licensee may report to an EOC on GMRS frequencies, provided that the EOC has a licensed GMRS operator.
GMRS licensing, FRS communication, local disaster relay, EOC support
Leverage Business Radio Fleets for Communication Support
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Utilize business radio fleets to support communication during a disaster.

Procedure:
1. OEMs (Office of Emergency Management) should not overlook companies with fleets of radios in various business radio services.
2. These...
Objective: Utilize business radio fleets to support communication during a disaster.

Procedure:
1. OEMs (Office of Emergency Management) should not overlook companies with fleets of radios in various business radio services.
2. These systems may be used by CERT teams or other emergency response units for communication back to an EOC.
3. Coordinate with local businesses that have available radio-equipped vehicles and integrate them into the disaster response plan.
4. Ensure that these systems are compatible with existing communication protocols and frequencies.
business radio fleets, CERT teams, EOC communication, OEM coordination
Use of VHF Multi-Use Radio Service for Communication
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Use the VHF Multi-Use Radio Service (VHFRS) to support short-distance communication during a disaster.

Procedure:
1. The VHFRS can be used for short-distance neighborhood communication.
2. It may also be used for communication...
Objective: Use the VHF Multi-Use Radio Service (VHFRS) to support short-distance communication during a disaster.

Procedure:
1. The VHFRS can be used for short-distance neighborhood communication.
2. It may also be used for communication back to an EOC, depending on range and conditions.
3. This service is accessible to the public and does not require specialized training or licensing.
4. Consider deploying this system in areas where other communication systems are unavailable.
VHFRS, VHF radio, short-distance communication, EOC relay
Utilize Public Safety Mutual Aid Plans for Supplemental Communication
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Use existing public safety mutual aid plans to supplement communication needs during a disaster.

Procedure:
1. When normal communication systems are insufficient, agencies should first look to their own public safety mutual aid...
Objective: Use existing public safety mutual aid plans to supplement communication needs during a disaster.

Procedure:
1. When normal communication systems are insufficient, agencies should first look to their own public safety mutual aid plans.
2. These plans may provide access to additional resources and personnel for communication support.
3. If these plans are not sufficient, trained volunteers from the Amateur Radio Service (ARES/RACES) should be utilized.
4. Communication relay functions between disaster locations and EOCs/OES/OECs should be staffed by trained amateur radio operators.
public safety mutual aid, amateur radio service, EOC communication, disaster relay
Prioritize Normal Communication Systems
Standard Source: Disaster Communications
Objective: Use normal day-to-day communication systems whenever possible.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that all standard landline, cellular, and public safety radio systems are used first.
2. These systems should be staffed by public safety...
Objective: Use normal day-to-day communication systems whenever possible.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that all standard landline, cellular, and public safety radio systems are used first.
2. These systems should be staffed by public safety personnel at the municipal, county, state, or federal level.
3. Only escalate to alternative or supplemental communication systems when normal systems are overwhelmed or unavailable.
4. Return to normal systems as soon as they are restored or become adequate for the need.
normal communication systems, public safety radio, cellular networks, landline systems

Radio Procedures (5) -

Send a Situation Report (Sitrep) via Radio
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
A situation report (sitrep) is essential for informing monitoring groups or emergency services of your location and status.

Steps to send a sitrep:
1. Initiate the transmission: *WA 1 This is WA 2 Sitrep, Over.*
2. **Wait for...
A situation report (sitrep) is essential for informing monitoring groups or emergency services of your location and status.

Steps to send a sitrep:
1. Initiate the transmission: *WA 1 This is WA 2 Sitrep, Over.*
2. Wait for confirmation: *WA 2 This is WA 1 Send, Over.*
3. Provide detailed information:
- Location (GPS coordinates if available)
- Route and leg number
- Grid reference
- Heading (magnetic degrees)
- Status of the group (no problems or any issues)
- Estimated time of arrival at waypoints
4. End with *Over* to indicate you are ready for a reply.
5. Monitor for confirmation: *WA 2 This is WA 1 Roger, Out.*

This ensures that monitoring groups can track your progress and provide assistance if needed.
situation report, radio communication, emergency procedures, tracking location
Conduct a Radio Transmission Using Prowords
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Step-by-step procedure for conducting a radio transmission using prowords:

1. Identify the station calling and being called:
- Format: *WA 1 This is WA 2*
2. State the purpose of the transmission:
- Example: *Radio check* or...
Step-by-step procedure for conducting a radio transmission using prowords:

1. Identify the station calling and being called:
- Format: *WA 1 This is WA 2*
2. State the purpose of the transmission:
- Example: *Radio check* or *Sitrep*.
3. Provide necessary information:
- Include details such as location, heading, time of arrival at waypoints, etc.
4. Use prowords to end your message:
- Use *Over* if a reply is expected.
- Use *Out* if no reply is required.
5. Repeat or clarify messages when needed using prowords like *Say again*, *Verify*, or *Read back*.

Example transmission:
• WA 1: This is WA 2, Radio check, Over.
• WA 2: This is WA 1, You are loud and clear, how am I, Over.
• WA 1: This is WA 2, You are loud and clear, Over.
• WA 2: This is WA 1, Roger, Out.

This format ensures clarity and professionalism in all radio communications.
radio transmission, prowords usage, communication guidelines, emergency radio procedures
Understand Radio Abbreviations and Questions
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Interpret Abbreviation Questions: When an abbreviation is followed by a mark of interrogation (e.g., QSP?), it refers to the question indicated for that abbreviation.
2. Respond Appropriately: Use the corresponding...
Procedure:

1. Interpret Abbreviation Questions: When an abbreviation is followed by a mark of interrogation (e.g., QSP?), it refers to the question indicated for that abbreviation.
2. Respond Appropriately: Use the corresponding response as defined in the procedure for each abbreviation.

Purpose: This procedure helps users understand and respond correctly to radio abbreviations used in communication.
radio abbreviations, question format, Q codes
Use Prowords in Radio Communications
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Prowords are standardized words or phrases used in radio communications to keep transmissions brief and clear. Here is a list of common prowords and their meanings:

• Correct: You are correct.
• Correction: An error has been made; the...
Prowords are standardized words or phrases used in radio communications to keep transmissions brief and clear. Here is a list of common prowords and their meanings:

• Correct: You are correct.
• Correction: An error has been made; the correct message will be sent.
• I say again: Used when repeating all or part of a message.
• I spell: Indicates that you are about to spell the next word phonetically.
• Mayday: A distress signal used only in emergencies (not typically for land communications).
• Out: Transmission has ended; no reply is required.
• Over: Transmission has ended; a reply is expected.
• Read back: Receiving callsign repeats the message to confirm understanding.
• Roger: Message received and understood.
• Say again: Repeat your entire message when it is not understood.
• Send: Ready to receive a message.
• Sitrep: Situation report.
• Verify: Used when repeating part of a message that needs clarification.
• Wait: Indicates a pause is required in transmission.

Using these prowords ensures clarity and professionalism during radio communications.
prowords, radio communication, distress signals, emergency procedures, transmission guidelines
Use QSV Radio Communication Procedure
Standard Source: Radio Amateurs Handbook
Procedure:

1. Inquire About Public Correspondence: Use QSV to ask: *"Is public correspondence being handled?"
2. Respond to QSV: Answer with: *"Public correspondence is being handled. Please do not interfere."*

Note: Public...
Procedure:

1. Inquire About Public Correspondence: Use QSV to ask: *"Is public correspondence being handled?"
2. Respond to QSV: Answer with: *"Public correspondence is being handled. Please do not interfere."*

Note: Public correspondence refers to any radio work, official or private, on commercial wave lengths.

Purpose: This procedure is used for inquiring about and managing public radio communications.
QSV, public correspondence, radio communication

Radio Propagation (5) -

Calculate MUF by Hand for Shortwave Communication
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Gather the following information:
- Great circle distance between two stations.
- Zenith angle of the sun at the time of transmission.
- Sunspot number for the current period.
2. Use a reference book such as **PLANNING...
Procedure:

1. Gather the following information:
- Great circle distance between two stations.
- Zenith angle of the sun at the time of transmission.
- Sunspot number for the current period.
2. Use a reference book such as PLANNING AND ENGINEERING OF SHORTWAVE LINKS by Gerhard Braun to find step-by-step instructions for manual MUF calculation.
3. Follow the formulas and tables provided in the book to determine the appropriate frequency range for communication between two stations based on distance, sun angle, and ionospheric conditions.
4. This method is useful when computer programs are unavailable or not accessible.
manual MUF calculation, shortwave communication, propagation planning
Use Computer Programs for MUF Prediction Between Locations
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Obtain a computer program designed to calculate the Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) between two locations.
2. Input the following data into the program:
- Latitude and longitude of both stations.
- Month of the year.
-...
Procedure:

1. Obtain a computer program designed to calculate the Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) between two locations.
2. Input the following data into the program:
- Latitude and longitude of both stations.
- Month of the year.
- Sunspot number for that time period.
3. The program will generate MUF values at hourly intervals over a 24-hour period.
4. Be aware that calculated MUFs may differ from actual values by up to ±2 MHz, and accuracy improves with longer distances (above a few hundred miles).
5. One example of such a program is MINIMUF.
6. Consult your radio supplier or amateur radio sources for access to these programs.
MUF prediction, computer program, MINIMUF, propagation software
Determine Optimal Frequency Based on Distance Between Stations
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Measure the distance between two radio stations in kilometers.
2. Use the following general rule:
- For distances up to 100 km, the Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) is typically around 7-8 MHz during periods of low sunspot...
Procedure:

1. Measure the distance between two radio stations in kilometers.
2. Use the following general rule:
- For distances up to 100 km, the Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) is typically around 7-8 MHz during periods of low sunspot activity (e.g., sunspot number = 10).
- For longer distances (e.g., 1,300 km), higher MUF values are required (e.g., 12 MHz).
3. Note that at shorter distances, the ground wave may extend up to 20 km or more depending on terrain.
4. For reliable communication over ranges of 50-100 km, frequencies around 2 MHz may be necessary.
5. At higher frequencies (e.g., 27 MHz), communication beyond a few hundred kilometers is unlikely due to the lack of ionospheric reflection during periods of low sunspot activity.
distance, MUF, frequency selection, ground wave, HF communication
Understand the Impact of Sunspot Cycle on Radio Propagation
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize that the sunspot cycle is an 11-year cycle affecting ionospheric electron density.
2. During periods of high sunspot activity (e.g., sunspot maximum), the ionosphere becomes more reflective, allowing higher frequency...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that the sunspot cycle is an 11-year cycle affecting ionospheric electron density.
2. During periods of high sunspot activity (e.g., sunspot maximum), the ionosphere becomes more reflective, allowing higher frequency signals to propagate over long distances.
3. During low sunspot activity (e.g., sunspot minimum), the ionosphere is less dense, limiting propagation at higher frequencies.
4. Example: In 1980, high sunspot activity allowed CB radio communications on 27 MHz from Great Britain to Europe with only 4 watts of power. By the late 1980s, as sunspot numbers declined, these long-distance contacts became increasingly difficult due to reduced ionospheric reflection.
5. Use knowledge of the sunspot cycle (e.g., maxima in 1979/80 and 1990/91) to plan communication windows.
sunspot cycle, ionosphere, radio propagation, sunspot maximum, sunspot minimum
Understand the HF Band and Ionospheric Reflection
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Understanding how shortwave radio waves travel:

1. The HF (High Frequency) band covers frequencies from 2 to 30 MHz.
2. Radio waves travel in straight lines, but communication over long distances is possible due to the ionosphere, a...
Understanding how shortwave radio waves travel:

1. The HF (High Frequency) band covers frequencies from 2 to 30 MHz.
2. Radio waves travel in straight lines, but communication over long distances is possible due to the ionosphere, a layer of ionized gas located between 100 and 300 km above Earth's surface.
3. The ionosphere reflects radio waves back to Earth under certain conditions:
- Wave A: Reflected by the ionosphere (enabling long-distance communication)
- Wave B: Passes through the ionosphere without reflection (not useful for communication)
4. The effectiveness of reflection depends on the electron density in the ionosphere, which is influenced by:
- Time of day: Electron density increases from dawn to midday and decreases at night.
- Seasonal variation: Changes throughout the year.
- Sunspot cycle: An 11-year cycle that affects solar activity and electron density.
5. The Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) is the highest frequency that can be reflected by the ionosphere for communication between two points.
6. MUF varies:
- Throughout the day
- Seasonally
- With sunspot numbers (higher sunspot numbers = higher MUF)
7. Avoid using radio communications during dawn and dusk due to unstable ionospheric conditions.
ionosphere,reflection,HF band,MUF,electron density,sunspot cycle

Public Relations (5) -

Develop Communication Strategies for Pandemic Influenza Response
High Priority Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Procedure:

1. Create communication strategies tailored to different audiences including the public, government decision-makers, healthcare workers, mental health professionals, and law enforcement.
2. Ensure all communication respects privacy...
Procedure:

1. Create communication strategies tailored to different audiences including the public, government decision-makers, healthcare workers, mental health professionals, and law enforcement.
2. Ensure all communication respects privacy concerns and follows legal guidelines.
3. Use multiple channels such as social media, press releases, and community meetings for effective outreach.
4. Train spokespersons and ensure consistent messaging across all platforms.
communication strategy, pandemic response, public information
Develop and Practice a Public Information Strategy
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Prepare for the overwhelming volume of communications requests during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Create a Message Bank: Develop a catalog of approved messages to answer common questions from the press and public....
Objective: Prepare for the overwhelming volume of communications requests during a nuclear detonation.

Procedure:
1. Create a Message Bank: Develop a catalog of approved messages to answer common questions from the press and public. This should include both general and jurisdiction-specific responses.
2. Prioritize Information Requests: Establish clear priorities for responding to information needs from responders, the press, and the public. Practice this regularly through exercises.
3. Ensure Message Accessibility: Store approved messages in a format that allows easy access across jurisdictions (e.g., shared documents or databases). Ensure all relevant staff have access to these resources.
4. Plan for External Access: If your command structure requires external stakeholders to access the message bank, ensure systems support this and address any technological challenges in advance.

Notes:
• Reusing approved messages increases response timeliness and consistency.
• Regular exercises will help identify gaps or issues with the strategy before an incident occurs.
message bank, public information strategy, emergency communication, crisis messaging, nuclear response
Public Information Officer (PIO) Responsibilities During an Emergency
High Priority Source: Home Health Emergency Preparedness
- The Public Information Officer (PIO) will confer with the Incident Command Officer and other members of the Disaster Response Team to reach a joint decision regarding the information, if any, to be released to the media.
• The PIO will also be in...
- The Public Information Officer (PIO) will confer with the Incident Command Officer and other members of the Disaster Response Team to reach a joint decision regarding the information, if any, to be released to the media.
• The PIO will also be in charge of determining alternate means of contacting staff.
PIO responsibilities,incident command officer,disaster response team
Coordinate Public Information and Communications
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Role: Assistant Secretary for Public Affairs (ASPA)

Responsibilities:
1. Coordinates public information and communications.
2. Assists with public communication and education.
3. Coordinates development of after-action reports and lessons...
Role: Assistant Secretary for Public Affairs (ASPA)

Responsibilities:
1. Coordinates public information and communications.
2. Assists with public communication and education.
3. Coordinates development of after-action reports and lessons learned.
4. Maintains close communication with drug and vaccine manufacturers.
public,information,communication,pandemic,response
Use Simple and Action-Oriented Language in Preparedness Communication
Standard Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Title: Use Simple and Action-Oriented Language in Preparedness Communication

Procedure:
1. When communicating preparedness information to the public, use simple, non-technical language that is easy for people of all backgrounds to...
Title: Use Simple and Action-Oriented Language in Preparedness Communication

Procedure:
1. When communicating preparedness information to the public, use simple, non-technical language that is easy for people of all backgrounds to understand.
2. Focus on action-oriented messages that help constituents feel more in control and make informed decisions (e.g., “Get Inside, Stay Inside, and Stay Tuned”).
3. Empathetically address the public’s fear and concern by acknowledging their emotions and validating them.
4. Avoid using complex or jargon-filled language that may confuse or alienate people.

Details:
• This approach increases public trust and improves information retention.
• It is especially important in nuclear detonation preparedness, where the public may feel a sense of futility or fatalism.
• Use this strategy consistently across all outreach efforts to ensure clarity and effectiveness.
communication strategies, public trust, action-oriented language, nuclear preparedness, empathy in messaging

Public Health Communications (5) -

Create a National Communications Plan for Pandemic Response
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Objective: Increase public awareness and encourage preventive behaviors during a pandemic.

Procedure Steps:
1. Develop a national communications plan that includes clear, consistent messaging from government and health authorities.
2....
Objective: Increase public awareness and encourage preventive behaviors during a pandemic.

Procedure Steps:
1. Develop a national communications plan that includes clear, consistent messaging from government and health authorities.
2. Use multiple channels (e.g., social media, television, radio) to disseminate information about the outbreak, prevention measures, and available resources.
3. Ensure messages are culturally appropriate and accessible to all segments of the population, including vulnerable groups.
4. Provide guidance on good hygiene practices, such as handwashing, respiratory etiquette, and safe food handling.
5. Coordinate with media outlets to ensure accurate reporting and avoid misinformation during a public health crisis.
national communications plan, public awareness, hygiene practices, media coordination, pandemic messaging
Provide Public Information on Antivirals During a Pandemic
High Priority Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Procedure:

• Continuously provide information to health professionals and the public about antiviral availability, distribution, administration, side effects, and targeted use.
• Update communication materials as the situation evolves.
• Ensure...
Procedure:

• Continuously provide information to health professionals and the public about antiviral availability, distribution, administration, side effects, and targeted use.
• Update communication materials as the situation evolves.
• Ensure clarity in messaging regarding drug use, safety, and effectiveness.
• Coordinate with media outlets for timely dissemination of critical updates.
antiviral communication, public information, pandemic messaging
Accessing Crisis Communication Guidelines for Public Officials
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Communicating in a Crisis: Risk Communication Guidelines for Public Officials is a valuable resource for public officials during health crises. To access it:

1. Visit SAMHSA's website: Access the guidelines at...
Communicating in a Crisis: Risk Communication Guidelines for Public Officials is a valuable resource for public officials during health crises. To access it:

1. Visit SAMHSA's website: Access the guidelines at [http://www.riskcommunication.samhsa.gov/index.htm](http://www.riskcommunication.samhsa.gov/index.htm).

2. Order bound copies: Bound copies can be ordered for free from SAMHSA's National Mental Health Information Center:
- Online: [http://store.mentalhealth.org/publications/ordering.aspx](http://store.mentalhealth.org/publications/ordering.aspx)
- By phone: Call 1-800-789-2647.

3. Understand the content: This pocket reference describes basic skills and techniques for clear, effective crisis communications and provides tools of the trade for media relations.
crisis communication guidelines, public officials, SAMHSA resources, media relations tools
Using the CDCynergy CD-ROM Set for Pandemic Communication
