- Be aware that the person you are calling may be busy or unable to answer immediately.
- If the recipient is unavailable, rely on them to take detailed and accurate notes for later reference.

4. Minimize Time and Cost Considerations:
- Be mindful of potential costs (e.g., satellite phone calls can cost up to USD 8.00 per minute).
- Avoid wasting time by ensuring the call is necessary and well-planned.

5. Use Alternative Communication Methods When Possible:
- If the recipient is not available, consider calling them at home (if you have their number and permission).
- Use alternative methods such as email or messaging if they are more efficient for transmitting technical information.

Warnings/Notes:
• Be aware that voice communication via SSB radio or satellite may sound strange to new listeners.
• Ensure the recipient is familiar with simplex procedures or digitized voices, which may have long echo and delay.
• If notes are taken by someone else, ensure they are clear enough for another person to read and understand later.
phone calls, communication efficiency, technical information transfer, simplex procedures, satellite phone cost, time zones, note taking

Text Messages (1) -

Send a Text Message via Shortwave Radio
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Compose the message:
- Use only letters of the alphabet and numbers.
- Ensure clarity and coherence, avoiding unnecessary details or incoherent information.
- Compile the message in advance using a personal computer...
Procedure:

1. Compose the message:
- Use only letters of the alphabet and numbers.
- Ensure clarity and coherence, avoiding unnecessary details or incoherent information.
- Compile the message in advance using a personal computer for editing and review.
- Follow the principle: *'Measure it twice, cut it once.'*

2. Prepare equipment:
- Set up your communications system when ready (not necessarily immediately).
- Ensure that the equipment is functioning properly and has access to power if needed.

3. Send the message:
- Transmit the text message through a suitable communication channel, such as TELEX, Electronic Mailing, or via satellite systems like INMARSAT-C.
- If sending from a mobile unit in the field, ensure compatibility with FAX machines on an ordinary PSTN landline (SEMIFAX).

4. Ensure accuracy:
- The addressee will receive a 'hard copy' of the full text message, ensuring no distortion during transmission.
- If the recipient is unattended, the message can be left for them to retrieve and review at their convenience.

5. Benefits of sending via text:
- Avoids real-time pressure (unlike phone calls), allowing the addressee time to formulate a considered reply.
- Reduces costs significantly compared to voice communication (e.g., USD 6-8 for a short message vs. USD 20-60 for a short phone call via satellite).

6. Language considerations:
- Text messages can be handed over to someone more competent for translation, ensuring accurate understanding.
- Avoids the risk of miscommunication that often occurs during voice calls.
text message, shortwave radio, telex, electronic mailing, inmarsat-c, semifax, communication procedure

Data Backup And Recovery (1) -

Prepare Backup Strategy for Computer Files
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Create multiple backups of critical files:
- Use floppy disks or other removable storage media to back up your hard disk regularly.
- Ensure that both backup copies are stored in separate locations and loaded with...
Procedure:

1. Create multiple backups of critical files:
- Use floppy disks or other removable storage media to back up your hard disk regularly.
- Ensure that both backup copies are stored in separate locations and loaded with identical software.
2. Test backups periodically:
- Verify that the backup files can be accessed and used without issues.
3. Use compatible formats for file exchange:
- Save files in plain text ASCII format (*.TXT) to minimize compatibility problems during data transfer.
4. Avoid sending unreadable or corrupt files:
- Corrupt or incompatible files may cause communication failures and reduce professionalism.
5. Implement virus protection measures:
- Check all floppy disks and other removable media for viruses before using them on your computer.
6. Use licensed software only:
- Licensed software is less likely to cause unexpected crashes or data loss.
7. Keep spare hardware available:
- Carry at least two identical computers with compatible hardware and software in case one fails.
8. Test all equipment together:
- Ensure that both computers work properly with the communications gear before deployment in the field.
backup,computer,file,software,virus,format,compatibility

Text Communication Methods (1) -

Use Fax and Telex for Text Communication in the Field
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Send text messages via fax or telex:
- Use a FAX machine to send text messages from the field to an office location.
- If sending a message from the office to a field team, fax it to BT MARINE's Portishead Radio station...
Procedure:

1. Send text messages via fax or telex:
- Use a FAX machine to send text messages from the field to an office location.
- If sending a message from the office to a field team, fax it to BT MARINE's Portishead Radio station and then have it TELEXed from there.
- Alternatively, fax the message to a bureau or friendly Telex operator for relay.
2. Ensure compatibility of communication formats:
- Text messages cannot support graphical information such as maps, photos, or diagrams.
3. Use plain text format for file exchange:
- Prepare files in plain text ASCII format (*.TXT) to avoid issues with incompatible formats.
4. Receive text messages automatically:
- Any FAX machine back at the office can be used to receive a text message automatically (by Semi-Fax).
fax,telex,text,message,format,compatibility

Printing In The Field (1) -

Prepare and Use a Portable Printer in the Field
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Carry a portable printer:
- A printer is necessary to print out messages on paper for delivery to individuals who cannot access a computer screen.
2. Ensure compatibility with field equipment:
- Purchase only...
Procedure:

1. Carry a portable printer:
- A printer is necessary to print out messages on paper for delivery to individuals who cannot access a computer screen.
2. Ensure compatibility with field equipment:
- Purchase only printers recommended for portable operation, as many models (especially laser printers) may fail if moved roughly.
3. Prepare necessary supplies:
- Bring interface cables, printer driver programs, power supply systems, and sufficient paper and ink cartridges specific to the printer model.
4. Limit use of large data volumes:
- Large files such as detailed maps or diagrams may be impractical to print with a small portable printer.
5. Use larger printers for complex documents:
- For printing anything worthwhile, consider using large, heavy, and expensive mains-driven color printers if available.
printer,portable,field,equipment,supplies,compatibility

Fax Usage (1) -

Set Up and Use a FAX Machine for Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Connect the FAX machine to a PSTN phone line:
- Ensure the FAX is connected to an ordinary landline telephone network (PSTN).
- If using satellite or HF radio, ensure compatibility with INMARSAT-A, INMARSAT-B, or...
Procedure:

1. Connect the FAX machine to a PSTN phone line:
- Ensure the FAX is connected to an ordinary landline telephone network (PSTN).
- If using satellite or HF radio, ensure compatibility with INMARSAT-A, INMARSAT-B, or INMARSAT-M terminals and use a special port for FAX support.

2. Prepare the document to be sent:
- Place the document on the FAX machine's glass plate.
- Ensure the paper is compatible with your model (some models require special paper).

3. Enter the recipient’s phone number:
- Use the keypad or menu options to input the destination number.

4. Initiate the transmission:
- Press the 'Send' button or use the appropriate command on the machine.
- The FAX will scan and send the document as data via the PSTN line, usually taking less than 1 minute per page depending on detail.

5. Monitor the process:
- Ensure the machine is in a well-lit, dry location with no wind to prevent paper from blowing away.
- If using a portable model, avoid bumps or movement that could damage the document quality.

6. Check for received FAXes periodically:
- The FAX machine will automatically store incoming messages and may print them out depending on settings.
- Review the journal log to track sent/received messages.

7. Use additional features if available:
- Some models can also function as a photocopier, printer, or image scanner (use 'Local Copy' feature).

Notes:
• FAX is ideal for sending graphical information such as maps, diagrams, and handwritten text in various languages.
• It is useful in situations where showing a copy of an official document with stamps and signatures may be necessary (e.g., at checkpoints or borders).
fax, pstn, fax machine, communication, sending documents, graphical information, portable fax

Fax Troubleshooting (1) -

Troubleshoot Common FAX Issues in the Field
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Address sticky or jammed paper in hot/humid conditions:
- If the paper becomes sticky and won’t pass through, try feeding two sheets at once to bypass the issue.

2. Ensure compatibility with special paper:
- Carry...
Procedure:

1. Address sticky or jammed paper in hot/humid conditions:
- If the paper becomes sticky and won’t pass through, try feeding two sheets at once to bypass the issue.

2. Ensure compatibility with special paper:
- Carry sufficient quantities of the correct type of paper required by your FAX model (some models require specific types).

3. Avoid environmental damage:
- Place the machine in a dry, well-lit area without wind to prevent incoming sheets from blowing away.
- Use a portable model that is not sensitive to bumps or movement, as some plain paper FAXes may produce poor results when moved.

4. Handle power requirements:
- Ensure the FAX machine has access to mains power or regular battery charging if it’s portable.

5. Avoid using FAX for repeated transmissions:
- FAX documents become unreadable after about 4 re-generations, so avoid passing them on by FAX.
- Instead, request the sender to send a copy of the original directly to the intended recipient.

6. Use alternative methods if needed:
- If using a PC for FAX, ensure it is programmed correctly and that you have access to a printer for hard copies.
- Note that the PC may not be usable for other tasks while waiting for an incoming call.

7. Consider semi-FAX services (if available):
- Use BT’s automatic service to convert text messages from INMARSAT-C or HF Radio Telex terminals into a FAX image and send it to a specified number.
- Note that this only sends text, not pictures, and works one-way (from mobile to FAX machine).
fax troubleshooting, sticky paper, portable fax, power requirements, semi-fax, bt service

Fax Limitations (1) -

Understand the Limitations of FAX Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize bandwidth inefficiency:
- FAX is not efficient in its use of bandwidth or data, which can result in slow transmission speeds over Standard-M terminals.

2. Avoid re-transmitting FAX documents:
- Received...
Procedure:

1. Recognize bandwidth inefficiency:
- FAX is not efficient in its use of bandwidth or data, which can result in slow transmission speeds over Standard-M terminals.

2. Avoid re-transmitting FAX documents:
- Received FAXes become unreadable after about 4 re-generations, so avoid passing them on by FAX.
- Instead, request the sender to send a copy of the original directly to the intended recipient.

3. Handle document storage and retrieval:
- Once received, there is no way to digitally process or input the message into a word processor unless it is retyped.
- Store FAXes as physical paper documents for reference.

4. Consider alternatives for data sharing:
- Electronic mail (email) is more efficient and preferred by technical professionals due to its ability to handle digital text and graphics without degradation.

5. Use semi-FAX services when appropriate:
- Use BT’s automatic service to convert text messages from INMARSAT-C or HF Radio Telex terminals into a FAX image for one-way transmission (from mobile to FAX machine).
- Note that this only sends text, not pictures.

6. Be aware of power requirements:
- FAX machines require mains power or regular battery charging if they are portable.
fax limitations, bandwidth inefficiency, document degradation, semi-fax, power requirements

Internet Email (1) -

Set Up Internet Electronic Mail Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Use a personal computer to connect to an 'Host' (a special computer running mailing software and connected to the Internet) via:
- Local Area Network (LAN) wiring in your building, or
- A dial-up PSTN phone line using a...
Procedure:

1. Use a personal computer to connect to an 'Host' (a special computer running mailing software and connected to the Internet) via:
- Local Area Network (LAN) wiring in your building, or
- A dial-up PSTN phone line using a modem.
2. Ensure the start of your message includes the Internet address of the addressee, which is formatted like: 'eus.eusmwoo@memo.ericsson.se'.
3. Compose and send your message through the host to the recipient's mailbox.
4. The message will be passed from host to host until it reaches the recipient’s designated mailbox.
5. The recipient must connect their computer to the host again to retrieve the message, which will appear on their screen or be printed out.

Important Notes:
• There is no automatic alert system (like a bell or bleeper) to notify users of incoming messages. Users may not know urgent emails are pending until they log in next.
• Messages can take hours or even days to reach the recipient, so it's best to use an alternative method (e.g., phone call) for urgent communications.
internet email, electronic mail, host computer, PSTN dial-up, LAN connection, message delivery, urgent communication

Networking (1) -

Establish and Maintain a Communication Network for Emergency Situations
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
### Procedure: Establish and Maintain a Communication Network for Emergency Situations

Overview:
Maintaining an effective communication network is crucial during emergencies, especially when individuals or teams are on the move. This...
### Procedure: Establish and Maintain a Communication Network for Emergency Situations

Overview:
Maintaining an effective communication network is crucial during emergencies, especially when individuals or teams are on the move. This procedure outlines how to set up and manage such a network using HF radios, satellite terminals, and other communication tools.

### Steps to Establish a Communication Network
1. Identify Key Contacts:
- Determine who you need to communicate with (e.g., colleagues, sponsors, support teams).
- Know their preferred method of contact (radio, satellite terminal, phone, or Telex).
- Identify their manager and assistant for faster communication.

2. Track Equipment Availability:
- Keep track of who has the equipment you need and where it is located.
- Note when you can expect to receive or access the required equipment.
- Be aware of who may need your equipment and when they might require it.

3. Coordinate Communication Schedules (Skeds):
- Agree on scheduled reporting times for all team members, including yourself.
- Ensure that everyone reports their position, status, and who is with them.
- Include the organization each person belongs to so messages can be expedited.

4. Use a Traffic Dispatcher:
- Assign a traffic dispatcher back at base who tracks all communication information and relays it to field teams.
- This individual should be proactive in gathering and sharing information, acting as a central point of contact for all communications.

5. Utilize HF Radio Simplex Mode:
- Use HF radio in simplex mode for direct point-to-point communication when needed.
- Know the frequencies and times your colleagues monitor for updates or assistance.

6. Participate in Daily 'Round Table' Chat Shows:
- Attend local team chat shows where teams discuss daily challenges and support each other.
- Know the frequency and time these sessions occur to stay informed and connected.

7. Report Status Regularly:
- Report your position, status, and who is with you at scheduled times (Skeds).
- This ensures that all team members are accounted for and can be reached if needed.

8. Use a Single Contact Number for Retrieval:
- If you do not have an exhaustive phone directory, use one central number to retrieve the rest of the information.
- Your coast radio station can assist in finding contacts if warned in advance.

### Additional Considerations
• Power Management: Communications gear may only be switched on for a few hours a day due to power budgeting. Ensure you know when your equipment is active and available.
• Security: Avoid leaving communication devices unattended, as this can pose security risks.
• Cost Efficiency: Use HF radio in simplex mode directly from point to point to save money on satellite or phone calls.

### Example: BBC Traffic Dispatcher System
• Journalists call a 'Traffic' number at the BBC to receive messages and dictate reports.
• Reports are either tape recorded for transmission or typed out, as seen when you see a photo of the journalist and hear their voice on TV news.

### Conclusion:
By establishing and maintaining an effective communication network, teams can stay connected, share critical information, and respond efficiently to emergencies.
communication network, HF radio, simplex mode, traffic dispatcher, emergency communication, satellite terminal, reporting schedule, Skeds, power management

Message Formatting (1) -

Format a Trafficking Message with Preamble and Tracking Information
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Formatting a Trafficking Message:

1. Start with a unique message number in the preamble format: `=AIDCAMP4` (example: Aidcamp base 4 sent that day).
2. Include the date and time of sending in GMT (UTC) format, e.g.,...
Procedure for Formatting a Trafficking Message:

1. Start with a unique message number in the preamble format: `=AIDCAMP4` (example: Aidcamp base 4 sent that day).
2. Include the date and time of sending in GMT (UTC) format, e.g., `941005 1058`.
3. Specify the addressee clearly in the preamble, e.g., `=TO BLOGGS AIDBASELONDON`.
4. Reference received messages by number to confirm acknowledgment, e.g., `=RECEIVED YOUR AIDBASELONDON 3, 4 AND 7.`
5. Include details of the message content, such as arrivals or questions (e.g., `=DR SMITH AND NURSE JONES ARRIVED 0900 TODAY. WHERE IS THEIR EQUIPMENT?`).
6. End with a marker to indicate the end of the message, e.g., `+` or `more..` if more messages are expected.

Example:
```
=AIDCAMP4 941005 1058
=TO BLOGGS AIDBASELONDON
=RECEIVED YOUR AIDBASELONDON 3, 4 AND 7.
=DR SMITH AND NURSE JONES ARRIVED 0900 TODAY. WHERE IS THEIR EQUIPMENT?
=BILLBROWN+
```

Purpose: This format ensures messages are uniquely identifiable and can be tracked for confirmation of receipt.
trafficking message, preamble, tracking, message number, GMT time, addressee, log book, communication protocol

Network Testing (1) -

Perform Echo Tests to Verify Communication Links
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Conducting Echo Tests:

1. Send a test message periodically to stations below you in the network hierarchy.
2. Ask the recipient to reply with the time they received and read the message.
3. Use this feedback to...
Procedure for Conducting Echo Tests:

1. Send a test message periodically to stations below you in the network hierarchy.
2. Ask the recipient to reply with the time they received and read the message.
3. Use this feedback to determine how long it takes for messages to reach their destination.
4. If no response is received, suspect a technical fault and investigate or repair it before an emergency call could be lost.
5. Revise procedures if delays are consistently observed, to improve communication speed.
6. Note that echo tests cost money and should only be used when there is insufficient regular traffic to build confidence in the link.

Purpose: Echo tests help verify the reliability of communication links and identify potential issues before they become critical.
echo test, communication verification, network testing, message delay, technical fault detection

International Coordination (1) -

Utilizing the UN OSOCC for Disaster Communications
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Access information about the United Nations Department of Humanitarian Affairs (UNDHA) On Site Operations Co-ordination Centre (OSOCC).
2. The OSOCC is a field communications centre equipped with Satellite, HF, and VHF...
Procedure:

1. Access information about the United Nations Department of Humanitarian Affairs (UNDHA) On Site Operations Co-ordination Centre (OSOCC).
2. The OSOCC is a field communications centre equipped with Satellite, HF, and VHF equipment.
3. It links to a control room in Geneva for coordination during disasters.
4. Contact the UNDHA telecommunications centre at the Palais des Nations in Geneva for details on how to work with them.
5. Use this resource to enhance communication capabilities during large-scale emergencies.
UN OSOCC, disaster communications, satellite radio

Network Infrastructure (1) -

Understanding Bearer Network Function and Failure Impact
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand that the Bearer Network is a shared transmission infrastructure used by multiple services (PSTN, Telex, ISDN) for economic reasons.
2. Recognize that if the bearer network fails, all dependent services may be lost...
Procedure:

1. Understand that the Bearer Network is a shared transmission infrastructure used by multiple services (PSTN, Telex, ISDN) for economic reasons.
2. Recognize that if the bearer network fails, all dependent services may be lost simultaneously.
3. Plan communication strategies with this in mind to avoid complete system failure during disasters.
4. Consider redundancy or alternative systems to mitigate the impact of such failures.
bearer network, service dependency, disaster recovery

Role of Qualified Radio Officers in Disaster Communications

Procedure:

1. Identify and recruit qualified Radio Officers for disaster communications duties.
2. Ensure they are trained to a high level (up to 3-year HND) with expertise in both normal and emergency message handling.
3. Verify that they hold...
Procedure:

1. Identify and recruit qualified Radio Officers for disaster communications duties.
2. Ensure they are trained to a high level (up to 3-year HND) with expertise in both normal and emergency message handling.
3. Verify that they hold international permits, such as a Marine Radio General Certificate.
4. Consider their background in ships, expeditions, or the military for optimal performance in disaster scenarios.
radio officer training, disaster communication roles, international permits

Understanding Message Forwarding in Telex Systems

2. Ensure that message forwarding is properly configured and monitored during disaster scenarios.
3. Confirm with the...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that messages arriving at one telex may be forwarded to another for delivery to the addressee.
2. Ensure that message forwarding is properly configured and monitored during disaster scenarios.
3. Confirm with the recipient that the message has been passed on if necessary.

Using INMARSAT-C and HF Radio in Moving Vehicles
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

2. Ensure these systems are available and tested during disaster scenarios where mobility is required.
3....
Procedure:

1. Understand that only INMARSAT-C and HF radio (in good conditions) can be used for communication in a moving vehicle.
2. Ensure these systems are available and tested during disaster scenarios where mobility is required.
3. Consider cordless phones that can connect to some models of INMARSAT terminals for added flexibility.
INMARSAT-C, HF radio in vehicles, mobile disaster communication

Disaster Relief (1) -

Understand Communication Challenges in Disaster Relief Teams
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Understanding communication challenges within disaster relief teams is crucial for effective coordination.

1. Identify the scope of communication needs: In an area of approximately 100KM around a given point, communication between team...
Understanding communication challenges within disaster relief teams is crucial for effective coordination.

1. Identify the scope of communication needs: In an area of approximately 100KM around a given point, communication between team members or groups can range from simple to complex.
2. Assess the complexity of communication systems: A basic setup may suffice for some situations, while more complicated systems may be necessary in others depending on the scenario and requirements.
3. Consider all practical communication systems: The Disaster Relief Communications Foundation (DRCF) has compiled information on various communication systems used in disaster relief scenarios.
4. Note that mentioning a system does not imply endorsement: The DRCF provides factual information to help readers make informed decisions about which communication systems are best suited for their needs.
5. Recognize the limitations of the DRCF: As a small NGO with limited resources, the DRCF strives to provide accurate and valid information based on expert opinions but welcomes input from others for future revisions.
communication systems, disaster relief, team coordination, communication challenges, DRCF

Field Telephony (1) -

Set Up a Field Telephone System Using Local Battery (LB) Technology
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure for Setting Up a Field Telephone System Using Local Battery (LB) Technology:

1. Identify the Components Required:
- Two or more field telephones equipped with magneto handles or 'call'/'ring' buttons.
- A length of wire...
Procedure for Setting Up a Field Telephone System Using Local Battery (LB) Technology:

1. Identify the Components Required:
- Two or more field telephones equipped with magneto handles or 'call'/'ring' buttons.
- A length of wire suitable for connecting the phones. This can be jumper wire, field telephone wire, wire fences, or any other available conductor with an earth return.
- No external power source is required as the system is self-contained and powered by a built-in 3V battery (polarising battery) in each phone.

2. Connect the Phones Using Wire:
- Use any suitable wire to connect the phones together. The wiring can be done using large reels of jumper wire or field telephone wire, which are inexpensive and widely available.
- If using a fence or earth return system, ensure that the connection is secure and insulated where necessary to prevent short circuits or interference.

3. Powering the System:
- The phones do not require external power; they are self-contained with their own 3V batteries (polarising battery). These can be any standard disposable battery such as a bicycle or torch battery, and voltage is not critical.
- The system is activated either by lifting the phone off the hook or pressing the 'press to talk' button on the handset.

4. Testing the System:
- After connecting the phones and ensuring that all wiring is secure, test the system by cranking the magneto handle (or pressing the 'call'/'ring' button) on one phone. The bell on the other phone should ring, indicating a successful connection.

5. Using the System:
- To communicate, lift the receiver off the hook or press the 'press to talk' button on your handset. Speak into the microphone and wait for the other party to respond.
- The system is designed to be used in any environment where a cable can be laid, making it highly reliable even in adverse conditions.

6. Battery Maintenance:
- The built-in 3V batteries have a long lifespan and may only need replacement once per year or less, depending on usage. In some cases, the same battery has been known to function for up to three years without failure.
- Even when nearly flat, the system will still work, ensuring that it does not fail unexpectedly during critical situations.

7. Advantages of Field Telephones:
- They are immune to interference and channel overloading issues common with walkie-talkies.
- Privacy is enhanced as eavesdropping would require line tapping, which is more difficult to do without being detected.
- They can be used in any location where a cable can be installed, making them highly versatile for field use.

8. Limitations and Considerations:
- The range of the system depends on how much wire you have available and how quickly it can be deployed to the site. Over 100km is theoretically possible, but practical limitations may apply.
- Unlike walkie-talkies, field telephones are not affected by physical obstructions such as buildings or terrain dips, ensuring consistent performance in most environments.
field telephone, local battery system, magneto handle, polarising battery, communications setup, wired communication, emergency communication

Radio Terms And Usage (1) -

Understand and Use Alternative Terms for Walkie-Talkies
Reference Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Understanding Alternative Names for Walkie-Talkies:

• R/T or R/T set: This stands for *Radio Telephone*. It was used to distinguish from Wireless Telegraph (morse code) in the 1920s. Today, it is obsolete due to the rise of cellular...
Understanding Alternative Names for Walkie-Talkies:

• R/T or R/T set: This stands for *Radio Telephone*. It was used to distinguish from Wireless Telegraph (morse code) in the 1920s. Today, it is obsolete due to the rise of cellular networks but may still be used in regulations.

• Two-way radio: Refers to a device that allows communication between two parties. Avoid using this term if you are broadcasting, as it could lead to legal issues with government inspectors.

• P.R.set (Personal Radio Set): A term used by police forces for individual radios issued to officers.

• Portable radio: Designed to be carried in the hand and used while on the move. This is different from a *transportable* radio, which has handles and an internal battery but is intended to be placed on a table when not being carried.

• CB sets (Citizen's Band): Refers to radios operating under specific regulations for public use. They are technically similar to other radios but have unique legal considerations.

• Amateur Radio or HAM Radio: Refers to radio operations governed by amateur radio laws and regulations, not the equipment itself.

• Handie Talkie (HT): A modern term popular with amateur radio enthusiasts and manufacturers.

• Handset: Typically refers to a telephone handset but can also refer to a radio with a microphone designed to look like a telephone.

• Handheld: A widely accepted, straightforward term for a portable two-way radio.
walkie-talkie, r/t set, two-way radio, pr set, cb sets, ham radio, handie talkie, ht, handset, handheld

Base Station Setup (1) -

Establish a Base Station for Reliable Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select a permanent location: Choose a spot with an unobstructed view and optimal elevation for the antenna, ideally in a secure area where it can remain undisturbed.
2. Install a high-quality external antenna: Use a...
Procedure:

1. Select a permanent location: Choose a spot with an unobstructed view and optimal elevation for the antenna, ideally in a secure area where it can remain undisturbed.
2. Install a high-quality external antenna: Use a specialized antenna such as a dipole or helical counterpoise whip that is designed for long-term use and maximum signal efficiency.
3. Connect to a fixed radio setup: Leave the radio permanently connected to the external antenna, ensuring it remains in place and ready for immediate use.
4. Ensure security of equipment: If the location is unsecured, consider having someone on-site to monitor the base station or implement additional security measures (e.g., locks, alarms).
5. Document communication protocols: Establish procedures for relaying messages between handheld radios and the base station, allowing one person at the base station to act as a central hub for message passing.
6. Train personnel on setup and use: Ensure that anyone who may need to access or maintain the base station is trained in its operation and maintenance.

Notes:
• A base station can significantly improve communication reliability, especially in areas where handheld radios are limited by range or interference.
• The key factor for success is the antenna's position, particularly its height, rather than the power of the transmitter.
base station setup, communication hub, permanent antenna, signal reliability, radio security

Trunked Repeater Systems (1) -

Configure Trunked Repeater Systems for Wider Coverage
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify coverage areas: Determine the area of operation and compare it with the current repeater's coverage area.
2. Install additional repeaters: If the area of operation exceeds the coverage of a single repeater,...
Procedure:

1. Identify coverage areas: Determine the area of operation and compare it with the current repeater's coverage area.
2. Install additional repeaters: If the area of operation exceeds the coverage of a single repeater, install another repeater on a suitable site that can cover the new area.
3. Link repeaters together:
- Use either a landline link or a radio link to couple the two repeaters.
4. Program radios with multiple frequencies: Ensure users have their radios programmed with the frequency for each repeater in the system.
5. Use RACES (Radio Amateur Civil Emergency Service): If available, utilize RACES to allow amateur radio service repeaters to relay communications across different repeaters upon a command tone from a handheld station.
6. Implement multi-channel 'pool' systems: Allow each repeater to have multiple channels for use by different services.
7. Enable automatic channel scanning:
- Ensure the radios are equipped with control systems that can automatically scan for the strongest signal and re-tune accordingly.
8. Program radios correctly: Ensure all radios are properly programmed to function within the trunked system.
9. Use advanced trunked systems like EDACS:
- These systems provide digital scrambling, preventing unauthorized overhearing.
- Allow handsets to 'join' and 'unjoin' groups as needed for coordination (e.g., Red Cross units joining local emergency services).
10. Obtain specialized radios: Ensure users have radios that are specifically programmed to work with the trunked network, as ordinary radios may not function properly.

Notes:
• Trunked systems provide wide-area coverage and simplify radio operation, making it feel like a simplex walkie-talkie.
• However, these systems require specialized equipment and setup by engineers, which makes them unsuitable for the initial phase of a disaster but ideal for pre-planned regional emergency services.
trunked repeater systems,wider coverage,radio link,landline link,RACES,EDACS,multi-channel pool system

Radio Etiquette (1) -

Prepare for CB Band Etiquette and Discipline Issues
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:
1. Be prepared to deal with poor discipline on the CB band, as many users are untrained in communication procedures.
2. Expect potential hostility or 'jamming' from other users who may resent non-personal use of the band.
3. Avoid...
Procedure:
1. Be prepared to deal with poor discipline on the CB band, as many users are untrained in communication procedures.
2. Expect potential hostility or 'jamming' from other users who may resent non-personal use of the band.
3. Avoid using the band for commercial purposes, as this can lead to conflicts with other users.

Notes:
• CB is often used by recreational operators and may not be suitable for serious communication needs.
• Users may attempt to intimidate you into switching to proper commercial frequencies.
CB etiquette,jamming,discipline,hostility

Amateur Radio (1) -

Use Amateur Radio Bands for Emergency Communications
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand Legal Requirements: Ensure that you or someone in your team has an amateur radio licence. If not, find a licensed operator who can supervise and operate the equipment under their direct supervision.

2. **Access...
Procedure:

1. Understand Legal Requirements: Ensure that you or someone in your team has an amateur radio licence. If not, find a licensed operator who can supervise and operate the equipment under their direct supervision.

2. Access International Frequency Bands: Amateur radio bands are defined by international law (ITU Resolution 640), allowing immediate use of agreed frequencies without waiting for allocation.

3. Utilize Repeaters and Packet Nodes: Leverage existing amateur repeater systems and packet nodes in the local area to enhance communication range and reliability.

4. Follow Discipline and Etiquette: Adhere to good behaviour standards, avoid hogging channels, and refrain from causing interference to other operators.

5. Avoid Prolonged Use for Aid Operations: Using amateur bands for extended aid operations is illegal in some jurisdictions. Limit use to logistical matters unless explicitly permitted by local regulations.

6. Maintain Confidentiality: Avoid sending confidential or coded messages over amateur radio, as it is required to be in 'plain language'. Consider using packet radio with data scrambling if confidentiality is a concern.

7. Coordinate with Local Amateurs: Engage with local amateur radio operators for advice on deployment, hardware needs, and understanding of local repeater systems.

8. Prepare for Legal Frameworks: Be aware that invoking ITU Resolution 640 provides a legal framework to operate in crisis situations, but you must comply with locally applicable regulations.

Warnings:
• Operating without supervision or licence may result in legal consequences.
• Sending confidential information over amateur radio is not permitted and can compromise mission integrity.
amateur radio, ham radio, emergency communications, ITU Resolution 640, repeater systems, packet nodes, legal requirements, frequency bands

Data Radio (1) -

Implementing Data Radio Systems for Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
### Why Use Data Radio Systems
Data radio systems such as G Packet and G Tor are commonly used for transmitting data over radio waves. While they offer advanced communication capabilities, they require additional equipment like a Terminal Node...
### Why Use Data Radio Systems
Data radio systems such as G Packet and G Tor are commonly used for transmitting data over radio waves. While they offer advanced communication capabilities, they require additional equipment like a Terminal Node Controller (TNC), a computer or terminal, and special circuits.

### Considerations Before Implementation
• Cost: Data systems may be more expensive due to the need for specialized hardware and software.
• Reliability: Computers and data systems are not always reliable. Ensure you have a backup plan in case of failure.
• Simplicity: If possible, use simpler communication methods as a fallback, such as voice checks at scheduled times.

### Benefits of Data Systems
• Messages can be sent and received asynchronously, allowing the recipient to read them when convenient.
• No need for constant monitoring or real-time interaction.
• Can be used with regular radios if functioning properly.

### Implementation Steps
1. Acquire Necessary Equipment: Obtain a TNC (Terminal Node Controller) and connect it to your radio's microphone and headphone sockets.
2. Connect Terminal Device: Attach either a computer or a terminal device, such as a thermal printer with a keyboard (e.g., TI703), to the TNC.
3. Send Messages: Type `connect station 1` into the terminal to establish a connection. Once connected, type your message and it will be transmitted to the other end.
4. End Communication: Type `disconnect` to terminate the session. The system will confirm with `disconnected`.

### Example Scenario
• If the recipient is not available when you send a message, they can read it later at their convenience, eliminating the need for real-time interaction.

### Precautions
• Always have a backup communication method in case of failure (e.g., voice checks or simplex radio).
• Ensure your equipment is charged and reliable before deployment.
data radio,packet radio,G Packet,G Tor,TNC,terminal node controller,backup communication,voice check

Teleprinter Radio (1) -

Configure Teleprinter Over Radio (TOR) Communication
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure your TNC is connected to the radio and configured for TOR mode.
2. Type in the receiving station's selective callsign on the keyboard.
3. Wait for a 'connected' indication from the terminal, which confirms successful...
Procedure:

1. Ensure your TNC is connected to the radio and configured for TOR mode.
2. Type in the receiving station's selective callsign on the keyboard.
3. Wait for a 'connected' indication from the terminal, which confirms successful establishment of the link.
4. Begin typing your message; it will be sent three characters at a time with automatic error checking and retransmission if necessary.
5. The recipient will receive the message exactly as typed on their screen or paper printer.
6. No user intervention is required for error correction, as this process is handled automatically by the system.

Notes:
• TOR can operate unattended, freeing users for other tasks.
• It is used by organizations such as the International Red Cross (CICR).
• Mailboxes are also available for TOR, similar to those in packet radio systems.
tor,sitor,amtor,radio communication,error correction

Telephone Extension (1) -

Setting Up a Line Extender for Telephone Service Extension
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify a location with an existing working telephone system.
- This is typically in a town or area where the local phone infrastructure is operational.
2. Install the line extender station at this location.
-...
Procedure:

1. Identify a location with an existing working telephone system.
- This is typically in a town or area where the local phone infrastructure is operational.
2. Install the line extender station at this location.
- Connect a standard telephone line to the station.
3. At the remote end, connect a telephone to a special conversion device.
- This device automatically converts signals for transmission over radio frequencies.
4. Ensure compatibility with multiple mobile devices if needed.
- Some versions of line extenders allow multiple mobiles to access the extended line simultaneously.
5. Test the connection.
- Verify that calls can be made and received at both ends of the line extension.

Notes:
• Line extenders are compact, cost-effective, and widely used in areas where traditional wiring is not available.
• However, their effectiveness depends on the quality of the local telephone exchange they are connected to.
• In disaster scenarios, reliance solely on this method may be risky due to potential damage or overload of the local phone system.
• For long-term aid missions, line extenders can provide a low-cost solution for extending telephone service.
line extender, radio in the local loop, telephone extension, disaster communication, emergency phone setup

Legal Frameworks (1) -

Participate in International Legal Agreements for Emergency Telecommunications
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Stay informed about legal developments: In 1996, an *International Convention on Emergency Telecommunications* was introduced to simplify the process of setting up emergency communications systems.
2. **Understand the purpose...
Procedure:

1. Stay informed about legal developments: In 1996, an *International Convention on Emergency Telecommunications* was introduced to simplify the process of setting up emergency communications systems.
2. Understand the purpose of the convention: The goal is to make moving equipment across borders easier and allow for rapid setup of emergency communication systems by introducing new legislation.
3. Be aware of terms and conditions: While many countries may agree to waive usual red tape in acute emergencies, there will be specific terms and conditions that foreign rescue teams must meet.
4. Seek guidance from DRCF: The DRCF can help explain the legal framework and requirements for setting up emergency communications systems internationally.

Note: This convention is intended to streamline processes during disasters but requires compliance with established regulations.
international convention,emergency telecommunications,legal framework,DRCF

Training Programs (1) -

Train Key Staff in Emergency Telecommunications
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the need for training: It is essential to provide better training for key staff involved in emergency telecommunications.
2. Develop practical and theoretical courses: Courses should be designed to meet the needs...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the need for training: It is essential to provide better training for key staff involved in emergency telecommunications.
2. Develop practical and theoretical courses: Courses should be designed to meet the needs of decision-makers who may not have a background in telecommunications but require up-to-date knowledge of modern technology.
3. Keep language accessible: Training materials should use clear, non-technical language that is easy for busy professionals to understand and apply.
4. Collaborate with organizations: The DRCF and other groups are working on establishing frameworks for defining target groups and developing training programs.
5. Offer comprehensive coverage: Courses should cover all aspects of emergency telecommunications, from theory to practical application, in a coordinated manner.
6. Update resources regularly: As new information becomes available, the book *Disaster Communications* will be updated and expanded with input from experts.
7. Create smaller informational booklets: Additional materials on popular topics may be developed as supplementary guides for specific needs.

Note: These efforts aim to ensure that decision-makers are well-informed and capable of making appropriate use of telecommunications technology during emergencies.
emergency training,telecommunications education,DRCF,course development

Power Supply Planning (1) -

Planning a Reliable Power Supply for Communication Systems
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure the communication system has a reliable power supply.

• Planning an adequate power supply requires careful thought and consideration of long-term reliability.

Key considerations:
• Backup power sources (e.g., solar,...
Objective: Ensure the communication system has a reliable power supply.

• Planning an adequate power supply requires careful thought and consideration of long-term reliability.

Key considerations:
• Backup power sources (e.g., solar, batteries, generators) should be included in all installations.
• Power requirements must match the equipment's specifications to avoid failure during critical times.
• Redundancy is essential for disaster scenarios where power outages are likely.
power supply planning, communication systems, backup power, redundancy

Legal Compliance (1) -

Navigating Political and Bureaucratic Challenges in Equipment Deployment
Critical Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Avoid legal and political obstacles when deploying communication equipment.

• Getting the paperwork wrong can result in failure to use the equipment, despite significant investment.

Steps to ensure compliance:
1. Consult with...
Objective: Avoid legal and political obstacles when deploying communication equipment.

• Getting the paperwork wrong can result in failure to use the equipment, despite significant investment.

Steps to ensure compliance:
1. Consult with appropriate authorities before importing or deploying any communication equipment into a country.
2. Ensure all necessary permits, licenses, and import documentation are completed accurately.
3. Work with local experts or consultants who understand regional regulations.
import regulations, legal compliance, communication equipment deployment

System Integration (1) -

Hiring a Radio Officer or Consultant for System Integration
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Ensure proper integration and operation of communication systems.

• If possible, recruit a Radio Officer with varied current experience in radiocommunications to manage the system.

Alternative options:
1. Hire consultants...
Objective: Ensure proper integration and operation of communication systems.

• If possible, recruit a Radio Officer with varied current experience in radiocommunications to manage the system.

Alternative options:
1. Hire consultants who specialize in radio communications for expert assistance.
2. Collaborate with an organization that has a well-established communication network and either contract their technicians or study their successful strategies.
3. Establish a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with another organization to use their resources under specified conditions in exchange for some form of favor.
radio officer, system integration, consultant, MoU

Turnkey Contracts (1) -

Understanding Turnkey Contracts for Aid Projects
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Understand the benefits and limitations of turnkey contracts.

• Turnkey contracts are suitable for long-term aid projects but not emergency communications. They involve a contractor installing an entire working communication...
Objective: Understand the benefits and limitations of turnkey contracts.

• Turnkey contracts are suitable for long-term aid projects but not emergency communications. They involve a contractor installing an entire working communication network for you, with minimal involvement from your side.

Important considerations:
1. Turnkey contracts can be expensive but ensure the system is fully functional upon delivery.
2. Avoid under-specifying equipment requirements when ordering from manufacturers; this often leads to incomplete or non-functional systems in the field.
3. Ensure quality supervision by experienced specialists during installation and shipping.
turnkey contracts, aid projects, system installation

Internal Team Setup (1) -

Establishing an In-House Communications Department
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Objective: Create a dedicated team to manage communication systems internally.

• Having your own Communications Department with someone responsible for all complex matters related to radio systems is ideal for long-term projects.

**Key...
Objective: Create a dedicated team to manage communication systems internally.

• Having your own Communications Department with someone responsible for all complex matters related to radio systems is ideal for long-term projects.

Key steps:
1. Recruit the right person for this role—someone with experience in system integration, radio operations, and technical support.
2. Ensure they have access to training and resources to maintain and troubleshoot communication equipment.
3. This approach avoids dependency on external consultants or contractors.
in-house team, communications department, internal setup

Emergency Support (1) -

Utilize DRCF for Emergency Communications Support
High Priority Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Contact the Disaster Relief Communications Foundation (DRCF) for advice and support during a disaster situation.
2. Request assistance from DRCF's on-call personnel, who can provide field support with equipment and...
Procedure:

1. Contact the Disaster Relief Communications Foundation (DRCF) for advice and support during a disaster situation.
2. Request assistance from DRCF's on-call personnel, who can provide field support with equipment and expertise.
3. Be aware that DRCF resources are limited; professional help may still be required even after DRCF assistance has been provided.
4. Consider using Standard-C and HF radios together for optimal performance, as this combination provides reliable communication in both text and voice formats.
5. Use HF radio for backup or local mode communication, especially when contacting other local agencies within the area.
6. Consult with a professional before making large investments in equipment to ensure that your choices are appropriate for your specific needs.
DRCF,Standard-C,HF radio,disaster communication,backup systems

Long Term Support (1) -

Plan for Long-Term Equipment Maintenance and Support
Standard Source: Shortwave Radio Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify a reliable maintenance company that can provide ongoing support for your radio equipment.
2. Keep the maintenance company informed about the type of equipment you have, how it is used, and any specific...
Procedure:

1. Identify a reliable maintenance company that can provide ongoing support for your radio equipment.
2. Keep the maintenance company informed about the type of equipment you have, how it is used, and any specific requirements or challenges associated with its operation.
3. Develop contingency plans for situations where immediate repairs may not be possible due to limited resources or access.
4. Maintain a detailed inventory of all spare parts and components needed for your system so that replacements can be sourced quickly when necessary.
5. Regularly review and update maintenance schedules based on the performance of your equipment and any changes in operational needs.
maintenance planning,spare parts,long-term support,equipment inventory

Radio Signaling (1) -

Optimizing Radio Equipment for Survival Signaling
High Priority Source: Signaling And Direction Finding
- The AN/PRC-90 survival radio is a part of the Army aviator's survival vest. The AN/PRC-112 will eventually replace the AN/PRC-90.
• Both radios can transmit either tone or voice. Any other type of Army radio can do the same.
• To obtain maximum...
- The AN/PRC-90 survival radio is a part of the Army aviator's survival vest. The AN/PRC-112 will eventually replace the AN/PRC-90.
• Both radios can transmit either tone or voice. Any other type of Army radio can do the same.
• To obtain maximum performance from radios, use the following procedures:

- Try to transmit only in clear, unobstructed terrain. Since radios are line-of-sight communications devices, any terrain between the radio and the receiver will block the signal.
- Keep the antenna at right angles to the rescuing aircraft. There is no signal from the tip of the antenna.
- If the radio has tone capability, place it upright on a flat, elevated surface so that you can perform other survival tasks.
- Never let the antenna touch your clothing, body, foliage, or the ground. Such contact greatly reduces the range of the signal.
- Conserve battery power. Turn the radio off when you are not using it. Do not transmit or receive constantly. In hostile territory, keep transmissions short to avoid enemy radio direction finding.
- In cold weather, keep the battery inside your clothing when not using the radio. Cold quickly drains the battery's power. Do not expose the battery to extreme heat such as desert sun. High heat may cause the battery to explode. Try to keep the radio and battery as dry as possible, as water may destroy the circuitry.
survival radio, radio signaling, emergency radio use

Emergency Procedures (1) -

Use Emergency Radio Channels in a Crisis
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
In an emergency, specific radio channels are designated for distress calls and should be programmed into your radio for quick...
In an emergency, specific radio channels are designated for distress calls and should be programmed into your radio for quick access:

|Channel|Frequency|Band|
|---|---|---|
|9|27.065MHz|HF|
|5|476.525MHz|UHF|
|35|477.275MHz|UHF|

Procedure for sending an emergency message:
1. Call any station: *AB123 This is [your call sign or name] Urgent message, calling any station, Urgent message, Over.*
2. Wait for a reply: Typically from ACRM (Australian Civil Defence Radio Service) or monitoring groups.
3. Provide your location:
- Use GPS coordinates if available: *Our location is [GPS position].*
4. State the nature of the emergency:
- Example: *Nature of emergency is [describe issue].*
5. End with *Over* to indicate you are ready for a reply.

These channels are monitored 24/7 by voluntary groups who will assist in contacting emergency services on your behalf.
emergency radio channels, distress call, HF/UHF frequencies, ACRM

Language And Interpreters (1) -

Language Barriers and Use of Interpreters for Internees
Standard Source: Emergency War Surgery
Title: Language Barriers and Use of Interpreters for Internees

Procedure:
1. Recognize that internees may not speak any language other than their own.
2. Utilize NATO STANAG 2131, Multinational Phrase Book for Use by the NATO Medical...
Title: Language Barriers and Use of Interpreters for Internees

Procedure:
1. Recognize that internees may not speak any language other than their own.
2. Utilize NATO STANAG 2131, Multinational Phrase Book for Use by the NATO Medical Services (AMedP-5(B)) as a resource for basic medical communication in multiple languages.
3. Employ retained persons or internees (especially those with medical training) as translators when necessary.
4. Be aware that EPWs may simulate mental illness to evade interrogation, particularly if they have access to an interpreter.
language barriers, medical interpreters, EPW communication

Public Health Outreach (1) -

Develop and Implement Communication Strategies for Pandemic Response
High Priority Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Procedure:

1. Create communication materials: Develop outreach messages and educational resources to inform the public about pandemic risks and response measures.
2. Address psychosocial concerns: Plan strategies to manage mental health...
Procedure:

1. Create communication materials: Develop outreach messages and educational resources to inform the public about pandemic risks and response measures.
2. Address psychosocial concerns: Plan strategies to manage mental health impacts and misinformation during a crisis.
3. Provide timely information: Ensure that accurate, honest, and up-to-date information is disseminated to the public throughout the pandemic.
4. Evaluate communication effectiveness: Monitor the impact of outreach efforts and share lessons learned with healthcare providers and public health agencies.
5. Coordinate with all levels of government: Ensure consistent messaging across federal, state, local, and tribal jurisdictions.
pandemic communication, public outreach, health education

Public Outreach (1) -

Develop a Pandemic Influenza Communications Strategy
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Objective: Prepare U.S. citizens and communities for a pandemic influenza by developing a comprehensive communications strategy.

Steps:
1. Conduct ongoing surveillance of media and surveys to assess current public (or community)...
Objective: Prepare U.S. citizens and communities for a pandemic influenza by developing a comprehensive communications strategy.

Steps:
1. Conduct ongoing surveillance of media and surveys to assess current public (or community) knowledge.
2. Develop materials such as message maps, which have been tested in focus groups.
3. Perform formative audience research to understand the needs and concerns of different populations.
4. Coordinate communication efforts across federal agencies to ensure consistent, clear, honest messages are shared broadly.
5. Facilitate community and business continuity planning by helping these sectors communicate with their constituents and prepare for a pandemic.
6. Engage the public through forums and stakeholder meetings, addressing important policy issues such as allocation of limited drugs and vaccines.
7. Develop web communications through a consolidated, centralized U.S. government website.
8. Conduct international outreach to support global partners in cooperation with the WHO Secretariat.
9. Continue efforts to raise awareness about the importance of seasonal influenza vaccine, promoting increased yearly compliance with influenza and pneumococcal vaccination.
pandemic communication strategy, public outreach, risk communication, HHS communications plan

Public Engagement (1) -

Conduct a Public Engagement Pilot Project on Pandemic Influenza
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Objective: Initiate a pilot project to engage citizens in vaccine-related policy decisions during a pandemic.

Steps:
1. Initiate the project in July 2005, focusing on discussing goals for a pandemic influenza vaccination program.
2....
Objective: Initiate a pilot project to engage citizens in vaccine-related policy decisions during a pandemic.

Steps:
1. Initiate the project in July 2005, focusing on discussing goals for a pandemic influenza vaccination program.
2. Sponsor the project with interested organizations including the Atlanta Journal Constitution, Lounsbery Foundation, Keystone Center, Institute of Medicine, University of Georgia, CDC’s National Immunization Program, HHS National Vaccine Program Office, and Study Circles Resource Center.
3. Use an innovative model to engage stakeholders from various organizations and individual citizens-at-large from the 4 principal regions of the United States.

Expected Outcomes:
• Development of an improved plan to combat pandemic influenza that is more likely to gain public support.
• Demonstration that citizens can be productively engaged in informing vaccine-related policy decisions.
• Ongoing assessment of potential benefits, including improved relationships and increased trust among participants.
public engagement pilot project, vaccine policy, pandemic influenza outreach

Interagency Coordination (1) -

Coordinate Communications Leadership Across Jurisdictional Tiers
High Priority Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
- Work with state and local officials to involve communications professionals on senior leadership teams, including roles as liaisons to national communications teams at CDC and other agencies as necessary.

• Maintain strong working...
- Work with state and local officials to involve communications professionals on senior leadership teams, including roles as liaisons to national communications teams at CDC and other agencies as necessary.

• Maintain strong working relationships with colleagues in other jurisdictions and regions, even when an outbreak may not yet have affected your area directly or may have subsided locally. The following colleagues are especially important to consider:
- Public affairs directors and information officers from other local and state health departments
- City and state government public affairs officers
- Communications staff at congressional and other government offices
- Communications staff at local and regional police, fire, and emergency management offices
- Regional health and emergency preparedness colleagues
- State and local mental health agencies
- Hospital public relations/affairs departments
- State and local Emergency Operations Center coordinators
- Federal Emergency Operations Centers

• Promote public acceptance and support for national response measures and contingency plans.
communications, coordination, pandemic, interagency, leadership

Military Reporting (1) -

Implement NBC Reporting System
Standard Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. All units must have a copy of FM 3-7, which details the NBC reporting system.
2. Refer to JP 3-11 (Draft) for the new reporting system related to toxic industrial materials.
3. Note that **JP 3-11 is still in draft...
Procedure:

1. All units must have a copy of FM 3-7, which details the NBC reporting system.
2. Refer to JP 3-11 (Draft) for the new reporting system related to toxic industrial materials.
3. Note that JP 3-11 is still in draft form and not included in this document.
4. Ensure all personnel are trained on the NBC reporting procedures outlined in FM 3-7.
NBC reporting system, FM 3-7, JP 3-11, toxic industrial materials

Contact HAMMER ACE for Emergency Communication Support

Procedure:

1. Public Affairs Contact: HQ Air Force Communications, Special Purposes Communications Division.
2. Hammer ACE is the Air Force’s special purpose, quick reaction communication unit that supports:
- Worldwide emergency and...
Procedure:

2. Hammer ACE is the Air Force’s special purpose, quick reaction communication unit that supports:

- Civil disaster relief operations

- DSN: 576-3431

- Commercial: (618) 256-5891

Define and Use SATCOM (Satellite Communications)
