Air To Ground Signaling (1) -

Signal to Aircraft That Ground Signals Have Been Seen but Not Understood
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Make a complete, clockwise circle: This signal should be used during the day or in bright moonlight to indicate that ground signals have been seen but not understood by the aircraft.
aircraft signaling, circular motion, communication signals

Swift Water Rescue (1) -

Use Swift Water Rescue Communication Signals
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Understand the terminal learning objective: Given a casualty in a swift water environment and assignment as a member of a rescue team, employ swift water rescue communication signals to support mission requirements per the...
Procedure:

1. Understand the terminal learning objective: Given a casualty in a swift water environment and assignment as a member of a rescue team, employ swift water rescue communication signals to support mission requirements per the reference (FMST.07.24).
2. Learn enabling learning objectives:
- (1) In a summer mountainous environment, prior to swift water rescue operations, when the instructor verbally states a hand and arm signal, demonstrate that signal in accordance with the references (FMST.07.24a).
- (2) In a summer mountainous environment, standing at the edge of a swift water stream, when the instructor blasts a whistle a varied amount of times, focus attention in the proper direction in accordance with the references (FMST.07.24b).
3. Practice hand and arm signals as specified by the reference material.
4. Practice whistle signals:
- A single blast: Attention to the instructor or a specific direction.
- Two blasts: Move to a safe position.
- Three or more blasts: Emergency signal, indicating immediate danger or assistance needed.

Notes:
• These communication signals are essential for coordinating rescue efforts in swift water environments.
• Practicing these signals ensures clear and effective communication during operations.
communication signals,whistle,hand signals

Line Laying (1) -

Laying Line from Various Platforms
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Select Laying Method:
- Line can be laid from helicopter, oversnow vehicle, sled, or on foot.
- Oversnow vehicles may be equipped with reel units for laying line.

2. Use Treed or Poled Line for Long-Term Use:
-...
Procedure:

1. Select Laying Method:
- Line can be laid from helicopter, oversnow vehicle, sled, or on foot.
- Oversnow vehicles may be equipped with reel units for laying line.

2. Use Treed or Poled Line for Long-Term Use:
- If line is expected to be used over an extended period, it should be treed or poled if facilities are available.
- Laying line on the surface can lead to rapid burial by snow, making recovery and servicing difficult or impossible.

3. Allow Slack Factor When Laying from Helicopter:
- A slack factor of at least 50% must be allowed on level ground when laying line from a helicopter.
- In rolling or hilly terrain, a proportional percentage should be applied.
- Once laid by this method, recovery or servicing is rarely possible. If an outage occurs, the only recourse is to re-lay the line.

4. Select Appropriate Routes for Cable:
- Avoid laying cable on routes used by heavy tracked vehicles, oversnow vehicles, or snowshoe users where possible.
- Establish separate line routes away from vehicle or foot traffic paths.
- Avoid laying cable over frozen lakes or swamps as it may sink and become frozen in position. It is also subject to shearing due to ice movement caused by thermal contraction.
- Choose routes that skirt frozen bodies of water.
- For helicopter-laid line, select longer routes where cable may be less prone to damage than shorter ones requiring less cable but offering fewer advantages.
- Avoid using ground return circuits in areas with high soil resistance and difficulty obtaining grounds.
laying line, oversnow vehicle, helicopter laying, slack factor, cable routing

Hand Carriage (1) -

Hand-Carriage Service in Cold Weather
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Schedule Hand-Carriage Service Between Units:
- Coordinate hand-carriage service between units and integrate it with aerial resupply missions where possible.
- Helicopters are commonly used for this purpose, as...
Procedure:

1. Schedule Hand-Carriage Service Between Units:
- Coordinate hand-carriage service between units and integrate it with aerial resupply missions where possible.
- Helicopters are commonly used for this purpose, as fixed-wing aircraft landing areas may not be accessible in remote locations.

2. Use Vehicles for Hand-Carriage of Personnel:
- Oversnow vehicles can be used to transport personnel or supplies during hand-carriage operations.
- Surface travel is slow and subject to changing conditions; therefore, plan accordingly.
- Special considerations include:
- Runners or dispatch riders must be trained in northern navigational techniques.
- Personnel dispatched on foot should always travel in pairs with full survival gear and rations.
- Vehicles used for hand-carriage should be equipped with radios for communication purposes.
hand carriage, oversnow vehicle, cold weather transportation

Cold Weather Communications (1) -

Ensure Communication Equipment is Operational in Northern Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Objective: Maintain communication efficiency under northern environmental conditions.

Procedure:
1. Train all communication personnel on special techniques to prepare and maintain equipment and communication nets at operational efficiency...
Objective: Maintain communication efficiency under northern environmental conditions.

Procedure:
1. Train all communication personnel on special techniques to prepare and maintain equipment and communication nets at operational efficiency under northern conditions.
2. Ensure commanders are aware of environmental factors affecting communications.
3. Implement measures necessary to overcome these challenges.
communication equipment,northern conditions,environmental factors,equipment maintenance

Security Practices (1) -

Maintain Communication Security in Cold Weather Operations
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Objective: Ensure secure communication practices to prevent unauthorized interception or interference.

Procedure:
1. Be aware that most outports have HIT radio sets and continuously monitor available nets.
2. Interference on local HF and...
Objective: Ensure secure communication practices to prevent unauthorized interception or interference.

Procedure:
1. Be aware that most outports have HIT radio sets and continuously monitor available nets.
2. Interference on local HF and VHF bands may occur due to military operations in southern USA, South Vietnam, and Northwest Europe.
3. All ranks must be proficient in good voice procedure and maintain security consciousness.
4. Avoid transmitting sensitive information over unsecured channels or in areas where interception is likely.
communication security, radio interference, voice procedure, HF/VHF bands, information protection

Urban Operations (1) -

Overcome Urban Communications Challenges
High Priority Source: FM 3 06 Urban Operations
Methods to Overcome Urban Communications Challenges

1. Identify communication barriers in urban environments, such as signal interference and physical obstructions.
2. Utilize alternative communication methods, including satellite phones, radio...
Methods to Overcome Urban Communications Challenges

1. Identify communication barriers in urban environments, such as signal interference and physical obstructions.
2. Utilize alternative communication methods, including satellite phones, radio systems, and encrypted networks.
3. Coordinate with local authorities and civilian infrastructure to establish temporary communication hubs.
4. Deploy mobile communication units to support operations in areas with limited connectivity.
5. Implement redundancy measures for critical communications to ensure reliability during urban operations.
urban,communications,challenges,methods

Document Security (1) -

Destroying a Document Securely
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Ensure the document contains sensitive information that must not be disclosed.
2. Choose a destruction method that will prevent reconstruction of the document, such as:
- Shredding with a cross-cut shredder
- Burning in a...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the document contains sensitive information that must not be disclosed.
2. Choose a destruction method that will prevent reconstruction of the document, such as:
- Shredding with a cross-cut shredder
- Burning in a secure location
- Pulping or dissolving the paper
3. Verify that all pages and components of the document are completely destroyed.
4. Dispose of the remains in a manner that prevents recovery (e.g., landfill, incineration).
5. Document the destruction process for record-keeping purposes.

Warning: Do not use methods that may allow reconstruction or partial recovery of the document.
destroy document, secure destruction, prevent disclosure, shredding, burning, document security

Battery Installation (1) -

Install Primary Batteries in DAGR
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure for Installing Primary Batteries in DAGR

Steps:
1. Ensure power to the DAGR is off.
2. Hold the unit firmly upside down with the battery pack facing up.
3. Push or pull the latch located on the battery pack to release the battery...
Procedure for Installing Primary Batteries in DAGR

Steps:
1. Ensure power to the DAGR is off.
2. Hold the unit firmly upside down with the battery pack facing up.
3. Push or pull the latch located on the battery pack to release the battery pack. Lift up on the battery pack and remove it from the unit.
4. Position the battery removal strap into the channel of the battery pack before installing new batteries.
5. Install new batteries and ensure correct polarity installation for each battery (marked on battery pack).
6. Before installing the battery pack, inspect the battery pack gasket for damage or dirt. Lubricate or replace gasket if necessary. Ensure the battery removal strap is not protruding from the battery pack.

Important Notes:
• Do not mix new batteries with old batteries. Do not mix battery types. Do not reverse battery polarity. Use only fresh/new batteries.
• If the DAGR is being used for the first time and there are no memory settings to be saved, the memory battery is not important, but still needs to be installed.
• To ensure all settings from previous usage are retained, ensure a good memory battery is installed (check memory battery date on Battery page) or external power is applied to the unit before installing or replacing the primary batteries.
• If all primary and memory power is lost, memory information is lost, and the DAGR resets to default settings after power-up.

Battery Types:
|Type |Size and Voltage |Use |Life |Rechargeable ?|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|Lithium|AA 1.5 volt|Primary|16.5 hours|No|
|Alkaline|AA 1.5 volt|Primary|11.5 hours|No|
|Alkaline|AA 1.5 volt|Primary|7 hours|Yes|
|Nickel Metal Hydride|AA 1.5 volt|Primary|10 hours|Yes|
|Lithium|1/2 AA 3.6 volt|Memory|6 months|No|

Warnings:
• If abused, lithium batteries can explode causing severe injury. Be sure to store batteries in original packaging until ready to use, and observe polarity during installation. Reverse polarity can cause damage to the battery and receiver.
DAGR battery installation, primary battery, memory battery, lithium batteries, alkaline batteries, nickel metal hydride, battery life, polarity, safety warnings

Signaling Devices (1) -

Carry Signaling Devices for Mountain Climbing
High Priority Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Signaling devices are essential to increase the chances of being located in an emergency during mountain climbing.

1. Include items such as whistles, flares, mirrors, and signal lights.
2. Ensure these devices are easily accessible and in...
Signaling devices are essential to increase the chances of being located in an emergency during mountain climbing.

1. Include items such as whistles, flares, mirrors, and signal lights.
2. Ensure these devices are easily accessible and in good working condition at all times.
signaling devices, emergency location, mountaineering

Sound Conditions (1) -

Understanding Sound Transmission in Cold Weather Environments
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Assess sound transmission factors:
- Wind, temperature, and surface conditions affect how sound travels.
2. Note elevation effects:
- With an increase in elevation, wind speeds increase and temperatures decrease, resulting...
Procedure:

1. Assess sound transmission factors:
- Wind, temperature, and surface conditions affect how sound travels.
2. Note elevation effects:
- With an increase in elevation, wind speeds increase and temperatures decrease, resulting in above normal sound intensity downwind.
3. Recognize the impact of temperature inversion:
- Temperature inversions are common in northern areas and may reduce the effect of elevation on sound transmission.
4. Understand surface effects on sound absorption:
- Soft snow absorbs sound energy while hard-crusted snow or ice reflects it.
5. Use this information for communication planning:
- Normal conversation has been carried on at distances up to 2.4 km (1 1/2 miles), and shouted words have been heard at 4 km (2 1/2 miles).
sound transmission, cold weather, communication

Battery Handling (1) -

Handling and Storage of Dry Cell Batteries in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
### Handling and Storage of Dry Cell Batteries in Cold Weather

• Use low-temperature winter type batteries (2000-series), such as Battery BA-2279 for Radio Set AN/PRC-10 or BA-2386 for Radio Set, AN/PRC-25. Store 2000-series batteries at 0° F.
• In...
### Handling and Storage of Dry Cell Batteries in Cold Weather

• Use low-temperature winter type batteries (2000-series), such as Battery BA-2279 for Radio Set AN/PRC-10 or BA-2386 for Radio Set, AN/PRC-25. Store 2000-series batteries at 0° F.
• In low ambient temperatures, carry batteries inside clothing to keep them warm.
• Reactivate cold soaked batteries (other than 2000-series) by warming thoroughly at temperatures not to exceed 100° F. These batteries give the best performance when operated at 70° F.
• Carry a spare set of batteries for field phones on person and change with the batteries in the phone at frequent intervals.
dry cell batteries, cold weather, battery storage, reactivation, performance

Microphone Protection (1) -

Protecting Microphones in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
### Protecting Microphones in Cold Weather

• Place frost shield covers over microphone and earphone elements of handsets. If not available, such covers may be fabricated out of the plastic bag material in which dry batteries are packed.
• Carry a...
### Protecting Microphones in Cold Weather

• Place frost shield covers over microphone and earphone elements of handsets. If not available, such covers may be fabricated out of the plastic bag material in which dry batteries are packed.
• Carry a spare microphone, if available, under outer garments.
microphone protection, frost shield, cold weather

Wire Handling (1) -

Handling Wire Communication Equipment in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
### Handling Wire Communication Equipment in Cold Weather

• Install teletypewriters and switchboards in heated shelters.
• Remove all snow, ice, water, and dirt from cable stubs before connecting.
• Avoid tight loops.
• Prevent excessive...
### Handling Wire Communication Equipment in Cold Weather

• Install teletypewriters and switchboards in heated shelters.
• Remove all snow, ice, water, and dirt from cable stubs before connecting.
• Avoid tight loops.
• Prevent excessive lubrication.
• Insulate teletype and crypto equipment during tactical movement to reduce the warmup period required when reestablishing communications. Salvaged sleeping bags or blankets are excellent for this purpose.
• Keep snow or moisture from the inside of telephones and switchboards.
• Place wire lines well off frequently traveled cross-country trails.
wire communication, cold weather, insulation, equipment protection

Antenna Repair (1) -

Repair and Construct Emergency Antennas
Standard Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Procedure:

1. If an antenna is broken or damaged, check if a spare antenna is available.
2. If a spare antenna is available, replace the damaged antenna with the spare.
3. If no spare antenna is available, construct an emergency antenna using...
Procedure:

1. If an antenna is broken or damaged, check if a spare antenna is available.
2. If a spare antenna is available, replace the damaged antenna with the spare.
3. If no spare antenna is available, construct an emergency antenna using field-expedient methods.
4. When constructing an emergency antenna, ensure that it meets the required length and orientation for optimal performance.
5. Adjust the antenna as needed to improve communications quality.
6. Repair any damaged antenna supports if necessary to maintain structural integrity of the antenna system.
7. Ensure that all repairs or constructions are done in a way that minimizes further damage and improves communication reliability.
repair,construct,emergency antennas,field-expedient,antenna length,orientation,improve communications

Tactical Communication Selection (1) -

Select a Means of Communication for Transmitting the Message
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure for Selecting a Means of Communication to Transmit the Message

Steps:
1. Select a means of communication based on availability and mission significance, including:
- Radio: Fast and mobile but less secure.
- Wire:...
Procedure for Selecting a Means of Communication to Transmit the Message

Steps:
1. Select a means of communication based on availability and mission significance, including:
- Radio: Fast and mobile but less secure.
- Wire: More secure than radio but requires installation time and equipment.
- Cable: Secure but limited by terrain and enemy action.
- Messenger: Very secure but slower and dependent on weather and terrain conditions.

Considerations:
• Evaluate the potential significance of the information to your mission.
• Consider security, speed, and reliability of each communication method.
communication selection, radio, wire, messenger, tactical reporting

Tactical Message Transmission (1) -

Transmit the Message to the Receiving Authority
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure for Transmitting the Message to the Receiving Authority

Steps:
1. Using a messenger: Provide the messenger with explicit reporting instructions and a message that is clear, complete, and concise (preferably written).
2....
Procedure for Transmitting the Message to the Receiving Authority

Steps:
1. Using a messenger: Provide the messenger with explicit reporting instructions and a message that is clear, complete, and concise (preferably written).
2. Using radio: Use proper radio/telephone procedures according to unit SOP and FM 24-1. Transmit only as needed due to potential enemy interception or jamming.
3. If encountering jamming or interference on the radio net:
- Within 10 minutes of the incident, transmit a meaconing, intrusion, jamming, and interference (MIJI) report via messenger, wire, or cable to your net control station.
- Use the MIJI format provided in your signal operating instructions (SOI).
message transmission, radio communication, jamming report, MIJI format

Media Interaction (1) -

Respond to Media Questions in a Military Setting
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Responding to Media Questions in a Military Setting:

1. Answer the question with which you are most comfortable when asked multiple questions.
2. Keep remarks brief and concise.
3. **Assume everything you say may be...
Procedure for Responding to Media Questions in a Military Setting:

1. Answer the question with which you are most comfortable when asked multiple questions.
2. Keep remarks brief and concise.
3. Assume everything you say may be printed or broadcast.
4. Use 'I' not 'we' when stating your opinion.
5. If you do not know the answer to a question or cannot discuss it for a particular reason (for example, OPSEC, personal privacy, and so forth), say so. Avoid using 'No comment.'
6. Inform the chain of command of your interaction with the media.

Additional Guidelines:
• Maintain operational security (OPSEC) throughout the interview.
• Discuss only subject areas related to direct responsibility and personal knowledge.
• State answers while avoiding speculation.
• Perform the interview without using jargon, acronyms, slang, or technical terms.
• Avoid making 'off-the-record' comments.
• Do not use the phrase 'No comment.'

Evaluation Preparation:
• Provide the Soldier with a realistic scenario of a simulated media interview situation. Use mock press credentials when necessary.
• Brief the Soldier that he/she is about to be interviewed by the media and the commander has encouraged his/her participation. Public affairs representatives are not present.
• Evaluate the Soldier on his/her ability to interact with the media.

Performance Measures:
1. Checked media representative for identification and credentials.
2. Stated the three recognized rights when interacting with the media.
3. Maintained OPSEC throughout the interview.
4. Discussed subject areas related to direct responsibility and personal knowledge.
5. Stated answers while avoiding speculation.
6. Performed interview without using jargon, acronyms, slang, or technical terms.
7. Employed concise remarks.
8. Did not make 'off-the-record' comments.
9. Stated answers; avoided 'No comment' response.
10. Informed the chain of command of interaction with the media.
media interview, military communication, OPSEC, public affairs, press credentials

Tactical Communication (1) -

Communicate Via a Tactical Telephone
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Conditions: Given the need to communicate via a tactical telephone, an unclassified message (approximately 25 words), and a tactical telephone installed in point-to-point configuration operating in nonsecure mode. Required...
Procedure:

Conditions: Given the need to communicate via a tactical telephone, an unclassified message (approximately 25 words), and a tactical telephone installed in point-to-point configuration operating in nonsecure mode. Required materials: ACP 125 US Supp-1 and ACP 125(E).

Steps:

1. Initiate call: Lift the handset from the cradle.
2. Verify readiness: Ensure that the distant end is ready to receive the message.
3. Communicate the message: Deliver the prepared message (see figure 113-600-2001-1 and figure 113-600-2001-2 for reference).
4. Verify receipt: Confirm that the message was received correctly.
5. Disconnect call: Return the handset to the cradle.

Evaluation Preparation:

• Ensure both tactical telephones (DNVT, TA-838/TT, TA-341/TT, TA-1042A/U and TA-954/TT) are installed and connected in point-to-point mode.
• Provide the Soldier with a prepared message of approximately 25 words.
• Brief the Soldier to communicate the message within 10 minutes without errors.

Performance Measures:

• Initiated the call (lifted the handset from the cradle).
• Verified that the distant end was ready to receive the message.
• Communicated the message correctly.
• Verified that the message was received.
• Disconnected the call (returned the handset to the cradle).

References: ACP 125 US Suppl-1 and ACP 125(E).
tactical telephone, communication procedure, point-to-point configuration, ACP 125 US Supp-1, message verification

Intelligence Reporting (1) -

Report Information of Potential Intelligence Value Using SALUTE Format
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Report information of potential intelligence value using the SALUTE format within five minutes of observation.

### Conditions:
• The commander has tasked you to report enemy activity and significant terrain/weather features.
• You...
Objective: Report information of potential intelligence value using the SALUTE format within five minutes of observation.

### Conditions:
• The commander has tasked you to report enemy activity and significant terrain/weather features.
• You have observed such activity and features.
• You have a means of communication (radio, wire, cable, or messenger).

### Standards:
• Transmit information using the SALUTE format: Size, Activity, Location, Unit, Time, and Equipment.
• Include significant terrain and weather conditions in the report.
• Ensure all six SALUTE items are correctly identified and transmitted within five minutes of observation.

### Procedure:
1. Observe enemy activity, terrain features, and weather conditions.
2. Identify the following details from your observations:
- Size: Number of personnel or vehicles involved.
- Activity: What the enemy is doing (e.g., moving, attacking, setting up positions).
- Location: Specific geographic coordinates or landmarks where the activity occurred.
- Unit: Identify any unit insignia or markings observed.
- Time: Exact time of observation (in 24-hour format if possible).
- Equipment: Types and quantities of weapons, vehicles, or other equipment observed.
3. Include significant terrain features such as hills, rivers, roads, or buildings that may impact operations.
4. Include weather conditions such as temperature, precipitation, wind speed, or visibility that could affect mission execution.
5. Transmit the information using your available communication method (radio, wire, cable, or messenger) to the designated receiving authority within five minutes of observation.
6. Ensure that all six SALUTE items are correctly identified and included in the report.
SALUTE format, intelligence reporting, enemy activity, terrain features, weather conditions

Signal Exchange (1) -

Exchange Information on OP Locations and Unit Signals
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

1. Share observation post (OP) locations: Provide all units with the exact coordinates or descriptions of each OP to ensure situational awareness.

2. Standardize unit signals: Agree on a common set of signals, codes, or...
Procedure:

1. Share observation post (OP) locations: Provide all units with the exact coordinates or descriptions of each OP to ensure situational awareness.

2. Standardize unit signals: Agree on a common set of signals, codes, or communication methods that will be used by all units involved in the operation.

3. Verify understanding: Ensure that all personnel understand and can use the shared information effectively.
observation post,unit signals,combat communication

Radiation Detection (1) -

Operate the AN/UDR-13 Radiac Set
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

1. Assemble the equipment:
- Ensure you have the following items: an AN/UDR-13 radiac set, four AAA batteries, TM 11-6665-364-12, and STP 21-24-SMCT.

2. Install the batteries:
- Insert four AAA batteries into the...
Procedure:

1. Assemble the equipment:
- Ensure you have the following items: an AN/UDR-13 radiac set, four AAA batteries, TM 11-6665-364-12, and STP 21-24-SMCT.

2. Install the batteries:
- Insert four AAA batteries into the battery compartment of the AN/UDR-13 radiac set according to the manufacturer's instructions found in TM 11-6665-364-12.

3. Power on the device:
- Turn on the AN/UDR-13 radiac set using the power switch located on the unit.

4. Read the rate and total accumulated dose:
- Use the display panel to view the current radiation rate and the total accumulated dose.

5. Set the rate and dose alarms:
- Access the alarm settings menu via the control buttons on the device.
- Adjust the rate and dose alarm thresholds as needed, following the instructions in STP 21-24-SMCT.

6. Enter sleep mode:
- Press the sleep mode button to conserve battery life when the device is not in active use.

7. Exit sleep mode:
- Press any control button on the device to wake it from sleep mode and resume normal operation.

Notes:
• Always refer to TM 11-6665-364-12 for detailed technical specifications and troubleshooting guidance.
• Ensure that the device is calibrated regularly to maintain accuracy in radiation readings.
• If any alarm is triggered, immediately take appropriate protective actions as outlined in STP 21-24-SMCT.
AN/UDR-13, radiac set, operate, radiation detection, battery installation, alarm settings, sleep mode

Decision Making Integration (1) -

Involve Communications Staff in Critical Decision-Making
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Ensure that communications staff are integrated into the decision-making process to maintain consistent messaging.

Steps:
1. Involve communications staff in all critical discussions and planning sessions during a nuclear...
Objective: Ensure that communications staff are integrated into the decision-making process to maintain consistent messaging.

Steps:
1. Involve communications staff in all critical discussions and planning sessions during a nuclear detonation incident.
2. Provide them with access to the Common Operating Picture (COP) so they can stay informed about the overall situation.
3. Ensure that messages are drafted and released at appropriate times based on the COP.

Details:
• Involving communications staff early helps ensure that messaging is consistent and timely.
• Without this involvement, communication may become outdated or inconsistent in the initial days of a nuclear detonation.
• Communications staff should be aware of response priorities to align messages with overall goals.
communications staff, decision making, COP integration, consistent messaging, nuclear detonation

Community Engagement (1) -

Use Staff as Information Ambassadors for the Community
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Utilize well-informed staff to disseminate accurate information and combat misinformation in their personal networks.

Steps:
1. Train staff to serve as official information ambassadors within their communities and personal...
Objective: Utilize well-informed staff to disseminate accurate information and combat misinformation in their personal networks.

Steps:
1. Train staff to serve as official information ambassadors within their communities and personal networks.
2. Encourage them to share reliable, actionable information with friends, family members, and coworkers.
3. Provide staff with the tools and knowledge needed to address concerns and rumors effectively.

Details:
• Staff relationships can be leveraged to spread accurate information quickly.
• This helps maintain public trust and reduces the impact of misinformation.
• Communications staff should be especially trained in this role due to their existing communication skills.
information ambassadors, community engagement, misinformation combat, staff training, nuclear detonation

Infrastructure Monitoring (1) -

Report Community Lifelines Communications Status Post-Detonation
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Action Item: Report the status of Community Lifelines communications infrastructure post-nuclear detonation.

Key elements to report include:
1. The electrical power grid
2. Cellphone towers and broadcast towers
3. **Mobile...
Action Item: Report the status of Community Lifelines communications infrastructure post-nuclear detonation.

Key elements to report include:
1. The electrical power grid
2. Cellphone towers and broadcast towers
3. Mobile communication systems
4. Satellite networks and the internet
5. Other relevant networks, including regional, state, and local partners who have agreements in place with leadership or EOCs (Emergency Operations Centers) in the affected area.

This reporting is essential for assessing the availability of IPAWS-OPEN (Integrated Public Alerting and Warning System - Open) and other alerting systems that depend on internet connectivity and remaining operational infrastructure.
IPAWS-OPEN, communications status, EOCs, nuclear detonation

Public Communication (1) -

Access CDC Formative Research on Radiation Emergencies
Standard Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Title: Access CDC Formative Research on Radiation Emergencies

Procedure:
1. Visit the official website of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) at [https://www.cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov).
2. Locate and access '_Formative...
Title: Access CDC Formative Research on Radiation Emergencies

Procedure:
1. Visit the official website of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) at [https://www.cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov).
2. Locate and access '_Formative Research IND Message Testing with the General Public_' from 2011: [https://www.cdc.gov/nceh/radiation/emergencies/pdf/IND Message Testing-Final-Report.pdf](https://www.cdc.gov/nceh/radiation/emergencies/pdf/IND Message Testing-Final-Report.pdf).

Purpose: This research provides insights into how the general public perceives and responds to radiation emergency messages, which is essential for effective communication strategies.

Keywords: Radiation emergency communication, CDC, public perception, message testing.
Radiation emergency communication, CDC, public perception, message testing

Network Stability Operations (1) -

Ensuring Stability of Communication Networks
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Develop a plan to maintain the stability of communication networks during emergencies such as pandemics or natural disasters.
2. Implement backup systems such as satellite phones, radio transmitters, and alternative power sources...
Procedure:

1. Develop a plan to maintain the stability of communication networks during emergencies such as pandemics or natural disasters.
2. Implement backup systems such as satellite phones, radio transmitters, and alternative power sources for critical infrastructure.
3. Train personnel on emergency response procedures for maintaining communication services.
4. Coordinate with telecommunications providers to ensure that essential services remain operational.
5. Monitor network performance in real-time and adjust strategies as needed to prevent outages.
communication networks,backup systems,network stability,emergency training,telecom coordination

Network Management Operations (1) -

Addressing Telecommunications Network Overloads
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Identify potential causes of network overloads such as increased usage during emergencies or system failures.
2. Implement load balancing techniques and prioritize traffic for critical services like emergency response, healthcare,...
Procedure:

1. Identify potential causes of network overloads such as increased usage during emergencies or system failures.
2. Implement load balancing techniques and prioritize traffic for critical services like emergency response, healthcare, and government operations.
3. Coordinate with telecommunications providers to ensure that capacity is expanded when needed.
4. Train personnel on managing network congestion and implementing mitigation strategies.
5. Monitor network performance in real-time and adjust strategies as needed to prevent overloads.
network overload,load balancing,critical traffic prioritization,telecom coordination,real-time monitoring

Public Health Communication (1) -

Implementing Communication Strategies for Avian Flu Preparedness
High Priority Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Develop a communication strategy that outlines how information will be shared with the public, healthcare workers, and stakeholders during an avian flu outbreak.
2. Use multiple channels (e.g., social media, press releases,...
Procedure:

1. Develop a communication strategy that outlines how information will be shared with the public, healthcare workers, and stakeholders during an avian flu outbreak.
2. Use multiple channels (e.g., social media, press releases, community meetings) to ensure broad reach and accessibility.
3. Ensure that all communication is clear, accurate, and culturally appropriate for different populations.
4. Train public health officials and healthcare workers in effective communication techniques during emergencies.
5. Establish a centralized information hub for real-time updates on avian flu outbreaks and response efforts.

Details:
• Communication strategies should be reviewed and updated regularly to reflect new developments and stakeholder feedback.
communication strategy, avian flu, public health, information sharing

Event Registration (1) -

Registering for the Bird Flu Summit and Accessing Event Benefits
Reference Source: Bird Flu Summit Brochure
Procedure:

1. Visit the official website at [www.SyllabusX.com](http://www.SyllabusX.com) or scan the provided QR code using a smartphone camera.
2. Complete the registration form with your personal details and select the appropriate...
Procedure:

1. Visit the official website at [www.SyllabusX.com](http://www.SyllabusX.com) or scan the provided QR code using a smartphone camera.
2. Complete the registration form with your personal details and select the appropriate registration package (e.g., Group 1, Group 2, etc.).
3. Take advantage of exclusive discounts on airfare, hotels, and attractions in Washington, DC by registering early.
4. Contact the event organizers at 703-466-0022 or email info@birdflusummit.com for assistance with registration or inquiries.
5. Confirm your attendance by following up via email or phone to ensure your spot is reserved.
registration,event,conference,bird flu summit

Semaphore Signaling (1) -

Semaphore Signal Code - Alphabet Grouping and Arm Positions
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Using the Semaphore Signal Code:

1. Learn the Alphabet by Grouping Letters:
- For all letters from A to G, use only one arm, making a quarter of a circle for each letter in succession.
- For letters H to N (except J),...
Procedure for Using the Semaphore Signal Code:

1. Learn the Alphabet by Grouping Letters:
- For all letters from A to G, use only one arm, making a quarter of a circle for each letter in succession.
- For letters H to N (except J), the right arm stands at position A while the left arm moves around the circle for the other letters.
- For letters O to S, the right arm stands at position B while the left arm moves around as before.
- For letters T, U, Y and the 'annul' sign, the right arm stands at position C while the left arm moves to the next point of the circle successively.
- For numerical sign J (or alphabetical sign) and letter V, the right arm stands at position D while only the left arm is moved.
- For letters W and X, the left arm stands at position E while the right arm moves down 45 degrees to show letter X.
- For letter Z, the left arm stands at position G while the right arm crosses the breast taking the position F.

2. Numerical Sign Usage:
- Letters A to I also stand for figures 1 to 9 (K standing for 0). When sending numbers, make the numerical sign first, followed by the alphabetical sign J when the figures are finished.
- The receiving station will repeat back the numbers received. If they are wrong, the sending station sends the 'annul' sign (answered by the same sign), and then resends the group of figures.

3. Communication Protocol:
- The sender must always face the station to which he is sending.
- If a word fails to make sense, the writer down will say, 'no,' when the reader will stop the sending station by raising both arms horizontally to their full extent (letter R). This demand for repetition is acknowledged by the sending station with sign J.
- The signaller receiving the message will then send the last word he has read correctly, upon which the sender will continue the message from that word.
semaphore code,arm positions,alphabet grouping,signal code,scout signals

Whistle Signaling (1) -

Whistle Signal Code - Meaning and Usage
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Using Whistle Signals:

1. One long blast:
- Means 'Silence,' 'Alert,' 'Look out for my next signal.' Also used when approaching a station.

2. Two short blasts:
- Means 'All right.'

3. **A succession of long, slow...
Procedure for Using Whistle Signals:

1. One long blast:
- Means 'Silence,' 'Alert,' 'Look out for my next signal.' Also used when approaching a station.

2. Two short blasts:
- Means 'All right.'

3. A succession of long, slow blasts:
- Means 'Go out,' 'Get farther away,' or 'Advance,' 'Extend,' 'Scatter.'

4. A succession of short, sharp blasts:
- Means 'Rally,' 'Close in,' 'Come together,' 'Fall in,' 'Danger,' 'Alarm.'

5. Three short blasts followed by one long blast from scout master:
- Calls up the patrol leaders--i.e., 'Leaders, come here.'

6. Compliance Requirements:
- Any whistle signal must be instantly obeyed at the double (as fast as you can run), no matter what other job you may be doing at the time.
whistle signals,scout signals,long blast,short blast,emergency communication

Ham Radio Training (1) -

Join the American Radio Relay League (ARRL)
Standard Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
Join the American Radio Relay League (ARRL)

• Visit arrl.org to learn more about ARRL.
• The ARRL trains people interested in learning how to use ham radios.
• Participate in communication training and support emergency communications during...
Join the American Radio Relay League (ARRL)

• The ARRL trains people interested in learning how to use ham radios.
• Participate in communication training and support emergency communications during disasters.

Radiological Emergency Communications (1) -

Provide Timely Supplemental Information During Radiological Incidents
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Ensure the public receives timely, periodic supplemental information during a radiological incident.

### Steps:
1. Describe how supplemental information is provided periodically to inform the public throughout an incident.
2....
Objective: Ensure the public receives timely, periodic supplemental information during a radiological incident.

### Steps:
1. Describe how supplemental information is provided periodically to inform the public throughout an incident.
2. Identify supplemental topics/messages that may be disseminated (e.g., status updates, reiteration of critical instructions).
3. Define methods for dissemination, such as:
- Informational bullets/posts
- Broadcasts
- Social media
- ANS (Alert Notification System)
4. Develop special news broadcasts as supplemental information to the EAS message.
5. Prepare and disseminate special news broadcasts in a timely manner after the initial EAS message is broadcast.
6. Document methods and processes used for releasing special news broadcasts of supplemental information.

### Notes:
• Supplemental messages should be consistent with the content established by criterion E.4 (protective action instructions).
• Ensure communication systems are reliable and capable of handling increased traffic during an incident.
supplemental information,radiological emergency,public notification,EAS,message dissemination

Emergency Network Redundancy (1) -

Establish Redundant Communication Links for Emergency Response
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Ensure continuous capability for notification and activation of the emergency response network.

### Steps:
1. Implement a minimum of two independent communication links to ensure redundancy.
2. **Ensure these communication links...
Objective: Ensure continuous capability for notification and activation of the emergency response network.

### Steps:
1. Implement a minimum of two independent communication links to ensure redundancy.
2. Ensure these communication links are continuously operational during an incident.
3. Document procedures for activating and using both communication links in emergency scenarios.
4. Test the redundancy of communication systems regularly to confirm reliability.
5. Train personnel on the use of all communication links to ensure they can be activated quickly and effectively when needed.

### Notes:
• Communication links may include radio, satellite, wired networks, or other reliable methods.
• Redundancy is critical for maintaining situational awareness and coordination among principal response organizations.
communication redundancy,emergency activation,notification systems,radio,satellite

Information Control (1) -

Information Control and Release Procedures
Standard Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure for Controlling and Releasing Information to the Public/Media:

1. Establish formal control mechanisms to ensure consistency and reliability of information before release.
2. Use preapproved generic press statements as a tool...
Procedure for Controlling and Releasing Information to the Public/Media:

1. Establish formal control mechanisms to ensure consistency and reliability of information before release.
2. Use preapproved generic press statements as a tool for initial responses to media inquiries, especially when specifics are not yet available.
3. Ensure that all released information is:
- Consistent with official agency positions.
- Approved by an authorized individual(s) prior to dissemination.
4. Identify individuals responsible for obtaining, verifying, and coordinating the approval of any information before it is disseminated publicly or to media.
5. Address the release of sensitive information, including:
- Information about injuries or private details of persons with access and functional needs.
- Sensitive law-enforcement information (e.g., security capabilities, adversarial/terrorist threats, tactical responses, crime scene investigations).
6. Plans/procedures must specify the types of sensitive information that may be redacted, limited in release, or withheld from public/media disclosure.
7. Ensure that preapproved generic press statements are used initially to address media inquiries without revealing specific details about response efforts or crime scene investigations.
information control, sensitive information, preapproved statements, media release procedures, emergency communication

Emergency Transport Coordination (1) -

Maintain Communication During Medical Transport
Standard Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Develop plans/procedures for maintaining communication between transport crews and the receiving hospital/medical facility.
2. Ensure that transport crews notify the medical facility in advance to allow for treatment...
Procedure:

1. Develop plans/procedures for maintaining communication between transport crews and the receiving hospital/medical facility.
2. Ensure that transport crews notify the medical facility in advance to allow for treatment preparations.
3. Identify by title/position the individual responsible for:
- Receiving notification from the transport crew
- Notifying REA staff to begin setup at the facility
4. Provide the receiving hospital/medical facility with the following information:
- The individual’s physical condition
- Vital signs
- Type of radiological contamination
- Estimated time of arrival
communication during transport, medical facility coordination, emergency transport setup, radiological contamination info

Emergency Drills (1) -

Conduct Communications Drills in Plume and Ingestion Exposure Pathway EPZs
Standard Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Test communications among emergency response organizations within both the plume and ingestion exposure pathway EPZs, including message content checks.

Procedure Steps:
1. Drill Frequency: Conduct communications drills at...
Objective: Test communications among emergency response organizations within both the plume and ingestion exposure pathway EPZs, including message content checks.

Procedure Steps:
1. Drill Frequency: Conduct communications drills at frequencies determined in evaluation criterion F.3 (refer to FEMA HSEEP guidelines for details).
2. Participation: Include all applicable emergency response organizations within the plume and ingestion exposure pathway EPZs, including state, local, tribal, and federal entities as well as nuclear facilities.
3. Message Content Check: Ensure that all communications drills include a message content check to verify understanding of transmitted information.
4. Drill Objectives: Validate communication protocols, test interoperability between agencies, and ensure accurate dissemination of critical information during emergencies.
5. Documentation: Record the results of each drill for evaluation and improvement purposes.
communications drill, plume EPZ, ingestion pathway EPZ, message content check, FEMA HSEEP

Notification Verification (1) -

Verify and Receive Notifications for Emergency Response
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Receive notifications from the licensee or other designated source.
2. Verify the authenticity of the notification/information using established verification protocols.
3. For reverse notification, notify the licensee...
Procedure:

1. Receive notifications from the licensee or other designated source.
2. Verify the authenticity of the notification/information using established verification protocols.
3. For reverse notification, notify the licensee through pre-established communication channels.
4. Document the initial Emergency Condition Level (ECL) and any subsequent changes to ECLs, ensuring that all updates are communicated in the same manner as the original notification.

Key Questions for Evaluation:
• Who notified the ORO? Licensee or other?
• For reverse notification, how was the licensee notified?
• Was the notification/information verified? How?
• What was the initial ECL? Were changes to ECLs communicated in the same manner?
notifications, verification, reverse notification, ECL, communication protocols

Emergency Operations (1) -

Verify Communication System Functionality and Redundancy
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Ensure communication systems are fully functional, continuously available, and redundant.

Steps:
1. Identify the types of communication systems and methods available (e.g., radio, satellite, landline).
2. Confirm that each system...
Objective: Ensure communication systems are fully functional, continuously available, and redundant.

Steps:
1. Identify the types of communication systems and methods available (e.g., radio, satellite, landline).
2. Confirm that each system is fully functional and accessible during an emergency.
3. Demonstrate familiarity with the use of each communication system/method by personnel.
4. Conduct a communications check with other jurisdictions, field teams, and/or support organizations to ensure interoperability.

Details:
• Communication systems must be tested regularly for functionality.
• Redundancy should be ensured through multiple independent systems.
• Personnel must be trained in the use of all available communication methods.
communication system, redundancy, functionality check, interoperability

Training And Procedures (1) -

Perform Content Check for Message Transmission and Reception
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Ensure that messages are transmitted, received, and understood during an emergency.

Steps:
1. Transmit a test message using the communication system to be used during an actual radiological emergency.
2. Confirm that the message...
Objective: Ensure that messages are transmitted, received, and understood during an emergency.

Steps:
1. Transmit a test message using the communication system to be used during an actual radiological emergency.
2. Confirm that the message is received by all relevant personnel or organizations.
3. Verify that the message content is fully understood by recipients (i.e., perform a 'content check').
4. Document the results of the content check and any issues encountered.

Details:
• The test message should be representative of an actual emergency scenario.
• All personnel must be trained to understand and respond to such messages.
message transmission, content check, training

Radiological Emergency Communication (1) -

Communicating During and After a Nuclear Power Plant Incident
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Refer to FEMA/Federal Radiological Preparedness Coordinating Committee, *Communicating During and After a Nuclear Power Plant Incident*, June 2013.

2. Develop a communication plan that includes:
- Pre-incident...
Procedure:

1. Refer to FEMA/Federal Radiological Preparedness Coordinating Committee, *Communicating During and After a Nuclear Power Plant Incident*, June 2013.

2. Develop a communication plan that includes:
- Pre-incident communication strategies, such as public education campaigns about nuclear power plant risks and emergency response procedures.
- During an incident protocols for issuing timely, accurate, and consistent information to the public through various channels (e.g., media, social media, community alerts).
- Post-incident communication efforts, including updates on recovery progress, health advisories, and long-term monitoring of radiation exposure.

3. Coordinate with local, state, and federal agencies to ensure a unified message is delivered to the public during and after an incident.

4. Train personnel in effective communication techniques, especially those related to radiological emergencies and nuclear incidents.
nuclear incident communication, radiological emergency, FEMA guidance

Emergency Broadcast System (1) -

Review FCC Memorandum 98-329 on Emergency Broadcast System
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Purpose: Review the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) Memorandum 98-329 regarding emergency broadcast system regulations.

Steps:
1. Access *Memorandum 98-329, Legal Report and Order “In the Matter of Amendment of Part 73, Subpart G,...
Purpose: Review the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) Memorandum 98-329 regarding emergency broadcast system regulations.

Steps:
1. Access *Memorandum 98-329, Legal Report and Order “In the Matter of Amendment of Part 73, Subpart G, of the Commission’s Rules Regarding the Emergency Broadcast System,”* released December 23, 1990.
2. Review the amendments to Part 73, Subpart G, which governs the Emergency Broadcast System (EBS).
3. Ensure compliance with EBS regulations for emergency communication during radiological or other emergencies.

Details:
• This document outlines legal requirements for broadcasters and emergency management agencies related to public alerts.
• It includes procedures for activating and managing the EBS.
FCC Memorandum 98-329, Emergency Broadcast System, EBS regulations, emergency communication

2. Verify that all staff, children, and parents can receive alerts...
Objective: Ensure the emergency notification system is functional and reliable.

Steps:

2. Verify that all staff, children, and parents can receive alerts through the system.
3. Document test results and address any malfunctions immediately.

2. Use multiple communication channels (e.g.,...
Objective: Ensure parents are aware of drills and verification processes.
